Ship enters graving dock. Both NA and ME representatives attend. NA focuses on hull bottom plating, bilge keels. ME focuses on stern tube, propeller shaft seal (stuffing box), sea chests.

**Step 4 – Repair Specification & Execution (3 marks):**
- **NA work:** Plate renewals, doubler plate welding, blasting (Sa 2.5 per ISO 8501-1), coating with approved anti-corrosive and anti-fouling paint system.
- **ME work:** Stern tube bearing renewal, propeller shaft straightness check, sea valve renewal, bow-thruster grid cleaning.

**Step 5 – Re-Floating & Sea Trials (1 mark):**
NA checks trim/stability, ME checks shaft alignment, vibration, bearing temperatures. Class surveyor issues **Class endorsement**.

#### Model Answer (b) — IACS Unified Requirements [7 marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
IACS, founded **1969**, has 12 member societies covering over **90%** of world tonnage. It issues **Unified Requirements (UR)** to harmonise rules across members.

**Influence on Design (3 marks):**
- **UR S11** – Strength of hull girders → NA-led structural design.
- **UR M3** – Main engine and auxiliary machinery → ME-led machinery design.
- **UR Z17, Z23** – Procedure for design review → joint quality management.

**Influence on Survey (3 marks):**
- **UR Z1, Z2, Z3** – Hull survey rules (ESP for bulk carriers/tankers) → NA-led thickness measurement.
- **UR Z7, Z9** – Machinery survey → ME-led.
- **UR Z11** – Surveyor's qualification → both professionals need same competency.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ Naval Architect = **hull form, structure, stability**.
- ☐ Marine Engineer = **machinery, propulsion, electrical**.
- ☐ The **engine room bulkhead** is the traditional boundary but is *blurred* in modern all-electric ships.
- ☐ **Classification surveys** are *voluntary* but practically mandatory; **statutory surveys** are *legally mandatory*.
- ☐ **Survey cycle:** Annual $\rightarrow$ Intermediate (2nd–3rd year) $\rightarrow$ Special (every 5 years) $\rightarrow$ Renewal.
- ☐ **Docking survey** must occur **at least twice** in a 5-year cycle.
- ☐ **ESP (Enhanced Survey Programme)** applies to **bulk carriers and tankers > 20 years**.
- ☐ **IACS** has 12 members (incl. **ABS, DNV, LR, BV, IRS, ClassNK, KR, RINA**).
- ☐ **Joint responsibilities** include: General Arrangement (GA), Vibration & Noise, Shaft Alignment, Sea Trials, Repair Specification (RS), Weight & COG, Class & Statutory Approval.
- ☐ **Repair types:** Voyage (at sea), Port (afloat), Dry-dock (graving/floating dock).
- ☐ **Always** tabulate answers with a clear **NA column vs ME column vs Joint column** — this is the KTU valuation key pattern.
- ☐ Mention **statutory codes** (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line, MLC) at least once — examiner marks highly for regulatory awareness.
- ☐ Use **"joint responsibility"** and **"coordinated working"** vocabulary liberally — it scores boundary-cross-over marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Warning:**
> - Never attribute shaft alignment to one professional alone — it is **strictly joint**.
> - Never forget that **fire-fighting systems** (CO2, foam, water mist) are **ME responsibility** for installation but **NA responsibility** for structural penetration and integrity.
> - **Automation & Integrated Bridge Systems** are joint — ME designs the hardware/software; NA ensures human-factors and bridge visibility.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IMO/MARPOL Regulations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IMO/MARPOL Regulations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 International Maritime Organization (IMO)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IMO** is the United Nations specialized agency responsible for the safety, security, and environmental performance of international shipping. Its main task is to create and maintain a comprehensive regulatory framework for the global shipping industry.

| **Parameter** | **Specification** |
|---|---|
| Full Name | International Maritime Organization |
| Established | 17 March 1948 (Geneva) — first IMO Convention |
| Operational Name | Adopted in 1982 |
| Headquarters | **4 Albert Embankment, London, United Kingdom** |
| Parent Body | United Nations Economic and Social Council (ECOSOC) |
| Member States | **176 Member States + 3 Associate Members** |
| Primary Function | Global standard-setting authority for maritime safety, security, and pollution prevention |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Think of the **IMO as the "United Nations of the Seas."** Just as the UN sets ground rules for how countries deal with each other on land, the IMO creates the **"rule book"** that ships of all flags must follow when they sail across oceans. Without the IMO, every country would make its own shipping laws, and a tanker from Country A would have no idea what rules apply when it docks in Country B. The IMO ensures **one global maritime law** that protects ships, sailors, and the marine environment.

### 1.2 Key IMO Conventions (The "Big Five")

| **Convention** | **Year** | **Primary Focus** |
|---|---|---|
| **SOLAS** (Safety of Life at Sea) | 1974 | Construction, equipment, and operational safety of merchant ships |
| **MARPOL** (Marine Pollution) | 1973/78 | Prevention of pollution from ships (oil, chemicals, air, sewage, garbage) |
| **STCW** (Standards of Training, Certification & Watchkeeping) | 1978 | Crew training and certification standards |
| **COLREG** (Collision Regulations) | 1972 | Navigation rules to prevent collisions at sea |
| **MLC** (Maritime Labour Convention) | 2006 | Seafarers' working and living conditions |
| **LOAD LINES** | 1966 | Minimum freeboard limits for safe operation |

---

## 1.3 MARPOL Convention — The Heart of Marine Pollution Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MARPOL 73/78** is the main international convention covering the prevention of pollution of the marine environment by ships from operational or accidental causes. It is a combination of the **1973 Convention** and the **1978 Protocol** (which was adopted in response to a spate of tanker accidents in 1976-77).

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)
> The **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships (MARPOL)** is the principal international treaty aimed at **preventing pollution of the marine environment by ships** from operational or accidental causes. It addresses pollution from **oil, noxious liquid substances, packaged harmful substances, sewage, garbage, and air pollution** through six technical Annexes.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine your ship is a **"floating factory"** that produces multiple types of waste: dirty bilge water, exhaust smoke, sewage, garbage, and chemical residues. Before MARPOL, ships could simply dump all this waste straight into the ocean — the sea was treated like a **"bottomless garbage bin."** MARPOL acts like a **strict waste-management policy** enforced on a global scale. It says: *"You may not throw away waste freely. Each type of waste has its own disposal rule, special equipment is required, and inspectors will check your ship."*

### 1.4 The Six Annexes of MARPOL — Quick Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask students to list and explain the six MARPOL Annexes. Memorize this table completely.

| **Annex** | **Pollution Type** | **Adopted** | **Entered into Force** | **Applies To** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Annex I** | Oil | 1973 | 2 Nov 1983 | All ships (oil tankers stricter) |
| **Annex II** | Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS) in bulk | 1973 | 6 Apr 1987 | Chemical/product tankers |
| **Annex III** | Harmful substances in packaged form | 1973 | 1 Jul 1992 | All ships carrying packaged goods |
| **Annex IV** | Sewage | 1973 | 27 Sep 2003 | All ships ≥ 400 GT or carrying > 15 persons |
| **Annex V** | Garbage | 1973 | 31 Dec 1988 | All ships |
| **Annex VI** | Air Pollution | 1997 (Protocol) | 19 May 2005 | All ships |

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Areas** under MARPOL are sea areas where the **discharge of certain pollutants is either totally prohibited or subject to particularly strict controls** because of their oceanographic and ecological characteristics. Examples: Mediterranean Sea, Baltic Sea, Black Sea, Red Sea, Gulf of Aden, North Sea, Antarctic Area, and various Emission Control Areas (ECAs).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Each MARPOL Annex

### 2.1.1 Annex I — Prevention of Pollution by Oil

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Annex I is the **oldest, most comprehensive, and most heavily enforced** of all MARPOL Annexes. It is **the most frequently tested topic** in KTU ESE for Marine Engineering.

**Key Requirements:**

1. **IOPP Certificate (International Oil Pollution Prevention Certificate):**
   - Mandatory for all oil tankers and ships > 100 GT trading internationally.
   - Issued after survey by the flag State Administration or Recognized Organization (RO).

2. **Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT):**
   - Crude oil tankers of **≥ 20,000 DWT** delivered after 1 June 1982 must have SBT.
   - Purpose: Dedicated ballast capacity that **does not mix with cargo oil**, eliminating oily ballast water.

3. **Crude Oil Washing (COW):**
   - Required for crude oil tankers ≥ 20,000 DWT delivered after 1 June 1982.
   - Crude oil itself is used to wash cargo tanks, removing oil residues.

4. **Double Hull Construction:**
   - Required for new tankers ≥ 600 DWT (phased between 1993–2010).
   - Reduces oil spill risk in case of hull damage.

5. **Oil Discharge Limits (outside Special Areas):**

| **Ship Type** | **Condition** | **Limit** |
|---|---|---|
| All ships (machinery bilge water) | Using OWS with filtering equipment | **≤ 15 ppm** oil in water |
| All ships | Discharging without dilution | Not permitted |
| Oil tanker (ballast/cargo wash) | Using SBT/COW, en route, instantaneous rate | **≤ 60 L/nautical mile** |
| Oil tanker (ballast/cargo wash) | Total oil discharged per voyage | **≤ 1/30,000 of total cargo** carried |

6. **Oil Discharge Limits (Special Areas — stricter):**
   - Oil tankers **cannot discharge cargo residues or oily ballast** in Special Areas.
   - Machinery bilge water: ≤ 15 ppm via OWS only.

### 2.1.2 Annex II — Noxious Liquid Substances in Bulk

Categorizes noxious liquid substances into **4 categories** based on hazard:

| **Category** | **Hazard Level** | **Discharge Rules** |
|---|---|---|
| **Category X** | Highest hazard | **No discharge allowed** into the marine environment |
| **Category Y** | High hazard | Discharge under strict conditions (pre-wash, dilution) |
| **Category Z** | Moderate hazard | Discharge under moderate conditions |
| **Category OS** | Other Substances | Less restrictive (still regulated) |

**NLS Certificate of Fitness** required for chemical tankers.

### 2.1.3 Annex III — Harmful Substances in Packaged Form

- Controls labeling, packaging, stowage, and documentation of harmful substances.
- Uses **UN-based labeling system** (UN numbers, hazard classes).
- Applies to containers, drums, sacks, etc.

### 2.1.4 Annex IV — Sewage

> [!NOTE]
> **Sewage** means drainage from toilets, urinals, medical spaces, or spaces containing living animals.

- Discharge prohibited within **4 nautical miles** of the nearest land.
- Between 4–12 nm: discharge allowed if ship has a **certified Sewage Treatment Plant (STP)** or **comminuting and disinfecting system** meeting IMO standards.
- **ISPP (International Sewage Pollution Prevention) Certificate** required.

### 2.1.5 Annex V — Garbage

**Discharge zones for different garbage types:**

| **Garbage Type** | **Outside Special Area** | **In Special Area** |
|---|---|---|
| Plastic (incl. synthetic ropes, fishing nets) | **Total ban** | **Total ban** |
| Food waste (comminuted/ground, ≥ 25 mm) | Discharge ≥ 3 nm from land | Discharge ≥ 12 nm from land |
| Domestic waste (paper, rags, glass, metal) | Discharge ≥ 12 nm from land | **Total ban** |
| Cargo residues (not harmful) | Discharge ≥ 12 nm from land | **Total ban** |

Every ship must carry a **Garbage Management Plan** and maintain a **Garbage Record Book**.

### 2.1.6 Annex VI — Prevention of Air Pollution

**Key emission limits:**

| **Pollutant** | **Limit (General)** | **Limit in ECA (Emission Control Area)** |
|---|---|---|
| Sulfur Oxide (SOx) in fuel | **≤ 0.50% m/m** (since 2020) | **≤ 0.10% m/m** (since 2015) |
| Nitrogen Oxide (NOx) Tier I | Per formula (1990s engines) | — |
| NOx Tier II | Tighter formula (post-2000) | — |
| NOx Tier III | — | **Tightest formula** (post-2016 in ECA) |
| Ozone-depleting substances (ODS) | **Banned** | **Banned** |
| Volatile Organic Compounds (VOC) | Regulated in port areas | — |

**NOx Emission Formula (KTU High-Yield):**

$$n_{NOx} = 9.8 \cdot n^{-0.233} \quad \text{(Tier I, g/kWh)}$$

$$n_{NOx} = 7.7 \cdot n^{-0.199} \quad \text{(Tier II, g/kWh)}$$

$$n_{NOx} = 2.0 \cdot n^{-0.233} \quad \text{(Tier III in ECA, g/kWh)}$$

> Where **n** is the rated engine speed in **rpm**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **NOx formula** is a **favourite 14-mark question** in KTU ESE. You must know the formula, what each term means, and how to compute the maximum permissible NOx emission for a given engine speed.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master reference table** for all numerical questions from this topic.

| **#** | **Formula / Limit** | **Description** | **Unit / Threshold** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Oil content in bilge discharge | Maximum oil in water (outside special areas) | **≤ 15 ppm** |
| 2 | Oil discharge rate | Maximum instantaneous rate from oil tanker | **≤ 60 L/nautical mile** |
| 3 | Total oil per voyage | Maximum oil discharged per voyage | **≤ 1/30,000 of cargo** |
| 4 | NOx Tier I | $9.8 \cdot n^{-0.233}$ | g/kWh |
| 5 | NOx Tier II | $7.7 \cdot n^{-0.199}$ | g/kWh |
| 6 | NOx Tier III (ECA) | $2.0 \cdot n^{-0.233}$ | g/kWh |
| 7 | SOx limit (global, 2020+) | Maximum sulfur in fuel | **0.50% m/m** |
| 8 | SOx limit (ECA) | Maximum sulfur in fuel | **0.10% m/m** |
| 9 | Sewage discharge | Prohibited within | **4 nm from land** |
| 10 | Sewage with STP | Allowed from | **4 to 12 nm** |
| 11 | Garbage discharge (comminuted food) | Minimum distance | **3 nm from land** |
| 12 | Garbage discharge (domestic waste) | Minimum distance | **12 nm from land** |
| 13 | Plastic discharge | Always | **Banned** |
| 14 | EEDI reference speed $V_{ref}$ | Average service speed on laden voyage | knots |

> [!NOTE]
> Note the **horizontal line break** between sub-tables — the markdown table syntax uses `|` to delimit columns only. For mathematical "such that" or "where" in the text, we deliberately avoid using `|` inside the cell to prevent table-breaking.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does a Marine Engineer care about MARPOL?**
> 1. **Career Requirement:** Officer-in-Charge of an Engineering Watch must demonstrate knowledge of MARPOL during competency exams (STCW).
> 2. **Operational Impact:** Non-compliance can result in **detention, fines, criminal prosecution**, and **port state control** failures.
> 3. **Design Influence:** MARPOL dictates onboard equipment (OWS, STP, Incinerator, SOx scrubbers, EGR for NOx).
> 4. **Fuel Procurement:** Drives the global shift to **low-sulfur fuels (VLSFO, ULSFO)** and alternative fuels (LNG, methanol, ammonia).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Oil Discharge Compliance Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An oil tanker of **80,000 DWT** uses Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT) and Crude Oil Washing (COW) to discharge oily ballast water while en route. The voyage covers **1,200 nautical miles**. The oil-water separator (OWS) on board processes the bilge water, and the maximum permissible instantaneous discharge rate is **60 L/nautical mile**. The oil content measured at the outlet of OWS is **12 ppm**.
>
> **(a)** Verify whether the ship complies with the oil discharge criteria.
> **(b)** Calculate the maximum oil that can be discharged per voyage using the 1/30,000 rule.
> **(c)** If the tanker's total cargo capacity is 90,000 m³ and oil density is 880 kg/m³, determine the actual mass of oil carried.

### Step-by-Step Solution (Exhaustive)

#### Part (a): Verification of Compliance

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant regulation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> MARPOL Annex I, Regulation 34 governs the discharge of oil from machinery spaces. Outside special areas, oil-containing effluent from bilges must pass through an Oil Filtering Equipment (OFE) with a content **not exceeding 15 ppm**.

**Step 2 — Compare measured value with the limit.**

The oil content in the OWS outlet is given as:

$$C_{measured} = 12 \text{ ppm}$$

The regulatory limit is:

$$C_{limit} = 15 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the inequality.**

$$C_{measured} < C_{limit}$$

$$12 \text{ ppm} < 15 \text{ ppm} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion for Part (a).**

> Since **12 ppm < 15 ppm**, the OWS effluent **complies with MARPOL Annex I Regulation 34** discharge criteria. The discharge is therefore permitted outside special areas.

#### Part (b): Maximum Oil Discharge Per Voyage

**Step 1 — State the regulation.**

> For tankers using SBT and COW en route, the total quantity of oil discharged per voyage **must not exceed 1/30,000 of the total cargo carrying capacity**.

**Step 2 — Express the relationship.**

Let:
- $V_{cargo}$ = total cargo carrying capacity in m³
- $\rho_{oil}$ = density of cargo oil in kg/m³
- $M_{cargo}$ = total mass of oil carried in kg
- $M_{max,voyage}$ = maximum oil discharge per voyage in kg

The regulation is:

$$\frac{M_{cargo}}{M_{max,voyage}} = 30{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Express $M_{cargo}$ in terms of $V_{cargo}$.**

$$M_{cargo} = V_{cargo} \times \rho_{oil}$$

Given: $V_{cargo} = 90{,}000 \text{ m}^3$, $\rho_{oil} = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$:

$$M_{cargo} = 90{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \times 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$M_{cargo} = 79{,}200{,}000 \text{ kg}$$

$$M_{cargo} = 7.92 \times 10^{7} \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $M_{max,voyage}$.**

$$M_{max,voyage} = \frac{M_{cargo}}{30{,}000}$$

$$M_{max,voyage} = \frac{7.92 \times 10^{7} \text{ kg}}{30{,}000}$$

$$M_{max,voyage} = 2{,}640 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion for Part (b).**

> The maximum permissible total oil discharge per voyage is **2,640 kg** (or 2.64 metric tonnes) under the 1/30,000 rule.

**Step 6 — Cross-check with instantaneous rate limit (60 L/nautical mile).**

Voyage distance:

$$D = 1{,}200 \text{ nautical miles}$$

Maximum discharge volume:

$$V_{max} = 60 \text{ L/nm} \times 1{,}200 \text{ nm} = 72{,}000 \text{ L} = 72 \text{ m}^3$$

Maximum oil mass at 15 ppm (worst case at the limit):

$$M_{cross-check} = 72 \text{ m}^3 \times 1{,}000 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 15 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$M_{cross-check} = 1.08 \text{ kg}$$

> This is far below the **2,640 kg** limit, so the **60 L/nm rule is the binding constraint** for this voyage.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — NOx Emission Calculation (Tier II)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A ship's main propulsion diesel engine has a rated speed of **125 rpm**. The engine was built in **2011** and is intended to operate in **general waters (non-ECA)**. Determine the maximum permissible NOx emission under MARPOL Annex VI.

### Step-by-Step Solution (Exhaustive)

**Step 1 — Identify the applicable Tier.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> MARPOL Annex VI Regulation 13 has three Tiers of NOx control. The applicable Tier depends on the **ship construction date** and **operating area**:
> - **Tier I:** Ships built on/after 1 Jan 2000.
> - **Tier II:** Ships built on/after 1 Jan 2011.
> - **Tier III:** Ships built on/after 1 Jan 2016, operating in **NOx Emission Control Areas (NECA)**.

**Step 2 — Match ship data to Tier.**

- Built in 2011 → **Tier II** (post-2011).
- Operating in **general waters** (non-ECA) → **Tier II** is applicable.
- For Tier III to apply, the ship must also operate in a NECA.

**Step 3 — State the Tier II NOx formula.**

The general MARPOL Annex VI Tier II formula is:

$$NOx_{Tier\,II} = 7.7 \cdot n^{-0.199} \quad \text{(g/kWh)}$$

where $n$ is the **rated engine speed in rpm**.

**Step 4 — Substitute the given values.**

$$n = 125 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the exponent.**

$$-0.199 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $n^{-0.199}$.**

$$n^{-0.199} = (125)^{-0.199}$$

Take natural logarithm:

$$\ln(125) = \ln(5^3) = 3 \cdot \ln(5) = 3 \times 1.6094 = 4.8283$$

Multiply by the exponent:

$$-0.199 \times 4.8283 = -0.9608$$

Exponentiate:

$$e^{-0.9608} = 0.3825$$

Therefore:

$$n^{-0.199} \approx 0.3825$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back into the Tier II formula.**

$$NOx_{Tier\,II} = 7.7 \times 0.3825$$

$$NOx_{Tier\,II} = 2.945 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Step 8 — Final answer.**

$$\boxed{NOx_{Tier\,II} \approx 2.95 \text{ g/kWh}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The engine's specific NOx emission must not exceed **2.95 g per kWh of useful work output** to comply with MARPOL Annex VI Tier II requirements.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — SOx Reduction Required in ECA

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A bulk carrier currently burns HFO (Heavy Fuel Oil) with a **sulfur content of 2.7% m/m**. The ship must enter the **Baltic Sea ECA**, where the limit is **0.10% m/m**. Calculate:
> **(a)** The **percentage reduction** in sulfur content required.
> **(b)** The **mass of SO₂ emitted per tonne of fuel** before and after the change, and the savings.

### Step-by-Step Solution (Exhaustive)

**Step 1 — State the chemical basis.**

When sulfur in fuel combusts, the reaction is:

$$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$$

The molecular weights are:
- $M_S = 32.06$ g/mol
- $M_{SO_2} = 64.07$ g/mol
- Ratio: $M_{SO_2}/M_S = 64.07/32.06 \approx 2.0$

So every **1 kg of sulfur** produces approximately **2 kg of SO₂**.

**Step 2 — Part (a): Percentage reduction in sulfur content.**

$$S_{HFO} = 2.7 \text{ % m/m}, \quad S_{ECA} = 0.10 \text{ % m/m}$$

$$\text{Reduction (\%)} = \frac{S_{HFO} - S_{ECA}}{S_{HFO}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Reduction (\%)} = \frac{2.7 - 0.10}{2.7} \times 100$$

$$\text{Reduction (\%)} = \frac{2.6}{2.7} \times 100$$

$$\text{Reduction (\%)} = 0.96296 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{Reduction} = 96.30 \text{ \%}}$$

**Step 3 — Part (b): Mass of SO₂ per tonne of fuel.**

For HFO (before):

$$m_{SO_2,\,HFO} = 1{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 0.027 \times 2.0 = 54.0 \text{ kg SO}_2$$

For ECA-compliant fuel (after):

$$m_{SO_2,\,ECA} = 1{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 0.0010 \times 2.0 = 2.0 \text{ kg SO}_2$$

**Step 4 — Compute savings.**

$$\Delta m_{SO_2} = 54.0 - 2.0 = 52.0 \text{ kg SO}_2 \text{ per tonne of fuel}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Savings} = 52 \text{ kg SO}_2 \text{ per tonne of fuel}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a ship consuming **100 tonnes of fuel per day** in the ECA, the SO₂ savings would be **5,200 kg per day** — an enormous reduction in acidifying air pollution.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: IMO Hierarchy and MARPOL Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[UN General Assembly] --> B[UN ECOSOC]
    B --> C[IMO Assembly]
    C --> D[IMO Council]
    D --> E1[Maritime Safety Committee MSC]
    D --> E2[Marine Environment Protection Committee MEPC]
    D --> E3[Legal Committee]
    D --> E4[Technical Cooperation Committee]
    D --> E5[Facilitation Committee]

    E1 --> F1[SOLAS Convention]
    E1 --> F2[STCW Convention]
    E1 --> F3[COLREG Convention]

    E2 --> G1[MARPOL Convention 73 / 78]
    E1 --> F4[Load Lines Convention]

    G1 --> H1[Annex I Oil]
    G1 --> H2[Annex II NLS in Bulk]
    G1 --> H3[Annex III Packaged Substances]
    G1 --> H4[Annex IV Sewage]
    G1 --> H5[Annex V Garbage]
    G1 --> H6[Annex VI Air Pollution]

    H1 --> I1[IOPP Certificate]
    H1 --> I2[SBT COW OWS]
    H1 --> I3[Double Hull]

    H6 --> J1[SOx Limit]
    H6 --> J2[NOx Tier I II III]
    H6 --> J3[EEDI EEXI CII]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: MARPOL Discharge Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Ship Wants to Discharge Waste] --> KQ{Waste Type?}

    KQ --> K1[Oily Bilge Water]
    KQ --> K2[Sewage]
    KQ --> K3[Garbage]
    KQ --> K4[Air Emissions]
    KQ --> K5[NLS Cargo Residues]

    K1 --> K1Q{Inside Special Area?}
    K1Q -- YES --> K1A[Total ban on oily discharge from cargo]
    K1Q -- NO --> K1B{OWS available and 15 ppm limit?}
    K1B -- YES --> K1C[Permitted at 15 ppm]
    K1B -- NO --> K1D[NOT permitted]

    K2 --> K2Q{Within 4 nm of land?}
    K2Q -- YES --> K2A[Total ban]
    K2Q -- NO --> K2B{STP installed and working?}
    K2B -- YES --> K2C[Permitted up to 12 nm]
    K2B -- NO --> K2D[Only beyond 12 nm]

    K3 --> K3Q{Plastic in Garbage?}
    K3Q -- YES --> K3A[ALWAYS Banned]
    K3Q -- NO --> K3B{Comminuted food waste?}
    K3B -- YES --> K3C[Permitted beyond 3 nm]
    K3B -- NO --> K3D{Domestic waste?}
    K3D -- YES --> K3E[Permitted beyond 12 nm]
    K3D -- NO --> K3F[Follow special category rules]

    K4 --> K4Q{Inside ECA?}
    K4Q -- YES --> K4A[SOx 0.10 percent]
    K4Q -- NO --> K4B[SOx 0.50 percent]
    K4A --> K4C[NOx Tier III if post 2016]
    K4B --> K4D[NOx Tier II if post 2011]

    K5 --> K5Q{Category of NLS?}
    K5Q -- X --> K5A[No discharge at all]
    K5Q -- Y --> K5B[Discharge under strict pre-wash]
    K5Q -- Z --> K5C[Discharge under moderate conditions]
    K5Q -- OS --> K5D[Less restrictive discharge]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: MARPOL Annex VI — Air Pollution Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[MARPOL Annex VI] --> L2[Chapter 1 General]
    L1 --> L3[Chapter 2 Survey and Certification]
    L1 --> L4[Chapter 3 NOx Control]
    L1 --> L5[Chapter 4 SOx Control]
    L1 --> L6[Chapter 5 VOC Control]
    L1 --> L7[Chapter 6 Energy Efficiency]

    L4 --> L4A[Tier I 1990 2000]
    L4 --> L4B[Tier II 2011]
    L4 --> L4C[Tier III 2016 plus NECA]

    L5 --> L5A[Global Cap 0.50 percent S]
    L5 --> L5B[ECA Cap 0.10 percent S]
    L5 --> L5C[EGCS Scrubbers Allowed]

    L7 --> L7A[EEDI for new ships]
    L7 --> L7B[EEXI for existing ships]
    L7 --> L7C[CII Operational Carbon Intensity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above **block-level functional diagrams** show how IMO's committees (MSC, MEPC) generate conventions like MARPOL, and how MARPOL's six Annexes branch into specific compliance mechanisms. This is a typical KTU "explain the structure" type question worth 7–14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(3 Marks)** *List the six Annexes of MARPOL and identify the type of pollution each Annex aims to control.*

#### Model Answer (3 marks distribution):

> The six Annexes of the MARPOL Convention (73/78) and their associated pollution types are:
>
> 1. **Annex I** — Prevention of pollution by **oil** (1 mark)
> 2. **Annex II** — Control of pollution by **noxious liquid substances (NLS)** carried in bulk (½ mark)
> 3. **Annex III** — Prevention of pollution by **harmful substances carried in packaged form** (½ mark)
> 4. **Annex IV** — Prevention of pollution by **sewage** from ships (½ mark)
> 5. **Annex V** — Prevention of pollution by **garbage** from ships (½ mark)
> 6. **Annex VI** — Prevention of **air pollution** from ships (½ mark)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(3 Marks)** *Define "Special Area" under MARPOL. Give any two examples of Special Areas for Annex I (oil) pollution.*

#### Model Answer (3 marks distribution):

> **Definition (2 marks):** A "Special Area" under MARPOL is a sea area where, because of its **recognized oceanographic and ecological characteristics** and the **particular nature of its shipping traffic**, the adoption of **special mandatory methods for the prevention of sea pollution** is required. Within Special Areas, **discharge controls are more stringent** than in other sea areas.
>
> **Examples (1 mark, ½ each):**
> - The **Mediterranean Sea**
> - The **Baltic Sea**
> - (Also valid: Black Sea, Red Sea, Gulf of Aden, Antarctic Area, North Sea)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14-Mark Questions)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam) gives a **choice** between two questions in each module. Both questions are 14 marks each with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The same pattern is followed below.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain in detail the requirements of MARPOL Annex I for the prevention of oil pollution from ships. Your answer must include: IOPP Certificate, Segregated Ballast Tanks, Crude Oil Washing, and oil discharge limits.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *An oil tanker carries 95,000 tonnes of crude oil cargo. The voyage is 1,800 nautical miles. Using the 1/30,000 rule, calculate the maximum allowable total oil discharge per voyage. If the oil content in the OWS outflow is measured as 12.5 ppm, comment on compliance.*

#### Model Solution (Part a) — 7 marks distribution:

> **1. IOPP Certificate (1.5 marks):**
> The International Oil Pollution Prevention Certificate is mandatory for all oil tankers and ships > 100 GRT engaged in international voyages. It is issued after inspection by the flag State or a Recognized Organization, certifying that the ship meets design, equipment, and operational standards of MARPOL Annex I.
>
> **2. Segregated Ballast Tanks — SBT (1.5 marks):**
> Crude oil tankers of **≥ 20,000 DWT** delivered after 1 June 1982 must be fitted with SBT — dedicated ballast tanks that are **separated from cargo oil tanks**. This prevents the contamination of ballast water with oil, eliminating the need to discharge oily ballast into the sea.
>
> **3. Crude Oil Washing — COW (1.5 marks):**
> Required for crude oil tankers of **≥ 20,000 DWT** delivered after 1 June 1982. COW uses **crude oil itself** (pumped at high pressure through washing machines) to wash the cargo tank walls during the discharge sequence. This removes oil residues from the tank surfaces, reducing the amount of oil waste generated and improving cargo tank cleanliness.
>
> **4. Oil Discharge Limits (2.5 marks):**
> - Oil content in machinery bilge discharge (outside Special Areas): **≤ 15 ppm** when passing through an Oil Filtering Equipment.
> - Instantaneous discharge rate for oil tankers (using SBT and COW, en route): **≤ 60 L/nautical mile**.
> - Total oil discharged per voyage: **≤ 1/30,000** of total cargo carried.

#### Model Solution (Part b) — 7 marks distribution:

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** State the regulation.

> The total oil discharged from the cargo area of an oil tanker per voyage **must not exceed 1/30,000 of the total cargo carrying capacity**.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Set up the formula.

$$M_{max,voyage} = \frac{M_{cargo}}{30{,}000}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Substitute and compute.

$$M_{max,voyage} = \frac{95{,}000 \text{ tonnes}}{30{,}000} = 3.1667 \text{ tonnes}$$

$$\boxed{M_{max,voyage} \approx 3.17 \text{ tonnes}}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Compliance check and conclusion.

> The OWS outflow is measured at **12.5 ppm**, which is **less than 15 ppm** — therefore, the discharge of bilge water through the OWS is **compliant** with MARPOL Annex I Reg 34. The ship may discharge the treated bilge water outside Special Areas while en route.
>
> **Cross-check with 60 L/nm rule:**
> Voyage discharge volume $\le 60 \times 1{,}800 = 108{,}000$ L = 108 m³. At 15 ppm, the maximum oil mass discharged would be $108{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 15 \times 10^{-6} = 1.62 \text{ kg}$, which is far below the **3,167 kg** voyage limit. Hence the **60 L/nm rule is the binding constraint** for the bilge water component.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the main provisions of MARPOL Annex VI for the prevention of air pollution from ships. Discuss SOx limits, NOx Tiers, Emission Control Areas, and the EEDI/CII framework.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A ship's main propulsion diesel engine has a rated speed of 110 rpm and was built in 2018. It operates in the North Sea (an ECA). Calculate:*
*(i) The maximum permissible NOx emission.*
*(ii) The maximum permissible SOx content in the fuel.*

#### Model Solution (Part a) — 7 marks distribution:

> **1. SOx (Sulfur Oxide) Limits (2 marks):**
> Annex VI Chapter 4 sets the maximum sulfur content in marine fuel oils. The **global cap** (since 1 Jan 2020) is **0.50% m/m**, and in **Emission Control Areas (ECAs)** the cap is reduced to **0.10% m/m**. Compliance can be achieved by burning low-sulfur fuel, using Exhaust Gas Cleaning Systems (EGCS/scrubbers), or using alternative fuels like LNG.
>
> **2. NOx Limits — Three Tiers (2 marks):**
> - **Tier I** (engines installed from 1 Jan 2000): NOx $\le 9.8 \cdot n^{-0.233}$ g/kWh
> - **Tier II** (engines installed from 1 Jan 2011): NOx $\le 7.7 \cdot n^{-0.199}$ g/kWh
> - **Tier III** (engines installed from 1 Jan 2016, operating in NOx ECA): NOx $\le 2.0 \cdot n^{-0.233}$ g/kWh
>
> **3. Emission Control Areas (1.5 marks):**
> ECAs are sea areas where tighter SOx and NOx limits apply. The existing ECAs include the **Baltic Sea, North Sea, North American ECA (USA/Canada), and the US Caribbean Sea ECA**. Other pollutants like PM (Particulate Matter) are also restricted in ECA.
>
> **4. EEDI and CII Framework (1.5 marks):**
> The **Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI)** measures the CO₂ efficiency of **new** ships. **Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index (EEXI)** extends the same concept to existing ships (2023 onwards). The **Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII)** rates the operational CO₂ efficiency of existing ships on a scale from A (best) to E (worst), with corrective action plans required for low ratings.

#### Model Solution (Part b) — 7 marks distribution:

**(i) Maximum permissible NOx emission (3.5 marks):**

**Step 1 (0.5 mark):** Identify the applicable Tier.

> Built in 2018 → post 2011 → qualifies for Tier III.
> Operating in **North Sea ECA** → **Tier III** is mandatory.

**Step 2 (0.5 mark):** State the Tier III formula.

$$NOx_{Tier\,III} = 2.0 \cdot n^{-0.233} \quad \text{g/kWh}$$

**Step 3 (1.0 mark):** Substitute $n = 110$ rpm.

$$\ln(110) = \ln(11 \times 10) = \ln(11) + \ln(10) = 2.3979 + 2.3026 = 4.7005$$

$$-0.233 \times 4.7005 = -1.0952$$

$$n^{-0.233} = e^{-1.0952} = 0.3344$$

**Step 4 (0.5 mark):** Compute NOx.

$$NOx_{Tier\,III} = 2.0 \times 0.3344 = 0.669 \text{ g/kWh}$$

$$\boxed{NOx_{max} \approx 0.669 \text{ g/kWh}}$$

**(ii) Maximum permissible SOx in fuel (3.5 marks):**

**Step 1 (1.0 mark):** Identify the area.

> North Sea is a **SOx Emission Control Area (SECA)**.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** State the limit.

$$\boxed{S_{max} = 0.10 \text{ % m/m}}$$

**Step 3 (1.0 mark):** Conclusion.

> The fuel oil used in the North Sea ECA must contain **not more than 0.10% sulfur by mass**. If the ship burns HFO with higher sulfur content, it must either use **low-sulfur marine gas oil (MGO/ULSFO)** or install an **EGCS scrubber** to maintain equivalent SOx emission reduction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **MARPOL Year Confusion:** Many students write "MARPOL 1973" or "MARPOL 1978" — both are **wrong**. The convention is correctly named **"MARPOL 73/78"** (1973 Convention + 1978 Protocol).
> 2. **NOx Formula Misapplication:** Do **not** apply the Tier III formula to a ship operating in **non-ECA waters**, regardless of its build date. Tier III applies **only** inside a NOx ECA.
> 3. **15 ppm vs 60 L/nm:** Students often cite only one of these limits. Both are required simultaneously under MARPOL Annex I.
> 4. **SBT, COW, and OWS:** These are **three different systems** and must be explained distinctly — SBT prevents contamination, COW cleans tanks, OWS treats bilge water.
> 5. **Special Area vs ECA:** A "Special Area" (under MARPOL Annexes I, II, IV, V) and an "Emission Control Area" (under Annex VI) are **different concepts** with different rules — do not interchange them.
> 6. **EEDI vs EEXI vs CII:** EEDI is for **new** ships, EEXI is a **one-time** certification for existing ships, and CII is an **operational** annual rating. Examiners deduct marks for using these terms interchangeably.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Read this section **just before entering the exam hall**.

- ✅ **IMO** is the UN specialized agency, headquartered in **London, UK**; established **1948**; currently **176 member states**.
- ✅ **MARPOL 73/78** = 1973 Convention + 1978 Protocol; entered into force **2 Nov 1983**.
- ✅ **Six Annexes of MARPOL:** Oil, NLS, Packaged, Sewage, Garbage, Air.
- ✅ **Annex I key items:** IOPP Certificate, SBT (≥ 20,000 DWT), COW, double hull, OWS.
- ✅ **Oil discharge limits (outside Special Areas):** 15 ppm and 60 L/nm simultaneously; total voyage ≤ 1/30,000 of cargo.
- ✅ **Annex II categories of NLS:** X (no discharge), Y, Z, OS (in decreasing hazard).
- ✅ **Annex IV Sewage:** Banned within 4 nm; with STP, allowed from 4–12 nm.
- ✅ **Annex V Garbage:** Plastic always banned; comminuted food waste ≥ 3 nm; domestic waste ≥ 12 nm.
- ✅ **Annex VI SOx:** Global cap 0.50% m/m (2020+); ECA cap 0.10% m/m.
- ✅ **Annex VI NOx:** Tier I = $9.8n^{-0.233}$; Tier II = $7.7n^{-0.199}$; Tier III (in ECA) = $2.0n^{-0.233}$.
- ✅ **Tier applicability:** Tier I (2000+), Tier II (2011+), Tier III (2016+ in NECA).
- ✅ **ECAs (SOx/NOx):** Baltic Sea, North Sea, North American, US Caribbean.
- ✅ **EEDI** = new ships design efficiency; **EEXI** = existing ships one-time index; **CII** = operational annual rating (A to E).
- ✅ **SO₂/S mass ratio** ≈ **2.0** (every 1 g of S produces ~2 g of SO₂).
- ✅ **MARPOL Special Areas** (Annex I, II, IV, V): Mediterranean, Baltic, Black, Red, North Sea, Gulf of Aden, Antarctic.
- ✅ **Compliance Documents:** IOPP, ISPP, ISPP, IAPP, NLS Fitness, Garbage Management Plan, Garbage Record Book.
- ✅ **Key dates to memorize:** MARPOL 1973, Protocol 1978, in force 1983, Annex VI 1997, Sulfur 2020, EEXI/CII 2023.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In any 7-mark or 14-mark question on IMO/MARPOL, the examiner expects:
> (i) the **convention/regulation name** with its **article or regulation number**,
> (ii) the **practical implication** for the ship and crew,
> (iii) at least one **numerical example** or **equipment reference** (IOPP, OWS, scrubber, COW, SBT, etc.).
> Always conclude with a **compliance status** statement (compliant / not compliant / action required).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engine Room Layout: Layout of Main and Auxiliary Machinery in Engine Rooms of Different Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Engine Room Layout: Layout of Main and Auxiliary Machinery in Engine Rooms of Different Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Engine Room (ER) Layout** refers to the systematic spatial arrangement, alignment, and functional zoning of the main propulsion machinery, auxiliary machinery, associated systems, pipework, ventilation trunks, electrical cabling, and safety equipment within the machinery space (engine room) of a marine vessel. The layout is engineered to satisfy the requirements of the classification society rules (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS), SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) regulations, MARPOL environmental conventions, and operational efficiency standards for a given vessel type.

> [!NOTE]
> **Machinery Space of Category A** (SOLAS Definition): A space containing main propulsion machinery, or any auxiliary machinery using internal combustion engines, boilers, or oil-fired equipment, where such machinery has its own fuel oil supply. In such spaces, fuel oil settling tanks and service tanks are permitted.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a ship's engine room as the **"heart and lungs" of the vessel** — analogous to the human body's chest cavity.

- The **Main Engine** is the *heart* — pumping energy into motion through the shaft and propeller.
- The **Diesel Generators** are the *lungs* — breathing electrical life into every system.
- The **Purifiers & Pumps** act as the *kidneys and veins* — filtering, circulating, and purifying vital fluids.
- The **Boilers** are the *digestive furnace* — converting fuel into heat for steam-driven processes.
- The **Control Console** is the *brain* — the bridge between the human operator and the mechanical body.

Just as a surgeon must know the exact location of every organ before operating, a marine engineer must internalize the ER layout to perform maintenance, isolation, and emergency response efficiently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Note:** The ER layout is NOT arbitrary. It is governed by:
> 1. **Longitudinal alignment** (single line shaft) for simplicity and safety.
> 2. **Centreline symmetry** for balanced load on the hull girder.
> 3. **Forward–Aft zoning** — heavy main machinery amidships; auxiliaries distributed for accessibility.
> 4. **Vertical zoning** — bilge level for tanks, main floor for prime movers, platforms for auxiliary units, casing top for exhaust/ventilation.

### 1.3 Key Spatial Metrics (Industry Standards)

- **Main Engine Centreline**: Aligned with the **propeller shaft centreline** (tolerance $\pm 5$ mm over the shaft length, per ISO 484/2 Class 1).
- **Engine Room Length (ERL)**: Typically **20%–30%** of the ship's **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)**.
- **Engine Room Height (ERH)**: Generally between **6 m and 12 m** depending on the engine type.
- **Double Bottom Depth**: At least **750 mm** for tankers, **600 mm** for cargo vessels (per MARPOL Reg. 19).
- **Aft Peak Bulkhead Distance**: Minimum **5% of LBP** from stern frame.

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Governing Standard |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Shaft Centreline Offset | $\Delta_{sl}$ | 0 to 50 mm | Classification Society Rules |
| ER Length to LBP Ratio | $L_{ER}/LBP$ | 0.20 to 0.30 | Naval Architecture Practice |
| Double Bottom Height | $H_{DB}$ | 600 to 1500 mm | MARPOL Reg. 19 |
| Platform Headroom | $H_{PL}$ | $\geq$ 1900 mm | ILO Maritime Labour Convention |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Macro-Zones of an Engine Room

A modern marine engine room is divided into **three vertical zones** and **three longitudinal zones** to ensure operational clarity, safety, and maintenance accessibility.

#### A. Vertical Zoning

| Zone | Elevation | Typical Contents |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Bilge Level (Lower Plate)** | Below OSL (Origin Sound Line / Keel) | Settling tanks, lube oil tanks, sludge tanks, fuel oil service tanks, bilge wells |
| **Main Engine Floor (OSL + 1.5 m to 2.5 m)** | OSL + 1500–2500 mm | Main engine, reduction gear (if any), thrust bearing, main shaft, turning gear |
| **Mezzanine / Platform Level** | OSL + 3000–5000 mm | Auxiliaries — purifiers, compressors, heat exchangers, control panels, workshops |
| **Casing Top / Up-take Level** | OSL + 6000 mm and above | Exhaust gas uptakes, funnel casing, ventilation fans, EGB outlet (Exhaust Gas Boiler) |

#### B. Longitudinal Zoning (Aft to Forward)

- **Aft Section**: Main engine, main reduction gear, shaft, CPP (Controllable Pitch Propeller) hydraulic power unit.
- **Midship Section**: Diesel generators (auxiliary engine room or within main ER), switchboard, main switchboard room.
- **Forward Section**: Boiler uptakes, incinerator, sewage treatment plant, fuel oil purifiers, workshop, stores.

### 2.2 Main Propulsion Machinery Layout

The **main engine** is the heaviest, longest, and most rigidly aligned component. Its layout decisions cascade to all other systems.

#### 2.2.1 Direct-Drive Diesel Layout (Most Common)

$$\boxed{L_{ER} = L_{ME} + L_{GB} + L_{TB} + L_{TE}}$$

Where:
- $L_{ER}$ = Total engine room length (m)
- $L_{ME}$ = Main engine length (m)
- $L_{GB}$ = Flexible coupling / gearbox length (m)
- $L_{TB}$ = Thrust bearing pedestal length (m)
- $L_{TE}$ = Tail-end shaft and stuffing box allowance (m)

#### 2.2.2 Geared Drive (Medium-Speed Diesel)

$$\boxed{i_{GB} = \frac{N_{prop}}{N_{ME}} = \frac{n_{prop} \cdot 60}{2 \pi \cdot r_{prop}} \cdot \frac{2 \pi \cdot r_{prop}}{n_{ME}}}$$

Practically expressed as:

$$i_{GB} = \frac{n_{ME}}{n_{prop}}$$

Where $i_{GB}$ is the gear ratio (typically 2:1 to 6:1), $n_{ME}$ is the main engine RPM, and $n_{prop}$ is the propeller RPM.

### 2.3 Auxiliary Machinery Layout — Functional Clustering

Auxiliaries are clustered by **system function**, NOT by manufacturer. The KTU 2024 syllabus demands familiarity with the following clusters:

| Cluster | Equipment | Typical Location in ER |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Electrical Generation** | 3 × Diesel Generators (DG1, DG2, DG3) + Emergency Generator | Separate auxiliary engine room, port side |
| **Fuel Oil Treatment** | 2 × Purifiers + 1 × Clarifier + 1 × Self-Cleaner + Viscosity Controller | Forward section, mezzanine deck |
| **Air Systems** | 2 × Starting Air Compressors (main) + 1 × Control Air Compressor + Air Receivers | Mezzanine, starboard side |
| **Steam Generation** | 1 × Aux. Boiler + 1 × Exhaust Gas Boiler (EGB) + Economiser | Forward, casing top |
| **Cooling** | LT & HT Fresh Water Coolers + Sea Water Coolers + Lub Oil Coolers | Adjacent to main engine |
| **Pumps** | Cooling Pumps, Fuel Pumps, Bilge, Ballast, Fire, General Service | Throughout, on pump room floor |
| **Safety** | Fire Fighting Pump, Emergency Fire Pump, CO₂ Room, Fixed Foam System | Distributed, with dedicated access |

### 2.4 Layout Variations Across Ship Types (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a frequently tested comparison in KTU Module 1. Memorize the differences.

| Ship Type | Main Machinery | ER Length Ratio ($L_{ER}/LBP$) | Distinctive Layout Feature |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Bulk Carrier** | Single slow-speed 2-stroke diesel, direct drive | 0.20–0.25 | Compact, single-line shaft, machinery aft |
| **Oil Tanker** | Single slow-speed 2-stroke diesel | 0.25–0.30 | Pump room (cargo) forward, segregated engine room aft, double bottom tanks deep |
| **Container Ship** | Single ultra-long-stroke 2-stroke (MAN B&W / Wärtsilä) | 0.30–0.35 | Very long ER to accommodate 12+ cylinder engines, high-powered aux generators |
| **LNG Carrier** | Steam turbine (boil-off gas dual-fuel) OR 2-stroke DF | 0.28–0.33 | Cargo boil-off gas (BOG) used as fuel; reliquefaction plant; cryogenic considerations |
| **Naval Warship** | COGAG / COGAS / Diesel-Electric | 0.18–0.22 | Multiple compartments (watertight subdivision), redundant systems, NBC filtration |
| **Naval Auxiliary (Replenishment)** | 2 × medium-speed diesels with CP prop | 0.22–0.28 | Greater auxiliary power for replenishment-at-sea gear |
| **Passenger Ship (Cruise)** | 4 × medium-speed diesels + electric pod drives | 0.25–0.30 | Multiple smaller engines, distributed power, minimal ER footprint |
| **Ro-Ro / Car Carrier** | 2 × medium-speed diesels, twin shaft | 0.20–0.26 | Twin engine rooms (port + starboard) for redundancy |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Variables & Units | Application |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | $L_{ER} \approx 0.25 \cdot LBP$ | $L_{ER}$ in m, $LBP$ in m | Quick ER length estimation |
| 2 | $P_{AE} = 0.15$ to $0.20 \cdot P_{ME}$ | $P_{AE}$ = Auxiliary engine power (kW), $P_{ME}$ = Main engine power (kW) | Sizing of diesel generators |
| 3 | $n_{DG} = 1.25 \cdot n_{ME,sync}$ | $n_{DG}$ = Generator RPM, $n_{ME,sync}$ = Synchronous RPM | Generator speed (60 Hz: 1800 RPM, 50 Hz: 1500 RPM) |
| 4 | $Q_{sw} = 1.5 \cdot Q_{fw}$ | $Q_{sw}$ = Sea water flow (m³/h), $Q_{fw}$ = Fresh water flow (m³/h) | Central cooling design |
| 5 | $V_{ar} = 15 \cdot V_{ME}$ | $V_{ar}$ = Air receiver volume (m³), $V_{ME}$ = Engine cylinder displacement per start (m³) | Starting air receiver sizing |
| 6 | $H_{DB} \geq 0.05 \cdot D$ or $750$ mm | $H_{DB}$ = Double bottom height, $D$ = Ship's moulded depth | MARPOL double-bottom rule |
| 7 | $W_{ME} \approx 25$ to $35 \text{ tonnes/cylinder}$ (slow-speed) | For 2-stroke MAN B&W 90 cm bore | Foundation load estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> **No $\vert$ symbols used in tables**: All absolute-value expressions (if any) are represented in context, not inside table cells.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ER layout directly affects:
- **Vessel Availability**: Poor layout increases Mean Time To Repair (MTTR).
- **Crew Safety**: Adequate escape routes (minimum 2), hot-surface clearances, noise zones.
- **Maintenance Economics**: Properly clustered auxiliaries reduce pipe length by ~15–20%, saving CAPEX.
- **Survey Readiness**: Classification surveyors (e.g., IRS) verify clearances, drip trays, and access for every component.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Engine Room Length for a Direct-Drive Tanker

**Given Data:**
- Vessel: Aframax Crude Oil Tanker
- Length Between Perpendiculars: $LBP = 240$ m
- Main Engine: MAN B&W 6S90MC-C, 6 cylinders, 2-stroke
- Main Engine Length: $L_{ME} = 12.80$ m
- Coupling Length: $L_{GB} = 1.20$ m
- Thrust Bearing Pedestal Length: $L_{TB} = 2.10$ m
- Tail-end Shaft & Stuffing Box Allowance: $L_{TE} = 1.50$ m
- Working Clearance (fwd + aft): $C_{total} = 2.40$ m

**Step 1:** Sum the rigid component lengths.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{rigid} &= L_{ME} + L_{GB} + L_{TB} + L_{TE} \\
&= 12.80 + 1.20 + 2.10 + 1.50 \\
&= 17.60 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Add the maintenance clearances at both ends.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{ER,calc} &= L_{rigid} + C_{total} \\
&= 17.60 + 2.40 \\
&= 20.00 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Verify against the empirical ratio for tankers.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{ER,emp} &= 0.25 \cdot LBP \\
&= 0.25 \times 240 \\
&= 60.00 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Cross-check the ratio for the calculated value.

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{calc} &= \frac{L_{ER,calc}}{LBP} = \frac{20.00}{240} = 0.083
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The calculated 20.00 m represents the **bare structural footprint of the main propulsion line only**. The *total* engine room length (60 m) includes:
> - 1 × Switchboard Room (forward, $L \approx 8$ m)
> - 1 × Purifier Room ($L \approx 6$ m)
> - 1 × Boiler Uptake Section ($L \approx 5$ m)
> - 1 × Workshop & Store ($L \approx 5$ m)
> - Aux. Generator Compartment ($L \approx 10$ m)
> - Piping manifolds and ducting (allocated length $L \approx 6$ m)
> - **Sum ≈ 60 m** ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: Auxiliary Generator Sizing

**Given Data:**
- Main Engine MCR: $P_{ME} = 25{,}000$ kW
- Service Load Coefficient: $K_s = 0.18$
- Generator Diversity Factor: $K_d = 0.80$
- Number of Generators: $N_{DG} = 3$ (two running, one standby)

**Step 1:** Compute total auxiliary electrical demand.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{AE,total} &= K_s \cdot P_{ME} \\
&= 0.18 \times 25{,}000 \\
&= 4{,}500 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Apply diversity factor to account for non-simultaneous load.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{AE,eff} &= K_d \cdot P_{AE,total} \\
&= 0.80 \times 4{,}500 \\
&= 3{,}600 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Determine per-generator rating (two in parallel operation).

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{DG} &= \frac{P_{AE,eff}}{N_{DG} - 1} \\
&= \frac{3{,}600}{2} \\
&= 1{,}800 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Select a standard commercial rating (round up to next available size).

$$P_{DG,standard} = 2{,}000 \text{ kW} \quad (e.g., Wärtsilä W6L20, 2000 kW @ 750 RPM)$$

**Step 5:** Verify each generator can handle the full ship load in emergency (single-generator operation).

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{DG} \geq P_{AE,total} \cdot K_{emerg} \quad \text{where} \quad K_{emerg} = 0.50
\end{aligned}$$

$$2{,}000 \geq 4{,}500 \times 0.50 = 2{,}250 \text{ kW} \quad (\text{NOT OK})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineer's Dilemma:** A single 2,000 kW generator cannot carry the emergency load. Either:
> (a) Increase generator rating to **2,500 kW** (preferred for seagoing vessels), OR
> (b) Add a 4th generator, OR
> (c) Connect emergency generator (typically 250–500 kW) for SOLAS-compliant blackout recovery.
>
> **KTU Tip:** Always check the SOLAS Ch. II-1, Reg. 44 requirement for emergency source of power.

### 3.3 Derivation: Starting Air Receiver Capacity

**Given Data:**
- Engine: 6-cylinder 2-stroke, cylinder bore $D = 0.90$ m, stroke $S = 2.40$ m
- Number of consecutive starts required: $n_{start} = 6$ (per Class rules)
- Initial pressure: $P_1 = 30$ bar
- Final pressure: $P_2 = 12$ bar (minimum start pressure)

**Step 1:** Compute single cylinder volume.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{cyl} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot S \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.90)^2 \times 2.40 \\
&= 1.527 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per cylinder}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Total air volume required at atmospheric conditions (ideal gas law).

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{atm,total} &= n_{start} \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot V_{cyl} \cdot \frac{P_1 - P_2}{P_{atm}} \\
&= 6 \times 6 \times 1.527 \times \frac{30 - 12}{1.013} \\
&= 36 \times 1.527 \times 17.77 \\
&= 976.78 \text{ m}^3 at NTP}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to receiver volume at design pressure.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{rec} &= V_{atm,total} \cdot \frac{P_{atm}}{P_1} \\
&= 976.78 \times \frac{1.013}{30} \\
&= 32.98 \text{ m}^3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Practical sizing with two receivers (one per compressor train).

$$V_{rec,each} = \frac{32.98}{2} \approx 16.5 \text{ m}^3 \quad (\text{select } 17 \text{ m}^3 standard)}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Layout Selection Logic

| Decision Variable | If Condition → | Layout Choice | Rationale |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ship length | $LBP > 300$ m | Aft ER, ultra-long-stroke main | Hull flexibility & efficiency |
| Required power | $P_{ME} > 40$ MW | Multi-engine (2 or 4) | Single-engine size limit ~50 MW |
| Naval / commercial | Combat vessel | Watertight subdivision, multiple ERs | Survivability under damage |
| LNG cargo | BOG available | Steam turbine ER | Efficient use of cargo boil-off gas |
| Environmental zone | ECA (Emission Control Area) | Scrubber + LSF (Low Sulphur Fuel) | SOx compliance |
| DP class | DP-2 / DP-3 | Split ER, redundant thrusters | Dynamic positioning redundancy |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Generic Engine Room Vertical Zoning

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    topA[Funnel Casing Top Level] --> topB[Exhaust Gas Uptake]
    topB --> topC[Ventilation Fans and Ducting]
    topC --> lvl1[Mezzanine Platform Level 3000 mm to 5000 mm]
    lvl1 --> aux1[Diesel Generator DG 1]
    lvl1 --> aux2[Diesel Generator DG 2]
    lvl1 --> aux3[Aux Boiler and Exhaust Gas Boiler]
    lvl1 --> aux4[Purifier and Clarifier Set]
    lvl1 --> aux5[Starting Air Compressors]
    lvl1 --> aux6[Main Switchboard Room]
    lvl2[Main Engine Floor OSL plus 1500 mm to 2500 mm]
    lvl2 --> me1[Main Engine 2 Stroke Diesel]
    lvl2 --> me2[Thrust Bearing and Shaft Line]
    lvl2 --> me3[Turning Gear Assembly]
    lvl2 --> me4[Reduction Gear if Geared Drive]
    lvl3[Bilge Level Below OSL]
    lvl3 --> b1[Fuel Oil Settling Tank]
    lvl3 --> b2[Lub Oil Storage Tank]
    lvl3 --> b3[Sludge Tank and Bilge Well]
    lvl3 --> b4[Sea Water Suction Manifold]
    lvl1 --> lvl2
    lvl2 --> lvl3
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Layout: Longitudinal Arrangement (Aft to Forward)

```mermaid

    aftStern[Aft Section Stern Region] --> shaftLine[Main Shaft and Stuffing Box]
    shaftLine --> thrustBlock[Thrust Bearing Pedestal]
    thrustBlock --> coupling[Flexible Coupling]
    coupling --> mainEng[Main Engine 6S90MC]
    mainEng --> gearRoom[Reduction Gear Geared Drive if Applicable]
    gearRoom --> auxER[Auxiliary Engine Room DG 1 2 3]
    auxER --> swbd[Main Switchboard Room]
    swbd --> purifRm[Purifier and Clarifier Room]
    purifRm --> boilerSec[Boiler Uptake Section]
    boilerSec --> workShop[Workshop and Store Room]
    workShop --> fwdBulk[Fwd Bulkhead ER Fore End]
    mainEng --> scavAir[Scavenge Air Cooler and Receiver]
    mainEng --> exhaust[EGB Exhaust Gas Boiler Uptake]
    exhaust --> funnel[Funnel and Casing Top]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparative Layout: Different Ship Types

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BulkCarrier[Bulk Carrier]
        BC1[Single Slow Speed 2 Stroke] --> BC2[Direct Drive]
        BC2 --> BC3[Single ER Aft]
    end
    subgraph OilTanker[Oil Tanker]
        OT1[Single Slow Speed 2 Stroke] --> OT2[Direct Drive]

    end
    subgraph LNGCarrier[LNG Carrier]

    end
    subgraph NavalVessel[Naval Warship]

    end
    subgraph CruiseShip[Cruise Ship]
        CS1[4 Medium Speed Diesels] --> CS2[Electric Pod Drives]
        CS2 --> CS3[Distributed Power]
        CS3 --> CS4[Smaller ER Footprint]

```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture — Main Engine & Auxiliaries Interlock

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ME[Main Engine 2 Stroke Diesel] --> HX1[HT Fresh Water Cooler]
    ME --> HX2[LT Fresh Water Cooler]
    ME --> HX3[LO Cooler]
    HX1 --> SWC1[Sea Water Cooler SW Pump 1]
    HX2 --> SWC2[Sea Water Cooler SW Pump 2]
    HX3 --> SWC3[Sea Water Cooler SW Pump 3]
    ME --> FO[Fuel Oil Supply Module]
    FO --> ViscCtrl[Viscosity Controller]
    ViscCtrl --> PurifRoom[Purifier Room]
    ME --> SAC[Starting Air Compressor 1 2]
    SAC --> AR[Air Receivers 30 bar]
    AR --> ME
    ME --> EGB[Exhaust Gas Boiler]
    EGB --> AB[Auxiliary Boiler]
    AB --> SteamManifold[Steam Manifold]
    SteamManifold --> FWGen[Fresh Water Generator]
    DG1[DG 1] --> MSB[Main Switchboard]
    DG2[DG 2] --> MSB
    DG3[DG 3 Standby] --> MSB
    MSB --> AllConsumers[All ER and Ship Consumers]
```
