- $m_{f,\,th}$ = Theoretical (stoichiometric) mass of fuel for complete combustion
- $V_s$ = Swept volume of cylinder
- $\rho_a$ = Ambient air density

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse **scavenging efficiency** with **trapping efficiency**. Remember:
> - **Scavenging efficiency** refers to the **freshness** of the charge (how much of the supplied air is retained without mixing with exhaust).
> - **Trapping efficiency** refers to the **amount** of charge retained in the cylinder after the exhaust valve closes (some fresh air may "leak" out through the exhaust valve after scavenging ends).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the marine industry, the design of the scavenging system directly impacts:

1. **Specific fuel oil consumption (SFOC)** — Better scavenging → better combustion → lower fuel consumption.
2. **Compliance with MARPOL Annex VI** — Lower exhaust emissions (NOx, SOx, PM).
3. **Engine reliability** — Scavenge fires (if poorly scavenged) can damage turbochargers and cylinder liners.
4. **Power density** — Effective scavenging allows higher mean effective pressure (MEP) → more power from a smaller engine.
5. **Turbocharger matching** — Scavenge air demand determines turbocharger selection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Scavenging Efficiency, Trapping Efficiency, and Charging Efficiency

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A two-stroke marine diesel engine cylinder is supplied with a mass $m_{sup}$ of fresh air by the turbocharger. After scavenging, a fraction $\eta_{sc}$ of this is retained as fresh air. After the exhaust valve closes, a fraction $\eta_t$ of the supplied mass is trapped. Show that the charging efficiency is the product of the two.

### Step 1 — Define the masses

Let the mass of fresh air supplied by the turbocharger be $m_{a,\,sup}$.

The mass of fresh air **retained** in the cylinder at the end of scavenging (before exhaust valve closure) is:

$$m_{a,\,ret} = \eta_{sc} \cdot m_{a,\,sup}$$

This is by direct application of the definition of scavenging efficiency:

$$\eta_{sc} = \frac{m_{a,\,ret}}{m_{a,\,sup}} \implies m_{a,\,ret} = \eta_{sc} \cdot m_{a,\,sup}$$

### Step 2 — Define the trapped mass

After the exhaust valve closes, some additional air may escape back through the ports before they close. The mass **trapped** in the cylinder at the start of compression is:

$$m_{a,\,trap} = \eta_{t} \cdot m_{a,\,sup}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the charging efficiency

By definition, the **charging efficiency** is the ratio of the trapped mass of fresh air to the mass of air that would occupy the cylinder at atmospheric conditions:

$$\eta_{c} = \frac{m_{a,\,trap}}{m_{a,\,atm}}$$

But the **ideal mass of air that would fill the swept volume at atmospheric conditions** is itself related to the supply mass by the delivery ratio:

$$D_{r} = \frac{m_{a,\,sup}}{m_{a,\,atm}} \implies m_{a,\,atm} = \frac{m_{a,\,sup}}{D_{r}}$$

Substituting:

$$\eta_{c} = \frac{m_{a,\,trap}}{m_{a,\,sup} / D_{r}} = \frac{m_{a,\,trap} \cdot D_{r}}{m_{a,\,sup}}$$

### Step 4 — Final simplification

Now substitute $m_{a,\,trap} = \eta_{t} \cdot m_{a,\,sup}$:

$$\eta_{c} = \frac{\eta_{t} \cdot m_{a,\,sup} \cdot D_{r}}{m_{a,\,sup}} = \eta_{t} \cdot D_{r}$$

But $D_{r}$ itself equals the **scavenging efficiency** (under the simplification that $m_{a,\,atm}$ represents the ideal fill mass) — i.e., $D_{r} \approx \eta_{sc}$ when the comparison is to atmospheric fill:

$$\boxed{\eta_{c} = \eta_{sc} \cdot \eta_{t}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Gold:** The product form $\eta_{c} = \eta_{sc} \cdot \eta_{t}$ is a **favourite 7-mark derivation question** in KTU 2024 ESE.

## 3.2 Derivation: Air Mass Required for a Given Fuel Consumption

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A two-stroke marine diesel engine burns $m_f$ kg of fuel per cycle per cylinder. The relative air-fuel ratio is $\lambda$. The scavenging efficiency is $\eta_{sc}$ and the delivery ratio is $D_r$. Find the mass of air that must be **supplied** by the turbocharger.

### Step 1 — Mass of air required for stoichiometric combustion

The stoichiometric air requirement per kg of diesel fuel is approximately:

$$A_{st} = 14.7 \text{ kg air / kg fuel}$$

The actual mass of air required to burn $m_f$ kg of fuel at relative charge $\lambda$ is:

$$m_{a,\,th} = \lambda \cdot A_{st} \cdot m_{f} = 14.7 \cdot \lambda \cdot m_{f}$$

### Step 2 — Account for scavenging losses

The mass of air that must be **supplied** by the turbocharger is greater than the mass trapped, because some of the supplied air escapes during scavenging (short-circuiting, mixing with exhaust). Using the trapping efficiency:

$$m_{a,\,sup} = \frac{m_{a,\,trap}}{\eta_{t}}$$

### Step 3 — Combine expressions

Substituting $m_{a,\,trap} = 14.7 \cdot \lambda \cdot m_f$:

$$\boxed{m_{a,\,sup} = \frac{14.7 \cdot \lambda \cdot m_{f}}{\eta_{t}}}$$

If the question uses scavenging efficiency instead of trapping efficiency, replace $\eta_t$ with $\eta_{sc}$ accordingly.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 ESE Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example:** A two-stroke marine diesel engine has a **bore of 0.90 m**, a **stroke of 2.40 m**, and runs at **100 rpm**. The scavenge air pressure is **0.30 bar gauge**, the scavenge air temperature is **320 K**, the delivery ratio is **1.20**, and the scavenging efficiency is **95%**. Calculate:
> (a) The mass of air supplied per cycle per cylinder.
> (b) The mass of air retained per cycle per cylinder.
> (c) The mass of air that would fill the cylinder at atmospheric conditions.

**Given:**
- $D = 0.90$ m, $L = 2.40$ m, $N = 100$ rpm
- $p_{sc} = 0.30$ bar (g) = $1.30$ bar (abs) = $130{,}000$ Pa
- $T_{sc} = 320$ K
- $D_r = 1.20$, $\eta_{sc} = 0.95$
- $p_{atm} = 1.013$ bar = $101{,}300$ Pa
- $R = 287$ J/(kg·K) (for air)

### Step (a) — Mass of air supplied per cycle

Swept volume per cylinder:

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.90)^2 \cdot 2.40$$

$$V_s = 0.7854 \cdot 0.81 \cdot 2.40 = 1.527 \text{ m}^3$$

Density of air in the scavenge receiver:

$$\rho_{sc} = \frac{p_{sc}}{R \cdot T_{sc}} = \frac{130{,}000}{287 \cdot 320}$$

$$\rho_{sc} = \frac{130{,}000}{91{,}840} = 1.416 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Mass of air supplied (which fills the swept volume at scavenge conditions):

$$m_{a,\,sup} = \rho_{sc} \cdot V_s = 1.416 \cdot 1.527$$

$$m_{a,\,sup} = 2.162 \text{ kg per cycle per cylinder}$$

### Step (b) — Mass of air retained

$$m_{a,\,ret} = \eta_{sc} \cdot m_{a,\,sup} = 0.95 \cdot 2.162$$

$$m_{a,\,ret} = 2.054 \text{ kg per cycle per cylinder}$$

### Step (c) — Mass of air at atmospheric conditions

Atmospheric density at standard conditions ($T = 298$ K):

$$\rho_{atm} = \frac{101{,}300}{287 \cdot 298} = 1.184 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$m_{a,\,atm} = \rho_{atm} \cdot V_s = 1.184 \cdot 1.527 = 1.808 \text{ kg}$$

### Verification using delivery ratio:

$$D_r = \frac{m_{a,\,sup}}{m_{a,\,atm}} = \frac{2.162}{1.808} = 1.196 \approx 1.20 \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification Trick:** Always cross-check the final answer using the defining formula. If the calculated $D_r$ matches the given value, your calculation is correct.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Scavenging Types

| Feature | Uniflow | Loop | Cross |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direction of flow** | One-way (axial) | Looped (tangential) | Cross-wise |
| **Exhaust port location** | Cylinder head | Cylinder side | Cylinder side opposite to inlet |
| **Inlet port location** | Cylinder bottom | Cylinder side | Cylinder side |
| **Scavenging efficiency** | **95 – 99 %** | 85 – 92 % | 80 – 88 % |
| **Short-circuiting risk** | Very low | Moderate | High |
| **Use in 2-stroke marine main engines** | **Yes (universal)** | No | No (historical) |
| **Thermal loading** | Low | Higher | Higher |
| **Suitability for high BMEP** | Excellent | Limited | Poor |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Uniflow Scavenging — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Turbocharger Compressor] --> B[Scavenge Air Cooler]
    B --> C[Scavenge Air Receiver]
    C --> D[Scavenge Port Bottom of Cylinder]
    D --> E[Engine Cylinder Liner]
    E --> F[Exhaust Valve Cylinder Head]
    F --> G[Exhaust Manifold]
    G --> H[Turbocharger Turbine]
    H --> A
    I[Engine Cylinder] --> J[Combustion and Power Stroke]
    J --> K[Piston Descends to BDC]
    K --> D
    L[ECU and Governor] --> A
    L --> M[Control Scavenge Pressure via Wastegate or VTA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Air circulates in a closed loop. The turbocharger's **turbine** is driven by exhaust energy, which in turn drives the **compressor** that supplies fresh air. The **scavenge air cooler** reduces the temperature of the compressed air (increasing its density). The **scavenge air receiver** acts as a buffer tank to dampen pressure pulsations.

## 4.2 Scavenging Process — Crank-Angle Event Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BDC: Power Stroke Ends] --> B[Phase 1: Exhaust Blowdown 70-90 deg BTDC]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Scavenge Port Opens 50-60 deg BTDC]
    C --> D[Phase 3: Scavenging in Progress at BDC]
    D --> E[Phase 4: Exhaust Valve Closes 30-50 deg ATDC]
    E --> F[Phase 5: Scavenge Port Closes 60-80 deg ATDC]
    F --> G[Phase 6: Compression Stroke Begins]
    G --> H[TDC: Combustion]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Understanding "ATDC" and "BTDC":** ATDC = After Top Dead Centre; BTDC = Before Top Dead Centre. These refer to the crank angle position of the piston.

## 4.3 Scavenge Air System — Component Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atmospheric Air Inlet] --> B[Air Filter]
    B --> C[Turbocharger Compressor Impeller]
    C --> D[Scavenge Air Cooler SAC]
    D --> E[Scavenge Air Receiver SAR]
    E --> F[Scavenge Manifold]
    F --> G[Scavenge Ports on Cylinder Liner]
    G --> H[Engine Cylinder Combustion Chamber]
    H --> I[Exhaust Valve]
    I --> J[Exhaust Manifold]
    J --> K[Turbocharger Turbine]
    K --> C
    L[Drain Separator] --> D
    M[Safety Relief Valve] --> E
    N[Flame Arrester] --> E
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Note:** The **flame arrester** prevents any flame in the scavenge air receiver (due to oil carry-over or hot carbon particles) from propagating back to the turbocharger. The **safety relief valve** protects the receiver from over-pressure (which could be caused by a turbocharger surge or scavenge fire backflow).

## 4.4 Comparison Flowchart: Uniflow vs Loop Scavenging

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scavenging Type Decision] --> B{Engine Type?}
    B -->|Two-Stroke Marine Main Engine| C[Uniflow Scavenging]
    B -->|Two-Stroke Medium Speed| D[Loop Scavenging]
    B -->|Small Two-Stroke Outboard| E[Loop or Cross Scavenging]
    C --> F[Bottom Ports + Top Exhaust Valve]
    D --> G[Side Ports + Opposite Side Exhaust Ports]
    E --> H[Side Ports + Opposite Side Exhaust Ports]
    F --> I[Scavenging Efficiency 95-99 percent]
    G --> J[Scavenging Efficiency 85-92 percent]
    H --> K[Scavenging Efficiency 80-88 percent]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024 Style]**
> **Define scavenging. Why is it essential in a two-stroke marine diesel engine?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Scavenging is the process of **expelling the residual burnt exhaust gases** from the cylinder of an internal combustion engine and **replacing them with fresh, pressurized air** in preparation for the next compression and combustion stroke.

**Why essential in two-stroke engines (1.5 marks):**
- A two-stroke engine completes the **entire working cycle in one revolution (360°)**, leaving no dedicated exhaust or induction stroke.
- The piston must therefore uncover the **scavenge ports** near Bottom Dead Centre (BDC) to allow fresh air to enter and force out exhaust gases.
- Without scavenging, residual exhaust gases would dilute the fresh charge, reduce combustion efficiency, increase emissions, and cause power loss.
- Modern large two-stroke marine main engines (MAN B&W, Wärtsilä) use **uniflow scavenging** with bottom ports and a top-mounted exhaust valve, achieving scavenging efficiencies of 95–99%.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, June 2024 Style]**
> **List any THREE types of scavenging used in marine engines. Compare them based on air-flow direction.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three main types of scavenging are:

1. **Uniflow Scavenging (1 mark):** Air flows in **one direction only** — from bottom ports to a top-mounted exhaust valve. Used in all modern two-stroke marine main engines. Highest efficiency (95–99%).

2. **Loop Scavenging (1 mark):** Air enters through ports on **one side** of the cylinder, follows a **loop-shaped path** inside, and pushes exhaust gases out through ports on the **opposite side**. Used in medium-speed four-stroke and small two-stroke engines. Efficiency 85–92%.

3. **Cross Scavenging (1 mark):** A special case of loop scavenging where air enters horizontally and pushes exhaust gases out the opposite side directly. Mainly of historical interest; rarely used in modern marine engines. Efficiency 80–88%.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper Style]**
> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **uniflow scavenging system** used in large two-stroke marine diesel engines. Describe the role of the **turbocharger**, **scavenge air cooler**, and **scavenge air receiver** in the scavenging process. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A two-stroke marine diesel engine has a **bore of 0.75 m**, **stroke of 2.20 m**, and runs at **95 rpm**. The **scavenge air pressure** is **0.28 bar gauge**, the **scavenge air temperature** is **315 K**, the **delivery ratio** is **1.15**, and the **scavenging efficiency** is **94%**. Calculate: (i) the mass of air supplied per cycle per cylinder, (ii) the mass of air retained per cycle per cylinder, and (iii) the mass of air that would fill the cylinder at atmospheric conditions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Uniflow Scavenging System (4 marks):**
- In uniflow scavenging, **fresh air enters** through **scavenge ports** located near the **bottom of the cylinder liner**, while **exhaust gases leave** through **exhaust poppet valves** mounted in the **cylinder head**.
- The piston uncovers the scavenge ports as it approaches **Bottom Dead Centre (BDC)**.
- The air flows in a **single axial direction** from bottom to top, hence the name "uniflow."
- This arrangement **eliminates short-circuiting** (where fresh air escapes directly out the exhaust port), yielding the **highest scavenging efficiency** of any configuration.

**Role of Key Components (3 marks):**

1. **Turbocharger (1 mark):** A centrifugal compressor driven by the engine's exhaust gas turbine. It supplies the **pressurized air** (0.20–0.40 bar gauge) required for scavenging. Modern marine engines use **constant-pressure turbocharging** where the exhaust manifold acts as a plenum chamber.

2. **Scavenge Air Cooler (SAC) (1 mark):** Reduces the temperature of the compressed air (which gets hot due to compression). Cooling the air **increases its density**, so more air mass is delivered per cycle, improving combustion efficiency and reducing thermal loading on cylinder components.

3. **Scavenge Air Receiver (SAR) (1 mark):** Acts as a **buffer tank** between the SAC and the engine cylinders. It dampens pressure pulsations from individual cylinders, ensures a stable supply of scavenge air, and is fitted with safety devices (relief valve, flame arrester) to prevent scavenge fires.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 0.75$ m, $L = 2.20$ m, $N = 95$ rpm
- $p_{sc} = 0.28$ bar (g) = $1.28$ bar (abs) = $128{,}000$ Pa
- $T_{sc} = 315$ K
- $D_r = 1.15$, $\eta_{sc} = 0.94$
- $R = 287$ J/(kg·K), $p_{atm} = 101{,}300$ Pa, $T_{atm} = 298$ K

**Step 1 — Swept Volume per Cylinder (1 mark):**

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.75)^2 \cdot 2.20$$

$$V_s = 0.7854 \cdot 0.5625 \cdot 2.20 = 0.972 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Density of Scavenge Air (1 mark):**

$$\rho_{sc} = \frac{p_{sc}}{R \cdot T_{sc}} = \frac{128{,}000}{287 \cdot 315} = \frac{128{,}000}{90{,}405} = 1.416 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Mass of Air Supplied (1 mark):**

$$m_{a,\,sup} = \rho_{sc} \cdot V_s = 1.416 \cdot 0.972 = 1.376 \text{ kg/cycle}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of Air Retained (1 mark):**

$$m_{a,\,ret} = \eta_{sc} \cdot m_{a,\,sup} = 0.94 \cdot 1.376 = 1.294 \text{ kg/cycle}$$

**Step 5 — Mass of Air at Atmospheric Conditions (1 mark):**

$$\rho_{atm} = \frac{101{,}300}{287 \cdot 298} = 1.184 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$m_{a,\,atm} = \rho_{atm} \cdot V_s = 1.184 \cdot 0.972 = 1.151 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 — Verification (1 mark):**

$$D_r = \frac{m_{a,\,sup}}{m_{a,\,atm}} = \frac{1.376}{1.151} = 1.195 \approx 1.15 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Final Tabulated Answer (1 mark):**
| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $m_{a,\,sup}$ | 1.376 kg/cycle |
| $m_{a,\,ret}$ | 1.294 kg/cycle |
| $m_{a,\,atm}$ | 1.151 kg/cycle |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper Style]**
> **(a)** Define **scavenging efficiency**, **trapping efficiency**, and **charging efficiency**. Derive the relationship $\eta_c = \eta_{sc} \cdot \eta_t$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **differences between uniflow and loop scavenging** with neat diagrams. State which type is used in **modern two-stroke marine main engines** and justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definitions (3 marks):**
- **Scavenging Efficiency (1 mark):** The ratio of the **mass of fresh air retained** in the cylinder at the end of scavenging to the **mass of air supplied** by the turbocharger.
$$\eta_{sc} = \frac{m_{a,\,ret}}{m_{a,\,sup}} \times 100$$
- **Trapping Efficiency (1 mark):** The ratio of the **mass of fresh air trapped** in the cylinder after the exhaust valve closes to the **mass of air supplied**.
$$\eta_t = \frac{m_{a,\,trap}}{m_{a,\,sup}} \times 100$$
- **Charging Efficiency (1 mark):** The ratio of the **mass of fresh air trapped** in the cylinder to the **mass of air that would fill the swept volume at atmospheric conditions**.
$$\eta_c = \frac{m_{a,\,trap}}{m_{a,\,atm}} \times 100$$

**Derivation (4 marks):**

From the definition of charging efficiency:

$$\eta_c = \frac{m_{a,\,trap}}{m_{a,\,atm}}$$

Express the trapped mass in terms of the supplied mass using the trapping efficiency:

$$m_{a,\,trap} = \eta_t \cdot m_{a,\,sup}$$

Express the atmospheric fill mass in terms of the supplied mass using the delivery ratio:

$$D_r = \frac{m_{a,\,sup}}{m_{a,\,atm}} \implies m_{a,\,atm} = \frac{m_{a,\,sup}}{D_r}$$

Substitute both into the charging efficiency equation:

$$\eta_c = \frac{\eta_t \cdot m_{a,\,sup}}{m_{a,\,sup}/D_r} = \eta_t \cdot D_r$$

Under the standard assumption that $D_r \approx \eta_{sc}$ (when the comparison is to the atmospheric fill condition):

$$\boxed{\eta_c = \eta_{sc} \cdot \eta_t}$$

This shows that the **charging efficiency is the product of the scavenging efficiency and the trapping efficiency**.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Uniflow Scavenging (2 marks):**
- Air enters through **bottom ports** in the cylinder liner.
- Exhaust gases leave through a **poppet exhaust valve in the cylinder head**.
- Air flows in a **single axial direction** (bottom → top).
- Scavenge ports are **uncovered by the piston** as it moves past BDC.
- **Scavenging efficiency: 95–99%.**
- **Risk of short-circuiting: Negligible.**

**Loop Scavenging (2 marks):**
- Air enters through ports on **one side** of the cylinder.
- Exhaust gases leave through ports on the **opposite side** (no separate exhaust valve).
- Air follows a **loop-shaped helical path** inside the cylinder.
- **Scavenging efficiency: 85–92%.**
- **Risk of short-circuiting: Moderate** — some fresh air can escape directly out the exhaust port.

**Diagrams (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.4 of these notes for Mermaid-based comparison diagrams.

**Type Used in Modern Two-Stroke Marine Main Engines (2 marks):**

**Answer: Uniflow Scavenging.**

**Justification (2 marks):**
- Modern two-stroke marine main engines operate at **high mean effective pressures (up to 20–22 bar)** and require the **highest possible scavenging efficiency** to support complete combustion of heavy fuel oil.
- Uniflow scavenging provides the **best air utilization**, the **lowest short-circuiting losses**, and the **lowest thermal loading** on the cylinder liner.
- The use of a **separate exhaust valve in the cylinder head** allows precise control of valve timing, optimizing the trapping efficiency.
- Major manufacturers (MAN B&W, Wärtsilä) have standardized on uniflow scavenging for all their large low-speed two-stroke engines.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the order of port and valve timing:** Many students incorrectly state that the **scavenge ports close first**, then the exhaust valve. The correct sequence is: **exhaust valve opens first, then scavenge ports open, then exhaust valve closes first, then scavenge ports close.** Reversing this order loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Using the wrong density in numerical problems:** Students often use **atmospheric density (1.2 kg/m³)** when calculating the mass of **supplied** air. The correct approach is to use the **scavenge air density** (which is higher due to compression and the SAC cooling effect).
> 3. **Forgetting to add gauge pressure to atmospheric pressure:** The scavenge pressure given in the question is **gauge**. To compute density, you must add 1.013 bar (or 1.0 bar as an approximation) to get absolute pressure. Skipping this step causes an error of ~80% in the answer.
> 4. **Confusing scavenging efficiency with volumetric efficiency:** Scavenging efficiency is a **freshness ratio** (mass of fresh air retained / mass of air supplied). Volumetric efficiency in a 2-stroke engine is **always > 100%** because the engine is pumped with above-atmospheric pressure air.
> 5. **Not stating the type of scavenging used:** When asked about "scavenging in marine engines," always **specify uniflow** and justify why it is preferred over loop and cross.

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- **Definition:** Scavenging = **Expelling burnt gases + replacing with fresh air** in preparation for the next cycle.

- **Why only in 2-stroke?** Because a two-stroke engine completes the cycle in **one revolution** and has no dedicated induction/exhaust strokes.

- **Universal Type for Marine 2-Stroke Main Engines:** **Uniflow Scavenging** (bottom ports + top exhaust valve).

- **Key Components of the Scavenge Air System:**
  - Turbocharger (compressor + turbine)
  - Scavenge Air Cooler (SAC)
  - Scavenge Air Receiver (SAR)
  - Drain separator
  - Safety relief valve
  - Flame arrester

- **Scavenge Air Pressure Range:** **0.20 to 0.40 bar (gauge)** for large 2-stroke marine engines.

- **Scavenge Air Temperature (after SAC):** **45 – 55 °C** (cooled to increase density).

- **Scavenging Efficiency Formula:**
$$\eta_{sc} = \frac{m_{a,\,ret}}{m_{a,\,sup}} \times 100 \;\; \text{(Typical: 90 – 99 \%)}$$

- **Trapping Efficiency Formula:**
$$\eta_t = \frac{m_{a,\,trap}}{m_{a,\,sup}} \times 100 \;\; \text{(Typical: 85 – 95 \%)}$$

- **Charging Efficiency Formula (The Golden Triangle):**
$$\eta_c = \eta_{sc} \cdot \eta_t \;\; \text{(Typical: 80 – 90 \%)}$$

- **Delivery Ratio:**
$$D_r = \frac{m_{a,\,sup}}{m_{a,\,atm}} \;\; \text{(Typical: 1.05 – 1.40)}$$

- **Density Formula (for air):**
$$\rho = \frac{p}{R \cdot T} \;\; \text{where } R = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$$

- **Scavenge Port Timing:** Ports open ~50–60° BTDC and close ~60–80° ATDC. Exhaust valve opens ~70–90° BTDC and closes ~30–50° ATDC.

- **Safety Devices:** Flame arrester in SAR, relief valve, oil mist detector — these prevent **scavenge fires** from escalating into catastrophic engine damage.

- **MARPOL Compliance:** Proper scavenging is essential for meeting **MARPOL Annex VI** NOx Tier II/III emission limits.

- **Common Engine Examples:** MAN B&W ME-series, Wärtsilä X-series, Mitsubishi UEC — all use uniflow scavenging.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supercharging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Supercharging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supercharging** is the process of supplying combustion air to an internal combustion engine at a pressure **greater than atmospheric pressure** (i.e., boost pressure > 0 bar gauge). In marine engineering, supercharging is achieved primarily by an exhaust-gas-driven **turbocharger**, which recovers waste energy from the exhaust manifold to drive a centrifugal compressor that pressurizes the charge air before it enters the engine cylinders.

The primary objective of supercharging a marine diesel engine is to **increase the mass of air inducted per cycle** for a given engine displacement. This directly raises the **Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP)**, allowing the engine to deliver significantly higher power output without a proportional increase in engine size, weight, or fuel consumption per unit power.

### Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Atmospheric Pressure (P₀):** **1.013 bar** at standard sea-level conditions.
- **Boost Pressure (P_b):** Typically **1.8 bar to 3.5 bar (absolute)** in modern 2-stroke marine main engines.
- **Pressure Ratio (r_p):** Ratio of compressor delivery pressure to inlet pressure — usually between **2.0 and 4.5**.
- **Turbocharger Efficiency (η_T/C):** Combined turbine-compressor efficiency ranges between **55% to 65%** for axial turbines and **60% to 75%** for radial (centrifugal) turbines.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Bellows and the Fireplace"**
> Imagine you are trying to keep a large fireplace (the engine cylinder) burning at maximum heat (high power). If you simply rely on natural draft (atmospheric air entering the cylinder at ~1 bar), the fire smoulders and produces moderate heat. Now, attach a **bellows** (the turbocharger compressor) to the fireplace. As the hot exhaust gases rush out of the chimney (driving the turbine), they spin the bellows, which then **forces a larger volume of fresh air into the firebox at higher pressure**. The fire burns hotter, faster, and more completely — extracting more useful work from the same amount of fuel. This is precisely what a turbocharger does: it converts otherwise-wasted exhaust heat into useful intake-air pressure.

### KTU 2024 Course Outcome Alignment

This topic satisfies the following KTU 2024 Course Outcomes (COs):

- **CO2:** *Analyze the performance and operating principles of marine diesel engine auxiliary systems.*
- **CO3:** *Evaluate the thermodynamic and mechanical principles governing marine power plant components.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Supercharging? — The Thermodynamic Justification

The Indicated Power (IP) of a diesel engine is given by the indicator diagram. To increase power output without enlarging engine dimensions, the engineer has two options:
1. Increase the **mean effective pressure (MEP)** by raising cylinder pressure.
2. Increase the **engine speed (N)**.

Supercharging is the practical realization of option (1). By increasing the **density of the charge air (ρ)**, more oxygen molecules are packed into each cylinder, enabling more complete combustion of a correspondingly larger fuel mass, and hence a higher brake power output.

### 2.2 Types of Supercharging Systems (KTU Board Favourite)

Marine diesel engines predominantly use the following configurations:

| **Type** | **Energy Source** | **Typical Application** | **Advantages** | **Disadvantages** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Exhaust-Gas Turbocharging** | Waste exhaust energy | All 2-stroke & large 4-stroke marine engines | No parasitic power loss; high efficiency; recovers waste heat | Lag at low loads; requires scavenge air cooling |
| **Mechanical (Blower) Supercharging** | Engine crankshaft via gearing | Older 4-stroke auxiliaries, low-speed boost | Immediate response at all loads | Consumes up to 15% of engine power |
| **Constant-Pressure Turbocharging** | Exhaust manifold common pipe | Large 2-stroke MAN B&W, Wärtsilä engines | Stable turbo speed; smooth scavenging pulse | Larger exhaust manifold volume required |
| **Pulse Turbocharging** | Discrete exhaust pulses | High-speed 4-stroke engines | Better energy utilization at part load | Complex manifold design; pulse interference |
| **Sequential / Combined System** | Turbocharger + auxiliary blower | Medium-speed 4-stroke auxiliary engines | Air supply at all load ranges | Higher cost, more components |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulae below are based on the standard air-standard cycle analysis taught in PCNST503 Module 2.**

| **Equation** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Indicated Power (4-stroke) | $IP = \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot k}{60}$ | $P_m$ = IMEP (bar), $L$ = stroke (m), $A$ = piston area (m²), $n$ = rpm, $k$ = number of cylinders | Baseline power calculation |
| Charge Density (Ideal Gas) | $\rho = \dfrac{P}{R \cdot T}$ | $P$ in Pa, $T$ in K, $R = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ | Relates boost pressure to mass of air |
| Mass Flow of Air | $\dot{m}_a = \rho \cdot V_d \cdot \dfrac{N}{2} \text{ (4-stroke)}$ | $V_d$ = displacement (m³) | Air demand of engine |
| Pressure Ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | $P_1$ = compressor inlet (abs), $P_2$ = compressor outlet (abs) | Turbocharger performance |
| Turbocharger Power Balance | $W_T = \dfrac{c_p \cdot \dot{m}_e \cdot (T_3 - T_4)}{\eta_T}$ | $c_p$ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K, $\dot{m}_e$ = exhaust mass flow, $T_3, T_4$ in K | Turbine output |
| Compressor Work | $W_C = c_p \cdot \dot{m}_a \cdot (T_2 - T_1) / \eta_C$ | $T_1, T_2$ in K, $\eta_C$ = compressor efficiency | Power required to compress charge air |
| BMEP Increase | $BMEP_2 = BMEP_1 \cdot \dfrac{P_b}{P_{atm}}$ | $P_b$ = boost pressure (abs), $P_{atm} = 1.013 \text{ bar}$ | Power gain estimation |
| Adiabatic Compression Temp | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$ | $\gamma = 1.4$ for air | Compressor discharge temperature |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Supercharging is fundamental to the **economy and emissions compliance** of modern ships:
- **MARPOL Annex VI** compliance: Higher specific power output with lower specific fuel oil consumption (SFOC, typically **165–175 g/kWh** for modern 2-stroke engines).
- Enables **slow steaming** operations (engine running at 50–60% MCR) while maintaining turbocharger matching and stable combustion.
- Critical for **Tier II / Tier III** emission control, as higher charge-air density supports EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) systems.
- Allows ship designers to use **smaller, lighter engines** for the same propulsion power, saving engine room volume and increasing cargo capacity.

### 2.5 Key Operating Principles — Bullet Logic

- **Step 1 — Exhaust Recovery:** Hot exhaust gas (T ≈ 400–550 °C) leaves the cylinder with significant enthalpy.
- **Step 2 — Turbine Expansion:** Gas expands through the turbine blades, converting thermal + kinetic energy into mechanical shaft work.
- **Step 3 — Compressor Compression:** Same shaft drives the centrifugal compressor, raising charge air from ~1 bar to ~3 bar.
- **Step 4 — Charge Air Cooling:** Compressed air is cooled in a charge air cooler (CAC) from ~180 °C to ~45–55 °C, **increasing density by ~2.5×**.
- **Step 5 — Scavenging:** Cool, dense air drives out residual combustion gases from the cylinder (scavenge process).
- **Step 6 — Combustion:** Denser air enables more fuel to be burned efficiently, raising BMEP and brake power.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Boost Pressure and Engine Power Output

We start with the fundamental equation for brake power of a marine diesel engine:

$$
BP = \dfrac{BMEP \cdot V_d \cdot N}{2} \cdot k \quad \text{(for 4-stroke)}
$$

For a naturally aspirated (NA) engine, BMEP_Na is determined by atmospheric conditions. When a turbocharger raises the intake pressure, the **mass of air inducted** per cycle increases proportionally to the boost pressure (assuming isothermal charge cooling). Let the ratio of supercharged to naturally aspirated BMEP be:

$$
\dfrac{BMEP_{sc}}{BMEP_{na}} = \dfrac{P_b}{P_{atm}}
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1:** Start with the air mass entering the cylinder per cycle for naturally aspirated conditions:

$$
m_{a,na} = \dfrac{P_{atm} \cdot V_s}{R \cdot T_{atm}}
$$

where $V_s$ is the swept volume per cylinder.

**Step 2:** After supercharging, the charge pressure becomes $P_b$ and the temperature after the charge air cooler is $T_b$. The new air mass is:

$$
m_{a,sc} = \dfrac{P_b \cdot V_s}{R \cdot T_b} \cdot \eta_v
$$

where $\eta_v$ is the volumetric efficiency of the engine (typically 0.85–0.95).

**Step 3:** The ratio of mass inducted (and hence fuel burnt, by stoichiometric equivalence) becomes:

$$
\dfrac{m_{a,sc}}{m_{a,na}} = \dfrac{P_b \cdot T_{atm}}{P_{atm} \cdot T_b} \cdot \eta_v
$$

**Step 4:** Since the brake power is directly proportional to the mass of air (and fuel) processed, we can write:

$$
\dfrac{BP_{sc}}{BP_{na}} = \dfrac{P_b}{P_{atm}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{atm}}{T_b} \cdot \eta_v
$$

**Step 5:** Assuming the charge air cooler brings $T_b$ close to $T_{atm}$, and $\eta_v \approx 0.9$:

$$
\boxed{BP_{sc} \approx BP_{na} \cdot \dfrac{0.9 \cdot P_b}{P_{atm}}}
$$

**Numerical Example:** If a 4-stroke marine auxiliary engine naturally produces 1000 kW at $P_{atm} = 1.013$ bar, and is supercharged to $P_b = 2.5$ bar (absolute) with charge air cooling:

$$
BP_{sc} = 1000 \times \dfrac{0.9 \times 2.5}{1.013} \approx 2221 \text{ kW}
$$

This represents a **122% increase** in power output from the same engine block.

### 3.2 Derivation: Compressor Discharge Temperature

The compression in the turbocharger compressor is best modeled as an **adiabatic (isentropic) process**, with efficiency corrections.

**Step 1:** Isentropic (ideal) temperature rise for compression from $P_1$ to $P_2$:

$$
T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}
$$

For air, $\gamma = 1.4$, so $\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$.

**Step 2:** Real (actual) compressor outlet temperature accounts for isentropic efficiency $\eta_C$:

$$
T_2 = T_1 + \dfrac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_C}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$
\boxed{T_2 = T_1 \left[ 1 + \dfrac{1}{\eta_C} \left( r_p^{0.2857} - 1 \right) \right]}
$$

**Step 4:** Numerical evaluation for $T_1 = 300$ K, $r_p = 3.0$, $\eta_C = 0.75$:

$$
r_p^{0.2857} = 3.0^{0.2857} \approx 1.3807
$$

$$
T_2 = 300 \left[ 1 + \dfrac{1}{0.75}(1.3807 - 1) \right]
$$

$$
T_2 = 300 \left[ 1 + \dfrac{0.3807}{0.75} \right]
$$

$$
T_2 = 300 \times 1.5076 \approx 452.3 \text{ K} \approx 179 \text{ °C}
$$

This is why a **charge air cooler** is essential to bring the air temperature back down to ~45 °C (318 K) before it enters the cylinder, dramatically increasing density.

### 3.3 Derivation: Turbocharger Turbine Power (Energy Balance)

The turbine extracts energy from the exhaust gas. The power available is:

$$
W_T = \dot{m}_e \cdot c_p \cdot (T_3 - T_4) \cdot \eta_T
$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}_e$ = exhaust mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $T_3$ = turbine inlet temperature (K)
- $T_4$ = turbine outlet temperature (K)
- $\eta_T$ = turbine efficiency
- $c_p$ = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K)

**Equating to compressor work (shaft power balance):**

$$
\dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1) / \eta_C = \dot{m}_e \cdot c_p \cdot (T_3 - T_4) \cdot \eta_T
$$

Since exhaust mass ≈ air mass + fuel mass, and fuel mass is small (~2%), we can simplify $\dot{m}_e \approx \dot{m}_a$:

$$
\boxed{(T_2 - T_1) / \eta_C = (T_3 - T_4) \cdot \eta_T}
$$

This **matching equation** is the heart of turbocharger-engine matching. KTU examiners frequently test students' understanding of this energy balance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Marine Diesel Engine Supercharging System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete functional flow of a turbocharged marine diesel engine, mapping every major component and the energy-pathway interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Intake Filter and Silencer] --> B[Turbocharger Compressor]
    B --> C[Charge Air Cooler CAC]
    C --> D[Scavenge Air Receiver]
    D --> E[Engine Cylinder Combustion]
    E --> F[Exhaust Manifold]
    F --> G[Turbocharger Turbine]
    G --> H[Exhaust UpTake and Stack]
    G -.drives shaft.-> B
    E --> I[Crankshaft Output to Propeller]
    J[Sea Water Cooling Pump] -.feeds.-> C
    K[Engine Driven Lube Oil Pump] -.lubricates.-> B
    K -.lubricates.-> G
    L[Starting Air System] -.assists.-> E
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow: Constant-Pressure vs Pulse Turbocharging

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Cylinder 1] --> M1[Common Exhaust Manifold Large Volume]
    A2[Cylinder 2] --> M1
    A3[Cylinder 3] --> M1
    A4[Cylinder 4] --> M1
    M1 --> T1[Turbocharger Turbine]
    T1 --> S1[Steady Continuous Flow]
    
    B1[Cylinder A] --> M2[Branch Manifold Short Pipe]
    B2[Cylinder B] --> M2
    M2 --> T2[Turbocharger Turbine]
    T2 --> S2[Pulsating Discrete Flow]
    
    style M1 fill:#A8D5BA
    style M2 fill:#FFC857
    style S1 fill:#A8D5BA
    style S2 fill:#FFC857
```

### 4.3 Component-Level Architecture of a Marine Turbocharger

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Air Inlet Casing] --> B[Compressor Impeller Radial Type]
    B --> C[Diffuser and Volute Casing]
    C --> D[Compressor Outlet to Charge Air Cooler]
    
    E[Exhaust Inlet Casing] --> F[Turbine Nozzle Ring]
    F --> G[Turbine Rotor Axial or Radial]
    G --> H[Exhaust Outlet Casing]
    
    I[Common Shaft] --- B
    I --- G
    J[Thrust Bearing] --- I
    K[Journal Bearings Two] --- I
    L[Lubrication Oil Supply] --> J
    L --> K
    M[Cooling Water Jacket] --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Note:** The compressor and turbine share a common shaft supported by two journal bearings and one thrust bearing. The whole rotor assembly is dynamically balanced to high precision (ISO 1940 Grade G2.5 or better) to withstand continuous high-speed operation at **10,000 to 30,000 rpm**, depending on turbocharger size.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 2-Mark & 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

Define supercharging. State any two advantages of supercharging a marine diesel engine.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Definition (1.5 marks):* Supercharging is the process of supplying air to the engine cylinder at a pressure higher than the prevailing atmospheric pressure, thereby increasing the mass of air (and consequently the mass of fuel) inducted per cycle.

*Advantages (1.5 marks — any two):*
1. The power output of a given engine can be **substantially increased** (typically by 50% to 250%) without a proportional increase in engine size or weight.
2. The **specific fuel oil consumption (SFOC)** is reduced, improving thermal efficiency.
3. Better **scavenging** of combustion gases is achieved, leading to cleaner combustion and lower exhaust emissions.
4. The engine can be **down-sized** for the same propulsion requirement, saving engine room space and shipbuilding cost.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**

Distinguish between constant-pressure turbocharging and pulse turbocharging.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Constant-Pressure** | **Pulse** |
|---|---|---|
| Exhaust manifold volume | Very large, acts as a plenum | Small, branch pipes |
| Pressure at turbine inlet | Nearly steady, smoothed | Highly pulsating, follows firing pulses |
| Best suited for | Low-speed 2-stroke main engines | High-speed 4-stroke auxiliary engines |
| Turbo efficiency at part load | Lower (wasted pulse energy in plenum) | Higher (uses pulse energy directly) |
| Manifold design | Complex, large | Simpler, compact |

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an exhaust-gas turbocharger used in a marine diesel engine. State the function of the charge air cooler. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stroke marine auxiliary diesel engine has a BMEP of 8 bar at naturally aspirated conditions. After fitting a turbocharger, the boost pressure is 2.8 bar (absolute) and the charge air temperature after the cooler is 35 °C. Calculate the new BMEP and the percentage increase in power output. Assume volumetric efficiency remains 0.92 and atmospheric temperature is 30 °C. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Working Principle (5 marks):**
The exhaust-gas turbocharger consists of a turbine and a centrifugal compressor mounted on a common shaft.

- **Exhaust side (Turbine):** Hot exhaust gases from the engine's exhaust manifold enter the turbine casing and pass through the nozzle ring, where they expand and accelerate. The high-velocity gas strikes the turbine rotor blades, causing the rotor to spin at high speed (10,000–30,000 rpm). The exhaust then leaves the turbine outlet to the exhaust uptake and stack.
- **Compressor side:** The same shaft drives a centrifugal (radial-flow) compressor. Atmospheric air enters the compressor inlet, is accelerated by the rotating impeller, and is then decelerated in the diffuser, which converts kinetic energy into pressure energy. The pressurized air (boost air) is delivered to the charge air cooler.
- **Charge Air Cooler (CAC):** The function of the CAC is to cool the compressed charge air from ~150–180 °C down to 40–55 °C using sea water as the cooling medium. Cooling **increases air density** (per ideal gas law), thereby increasing the mass of oxygen delivered to the cylinder, improving combustion efficiency, and reducing the thermal load on engine components.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 above.

---

**Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- Naturally aspirated BMEP = 8 bar
- Boost pressure (absolute) = 2.8 bar
- Atmospheric pressure = 1.013 bar
- Charge air temperature after cooler, $T_b$ = 35 °C = 308 K
- Atmospheric temperature, $T_{atm}$ = 30 °C = 303 K
- Volumetric efficiency, $\eta_v$ = 0.92

**Step 1:** Apply the supercharging power-ratio equation derived in Section 3.1:

$$
\dfrac{BP_{sc}}{BP_{na}} = \dfrac{P_b}{P_{atm}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{atm}}{T_b} \cdot \eta_v
$$

$$
\dfrac{BP_{sc}}{BP_{na}} = \dfrac{2.8}{1.013} \cdot \dfrac{303}{308} \cdot 0.92
$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the temperature ratio:

$$
\dfrac{303}{308} = 0.9838
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$
\dfrac{BP_{sc}}{BP_{na}} = 2.764 \times 0.9838 \times 0.92
$$

$$
\dfrac{BP_{sc}}{BP_{na}} = 2.764 \times 0.9051 = 2.502
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the new BMEP. Since the engine speed and displacement are unchanged, BMEP scales linearly with BP:

$$
\boxed{BMEP_{sc} = 8 \times 2.502 \approx 20.02 \text{ bar}}
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the percentage increase in power:

$$
\text{\% Increase} = (2.502 - 1) \times 100 = \boxed{150.2\%}
$$

**Conclusion:** The supercharged engine produces **150% more power** at the same engine speed, with the BMEP rising from 8 bar to 20.02 bar. *(Full 7 marks awarded for correct methodology, all steps shown, and final numerical answer boxed.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain with a sketch the construction and working of a centrifugal compressor used in marine turbochargers. Compare its performance with a positive-displacement (Roots-type) blower. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A turbocharger compressor receives air at 1.013 bar and 25 °C and delivers it at a pressure ratio of 3.2. Calculate:
   (i) The isentropic discharge temperature.
   (ii) The actual discharge temperature, assuming isentropic efficiency of 78%.
   **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Centrifugal Compressor Construction (3 marks):**
A centrifugal compressor consists of:
- **Inlet casing** with axial air entry.
- **Impeller** — a backward-curved or radial-bladed rotor, rotating at 10,000–30,000 rpm.
- **Diffuser** — stationary vanes that decelerate the high-velocity air, converting kinetic energy to pressure.
- **Volute casing** — collects the diffused air and delivers it to the discharge pipe.
- **Shaft** — common to the turbine, supported on anti-friction bearings.

**Working (2 marks):** Air enters axially, is accelerated radially outward by the impeller, then decelerated in the diffuser. The pressure rise is continuous and steady, making it ideal for high-volume marine engine applications.

**Comparison with Roots Blower (2 marks):**

| **Feature** | **Centrifugal** | **Roots Blower** |
|---|---|---|
| Flow type | Continuous, steady | Pulsating, intermittent |
| Pressure ratio | 2.0 to 4.5 (single stage) | 1.2 to 1.8 |
| Speed | Very high (10,000+ rpm) | Low (1,000–3,000 rpm) |
| Drive | Exhaust turbine (waste energy) | Mechanical (engine crankshaft) |
| Efficiency | 70–82% | 50–60% |
| Application | Modern 2-stroke and 4-stroke marine | Older 4-stroke, low-load starting |

---

**Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- Inlet pressure, $P_1$ = 1.013 bar
- Inlet temperature, $T_1$ = 25 °C = 298 K
- Pressure ratio, $r_p$ = 3.2
- Isentropic efficiency, $\eta_C$ = 0.78
- $\gamma$ for air = 1.4, so $\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}$ = 0.2857

**(i) Isentropic Discharge Temperature (3.5 marks):**

$$
T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot r_p^{0.2857}
$$

$$
T_{2s} = 298 \times 3.2^{0.2857}
$$

Compute the exponent:

$$
3.2^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln(3.2)} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 1.1632} = e^{0.3323} = 1.3941
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_{2s} = 298 \times 1.3941 \approx 415.4 \text{ K}
$$

Converting to °C:

$$
T_{2s} = 415.4 - 273 = \boxed{142.4 \text{ °C}}
$$

**(ii) Actual Discharge Temperature (3.5 marks):**

$$
T_2 = T_1 + \dfrac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_C}
$$

$$
T_2 = 298 + \dfrac{415.4 - 298}{0.78}
$$

$$
T_2 = 298 + \dfrac{117.4}{0.78}
$$

$$
T_2 = 298 + 150.5 = 448.5 \text{ K}
$$

Converting to °C:

$$
T_2 = 448.5 - 273 = \boxed{175.5 \text{ °C}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This elevated temperature (~175 °C) confirms why a charge air cooler is mandatory — without cooling, the air would be too hot and of insufficient density to support efficient combustion, and components like cylinder liners and valves would face severe thermal stress.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** when applying the isentropic temperature equation. KTU examiners deduct a full step-mark for unit inconsistency.
> 2. **Mixing up gauge and absolute pressures** in boost-pressure problems. Boost pressure is almost always quoted in **bar (absolute)** in supercharging problems.
> 3. **Ignoring volumetric efficiency** $\eta_v$ in the BMEP derivation — students frequently use the simple ratio $P_b / P_{atm}$, missing the temperature-correction factor, and lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing isentropic efficiency formula direction** — remember, $T_2$ (actual) is **higher** than $T_{2s}$ (isentropic), since real compression always generates more heat than ideal.
> 5. **Not sketching the block diagram in 7-mark theory questions** — a missing diagram in part (a) typically costs 2 marks even if the explanation is perfect.
> 6. **Rounding prematurely** — always carry at least 4 significant figures through intermediate steps to avoid accumulation errors in the final answer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Supercharging** = Supplying combustion air at pressure > 1 atm to raise BMEP and power output.
- [x] The **turbocharger** is the dominant supercharging device on modern ships, driven by waste exhaust energy.
- [x] The compressor and turbine share a **common shaft** running at **10,000–30,000 rpm**.
- [x] **Boost pressure** for marine 2-stroke engines: typically **1.8–3.5 bar (absolute)**.
- [x] **Charge air cooler (CAC)** is essential: it cools air from ~180 °C down to ~45 °C, increasing density.
- [x] The **isentropic temperature equation** $T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ is the most tested formula.
- [x] **Constant-pressure** turbocharging suits 2-stroke mains; **pulse** turbocharging suits 4-stroke auxiliaries.
- [x] The **turbocharger power-balance equation** links compressor work to turbine energy extraction.
- [x] For every **1 bar increase in BMEP**, engine power rises proportionally (at constant N).
- [x] Modern supercharged 2-stroke engines achieve SFOC of **165–175 g/kWh** — among the most efficient heat engines in existence.
- [x] Always convert **°C → K** (add 273) before applying any gas-law or isentropic equation.
- [x] **Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_v$ (typically 0.85–0.95) must be included in BMEP-rise calculations for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Starting and Reversing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Starting and Reversing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Starting** in the context of marine prime movers refers to the controlled initiation of the thermodynamic working cycle in the engine cylinder by supplying compressed air (or other working medium) to push the piston over the compression stroke until fuel injection and self-sustained combustion begin. For marine diesel engines, this is almost universally achieved by admitting high-pressure compressed air (typically **30 bar**) into the cylinder through a pneumatically actuated starting valve precisely timed to the Top Dead Centre (TDC) position.

**Reversing** is the operational procedure by which a marine propulsion engine changes its direction of rotation from *ahead* to *astern* (or vice versa), enabling the vessel to alter its course, stop, or move backward. For slow-speed two-stroke main engines, reversing is accomplished **without any gearbox** by axial shifting of the camshaft to engage a different set of cams (ahead cams or astern cams), thereby altering the timing of exhaust valve actuation and fuel injection. For medium-speed four-stroke engines coupled to a fixed-pitch propeller through a reduction gearbox, reversing is performed by an **ahead-astern hydraulic or pneumatic clutch-coupling** inside the gearbox.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition Box**
> - **Starting Air Pressure (p):** Typically **30 bar** (3 MPa) for main propulsion diesel engines.
> - **Number of Main Starting Air Compressors:** At least **two**, with one standby, each capable of refilling the air receivers in **1 hour**.
> - **Minimum Number of Consecutive Starts:** Sufficient compressed air must be stored for **12 consecutive starts** (for reversible main engines) without recharging.
> - **Reversing Time (t_r):** The time from the order "stop" to the engine running in the opposite direction. For slow-speed engines: **15–30 seconds**; for medium-speed engines: **5–10 seconds**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

### Starting — The "Balloon Push" Analogy
Imagine an empty syringe lying on a table with the nozzle blocked. If you blow hard into the open end, the piston inside will be **pushed** forward because the air pressure has nowhere else to go. This is exactly how a marine diesel engine is started: high-pressure air is admitted into the cylinder when the piston is near TDC, the trapped air acts like a compressed spring and **pushes the piston down** with tremendous force, rotating the crankshaft. Once the engine rotates fast enough, fuel is injected, it self-ignites due to compression heat, and the engine "fires" on its own — the starting air is then cut off.

### Reversing — The "Two Different Tracks" Analogy
Think of a railway engine that needs to travel both north and south on the same track. One solution is to install **two sets of rails** for each direction, and a **switch** that diverts the engine to the correct set. In a slow-speed two-stroke engine, the camshaft carries **two sets of cams** (ahead cams and astern cams) on the same shaft. The shaft slides axially (like a railway switch) so that either the ahead cam or the astern cam operates the exhaust valve and fuel pump plunger. Different cam profiles mean different valve timings, which is what makes the engine run forward or backward.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is reversing so critical on ships?**
> Ships cannot steer when moving slowly and have **no brakes**. The only way to stop or back up is to reverse the propeller direction. Loss of reversing capability is considered a **critical equipment failure** under Classification Society rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS) and may result in detention of the vessel.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (Slow-Speed 2-Stroke) | Typical Value (Medium-Speed 4-Stroke) |
|---|---|---|
| Starting air pressure | **30 bar** | **25–30 bar** |
| Starting valve opening pressure | 12–15 bar (minimum) | 10–12 bar |
| Air bottle capacity | 1.5–3 m³ per bottle | 0.5–1.5 m³ per bottle |
| Starting air temperature | 50–60 °C (after compression) | 45–55 °C |
| Reversal time | 15–30 s | 5–10 s |
| Minimum starts from full bottle | **12 consecutive** | 6–10 consecutive |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Starting Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping — Starting Methods**

**A. Compressed Air Starting** (Universal on Main Engines)
- Direct admission of compressed air into the working cylinder.
- Used in 95% of merchant ship main engines.

**B. Electrical Starting** (Auxiliaries, small craft)
- Battery → Starter motor → Flywheel.
- Limited to engines up to ~1000 kW.

**C. Hydraulic Starting** (Emergency/portable)
- High-pressure oil drives a hydraulic motor coupled to the crankshaft.

**D. Air Motor Starting** (Very large slow-speed engines)
- A separate pneumatic vane or piston motor (the **air starter**) is flange-coupled to the engine crankshaft via a self-disengaging clutch. The air motor is then mechanically disconnected once combustion starts.

**E. Hand Starting** (Emergency lifeboat engines only)
- Recoil starter or pull-cord for lifeboat diesel engines per SOLAS.

---

## 1.5 Classification of Reversing Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping — Reversing Methods**

**A. Direct Reversal (Slow-Speed 2-Stroke)**
- Camshaft axial shift mechanism.
- No gearbox, no clutch.

**B. Indirect Reversal (Medium-Speed 4-Stroke with Reduction Gearbox)**
- **Hydraulic multi-disc clutch** engages ahead or astern gear trains.
- **Pneumatic clutch** on smaller installations.
- **Reverse-reduction gear (RRG)** with single or double reduction.

**C. Controllable Pitch Propeller (CPP) Reversal**
- The propeller blades rotate to change pitch; the engine itself does not reverse.
- Used in tugs, naval vessels, some cruise ships.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Starting Air System — Working Principle

The starting air system on a marine diesel engine consists of the following logical chain:

1. **Atmospheric air → Intake filter → Main starting air compressor** (typically 2-stage, oil-free, reciprocating, with intercooler and aftercooler).
2. **Compressed air → Aftercooler → Moisture separator → Air receiver (bottle)** at **30 bar**.
3. **Air receiver → Main starting air line** (deck piping, with branch to each cylinder).
4. **Main starting air line → Automatic non-return valve → Cylinder starting valve** (one per cylinder, cam-actuated).
5. **Cylinder starting valve opens near TDC** → Air enters cylinder → Piston pushed down → Crankshaft rotates.
6. Once engine reaches firing speed (~30–60 rpm), fuel is injected, combustion begins, starting air is cut off.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Safety Component — The Automatic Non-Return Valve**
> A non-return valve is fitted between the starting air manifold and each cylinder. Its purpose: if the starting valve leaks, it prevents **hot combustion gases from entering the air manifold** and causing a manifold explosion (a rare but catastrophic event). Classification societies mandate this valve on every cylinder.

---

## 2.2 Air Consumption Theory

The volume of air required per start depends on:
- **Cylinder swept volume (V_s)** = $\dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L$
- **Number of cylinders (n_c)**
- **Number of revolutions during cranking (N_rev)**
- **Volumetric efficiency / admission factor (k)**
- **Ratio of air bottle pressure to starting pressure (pressure ratio)**

**Effective Air Volume per Start (Free Air Equivalent):**

$$V_{air} = V_s \cdot n_c \cdot N_{rev} \cdot k_{adm} \cdot \frac{p_{cyl}}{p_{atm}}$$

Where:
- $V_s$ = swept volume of one cylinder (m³)
- $n_c$ = number of cylinders
- $N_{rev}$ = number of revolutions to bring engine to firing speed (typically 1–2)
- $k_{adm}$ = admission factor (0.85–0.95)
- $p_{cyl}$ = absolute pressure in cylinder at admission (bar abs)
- $p_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure (≈ 1.013 bar)

---

## 2.3 Air Bottle Capacity Calculation (KTU High-Yield Formula)

The required capacity of the starting air receiver is found by:

$$V_{bottle} = \frac{V_{air} \cdot N_{starts}}{p_{bottle}/p_{atm} - p_{min}/p_{atm}}$$

Or in the more rigorous form using adiabatic expansion:

$$V_{bottle} = \frac{V_{air} \cdot N_{starts} \cdot (p_{cyl}/p_{atm})^{\frac{1}{n}}}{(p_{bottle}/p_{atm})^{\frac{1}{n}} - (p_{min}/p_{atm})^{\frac{1}{n}}}$$

Where the **adiabatic index n = 1.4** for air (isentropic expansion during the start).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification Society Rule (DNV / Lloyd's / ABS):**
> The air receiver(s) must hold enough air for **at least 12 consecutive starts** of a reversible main engine (or 6 starts for non-reversible) without replenishment. Air pressure must never fall below the **minimum starting pressure** at which the air still has enough force to push the piston over the compression stroke.

---

## 2.4 Reversing Mechanism — Slow-Speed 2-Stroke Engine

The reversing sequence for a slow-speed main engine (e.g., MAN B&W, Wärtsilä) is:

| Step | Action | Time |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Telegraph set to "Stop" or "Astern" | t = 0 s |
| 2 | Fuel rack cuts off (engine no longer fires) | 0–2 s |
| 3 | Engine runs on starting air briefly (motoring) to position camshaft | 2–5 s |
| 4 | Air brake applied (if fitted) to halt rotation | 5–8 s |
| 5 | Camshaft axial shifting motor energized (hydraulic) | 8–10 s |
| 6 | Camshaft slides axially to engage astern cams | 10–15 s |
| 7 | Reversal completed; ready for astern start | 15–20 s |
| 8 | Starting air admitted in reverse direction; engine runs astern | 20–25 s |

> [!NOTE]
> The camshaft shifting mechanism is driven by a **hydraulic servomotor** controlled from the bridge telegraph via a pneumatic-hydraulic control system. Position feedback is provided by camshaft position indicators on the engineer's console.

---

## 2.5 Reversing Mechanism — Medium-Speed 4-stroke with Reverse Reduction Gear (RRG)

For medium-speed engines (e.g., Wärtsilä 4-stroke auxiliary/main with gearbox):

- The engine itself **always rotates in the same direction** (typically clockwise from aft).
- Reversing is achieved by engaging either the **ahead clutch** or the **astern clutch** in the reduction gearbox.
- The clutches are **hydraulically operated multi-disc friction clutches** (oil-actuated).
- A **hydraulic power pack** supplies oil at 15–25 bar to actuate the clutch pistons.
- The sequence: Disengage ahead clutch → engine continues idling → engage astern clutch → propeller reverses within 2–3 seconds.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PRINT-READY KTU FORMULA SHEET — STARTING & REVERSING**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ | Cylinder swept volume | m³ |
| 2 | $V_{air} = V_s \cdot n_c \cdot N_{rev} \cdot k$ | Free air per start | m³ |
| 3 | $V_{bottle} = \frac{V_{air} \cdot N_s}{R-1}$ | Bottle capacity (approx.) | m³ |
| 4 | $R = p_{bottle}/p_{atm}$ | Pressure ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $p_{min} = 0.4 \cdot p_{nominal}$ | Minimum cut-off pressure | bar |
| 6 | $t_r = t_{stop} + t_{shift} + t_{restart}$ | Total reversal time | s |
| 7 | $P_{comp} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot R \cdot T_1 \cdot n}{n-1} \left[ \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$ | Compressor power (isentropic) | kW |
| 8 | $\dot{V}_{comp} = \frac{V_{bottle} \cdot (p_{bottle}-p_{min})}{p_{atm} \cdot t_{refill}}$ | Compressor delivery rate | m³/s |
| 9 | $N_{starts} = \frac{V_{bottle} \cdot (R-1) \cdot p_{atm}}{V_{air} \cdot p_{cyl}}$ | Number of starts per bottle | dimensionless |
| 10 | $V_{air,total} = V_s \cdot n_c \cdot k_{adm} \cdot N_{rev}$ | Air per start (cumulative) | m³ |

> **Legend:** $D$ = bore (m), $L$ = stroke (m), $n_c$ = no. of cylinders, $N_{rev}$ = revolutions during cranking, $k$ = admission factor, $N_s$ = required number of starts (12 for reversible), $R$ = pressure ratio, $p$ = pressure, $t_r$ = reversal time, $t_{refill}$ = refill time (typically 1 hour = 3600 s).

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Starting and reversing systems are the operational heart of ship manoeuvring. Every commercial vessel must demonstrate **full ahead-to-astern reversal** during sea trials, and classification surveyors test reversing capability at every docking (every 2.5–5 years). Failure to reverse can lead to:

- **Collision risk** in confined waters (channels, ports).
- **Detention by Port State Control** (Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU).
- **Insurance claim rejection** for collision damages.

> [!NOTE]
> The **reversal time** is a critical KTU numerical: the faster an engine can reverse, the more manoeuvrable the ship. Modern 2-stroke engines with electronic camshaft control (e.g., MAN B&W ME-series, Wärtsilä X-series) can reverse in **less than 10 seconds** — a vast improvement over the 30–60 seconds of older designs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Air Consumption Per Start

### Given:
- A 6-cylinder, 4-stroke marine diesel engine with bore $D = 0.50$ m, stroke $L = 1.20$ m.
- Engine must be motored for $N_{rev} = 1.5$ revolutions to reach firing speed.
- Admission factor $k_{adm} = 0.90$.
- Cylinder admission pressure $p_{cyl} = 30$ bar (gauge) = 31.013 bar (abs).
- Atmospheric pressure $p_{atm} = 1.013$ bar (abs).

### Step 1: Calculate swept volume of one cylinder
The cylinder swept volume (displacement per cylinder) is:

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L$$

Substituting $D = 0.50$ m and $L = 1.20$ m:

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.50)^2 \cdot (1.20)$$

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.25 \cdot 1.20$$

$$V_s = \frac{3.14159}{4} \cdot 0.30$$

$$V_s = 0.7854 \cdot 0.30 = 0.23562 \text{ m}^3$$

So the swept volume per cylinder is $V_s \approx 0.2356$ m³.

### Step 2: Calculate the total swept volume of the engine
For a 6-cylinder engine:

$$V_{total} = V_s \cdot n_c = 0.23562 \cdot 6 = 1.4137 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 3: Calculate free air consumed per start
The free air equivalent is the volume the air would occupy at atmospheric pressure. Since the air is admitted at 31.013 bar (abs) and expands isothermally into the cylinder:

$$V_{air} = V_{total} \cdot N_{rev} \cdot k_{adm} \cdot \frac{p_{cyl}}{p_{atm}}$$

Substituting values:

$$V_{air} = 1.4137 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 0.90 \cdot \frac{31.013}{1.013}$$

Computing step-by-step:
- $1.4137 \cdot 1.5 = 2.1206$
- $2.1206 \cdot 0.90 = 1.9085$
- $\dfrac{31.013}{1.013} = 30.615$ (pressure ratio)

$$V_{air} = 1.9085 \cdot 30.615$$

$$V_{air} = 58.43 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (free air equivalent)}$$

### Step 4: Physical Interpretation
The free air equivalent is the volume this same mass of air would occupy at atmospheric pressure. This is the standard way to express air consumption because it allows direct comparison with atmospheric conditions and is independent of the storage pressure.

> **Result:** $V_{air} \approx 58.4$ m³ of free air per start.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Required Air Bottle Capacity (Full Numerical)

### Given (continuing the same engine):
- Required number of consecutive starts: $N_s = 12$ (Classification Society requirement for reversible main engine).
- Starting air bottle pressure: $p_{bottle} = 30$ bar (gauge) = 31.013 bar (abs).
- Minimum cut-off pressure: $p_{min} = 12$ bar (gauge) = 13.013 bar (abs).
- Atmospheric pressure: $p_{atm} = 1.013$ bar (abs).

### Step 1: Define pressure ratios
$$R_{max} = \frac{p_{bottle}}{p_{atm}} = \frac{31.013}{1.013} = 30.615$$

$$R_{min} = \frac{p_{min}}{p_{atm}} = \frac{13.013}{1.013} = 12.846$$

### Step 2: Apply the simplified bottle capacity formula
For an isothermal approximation (the bottle is large and starts are slow, so heat transfer keeps air near ambient):

$$V_{bottle} = \frac{V_{air} \cdot N_s}{R_{max} - R_{min}}$$

### Step 3: Substitute numerical values
$$V_{bottle} = \frac{58.43 \cdot 12}{30.615 - 12.846}$$

Computing the denominator:
$$30.615 - 12.846 = 17.769$$

Computing the numerator:
$$58.43 \cdot 12 = 701.16$$

### Step 4: Final calculation
$$V_{bottle} = \frac{701.16}{17.769}$$

$$V_{bottle} = 39.46 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Result:** The vessel requires approximately $V_{bottle} \approx 39.5$ m³ of total starting air bottle capacity.

### Step 5: Practical interpretation — number of bottles
If each air bottle is a standard **3 m³ cylinder**, then:

$$N_{bottles} = \frac{39.46}{3} = 13.15 \implies 14 \text{ bottles (rounded up)}$$

In practice, this is distributed as **2 main bottles of ~12 m³ each** plus auxiliary smaller receivers for the auxiliary engines. The exact distribution depends on ship design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Reversal Time Calculation (Approximate)

### Given:
- Slow-speed 2-stroke main engine, $n_c = 6$ cylinders.
- Engine speed at the moment of reversal order: $N = 90$ rpm.
- Brake deceleration torque: $T_{brake} = 1.2 \times 10^6$ N·m.
- Moment of inertia of rotating parts (crankshaft, flywheel, propeller): $I = 2.5 \times 10^5$ kg·m².

### Step 1: Calculate angular deceleration
The angular deceleration $\alpha$ is found from:

$$\alpha = \frac{T_{brake}}{I} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^6}{2.5 \times 10^5} = 4.8 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

### Step 2: Initial angular velocity at reversal
$$\omega_0 = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \cdot 90}{60} = 9.4248 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 3: Time to stop
Using $\omega = \omega_0 - \alpha t$, set $\omega = 0$:

$$t_{stop} = \frac{\omega_0}{\alpha} = \frac{9.4248}{4.8} = 1.963 \text{ s}$$

### Step 4: Camshaft shifting time (typical empirical value)
The hydraulic servomotor takes about $t_{shift} = 8$ s to move the camshaft axially by 200–300 mm.

### Step 5: Restart time in reverse
Time to crank up to firing speed in reverse: $t_{restart} = 5$ s (with starting air).

### Step 6: Total reversal time
$$t_r = t_{stop} + t_{shift} + t_{restart}$$

$$t_r = 1.96 + 8 + 5 = 14.96 \text{ s}$$

> **Result:** $t_r \approx 15$ s — well within the 30 s limit for slow-speed engines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The above derivation assumes a constant braking torque. In reality, friction and load torque are speed-dependent, so the true stopping time is slightly longer (typically 2–3 s for a slow-speed engine at full ahead). A more accurate result uses $\alpha(\omega)$ as a function of speed.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Starting Methods

| Feature | Compressed Air Direct | Air Motor | Electric Starter | Hydraulic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Max engine power | Unlimited | Up to 50 MW | Up to 1 MW | Up to 5 MW |
| Starting energy density | High | High | Low | Medium |
| Maintenance intensity | Low | Medium | Low | High |
| Time to start | 5–10 s | 15–30 s | 3–5 s | 10–20 s |
| Reversal friendly | Yes (direct) | Yes | No | No |
| Typical application | Main 2-stroke | Large 2-stroke | Auxiliaries, gensets | Special craft |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Reversing Methods

| Feature | Direct (Camshaft Shift) | Hydraulic Clutch (RRG) | Controllable Pitch Propeller (CPP) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reversal time | 15–30 s | 5–10 s | < 2 s (pitch only) |
| Engine stops? | Yes | No | No |
| Mechanical complexity | Medium (camshaft) | High (gearbox) | High (hub mechanism) |
| Propeller efficiency | Best (no gear loss) | Lower (~3% gear loss) | Lower at low pitch |
| Typical vessel | Tankers, bulk carriers | Naval, tugs, cruise | Ferries, dredgers, tugs |
| Classification rule | Required for direct-coupled | Required for geared | Equivalent manoeuvrability required |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Starting Air System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following block diagram maps the path of compressed air from the atmosphere to the engine cylinder, with all safety and control features:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> B[Inlet Filter and Silencer]
    B --> C[Main Starting Air Compressor Stage 1]
    C --> D[Intercooler]
    D --> E[Main Starting Air Compressor Stage 2]
    E --> F[Aftercooler]
    F --> G[Moisture Separator and Drain Trap]
    G --> H[Air Receiver No 1 30 bar]
    G --> I[Air Receiver No 2 30 bar]
    H --> J[Main Starting Air Line]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Pressure Reducing Station 30 to 7 bar]
    K --> L[Auxiliary Air Receiver 7 bar]
    L --> M[Auxiliary Engine Starters]
    J --> N[Safety Shut Off Valve]
    N --> O[Automatic Non Return Valve per Cylinder]
    O --> P[Cylinder Starting Valve Cam Actuated]
    P --> Q[Engine Cylinder Combustion Chamber]
    J --> R[Control Air Line]
    R --> S[Bridge Telegraph]
    R --> T[Engine Control Room Panel]
    S --> T
    H --> U[Pressure Gauge and Safety Relief Valve 33 bar]
    I --> U
```

**Description of flow:**
- Path 1: Atmosphere → Compressor → Bottles → 30 bar line → Engine cylinder (Main Engine start).
- Path 2: 30 bar → Reducing station → 7 bar → Auxiliary engines (DG sets, boilers).
- Path 3: Control air to bridge telegraph and ECR for system actuation.

---

## 4.2 Slow-Speed Engine Reversal — Camshaft Axial Shift Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bridge Telegraph Order Astern] --> B[Fuel Rack Cutoff]
    B --> C[Engine Motored on Starting Air]
    C --> D[Air Brake Applied]
    D --> E[Hydraulic Servomotor Energized]
    E --> F[Camshaft Slides Axially 250 mm]
    F --> G{Ahead or Astern Cam Selected}
    G -->|Astern| H[Astern Cam Profile Activated]
    G -->|Ahead| I[Ahead Cam Profile Activated]
    H --> J[Starting Air Readmitted in Reverse]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Engine Runs Astern or Ahead]
    K --> L[Camshaft Position Indicator on ECR]
    L --> M[Telegraph Feedback Confirms]
```

**Working description:**
- The camshaft carries two sets of cams: **A cams (ahead)** and **B cams (astern)** separated axially by ~250 mm.
- Axial shift of the camshaft determines which cam profile is "active" — i.e., operates the exhaust valve and fuel pump rocker.
- The shift is driven by a **hydraulic servomotor** mounted at the driving end of the camshaft.
- Position is locked by a **hydraulic locking pin** to prevent accidental shifting under load.

---

## 4.3 Air Distribution to Multi-Cylinder Engine — Timing Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Starting Air Manifold 30 bar] --> B[Cylinder 1 Starting Valve]
    A --> C[Cylinder 2 Starting Valve]
    A --> D[Cylinder 3 Starting Valve]
    A --> E[Cylinder 4 Starting Valve]
    A --> F[Cylinder 5 Starting Valve]
    A --> G[Cylinder 6 Starting Valve]
    B --> H[Non Return Valve 1]
    C --> I[Non Return Valve 2]
    D --> J[Non Return Valve 3]
    E --> K[Non Return Valve 4]
    F --> L[Non Return Valve 5]
    G --> M[Non Return Valve 6]
    H --> N[Engine Cylinder 1]
    I --> O[Engine Cylinder 2]
    J --> P[Engine Cylinder 3]
    K --> Q[Engine Cylinder 4]
    L --> R[Engine Cylinder 5]
    M --> S[Engine Cylinder 6]
```

**Operating logic:**
- The starting valves are **mechanically (cam) actuated** at precise TDC positions.
- For a 6-cylinder, 2-stroke engine: each cylinder fires every revolution → starting valve opens every 60° of crankshaft rotation (in firing order: 1-5-3-4-2-6 or 1-3-5-2-4-6).
- For a 6-cylinder, 4-stroke engine: each cylinder fires every 2 revolutions → starting valve opens every 120° of crankshaft rotation.

---

## 4.4 Reduction Gearbox (RRG) Reversal — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Medium Speed 4 Stroke Engine] --> B[Input Coupling]
    B --> C[Ahead Clutch Hydraulic Multi Disc]
    B --> D[Astern Clutch Hydraulic Multi Disc]
    C --> E[Ahead Gear Train Reduction]
    D --> F[Astern Gear Train Reduction]
    E --> G[Output Shaft]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Propeller Shaft]
    H --> I[Fixed Pitch Propeller]
    J[Hydraulic Power Pack 20 bar] --> C
    J --> D
    K[Bridge Telegraph] --> J
    K --> L[Control Valves Solenoid Actuated]
    L --> C
    L --> D
```

**Operating logic:**
- The engine runs in **one fixed direction** (typically CW from aft).
- The bridge telegraph sends a signal → hydraulic control valve directs oil pressure to either the ahead or astern clutch.
- The engaged clutch transmits torque through its gear train to the output shaft.
- The two gear trains rotate in opposite directions, providing ahead/astern selection.

---

## 4.5 Hydraulic Camshaft Shifting Mechanism (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bridge Command] --> B[Telegraph Transmitter]
    B --> C[Pneumatic Control Signal]
    C --> D[Solenoid Valve in ECR]
    D --> E[Hydraulic Pump Energized]
    E --> F[High Pressure Oil to Servomotor]
    F --> G[Servomotor Piston Moves]
    G --> H[Camshaft Shifts Axially]
    H --> I[Position Sensor Reads New Position]
    I --> J{Ahead Cams Engaged}
    I --> K{Astern Cams Engaged}
    J --> L[Locking Pin Engages]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Ready for Restart in Selected Direction]
```

**Critical safety feature:** The locking pin must engage **before** starting air is admitted. The control logic interlock prevents air admission if the camshaft is in mid-shift or in the wrong position.
