# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A carries 3 marks per question, answer in **80–100 words**. Direct, definition-based, or one-line numerical problems.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define auxiliary machinery as applied to a marine power plant. Why is it considered essential for the operation of a ship?**

**Model Answer:**
Auxiliary machinery refers to all supporting systems and equipment on board a ship that are not part of the main propulsion plant but are essential for the ship's safe and efficient operation. It includes diesel generators, boilers, pumps, compressors, purifiers, heat exchangers, steering gear, and deck machinery. It is essential because the main engine alone cannot provide electrical power, compressed air for starting, fuel heating, cooling water circulation, lubrication, fresh water, firefighting capability, or cargo handling. Without these supporting systems, the main engine cannot start, run continuously, or sustain a voyage. Hence, auxiliary machinery is the **operational backbone** of every merchant vessel. *(3 marks)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any six auxiliary machineries fitted on board a merchant ship and state one function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Auxiliary Machinery | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Auxiliary Diesel Generator | Generates electrical power for the ship |
| 2 | Main Air Compressor | Supplies compressed air for main engine starting |
| 3 | Auxiliary Boiler | Provides steam for fuel oil heating and domestic use |
| 4 | Bilge Pump | Removes accumulated water from the engine room and holds |
| 5 | Steering Gear | Controls the rudder for ship direction |
| 6 | Fuel Oil Purifier | Cleans heavy fuel oil to remove water and sediments |

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer **internal choice**. Each choice has sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. We provide two complete alternative choices.

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **requirement of auxiliary machinery** on board a ship. Discuss how the absence of these systems would affect the ship's operability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **compressed air starting system** for a marine diesel engine. List the components and explain their functions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Requirement of Auxiliary Machinery

The requirement of auxiliary machinery on board a ship arises because the **main propulsion engine** can only convert fuel into rotational mechanical energy and ultimately into thrust — it cannot, by itself, sustain any of the support functions essential for a voyage.

The key requirements are:

1. **Electrical Power Generation:** Every modern ship needs AC electrical power for navigation, communication, lighting, automation, and motor-driven auxiliaries. This is provided by **Auxiliary Diesel Generators** (typically 440 V, 60 Hz, three-phase).

2. **Compressed Air for Starting:** Large marine diesel engines are started by compressed air supplied from **main air compressors and high-pressure air receivers**. Without this, the engine cannot be started.

3. **Steam / Hot Water:** Heavy fuel oil (HFO) must be heated to **~120–150 °C** to flow and atomize properly. This is provided by **auxiliary boilers**, which also serve domestic heating.

4. **Cooling Systems:** Main engines, lube oil, and charge air must be cooled using **sea water pumps and heat exchangers** to prevent overheating.

5. **Pumping of Liquids:** Transfer of fuel, lube oil, ballast, bilge, fire water, and cargo is performed by **various centrifugal and positive-displacement pumps**.

6. **Steering and Mooring:** Ships must remain controllable and secured — provided by **steering gear, windlasses, and mooring winches**.

7. **Safety:** Fire pumps, CO₂ systems, emergency generator, and bilge pumping are mandated by **SOLAS**.

8. **Habitability:** Fresh water, HVAC, sewage treatment, and galley services sustain the crew for months at sea.

**Without these auxiliaries**, the ship would be unable to start, unable to maintain electrical supply, unable to cool its engines, unable to handle cargo, unable to steer, and unable to comply with safety regulations. In short, **a ship without auxiliary machinery is a stationary, dark, and dangerous hulk.**

#### Model Solution for (b) — Compressed Air Starting System

The compressed air starting system is a **closed-loop pneumatic plant** that delivers high-pressure air to the main engine's air-starting valves to crank the engine until combustion becomes self-sustaining.

**Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```
Atmospheric Air Intake → Main Air Compressor (2-stage) → Air Receiver (30 bar)
        ↓                                                    ↓
Safety Valve / Pressure Switch                         Starting Air Valve
                                                              ↓
                                                     Main Engine Cylinders
                                                              ↓
                                              (Engine begins to rotate)
```

**Components and Functions:**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Air Intake Filter** | Removes dust and moisture from atmospheric air |
| 2 | **Main Air Compressor (2-stage reciprocating)** | Compresses air from 1 bar to **30 bar** |
| 3 | **Inter-cooler & After-cooler** | Reduces air temperature between stages for efficiency |
| 4 | **Air Receiver** | Stores high-pressure air (capacity = **12 × engine swept volume**) |
| 5 | **Pressure Reducing Valve** | Reduces 30 bar to 8–10 bar for service air |
| 6 | **Starting Air Valve** | Admits air to each cylinder in firing order |
| 7 | **Safety Valve** | Releases excess pressure if receiver over-pressurizes |
| 8 | **Pressure Switch / Governor** | Auto-starts/stops compressor based on receiver pressure |
| 9 | **Drain Traps** | Removes condensate from receiver |
| 10 | **Control Air Line** | Provides 4–6 bar oil-free air for pneumatic automation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention inter-coolers and after-coolers** — these are critical for multi-stage compression.
> 2. **Wrong pressure value** — receiver pressure is **30 bar (not 10 bar)** for main starting; only service air is reduced to 8 bar.
> 3. **Missing SOLAS rule** — capacity of receiver must permit **12 consecutive starts without recharging**.
> 4. **Not labeling starting valves** — examiners specifically check for **"admission to each cylinder in firing order."**

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Classify the **auxiliary machinery** fitted on board a merchant ship and give at least **two examples** under each classification with their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working principle of an **Auxiliary Diesel Generator (DG) set**. Discuss its rating selection criteria and the role of the **Emergency Generator** as per SOLAS. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Classification of Auxiliary Machinery

Marine auxiliary machinery is classified into **six major categories** based on the function performed:

**1. Electrical Power Generation**
- *Auxiliary Diesel Generators:* Produce 3-phase AC power (440 V / 60 Hz) for all ship consumers.
- *Emergency Diesel Generator:* Provides power during blackout or main-gen failure.
- *Shaft Generator:* Driven by main engine for fuel-efficient cruising.

**2. Steam Generation**
- *Auxiliary Boiler (Oil-fired):* Produces steam at 5–10 bar for fuel heating and domestic use.
- *Exhaust Gas Boiler (EGE):* Uses main engine exhaust heat to generate steam.

**3. Compressed Air Systems**
- *Main Air Compressor:* Supplies 30 bar air for starting main engine.
- *Service Air Compressor:* Provides 8 bar air for tools and whistle.

**4. Pumping Systems**
- *Bilge Pump:* Removes water from machinery spaces.
- *Ballast Pump:* Fills/empties ballast tanks for stability.
- *Fire Pump:* Provides high-pressure water for firefighting.
- *Cargo Pump (Tankers):* Discharges liquid cargo.

**5. Heat Exchangers and Fresh Water**
- *LT Cooler / HT Cooler:* Cool jacket water before discharge to sea.
- *Fresh Water Generator:* Distills sea water using engine waste heat.

**6. Deck and Cargo Handling**
- *Windlass:* Lifts and drops anchor.
- *Mooring Winch:* Tightens mooring lines.
- *Steering Gear:* Turns the rudder.
- *Cargo Crane:* Loads/discharges deck cargo.

**7. Environmental and Safety**
- *Sewage Treatment Plant:* Treats waste water before discharge.
- *Incinerator:* Burns sludge and solid waste.
- *CO₂ Fixed System:* Floods engine room with CO₂ on fire.

**8. Fuel and Lubricating Oil Treatment**
- *Fuel Oil Purifier:* Removes water and sediments from HFO.
- *Lub Oil Purifier:* Cleans lube oil for reuse.

#### Model Solution for (b) — Auxiliary Diesel Generator (Working & SOLAS Role)

**Working Principle:**
A marine diesel generator converts **chemical energy of fuel (HFO/MDO) → mechanical energy (diesel engine) → electrical energy (alternator)**. The diesel engine drives a 3-phase synchronous alternator. The alternator's rotor carries a DC-excited field winding supplied through slip rings. As the rotor turns inside the stator winding (governed by a hydraulic or electronic governor at constant speed, typically **900 or 1800 RPM** for 60 Hz / 720 or 1500 RPM for 50 Hz), it induces a sinusoidal AC EMF.

**Key stages:**
1. **Air intake** through filters and turbocharger.
2. **Compression** of air in cylinders.
3. **Fuel injection** and combustion.
4. **Power stroke** rotates the crankshaft.
5. **Alternator** converts rotation to electrical power.
6. **AVR (Automatic Voltage Regulator)** maintains voltage at **±2.5%**.
7. **Governor** maintains frequency at **±5%** transient, **±1%** steady.

**Rating Selection Criteria:**

| Criterion | Typical Requirement |
|---|---|
| Total auxiliary load (sea-going) | ~15–25% of MCR |
| Number of sets | N+1 (e.g., 2+1) |
| Generator voltage | 440 V (or 380 V) |
| Frequency | 60 Hz (or 50 Hz) |
| Power factor | 0.8 lagging |
| Voltage regulation | ±2.5% |
| Frequency regulation | ±5% transient |
| Cooling | Air-cooled or water-cooled |
| Duty | Continuous (S1) |

**Role of Emergency Generator (SOLAS Ch. II-1, Reg. 43):**

As per SOLAS:
- Every passenger ship and cargo ship of **≥ 500 GT** must have an **Emergency Source of Power**.
- It must be capable of supplying **all safety-critical loads** (steering gear, fire pumps, navigation lights, communication, emergency lighting).
- It must start automatically within **45 seconds** of main power failure.
- Located **above the bulkhead deck**, in a separate space.
- Capable of running for **at least 18 hours** (passenger) or **6 hours** (cargo).
- Self-contained fuel, cooling, and starting systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. **Confusing Emergency Generator with Emergency Battery** — emergency DG is a *diesel-driven* set, distinct from UPS batteries.
> 2. **Wrong starting time** — it must auto-start in **45 seconds (NOT 10 seconds)**.
> 3. **Missing capacity rule** — emergency generator runs for **18 hours (passenger ships)** but only **6 hours (cargo ships)** — students often quote the wrong number.
> 4. **No mention of governor type** — list **electronic governor** for frequency stability; this is a key KTU differentiator.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Keep This List in Your Head Before Every KTU Exam**

- [ ] **Definition:** Auxiliary machinery = *all supporting plant except main propulsion engine*.
- [ ] **Six-to-Eight Functional Families:** Power, Steam, Air, Pumps, Heat Exchange, Deck/Cargo, Safety, Hotel.
- [ ] **Starting air pressure = 30 bar**; service air = **8 bar**; control air = **4–6 bar**.
- [ ] **Air receiver capacity = 12 × engine swept volume** (SOLAS).
- [ ] **Aux. electrical load ≈ 15–25% of MCR.**
- [ ] **Generator standard ratings:** 250, 500, 750, 1000, 1500 kW.
- [ ] **Voltage standard:** **440 V / 60 Hz** (or 380 V / 50 Hz); **PF = 0.8 lagging.**
- [ ] **Redundancy Rule = N+1** (e.g., 2 running + 1 standby).
- [ ] **Boiler pressure = 5–10 bar**, steam for fuel heating.
- [ ] **HFO heating temperature = 120–150 °C** (atomization quality).
- [ ] **Emergency generator auto-starts in ≤ 45 seconds.**
- [ ] **Emergency generator capacity = all SOLAS safety loads.**
- [ ] **Emergency generator runtime = 18 hrs (passenger) / 6 hrs (cargo).**
- [ ] **Fuel purifier type = self-cleaning disc-stack (Alfa Laval/Westfalia).**
- [ ] **Jacket cooling = HT cooler + LT cooler (two-stage).**
- [ ] **Sea water inlet ≈ 30 °C (tropical), outlet ≈ 38 °C after cooler.**
- [ ] **Steering gear = electro-hydraulic, 2 pumps (1 running + 1 standby).**
- [ ] **Fire pump pressure = 10–12 bar; capacity = 100–250 m³/h.**
- [ ] **Bilge pump discharge = overboard (with OWS) or to sludge tank.**
- [ ] **SOLAS = international convention**; classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS) verify compliance.
- [ ] **MARPOL = pollution prevention**; STP and incinerator are MARPOL-driven.
- [ ] **Auxiliary load dominates during port operations** (cargo pumps, cranes).
- [ ] **VLCC aux. load ≈ 3–4 MW in port; container ship ≈ 8–10 MW (reefer).**
- [ ] **Remember the equation:** $P_{genset} = \dfrac{D_f \cdot P_{continuous}}{\eta_g} \cdot 1.15$ (margin).
- [ ] **Specific Fuel Consumption (sfc) for aux. DG ≈ 0.200–0.230 kg/kWh.**
- [ ] **Voltage regulation:** ±2.5%; **Frequency regulation:** ±5% transient, ±1% steady.
- [ ] **Always draw block diagrams** with arrows showing flow direction in Part B answers.
- [ ] **Use SI units consistently:** kW (not HP), m³/h (not GPM), bar (not psi) unless specifically asked.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 — Auxiliary Machinery (Part 1).** Students preparing for the KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination (ESE) should be able to confidently classify, list functions, and explain the operational principle of at least **8 out of the 10** auxiliary systems covered above. Higher marks will be awarded to answers that **integrate numerical reasoning, SOLAS/class rules, and correct pressure/temperature/rating values** — rote definitions alone are insufficient for KTU 2024 evaluation standards.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differentiation Between Auxiliary Engines and Main Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differentiation Between Auxiliary Engines and Main Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):** The student must clearly articulate the engineering, operational, and constructional distinction between *Main Propulsion Engines* and *Auxiliary Engines* installed in a marine power plant.

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions

**Main Engine (Prime Mover / Propulsion Engine)**
The **Main Engine** of a ship is the largest prime mover installed in the engine room, whose primary function is to convert the chemical energy of fuel into mechanical rotational energy and deliver it to the **propeller shaft** (directly or through a reduction gearbox) to generate thrust for propelling the vessel through water. In merchant marine practice, the main engine is almost always a **large two-stroke, low-speed, crosshead, reversible marine diesel engine** (e.g., MAN B&W, Wartsila-Sulzer) for large ocean-going vessels, or a **medium-speed four-stroke engine** with a reduction gearbox for smaller or specialized vessels (naval auxiliaries, offshore support vessels, tugs).

**Auxiliary Engine (Auxiliary Diesel Generator / Auxiliary Prime Mover)**
An **Auxiliary Engine** is a smaller prime mover, typically a **four-stroke, medium-speed, trunk-piston diesel engine**, used on board a ship to drive **alternators** (synchronous AC generators) for producing electrical power, or to drive **stand-alone auxiliaries** such as emergency generators, harbor generators, and shaft generators. The electrical power generated is fed to the **main switchboard** and distributed throughout the ship for lighting, navigation, communication, pumps, fans, galley equipment, deck machinery, and emergency services.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Clarification:** In KTU terminology, "Auxiliary Engine" most commonly refers to the **Auxiliary Diesel Generator (ADG)** set, i.e., a diesel engine directly coupled to an alternator. The term *auxiliary machinery*, on the other hand, refers to all the pumps, compressors, purifiers, and heat exchangers *serviced by* the main engine and auxiliary engines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car" Model

Imagine a modern, fully electric hybrid car:

- The **big gasoline/diesel engine** that drives the wheels is your **Main Engine**. It is huge, heavy, optimized to deliver sustained mechanical power for thousands of hours, and is the reason the car moves forward.
- The **small battery-charging engine (the range-extender/ISG)** that generates electricity to run the headlights, air-conditioner, music system, power windows, and recharges the small battery is your **Auxiliary Engine**. It is smaller, must start/stop frequently, runs at variable loads, and is dedicated to producing *electrical* energy, not *mechanical thrust*.

Now extend that car analogy to a 300-meter container ship:
- The Main Engine is a colossal 14-cylinder, 2-stroke engine producing ~**70,000 kW (~94,000 BHP)**, turning a 9-meter bronze propeller.
- The 3 or 4 **Auxiliary Engines** are 4-stroke units of ~**2,000–4,000 kW** each, sitting in their own compartments, turning alternators that produce **450 V, 60 Hz, 3-phase AC** power for the entire ship.

> [!TIP]
> **A single sentence to remember:** *The Main Engine moves the ship; the Auxiliary Engines light up the ship.*

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Board-Standard Values)

The following typical operating ranges must be memorized for KTU viva and written examinations:

| Parameter | Main Engine (2-Stroke) | Auxiliary Engine (4-Stroke) |
|---|---|---|
| Rated Power per unit | **10,000 – 80,000 kW** | **500 – 5,000 kW** |
| Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP) | **16 – 20 bar** | **18 – 25 bar** |
| Rated Speed | **60 – 120 RPM** (slow speed) | **720 – 1000 RPM** (medium speed) |
| Specific Output | **~1000 kW/cyl** (XL bore engines) | **~200 – 500 kW/cyl** |
| Fuel used | **Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO)** — 380 cSt @ 50 °C | **Marine Diesel Oil (MDO) / Marine Gas Oil (MGO)** |
| Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (SFOC) | **~170 – 185 g/kWh** | **~190 – 210 g/kWh** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why the Differentiation Exists — The "Why" Behind the Design

The differentiation is **not arbitrary**; it is the outcome of decades of marine engineering optimization driven by the fundamental physics of ship propulsion:

1. **Propeller Law (Cube Law):** The power required to turn a propeller at constant pitch is proportional to the *cube* of the speed. A free-running vessel demands **constant, sustained, high torque** at low RPM. This favors a **slow-speed, long-stroke, 2-stroke engine** that can deliver massive torque directly to a large-diameter, low-RPM propeller without a reduction gearbox (improving efficiency by 3–5 %).

2. **Electrical Load Diversity:** A ship's hotel and operational electrical load is highly *variable* — bow thrusters cycle, galley equipment starts, deck cranes operate intermittently. This favors a **medium-speed, 4-stroke engine** that responds quickly to load changes, is light, and can be started/stopped frequently without thermal stress damage.

3. **Redundancy & Safety (SOLAS Convention):** International maritime safety regulations (SOLAS Chapter II-1) mandate that a ship must be able to generate essential electrical services even with one generator out of service. Hence **3 or 4 auxiliary engines** are installed in parallel — a *prime mover* philosophy that does not apply to the main engine (one shaft, one main engine, except for twin-screw vessels).

4. **Fuel Flexibility & Cost Economics:** Main engines burn cheap **HFO** because they run continuously for 15–30 days on ocean voyages, amortizing the high cost of lube-oil and maintenance. Auxiliary engines burn cleaner **MDO/MGO** because they must start reliably in port, in cold conditions, and meet **MARPOL Annex VI** emission limits in Emission Control Areas (ECAs).

### 2.2 Operational Cycle Differences

**Main Engine Operational Philosophy:**
- Continuous running for **15 – 30 days** between port calls.
- Operating point (RPM, load) changes only during **manoeuvring** (full ahead → stop → full astern in 15–20 seconds).
- Speed is *relatively constant* at "service RPM" once the voyage speed is set.
- **Single-screw vessel** = single main engine, single point of failure (hence twin-screw designs in tankers, naval vessels).

**Auxiliary Engine Operational Philosophy:**
- Operates in **Stand-by ↔ Running** cycles.
- 1 unit is "on the bus" (synchronized to switchboard), 1 is "stand-by" hot, others cold.
- Load fluctuates rapidly with thruster, crane, or compressor starts.
- Frequency and voltage must be tightly regulated by an **electronic governor + AVR** (Automatic Voltage Regulator) to maintain **±5 % frequency** and **±2.5 % voltage** tolerance for shipboard electronics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** *The Main Engine is a "constant speed, constant load" machine; the Auxiliary Engine is a "variable speed, variable load" machine.* This single statement differentiates ~40 % of the KTU questions on this topic.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are mandatory for KTU Module 2 numerical problems. Memorize them along with the unit conventions.

**Indicated Power (IP) per cylinder:**

$$IP = \frac{P_{m} \times L \times A \times n \times K}{60 \times 10^{3}} \;\; \text{(kW)}$$

where $K = 1$ for two-stroke engines and $K = 0.5$ for four-stroke engines.

**Brake Power (BP) / Brake Horse Power (BHP):**

$$BP = IP - Friction\;Power\;(FP)$$

**Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (SFOC):**

$$SFOC = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel} \times 10^{6}}{BP} \;\; \text{(g/kWh)}$$

**Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP):**

$$BMEP = \frac{BP \times 60 \times 10^{3}}{L \times A \times n \times K} \;\; \text{(kPa)}$$

**Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th,b} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \times CV} \times 3600 \times 100 \;\; \text{(\%)}$$

**Heat Balance Equation:**

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{Q}_{useful\;(BP)} + \dot{Q}_{cooling} + \dot{Q}_{exhaust} + \dot{Q}_{losses}$$

**Propeller Thrust Power (to relate Main Engine to ship motion):**

$$P_{thrust} = T \times V_{ship} \;\; \text{(kW)}$$

where $T$ is the thrust in **kN** and $V_{ship}$ is the ship speed in **m/s**.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of This Differentiation

This system of **"one main + many auxiliaries"** is the **universal architecture** of every commercial ship in the world — from a 100-meter coaster to a 400-meter ULCC (Ultra Large Crude Carrier). The advantages are:

- **Operational flexibility:** In port, main engine is stopped; ship runs entirely on auxiliary engines.
- **Maintenance segregation:** Main engine overhaul (every 15,000–30,000 hours) is a long, planned drydock event; auxiliary engine maintenance is in-situ, quick, and modular.
- **Safety redundancy:** Loss of main engine = ship drifts (deployable, recoverable); loss of all auxiliary engines = blackout = SOLAS emergency (immediate activation of Emergency Generator).
- **Emission compliance:** Auxiliary engines can be switched off and replaced by **shore power (cold ironing)** in port to meet ECA regulations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Example:** The Emma Mærsk (Triple-E class) container ship has **1 × MAN B&W 14K98ME-C** main engine (~80 MW) and **5 × Wartsila 8L46F** auxiliary engines (4 × 3,250 kW running + 1 stand-by) — a perfect KTU case study of the main–auxiliary architecture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Indicated Power Formula from First Principles

The Indicated Power is the work done by the gas pressure on the piston per unit time.

**Step 1 — Work done per power stroke per cylinder:**

$$W_{stroke} = P_{m} \times A \times L \;\; \text{(Joules)}$$

where $P_m$ is the mean effective pressure (Pa), $A$ is the piston area (m²), and $L$ is the stroke length (m).

**Step 2 — Convert to work per second (Power per cylinder):**

For a 2-stroke engine, every revolution produces one power stroke, so work per second is:

$$\dot{W} = P_{m} \times A \times L \times \frac{n}{60} \;\; \text{(W)}$$

For a 4-stroke engine, only every alternate revolution is a power stroke:

$$\dot{W} = P_{m} \times A \times L \times \frac{n}{2 \times 60} \;\; \text{(W)}$$

**Step 3 — Generalize using the constant $K$:**

$$\dot{W} = \frac{P_{m} \times A \times L \times n \times K}{60} \;\; \text{(W)}$$

**Step 4 — Sum over all $N$ cylinders and convert to kW:**

$$IP_{total} = \frac{P_{m} \times L \times A \times n \times K \times N}{60 \times 10^{3}} \;\; \text{(kW)}$$

> **Logic of the conversion:** We divide by 60 to convert revolutions per minute to revolutions per second, multiply by $N$ for all cylinders, and divide by 1000 to express the result in **kW** (a KTU-required SI unit).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style Problem

> **Problem (KTU 2019 Pattern — Adapted):** A four-stroke, 6-cylinder marine auxiliary engine has a bore of **300 mm**, stroke of **400 mm**, runs at **750 RPM**, develops a BMEP of **18 bar**, and consumes **180 kg/h** of diesel oil. Calculate:
> (a) Brake Power output
> (b) Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (SFOC)
> (c) Brake Thermal Efficiency if the calorific value of fuel is **42,500 kJ/kg**

**Given Data:**

$$B = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}, \quad L = 400 \text{ mm} = 0.4 \text{ m}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times B^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.3^{2} = 0.0707 \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$n = 750 \text{ RPM}, \quad N = 6, \quad K = 0.5 \text{ (4-stroke)}, \quad BMEP = 18 \text{ bar} = 1800 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = 180 \text{ kg/h} = \frac{180}{3600} = 0.05 \text{ kg/s}, \quad CV = 42500 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Solution (a) — Brake Power:**

$$BP = \frac{BMEP \times L \times A \times n \times K \times N}{60 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$BP = \frac{1800 \times 0.4 \times 0.0707 \times 750 \times 0.5 \times 6}{60 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$BP = \frac{1800 \times 0.4 \times 0.0707 \times 750 \times 3}{60 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$BP = \frac{1800 \times 0.4 \times 0.0707 \times 2250}{60 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$BP = \frac{114{,}561}{60 \times 10^{3}} = 1.909 \text{ kW}$$

> **Wait — the value is absurdly low. The student has made a unit error.**

**Corrected unit handling** — BMEP is in **kPa**, and the formula yields **Watts** when we use SI base units:

$$BP_{Watts} = \frac{1800 \text{ kPa} \times 0.4 \text{ m} \times 0.0707 \text{ m}^{2} \times 750 \times 0.5 \times 6}{60}$$

$$BP = \frac{1800 \times 0.4 \times 0.0707 \times 2250}{60}$$

$$BP = 1909.4 \text{ kW}$$

> **Logic of the corrected step:** When BMEP is in kPa, the formula outputs power directly in kW (not in Watts), because $1 \text{ kPa} \cdot \text{m}^{3} = 1 \text{ kJ}$, and the divisor 60 gives per-second work. The $10^{3}$ in the original formula is for when BMEP is in **Pa** (so result is in W, and we divide by 1000 to get kW).

**Solution (b) — SFOC:**

$$SFOC = \frac{0.05 \text{ kg/s} \times 10^{6}}{1909.4 \text{ kW}} = 26.18 \text{ g/kWh (incorrect)}$$

> **Recheck:** $\dot{m}_{fuel}$ must be in **kg/h** for g/kWh, not kg/s.

$$SFOC = \frac{180 \text{ kg/h} \times 1000 \text{ g/kg}}{1909.4 \text{ kW}} = \frac{180{,}000}{1909.4} = 94.27 \text{ g/kWh (still low)}$$

> **Note to the student:** Realistic SFOC for a 4-stroke auxiliary engine is **190–210 g/kWh**. The chosen problem data gives an unrealistically low SFOC. A realistic problem would specify a **fuel consumption of ~400 kg/h** for this engine. With $\dot{m}_{fuel} = 400 \text{ kg/h}$:

$$SFOC_{realistic} = \frac{400{,}000}{1909.4} = 209.5 \text{ g/kWh} \quad \checkmark$$

**Solution (c) — Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th,b} = \frac{1909.4 \text{ kW} \times 3600}{0.111 \text{ kg/s} \times 42500 \text{ kJ/kg}} \times 100$$

where $\dot{m}_{fuel} = 400/3600 = 0.111$ kg/s.

$$\eta_{th,b} = \frac{6{,}873{,}840}{4722.2} \times 100 \approx 145\% \quad \text{(ERROR — Efficiency > 100 % is impossible)}$$

> **Resolution:** The problem data is internally inconsistent (cannot have $BP = 1909.4$ kW with 400 kg/h fuel at 42,500 kJ/kg). For teaching purposes, the expected answer is:

$$\eta_{th,b} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \times CV} = \frac{1909.4 \text{ kJ/s}}{0.111 \text{ kg/s} \times 42500 \text{ kJ/kg}} = \frac{1909.4}{4722.2} = 40.4 \text{ \%}$$

> **This is the standard KTU-board method.** A realistic 4-stroke auxiliary engine has a brake thermal efficiency of **~40–45 %**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students frequently confuse the units of BMEP (bar vs. kPa vs. Pa) and $\dot{m}_{fuel}$ (kg/s vs. kg/h). A single dimensional mismatch costs **3 marks** out of 7 in a Part B sub-question. Always write the units in your equation first.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Constructional & Operational

The following matrix is the **single most important visual aid** for the KTU board examination. It is reproduced here in a layout that students can redraw in the answer script for full marks.

| Comparison Parameter | Main Engine | Auxiliary Engine |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Function** | Propels the ship via shaft line & propeller | Generates electrical power for ship services |
| **Cycle Type** | 2-stroke crosshead (preferred) or 4-stroke medium speed | 4-stroke trunk-piston |
| **Speed** | Slow: 60 – 120 RPM (2-stroke) or 450 – 600 RPM (4-stroke) | Medium: 720 – 1000 RPM |
| **Cylinder Size** | Very large: bore 300 – 960 mm | Smaller: bore 200 – 500 mm |
| **Cylinder Liner Lubrication** | Separate (splash from crosshead, no contamination of lube oil) | Common with crankcase (oil bath / splash from connecting rod) |
| **Starting Method** | Compressed air @ 30 bar; air distributors & starting valves | Compressed air OR electric starter motor |
| **Reversibility** | Yes (essential for manoeuvring) | Not required (unidirectional) |
| **Number per Ship** | 1 (single-screw) or 2 (twin-screw) | 3 – 5 units (parallel operation) |
| **Fuel Used** | Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO) — 380 cSt residual | Marine Diesel Oil (MDO) / Marine Gas Oil (MGO) — distillate |
| **Fuel Injection Pressure** | Very high: 1000 – 1500 bar (common rail in modern units) | High: 600 – 1000 bar |
| **Turbocharging** | Single-stage or two-stage TC + scavenging blower | Single-stage TC, often with constant-pressure system |
| **Cooling System** | Cylinder jacket + piston + exhaust valve cooling | Cylinder jacket + lube oil cooler |
| **Lubricating Oil Type** | System oil (crankcase) + Cylinder oil (separate SAE 50) | Single lubricating oil for crankcase + cylinder |
| **Governor Type** | Hydraulic / electronic with propeller curve | Electronic / digital with frequency + load control |
| **Operational Hours/Year** | 6,000 – 7,000 hours (continuous) | 4,000 – 5,000 hours (intermittent, with stops) |
| **Overhaul Interval** | 15,000 – 30,000 hours (major) | 12,000 – 24,000 hours (major) |
| **SFOC (typical)** | 168 – 185 g/kWh | 190 – 210 g/kWh |
| **Exhaust Gas Temperature** | 250 – 350 °C (with TC) | 350 – 450 °C (with TC) |
| **Manoeuvring** | Full ahead ↔ full astern cycle required | No manoeuvring; load is varied |
| **Emergency Requirement** | Loss = vessel drift, but SOLAS allows towing | Loss = blackout; Emergency Generator (≤ 45 s start) takes over |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Logic of Why SFOC Differs

The reason the main engine has *lower* SFOC than the auxiliary engine is a multi-step thermodynamic argument:

**Step 1 — Larger cylinders = lower surface-to-volume ratio:**
A 2-stroke main engine with a 900 mm bore has a surface-to-volume ratio of $\approx 0.005 \text{ m}^{-1}$, while a 4-stroke auxiliary engine with a 300 mm bore has a ratio of $\approx 0.015 \text{ m}^{-1}$. Lower ratio = less heat lost to walls.

**Step 2 — Longer expansion ratio:**
Main engines operate with a higher **expansion ratio** (cut-off at ~8–10 % of stroke) allowing more complete conversion of heat to work.

**Step 3 — Optimized combustion chamber geometry:**
Large, slow engines can use deep $\omega$-shaped pistons and optimized spray patterns for **near-stoichiometric, complete combustion**.

**Step 4 — Resulting efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th,b,main} \approx 0.45 - 0.50 \quad (45 - 50\%)$$

$$\eta_{th,b,aux} \approx 0.38 - 0.43 \quad (38 - 43\%)$$

**Step 5 — SFOC relationship (inverse of efficiency):**

$$SFOC = \frac{3600 \times 10^{6}}{\eta_{th,b} \times CV} \;\; \text{(g/kWh)}$$

For $CV = 42{,}500$ kJ/kg and $\eta = 0.47$:

$$SFOC = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{9}}{0.47 \times 42500 \times 10^{3}} = 180.2 \text{ g/kWh} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Take-away:** Main engines are thermodynamically more efficient *per kWh of mechanical work* because they are designed for *sustained, high-load, continuous operation* on cheap HFO. Auxiliary engines are designed for *flexibility, rapid load acceptance, and clean combustion* on distillate fuels — at the cost of higher SFOC.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Ship Power Plant Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a typical merchant ship's main–auxiliary engine system. This is the standard KTU-board-expected sketch for the question *"With a block diagram, explain the marine main–auxiliary power plant."*

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ShipPowerPlant["SHIP POWER PLANT ARCHITECTURE"]
        subgraph FuelSupply["FUEL SUPPLY SYSTEM"]
            HFO["HFO TANKS AND PURIFIERS"]
            MDO["MDO MGO SERVICE TANKS"]
        end

        subgraph MainEngineSys["MAIN ENGINE SYSTEM"]
            ME["MAIN ENGINE 2 STROKE LOW SPEED 10000 TO 80000 kW"]
            TC1["TURBOCHARGER"]
            AirStart["COMPRESSED AIR START 30 BAR"]
            MEshaft["PROPELLER SHAFT"]
            Prop["PROPELLER"]
        end

        subgraph AuxEngineSys["AUXILIARY ENGINE SYSTEM 3 TO 4 UNITS"]
            AE1["AUX ENGINE 1 4 STROKE 2000 kW"]
            AE2["AUX ENGINE 2 4 STROKE 2000 kW"]
            AE3["AUX ENGINE 3 STANDBY"]
            ALT1["ALTERNATOR 1 AC 450V 60HZ"]
            ALT2["ALTERNATOR 2 AC 450V 60HZ"]
            ALT3["ALTERNATOR 3 AC 450V 60HZ"]
        end

        subgraph ElectricalNet["ELECTRICAL DISTRIBUTION"]
            MSB["MAIN SWITCHBOARD"]
            EmergGen["EMERGENCY GENERATOR DIESEL SEPARATE"]
            ShipLoad["SHIP ELECTRICAL LOADS"]
        end
    end

    HFO --> ME
    MDO --> AE1
    MDO --> AE2
    MDO --> AE3
    AirStart --> ME
    ME --> TC1
    ME --> MEshaft
    MEshaft --> Prop

    AE1 --> ALT1
    AE2 --> ALT2
    AE3 --> ALT3
    ALT1 --> MSB
    ALT2 --> MSB
    ALT3 --> MSB
    EmergGen --> MSB
    MSB --> ShipLoad

    ShipLoad -.thruster cranes pumps lighting.-> ShipLoad
```

### 4.2 Comparative Decision Flowchart — Main vs. Auxiliary

This flowchart guides a marine engineer (or a KTU student) through the decision logic for selecting the engine type based on operational requirement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["REQUIREMENT IDENTIFIED"] --> Q1{"Need propulsion or power?"}
    Q1 --PROPULSION--> Q2{"Vessel size and route?"}
    Q1 --POWER--> Aux["AUXILIARY ENGINE 4 STROKE MEDIUM SPEED"]

    Q2 --LARGE OCEAN GOING--> Main2S["MAIN ENGINE 2 STROKE SLOW SPEED CROSSHEAD"]
    Q2 --SMALL OR SPECIALIZED--> Main4S["MAIN ENGINE 4 STROKE MEDIUM SPEED WITH GEARBOX"]

    Main2S --> UseHeavy["HFO FUEL LONG VOYAGES"]
    Main4S --> UseDiesel["MDO OR MGO FUEL"]
    Aux --> UseDiesel

    UseHeavy --> Output1["CONSTANT RPM CONTINUOUS OPERATION"]
    UseDiesel --> Output2["VARIABLE RPM INTERMITTENT OPERATION"]

    Output1 --> Final1["ONE UNIT PER SHAFT LARGE CYLINDER"]
    Output2 --> Final2["MULTIPLE UNITS IN PARALLEL SMALL CYLINDER"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Cylinder Lubrication Comparison (Crosshead vs. Trunk-Piston)

This block schematic shows *why* the crosshead main engine has **separate cylinder lubrication**, while the trunk-piston auxiliary engine uses a **common sump**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Crosshead2S["MAIN ENGINE CROSSHEAD 2 STROKE"]
        CYLA["CYLINDER LINER"] --separate oil--> CYL_OIL["CYLINDER OIL SAIL SAIL 50 QUILL FEED"]
        CRANKA["CRANKCASE"] --separate oil--> SYS_OIL["SYSTEM OIL TURBINE 300"]
        CROSS["CROSSHEAD GUIDE"] --> CRANKA
        CYLA -.power stroke.-> CROSS
    end

    subgraph Trunk4S["AUXILIARY ENGINE TRUNK PISTON 4 STROKE"]
        CYLB["CYLINDER LINER"] --same oil--> COMMON["COMMON SUMP TURBINE OIL T30"]
        CRANKB["CRANKCASE / SUMPBREATHERS"]
        PISTON["PISTON WITH RINGS"] --> CRANKB
        CYLB -.power stroke.-> PISTON
    end
```

> **Engineering Insight:** The crosshead design physically separates the cylinder space from the crankcase, allowing **heavy, alkali (BN ~70–100) cylinder oil** to be used without contaminating the **system oil**. The trunk-piston auxiliary engine cannot do this — its lube oil must work for both bearings and cylinder walls, so it uses a single **TBN ~10–15** oil.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*"State the principal function of an auxiliary engine on board a ship. Why are multiple auxiliary engines installed in parallel on the same switchboard?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An auxiliary engine on board a ship is a **medium-speed, four-stroke, trunk-piston diesel engine** directly coupled to an **alternator** for the purpose of generating **3-phase, 450 V, 60 Hz electrical power** for all shipboard consumers including navigation, communication, lighting, pumps, deck machinery, and galley equipment. Multiple auxiliary engines (typically 3 or 4) are installed in parallel on the same **main switchboard** to provide:
- **Operational redundancy** in case one unit trips (SOLAS requirement — loss of all generators constitutes a "blackout" emergency).
- **Load sharing and fuel economy** — at low hotel load, only one engine runs at high efficiency; additional units start as load increases.
- **Maintenance flexibility** — one unit can be overhauled while the others supply the ship.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*"Why are two-stroke crosshead diesel engines universally preferred as main propulsion engines in large ocean-going vessels?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Two-stroke crosshead engines are preferred as main engines in large vessels because of four engineering reasons:
- **Direct drive to propeller:** Slow speed (60 – 120 RPM) eliminates the need for a reduction gearbox, improving transmission efficiency by ~5 %.
- **High propulsive efficiency:** A slow, large-diameter propeller is hydrodynamically more efficient than a fast, small one, following the **propeller cubic law**.
- **Heavy fuel oil (HFO) capability:** Long stroke, low BMEP operation allows burning cheap residual fuel with separate cylinder lubrication (alkaline cylinder oil) preventing acid corrosion.
- **High reliability and long TBO:** Continuous low-stress operation gives overhaul intervals of 25,000 – 30,000 hours, ideal for long ocean voyages.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination, Module 2 is typically covered by **one full 14-mark question with internal choice**. You must attempt *either* Question A *or* Question B. Each question has two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU Dec 2023 Pattern**

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]:** *"Compare the constructional and operational features of a marine main engine and a marine auxiliary engine with the help of a comparison table. Mention at least 8 parameters."*

**Model Answer:**

The following tabular comparison must be produced in the answer script:

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Main Engine (2-Stroke Crosshead) | Auxiliary Engine (4-Stroke Trunk-Piston) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Primary Function | Propels the vessel via shaft and propeller | Generates electrical power via alternator |
| 2 | Speed Range | Slow: 60 – 120 RPM | Medium: 720 – 1000 RPM |
| 3 | Power Output | 10,000 – 80,000 kW per unit | 500 – 5,000 kW per unit |
| 4 | Cylinder Bore | 300 – 960 mm (very large) | 200 – 500 mm (moderate) |
| 5 | Stroke / Bore Ratio | > 3 (long stroke) | ~ 1.0 – 1.2 (square or short stroke) |
| 6 | Cylinder Lubrication | Separate (splash from crosshead) | Common crankcase oil |
| 7 | Reversibility | Yes (manoeuvring) | Not required |
| 8 | Number per Ship | 1 (single-screw) or 2 (twin-screw) | 3 – 4 (parallel on switchboard) |
| 9 | Fuel | HFO (Heavy Fuel Oil) | MDO / MGO (Marine Diesel / Gas Oil) |
| 10 | Starting Method | Compressed air @ 30 bar | Compressed air or electric starter |
| 11 | SFOC | 168 – 185 g/kWh | 190 – 210 g/kWh |
| 12 | Continuous Operating Hours | 6,000 – 7,000 h/year | 4,000 – 5,000 h/year (intermittent) |
| 13 | Exhaust Temperature | 250 – 350 °C | 350 – 450 °C |
| 14 | Overhaul Interval (TBO) | 25,000 – 30,000 h | 12,000 – 24,000 h |
| 15 | Governor | Hydraulic / electronic with propeller curve | Digital electronic with frequency + load sharing |

*(Any 8 of the above rows are sufficient for full marks. Drawing the table neatly earns 1 mark; correct content earns 6 marks.)*

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]:** *"Explain the starting air system and manoeuvring sequence of a marine main engine. Why is compressed air used as the starting medium?"*

**Model Answer:**

**Starting Air System (3 marks):**
The main engine is started by **compressed air at 30 bar** admitted into the cylinders through pneumatically or hydraulically operated **starting air valves** mounted on the cylinder head. The compressed air is supplied by **main air compressors** (electrically driven, typically 2 or 3 units) which charge **starting air receivers** (capacity sized to give **at least 12 consecutive starts** without recharging, per SOLAS). The air admission is controlled by a **starting air distributor** driven by the camshaft, which sequences the starting valves to deliver air to the correct cylinder at the correct TDC. A **flame arrestor** and **safety relief valve** are fitted on each receiver for safety.

**Manoeuvring Sequence (3 marks):**
The manoeuvring sequence is the procedure by which the engine's rotation is reversed from **ahead to astern** (or vice-versa) to control the ship during berthing/unberthing. The sequence is:
1. **Stop** — fuel is cut off, engine runs on compressed air as an air motor.
2. **Reversing** — the camshaft is hydraulically shifted from "Ahead" to "Astern" position, changing the timing of fuel injection and exhaust valve actuation.
3. **Brake** — compressed air is admitted to bring the propeller to rest (counter-torque braking).
4. **Starting in reverse** — fuel is re-admitted in the new cam position; engine starts on astern rotation.
5. The full ahead → stop → full astern cycle must be completed in **≤ 15 seconds** for SOLAS-class vessels.

**Why Compressed Air (1 mark):**
Compressed air is used because:
- It is the **only practical stored-energy medium** for an instant, high-torque, reversible start of a multi-megawatt engine.
- It is intrinsically **safe** (no spark, no flame) and is already available on board for service air.
- Air can be **metered precisely** to the cylinder for slow turning (warming up) or full torque start.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — KTU May 2024 Pattern (Alternative Choice)**

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]:** *"Discuss the fuel oil system differences between a marine main engine and a marine auxiliary engine. Explain the role of fuel viscosity control in main engines burning HFO."*

**Model Answer:**

**Fuel System Differences (4 marks):**
The main engine burning HFO requires a **complex fuel treatment system** because residual fuel is highly viscous (up to 380 cSt at 50 °C), contains water, sediments, and cat-fines. The system includes:
- **HFO settling tanks** (with steam heating coils to keep fuel at 50 – 60 °C).
- **HFO purifiers** (centrifugal separators — 2 in series for optimum separation of water and solids).
- **HFO service tank** (final heated tank before the engine).
- **Fuel viscosity controllers** (automated steam-heated or electric-heated fuel lines, with a **Visco-Fueller** maintaining fuel at **~12 – 18 cSt** before injection).
- **Mixing tank** for transition between HFO and MDO when manoeuvring (per ISO 8217 fuel standard).

The auxiliary engine uses a **simpler system** because MDO/MGO is a clean distillate with viscosity 2 – 6 cSt at 40 °C. It only needs:
- A **service tank** with day-tank capacity (~8 hours runtime).
- **Duplex filters** for particulate removal.
- **No purifier** required (distillate is clean).
- **No viscosity control** needed (no heating required at full flow).
- **No fuel change-over** procedure at manoeuvring.

**Role of Viscosity Control (3 marks):**
If HFO is injected at high viscosity (> 25 cSt), the fuel does not atomize properly — leading to **poor combustion, unburnt fuel, smoke, fouling of injectors, and cylinder liner deposits**. The viscosity controller:
- Heats the fuel to maintain injection viscosity at **10 – 14 cSt** (per engine maker's spec, e.g., Wartsila).
- Uses a **bypass loop** with a steam heater; the outlet temperature is modulated by a **viscometer sensor**.
- Ensures **complete atomization**, low SFOC, and minimum exhaust smoke (Bacharach scale < 2).

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]:** *"Explain the role and arrangement of turbocharging in a marine main engine. How does the turbocharging arrangement differ in auxiliary engines?"*

**Model Answer:**

**Turbocharging in Main Engines (4 marks):**
Modern 2-stroke main engines use **one or two-stage turbocharging** to supply high-pressure scavenging air to the cylinders. The arrangement typically includes:
- **Exhaust Gas Turbocharger (TC):** Driven by exhaust gas energy; supplies low-pressure scavenging air.
- **Auxiliary Blower (Motor-driven):** Used during low load and starting, when exhaust energy is insufficient.
- **Sequential or parallel TC arrangements** (e.g., MAN B&W ME-C uses a single large TC + electrically driven blower; Wartsila X62 uses constant-pressure TC with electrically driven scavenge air cooler fan).
- **TC cut-out** is provided for emergency operation with the blower alone.

The main engine turbocharger handles **massive mass flow rates** (10 – 80 kg/s of air for a 50,000 kW engine) and operates at very high turbo speeds (10,000 – 25,000 RPM).

**Turbocharging in Auxiliary Engines (3 marks):**
Auxiliary engines use a **single-stage, constant-pressure or pulse turbocharger** of moderate size. The arrangements are:
- A single **axial or radial TC** coupled to the engine.
- **No auxiliary blower** required — the engine always has exhaust energy even at low load due to its higher BMEP.
- The TC is **integrated with the engine** as a compact package, often mounted directly on the exhaust manifold.
- TC speed is lower (15,000 – 20,000 RPM) because the engine speed is higher (medium-speed) and air demand is lower.

**Key Functional Difference:**
- Main engine: TC is **assist-only** at high load; **auxiliary blower** is essential for low-load scavenge.
- Auxiliary engine: TC is **always adequate** for the air demand; no separate blower is required.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Main Engine" with "Main Switchboard":** The Main Engine is a *mechanical prime mover*; the Main Switchboard is an *electrical panel*. They are *not* the same.
> 2. **Mixing up "2-stroke" with "4-stroke":** A 4-stroke auxiliary engine has **half the power strokes** per revolution of a 2-stroke main engine of the same size. This is the most common conceptual error in the KTU viva.
> 3. **Forgetting cylinder lubrication differences:** Crosshead main engines use **separate cylinder oil (BN 70–100)**; trunk-piston auxiliaries use **common system oil (BN 10–15)**. Confusing these loses 2 marks in any Part B question.
> 4. **Unit error in SFOC calculations:** $\dot{m}_{fuel}$ must be in **kg/h**, not kg/s, when computing g/kWh. Always write the unit next to the number.
> 5. **Stating "auxiliary engine drives the propeller":** This is **incorrect**. Auxiliary engines drive alternators. Only main engines drive propellers.
> 6. **Reversibility:** Main engines are reversible; auxiliary engines are not. Do not write that auxiliary engines "go astern" — they are electrically reversed by the alternator acting as a motor in a "shaft generator" mode (a separate advanced concept, not on KTU Module 2 syllabus).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- **Main Engine** = *Prime Mover* = Propels the ship = 2-stroke crosshead (large vessels) or 4-stroke medium-speed with gearbox (small vessels).
- **Auxiliary Engine** = *Diesel Generator* = Lights up the ship = 4-stroke medium-speed trunk-piston.
- Main engines are **slow, large, continuous, reversible, use HFO**.
- Auxiliary engines are **medium-speed, smaller, intermittent, non-reversible, use MDO/MGO**.
- **Number per ship:** Main = 1 or 2; Auxiliary = 3 or 4.
- **Cylinder lubrication:** Separate in main; common in auxiliary.
- **SFOC:** Main ~ 175 g/kWh; Auxiliary ~ 200 g/kWh.
- **BMEP:** Main ~ 18 bar; Auxiliary ~ 20 bar.
- **Speed:** Main ~ 100 RPM; Auxiliary ~ 750 RPM.
- **Fuel system:** Main needs purifier + viscosity controller; Auxiliary needs only filter.
- **Starting:** Both use compressed air at 30 bar; auxiliary can also use electric starter.
- **Turbocharging:** Main = 1 or 2 stage TC + aux. blower; Auxiliary = single stage TC, no blower.
- **SFOC formula (always memorize):**

$$SFOC = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel,h} \times 1000}{BP_{kW}} \;\; \text{g/kWh}$$

- **Power formula (always memorize):**

$$BP = \frac{BMEP \times L \times A \times n \times K \times N}{60} \;\; \text{kW (if BMEP in kPa)}$$

- **SOLAS requirement:** At least **2** auxiliary generators with one in reserve; **1** emergency generator for blackout.
- **MARPOL Annex VI:** Auxiliary engines must meet **Tier II / Tier III** NOx limits in ECAs (NOx Technical Code 2008).
- **MARPOL Annex V:** No oil discharge from any engine overboard — bilge and sludge must go to sludge tank.
- **Manoeuvring main engine:** Full ahead → Stop → Full astern must be possible in **≤ 15 s** (SOLAS Reg. II-1/31).
- **Blackout drill:** Crew must demonstrate emergency generator start in **≤ 45 s** (SOLAS Reg. II-1/43).
- **Word to remember the difference in 5 seconds:** *"Main = Mechanical, Auxiliary = Electrical."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Working Principle and Onboard Applications of Different Types of Marine Air Compressors, Marine Pumps, Marine Boilers, Purifiers, Marine Valves, Heat Exchangers, Oil Water Separators, Incinerators, Sewage Treatment Plant, Fresh Water Generator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Marine Air Compressors

### Formal Definition
A **Marine Air Compressor** is a positive-displacement or dynamic machine that compresses atmospheric air to elevated pressures (typically **30 bar** for starting air and **7–10 bar** for control/service air) for use in main engine starting, pneumatic control systems, and auxiliary services aboard ships.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Starting air must be oil-free and dry to prevent auto-ignition in the main engine cylinder during the compression stroke.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of an air compressor as the **"lungs" of a ship**. Just as your lungs take a large gulp of air and push it out at high pressure through a small opening, the compressor draws in huge volumes of ambient air and squeezes it into small, high-pressure tanks. When the engine needs a powerful burst of air to spin up, the compressor has it ready in storage bottles.

---

## 1.2 Marine Pumps

### Formal Definition
A **Marine Pump** is a mechanical device used to move fluids (water, fuel oil, lube oil, seawater, bilge) through shipboard piping systems by imparting energy to the fluid via kinetic energy (centrifugal) or displacement (positive).

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Memory Hook:** *Centrifugal = fast spinning (kinetic); Positive = pushing/plunging (displacement)*.

### Intuitive Analogy
Picture a **fountain water pump** in a garden. Water gets sucked in from a low pond and shot out at the top — that's centrifugal action (kinetic). Now imagine a **doctor's syringe** — pulling the plunger back draws liquid in, pushing it forward ejects liquid. That's a positive displacement pump. Ships use both, depending on whether they need **high flow at low pressure** or **high pressure at low flow**.

---

## 1.3 Marine Boilers

### Formal Definition
A **Marine Boiler (Auxiliary Steam Boiler / Exhaust Gas Economizer)** is a closed pressure vessel that converts feed water into steam by transferring heat from combustion of fuel oil (auxiliary boiler) or waste heat from main engine exhaust gas (economizer).

### Key Types
- **Fire-tube boiler** (e.g., Cochran, Scotch Marine) — high-pressure hot gases flow through tubes immersed in water.
- **Water-tube boiler** (e.g., Aalborg, D-type) — water circulates through tubes exposed to hot gases.
- **Exhaust Gas Economizer (EGE)** — no combustion; recovers waste heat.

### Intuitive Analogy
A boiler is essentially the **"kettle of the ship"**, but on steroids. Just as a kitchen kettle boils water on a stove, a marine boiler boils water — but at **8–10 bar** pressure and produces hundreds of kilograms of steam per hour. The waste-heat economizer is like a **rooftop solar heater** — it captures otherwise wasted heat from engine exhaust.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Many students confuse *fire-tube* with *water-tube*. Memory trick: **"fire in tubes" = fire-tube; "water in tubes" = water-tube.**

---

## 1.4 Purifiers (Fuel Oil & Lube Oil Purifiers)

### Formal Definition
A **Marine Purifier** (Disc-type Centrifuge) is a high-speed rotating separator (3,000–9,000 rpm) that uses centrifugal force to separate two immiscible liquids (oil & water) or a liquid from solid contaminants (sludge) based on **density difference**.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a **salad spinner**: the faster you spin, the more water flies out of the lettuce leaves. A purifier does the same thing — spins fuel oil at high speed so that water and impurities (denser) are thrown outward against the bowl wall, while pure oil (lighter) collects at the center and flows out through a separate outlet.

---

## 1.5 Marine Valves

### Formal Definition
A **Marine Valve** is a mechanical device installed in piping systems to start, stop, throttle, or regulate fluid flow. They are categorized by function (stop, check, safety, control) and by body type (gate, globe, ball, butterfly).

### Common Onboard Types
| Valve | Function | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Gate Valve** | Full open/close (isolation) | A sliding wall in a pipe |
| **Globe Valve** | Throttling (flow regulation) | A screw-clamp squeezing a hose |
| **Ball Valve** | Quick shut-off | A rotating marble with a hole |
| **Check Valve** | One-way flow prevention | A swing door that only opens one way |
| **Safety Valve** | Overpressure relief | A spring-loaded pressure cooker whistle |
| **Sounding/Butterfly** | Tank level/air release | Lightweight wafer between flanges |

---

## 1.6 Marine Heat Exchangers

### Formal Definition
A **Heat Exchanger (HX)** is a device that transfers thermal energy between two or more fluids at different temperatures without mixing them. On ships, HX is used for cooling lube oil, charge air, jacket cooling water, and freshwater generation.

### Intuitive Analogy
A heat exchanger is like a **radiator in a car** — hot fluid flows through pipes surrounded by cool air (or vice versa), and heat moves across the pipe walls without the two fluids ever touching. On ships, the **lube oil cooler** is a perfect example: hot oil passes through tubes while cool seawater flows around them.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Classification:**
> - **Shell & Tube** (most common on ships)
> - **Plate Type** (compact, gasketed)
> - **Fin-Tube** (air-cooled)

---

## 1.7 Oil-Water Separator (OWS)

### Formal Definition
An **Oily Water Separator (OWS)** is a pollution-prevention device that separates oil from bilge water to a discharge limit of **≤15 ppm** before pumping overboard, in compliance with **MARPOL Annex I** regulations.

### Working Principle
Three-stage separation: **Gravity (coalescer) → Centrifugal (hydrocyclone) → Adsorption (polishing filter)**.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a **home oil-stain remover** in a washing machine: the spinning basket separates water from heavier dirt, then a fine filter catches the last tiny oil droplets. The ship's OWS does the same, but on a much larger scale to protect the sea.

---

## 1.8 Marine Incinerator

### Formal Definition
A **Marine Incinerator** is a combustion chamber designed to safely burn ship-generated waste — including **sludge, bilge water residues, used lube oil, garbage, and solid waste** — at temperatures typically above **850°C** with a residence time of ≥2 seconds, complying with **MARPOL Annex VI**.

### Intuitive Analogy
Picture a **mini industrial furnace** in the ship's engine room. Instead of dumping oily rags and food waste overboard (illegal), the crew feeds them into the incinerator, where a powerful burner turns them into harmless ash and flue gas.

---

## 1.9 Sewage Treatment Plant (STP)

### Formal Definition
A **Sewage Treatment Plant** onboard is a biological/chemical system that treats black and grey water from toilets, galleys, and laundries to meet IMO **MARPOL Annex IV** discharge standards (≤25 mg/L of suspended solids, faecal coliform count ≤100/100 mL).

### Process Stages
**Collection → Maceration → Biological Aeration → Disinfection (chlorination) → Discharge**.

### Intuitive Analogy
A marine STP is the ship's **"mini municipal sewage works."** Just like a city treatment plant uses bacteria to break down waste, the ship uses a compact bioreactor with aerators and chlorine tablets to sterilize effluent.

---

## 1.10 Fresh Water Generator (FWG)

### Formal Definition
A **Fresh Water Generator** is a **vacuum distillation plant** that converts seawater into potable freshwater by boiling it under **vacuum (typically 0.1–0.3 bar abs)** at low temperatures (35–45°C), condensing the vapor and rejecting the brine.

### Intuitive Analogy
A marine FWG is like a **covered pot of simmering seawater on a cool day**. When the lid is cool, water vapor condenses on it as fresh water droplets — exactly the same physics, but engineered for thousands of tons of water per day.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Memory Aid:** Vacuum lowers the boiling point. So, even at 40°C, seawater will boil if you suck out most of the air. This saves massive energy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Marine Air Compressors — Deep Analysis

### Types and Working Principles

**(a) Reciprocating (Positive Displacement) — Most Common Onboard**
1. Piston moves down → **suction stroke** → air enters through inlet valve at atmospheric pressure.
2. Piston moves up → **compression stroke** → air is squeezed and discharged through outlet valve into receiver.
3. **Two-stage** with intercooler: stage 1 compresses to ~3 bar → cooled → stage 2 compresses to 30 bar. **Intercooling reduces work of compression by ~15%.**

**(b) Screw (Rotary Screw)**
- Two helical rotors mesh; air is trapped in cavities and pushed toward discharge port. Oil-injected for cooling and sealing.

### Key Operating Parameters
- **Cut-in pressure:** 25 bar (compressor starts)
- **Cut-out pressure:** 30 bar (compressor stops)
- **Capacity:** 60–150 m³/hr
- **Safety devices:** bursting disc, pressure switch, thermal relief.

---

## 2.2 Marine Pumps — Deep Analysis

### (A) Centrifugal Pump
- Impeller rotates at high speed → imparts **kinetic energy** to fluid → kinetic energy converts to **pressure energy** in volute casing.
- Used for: seawater cooling, freshwater cooling, ballast, firefighting (large flow, low head).

**Performance Equation (Euler's Pump Equation):**

$$H = \frac{u_2 v_{2u} - u_1 v_{1u}}{g}$$

where:
- $H$ = head developed (m)
- $u$ = blade velocity (m/s)
- $v_u$ = tangential component of absolute velocity

### (B) Positive Displacement Pumps
- **Gear pump** (external/internal gear): two meshing gears displace fluid.
- **Screw pump** (3-screw): used for lube oil and fuel oil transfer.
- **Reciprocating pump**: piston-driven; used as bilge pump in some ships.

---

## 2.3 Marine Boilers — Deep Analysis

### Cochran Boiler (Fire-Tube)
- Spherical shell, fire tubes run through water.
- Capacity: up to 3000 kg/hr.
- Steam pressure: up to 10 bar.

### Water-Tube Boiler (Aalborg)
- Water circulates in tubes; combustion in furnace.
- Higher pressure (up to 60 bar), faster steam generation.

### Critical Auxiliary Components
- **Safety valve** (set at MAWP + 3%)
- **Water level gauge** (sight glass)
- **Feed check valve**
- **Boiler mountings:** pressure gauge, stop valve, blow-down valve, manhole

---

## 2.4 Purifier — Deep Analysis

### Two-Phase Separation Theory

The centrifugal force acting on a particle is:

$$F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 r = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r$$

For separation efficiency (Stokes regime):

$$v_{sep} = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r}{18 \mu}$$

where:
- $d$ = particle diameter
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)
- $r$ = bowl radius
- $\mu$ = fluid dynamic viscosity
- $\rho_p, \rho_f$ = particle and fluid densities

**Separation Number (Z):**

$$Z = \frac{\omega^2 r \Delta \rho d^2}{18 \mu g}$$

### Bowl Speed & Specific Gravity (SG)
- Bowl speed $N = 6000\text{–}9000$ rpm
- **Density of fuel oil** ≈ 0.85–0.98 g/cm³; **water** ≈ 1.025 g/cm³; difference $\Delta\rho \approx 0.1$ g/cm³

### Purifier vs Clarifier
- **Purifier**: separates oil + water + sludge (3-phase).
- **Clarifier**: separates solid impurities only (2-phase solid-liquid).

---

## 2.5 Marine Valves — Deep Analysis

### Sizing / Flow Equation

$$Q = C_v A \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{\rho}}$$

where:
- $Q$ = flow rate
- $C_v$ = valve flow coefficient
- $A$ = cross-sectional area
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across valve

### Safety Valve Sizing (API 520)

$$A = \frac{W}{K \cdot P \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c}$$

where $W$ = required relieving capacity (kg/hr); $K$ = coefficient of discharge.

---

## 2.6 Heat Exchangers — Deep Analysis

### LMTD Method (Log Mean Temperature Difference)

$$\Delta T_{lmtd} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

**Heat transfer rate:**

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd} \cdot F$$

where:
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²K)
- $A$ = heat transfer area
- $F$ = correction factor for multi-pass shells

### Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For marine lube oil coolers, $U \approx 200\text{–}500$ W/m²K. For freshwater generators, $U \approx 1500\text{–}2500$ W/m²K (due to condensation).

---

## 2.7 Oil-Water Separator — Deep Analysis

### Coalescer Stage (Stokes' Law):

$$v = \frac{g \, d^2 (\rho_w - \rho_o)}{18 \mu}$$

A 60-µm droplet rises ≈ 1 cm in 10 minutes → coalescer pad enlarges droplets to 200+ µm for faster rise.

### Hydrocyclone
Tangential inlet creates vortex; oil migrates to core and exits through top.

---

## 2.8 Incinerator — Deep Analysis

### Combustion Equation (Auxiliary Burner — Light Fuel Oil)

$$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \rightarrow x CO_2 + \frac{y}{2} H_2O + \text{Heat}$$

### Stoichiometric Air Requirement (for CₓHᵧ):
$$\text{Air (kg)} = 14.5 \times \text{Fuel (kg)} \text{ (approximately)}$$

### MARPOL Compliance:
- Combustion chamber ≥ **850°C**
- Residence time ≥ **2 seconds**
- Excess O₂ ≥ **6%** in flue gas
- CO ≤ **200 mg/MJ**

---

## 2.9 Sewage Treatment Plant — Deep Analysis

### Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD) Reduction

$$\text{BOD Reduction \%} = \frac{BOD_{in} - BOD_{out}}{BOD_{in}} \times 100$$

Aeration tank retention time: **8–24 hours**.

Chlorine dosing: **2–5 mg/L residual**.

---

## 2.10 Fresh Water Generator — Deep Analysis

### Evaporation & Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)
At 0.1 bar absolute, seawater boils at ≈ **43°C**. BPE ≈ **0.5°C**.

### Daily Capacity:
$$m_{fw} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{\lambda + c_p \Delta T}$$

where $\lambda$ = latent heat of vaporization (~2400 kJ/kg at 40°C).

### Conversion: 1 ton of fuel oil → **3.5–4.0 tons of freshwater** (with waste heat).

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | System | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centrifugal Pump | $H = \dfrac{u_2 v_{2u} - u_1 v_{1u}}{g}$ | velocities | m |
| 2 | Pump Power | $P = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta}$ | flow Q, head H, efficiency $\eta$ | W |
| 3 | Compressor (Isothermal) | $W = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | inlet pressure, volume, ratio | J |
| 4 | Compressor (Adiabatic) | $W = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | $\gamma = c_p/c_v = 1.4$ (air) | J |
| 5 | Centrifuge Separation | $Z = \dfrac{\omega^2 r \Delta \rho d^2}{18 \mu g}$ | Z separation number | — |
| 6 | Heat Exchanger (LMTD) | $Q = U A \Delta T_{lmtd}$ | U coeff., area, ΔT | W |
| 7 | Oil rise (Stokes) | $v = \dfrac{g d^2 \Delta \rho}{18 \mu}$ | droplet rise velocity | m/s |
| 8 | Fresh Water Gen | $Q = U A \Delta T$ | shell-tube HX | W |
| 9 | Safety Valve Area | $A = \dfrac{W}{K P K_d K_b K_c}$ | per API 520 | mm² |
| 10 | Incinerator Excess Air | $EA\% = \dfrac{O_2 - \dfrac{CO}{2}}{0.264 \cdot N_2 - (O_2 - \dfrac{CO}{2})} \times 100$ | flue gas analysis | % |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Work Required by a Two-Stage Reciprocating Air Compressor with Intercooling

### Given
- Suction pressure: $P_1 = 1$ bar
- Discharge pressure: $P_2 = 30$ bar
- $\gamma = 1.4$ for air
- $V_1 = 1 \text{ m}^3$ at suction conditions

### Step 1: Optimal Intermediate Pressure for Two-Stage Compression

For **minimum work**, the pressure ratio per stage should be equal:

$$\frac{P_x}{P_1} = \frac{P_2}{P_x} \implies P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$$

Substituting values:

$$P_x = \sqrt{1 \times 30} = \sqrt{30} \approx 5.477 \text{ bar}$$

### Step 2: Compression Ratio per Stage

$$r_{stage} = \frac{P_x}{P_1} = \frac{5.477}{1} = 5.477$$

### Step 3: Work Per Stage (Adiabatic Compression)

$$W_{stage} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} P_1 V_1 \left[ (r_{stage})^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$$

Compute the exponent:

$$\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$

$$(5.477)^{0.2857}$$

Let us expand using logarithm:

$$\ln(5.477) \approx 1.7011$$
$$0.2857 \times 1.7011 \approx 0.4860$$
$$e^{0.4860} \approx 1.6263$$

So:

$$(5.477)^{0.2857} \approx 1.6263$$

### Step 4: Substitute into Stage Work

With $P_1 = 1 \times 10^5$ Pa and $V_1 = 1$ m³:

$$W_{stage} = \frac{1.4}{0.4} \times (1 \times 10^5) \times 1 \times (1.6263 - 1)$$

$$W_{stage} = 3.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.6263$$

$$W_{stage} \approx 2.192 \times 10^5 \text{ J} = 219.2 \text{ kJ}$$

### Step 5: Total Work for Two Stages (with intercooling back to $T_1$)

$$W_{total} = 2 \times W_{stage} = 2 \times 219.2 = 438.4 \text{ kJ}$$

### Step 6: Compare with Single-Stage Compression

For single stage, ratio = 30:

$$(30)^{0.2857}: \ln 30 = 3.401, \times 0.2857 = 0.9717, e^{0.9717} = 2.6425$$

$$W_{single} = 3.5 \times 10^5 \times (2.6425 - 1) = 3.5 \times 10^5 \times 1.6425 \approx 574.9 \text{ kJ}$$

### Step 7: Work Saved by Intercooling

$$\text{Saving} = \frac{W_{single} - W_{total}}{W_{single}} \times 100 = \frac{574.9 - 438.4}{574.9} \times 100 \approx 23.7\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Two-stage compression with intercooling saves ~**24%** of compression work. This is why all marine starting air compressors are two-stage.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: LMTD for Shell-and-Tube Lube Oil Cooler

### Given
- Lube oil enters: $T_{o,in} = 80°C$, leaves $T_{o,out} = 50°C$
- Seawater enters: $T_{w,in} = 25°C$, leaves $T_{w,out} = 45°C$

### Step 1: Identify End Temperature Differences (Counter-Flow)

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{o,in} - T_{w,out} = 80 - 45 = 35°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{o,out} - T_{w,in} = 50 - 25 = 25°C$$

### Step 2: Compute LMTD

$$\Delta T_{lmtd} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{35 - 25}{\ln(35/25)} = \frac{10}{\ln(1.4)}$$

$$\ln(1.4) = 0.3365$$

$$\Delta T_{lmtd} = \frac{10}{0.3365} \approx 29.72°C$$

### Step 3: Heat Transfer Rate

If $U = 300$ W/m²K and $A = 5$ m²:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd} = 300 \times 5 \times 29.72 = 44,580 \text{ W} \approx 44.58 \text{ kW}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Capacity of Fresh Water Generator

### Given
- Engine jacket water inlet: $T_{hw,in} = 85°C$, outlet $T_{hw,out} = 75°C$
- Seawater in evaporator: $T_{sw,in} = 30°C$, distiller: $T_{sw,out} = 38°C$
- Sea pressure in shell: **0.1 bar abs**
- Latent heat at 40°C: $\lambda \approx 2400$ kJ/kg

### Step 1: Heat Available from Jacket Water

$$Q_{hw} = \dot{m}_{hw} \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{hw,in} - T_{hw,out}) = \dot{m}_{hw} \times 4.18 \times 10$$

For $\dot{m}_{hw} = 30$ kg/s:

$$Q_{hw} = 30 \times 4.18 \times 10 = 1254 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 2: Heat Required for Evaporation

$$Q_{evap} = \dot{m}_{fw} \cdot \lambda$$

Assume all jacket water heat is used:

$$\dot{m}_{fw} = \frac{Q_{hw}}{\lambda} = \frac{1254}{2400} \approx 0.5225 \text{ kg/s}$$

### Step 3: Convert to Tons/Day

$$\dot{m}_{fw} = 0.5225 \times 86400 / 1000 = 45.14 \text{ tons/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** A real FWG has ~60–70% thermal efficiency due to losses. So actual output ≈ **30 tons/day** from the above setup.

---

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix (Quick Comparison Table)

| Parameter | Reciprocating Compressor | Screw Compressor |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure ratio | High (30:1) | Moderate (15:1) |
| Flow rate | Pulsating | Continuous |
| Oil carryover | High (filter needed) | Low (oil-injected type) |
| Maintenance | High | Low |
| Marine use | **Starting air** | Service air, control air |

| Parameter | Centrifugal Pump | Positive Displacement Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Flow vs Pressure | High flow, low head | Low flow, high head |
| Efficiency at part load | Drops | Constant |
| Slip | No slip | Slip exists |
| Use | Seawater, ballast, FW | Lube oil, FO transfer |

| Parameter | Fire-tube Boiler | Water-tube Boiler |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure | < 10 bar | up to 60 bar |
| Steam generation | Slow | Fast |
| Risk of explosion | Higher (large water volume) | Lower |
| Ship application | Auxiliary | Main propulsion (modern) |

| Parameter | Purifier | Clarifier |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Oil + water + sludge | Solids only |
| Disc number | More discs | Fewer |
| Specific gravity disc | Yes | No |
| Sludge space | Larger | Smaller |
