# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Explain the working principle of a two-stage reciprocating air compressor with intercooling. State its advantage over single-stage.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a two-stage reciprocating air compressor, air is first compressed in the **low-pressure (LP) cylinder** to an intermediate pressure (typically 1/4 to 1/5 of final discharge pressure). The hot compressed air is then passed through an **intercooler**, where it is cooled back to (or near) the original suction temperature before entering the **high-pressure (HP) cylinder**, where final compression to 30 bar takes place.

**Advantage over single-stage (Key Points):**
1. Lower work of compression — saves **~24%** of compression work.
2. Lower discharge temperature → improves volumetric efficiency.
3. Safer operation (no risk of auto-ignition of lube oil in HP cylinder).
4. Allows compression ratios > 6:1 to be achieved economically.

---

### Question 2: What is a Marine Purifier? State the difference between a purifier and a clarifier.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Marine Purifier** is a high-speed (3,000–9,000 rpm) disc-type centrifuge that uses centrifugal force to separate **oil, water, and solid impurities** from fuel/lube oil based on density difference.

**Purifier vs Clarifier:**

| Feature | Purifier | Clarifier |
|---|---|---|
| Separation | 3-phase (oil-water-sludge) | 2-phase (oil-solids only) |
| Specific Gravity Ring | Yes (set for water seal) | Not required |
| Sludge capacity | Larger | Smaller |
| Density difference | Between oil & water | Between oil & solids only |
| Application | Heavy fuel oil treatment | Lub oil cleaning (dirt only) |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of an **Oil-Water Separator (OWS)**. Mention the MARPOL discharge limits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working principle and onboard applications of a **Fresh Water Generator (FWG)**. Why is it operated under vacuum? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**OWS Construction & Working (7 marks):**

The OWS is a three-stage device used to treat bilge water before overboard discharge.

**Stage 1 — Gravity/Coalescer:**
Bilge water enters the OWS through a coarse strainer (removes large debris). It then flows into a **coalescer chamber** containing oleophilic (oil-attracting) pads. Small oil droplets (10–50 µm) coalesce into larger droplets (200+ µm) which rise to the surface (Stokes' law). Oil is scraped off to a collecting tank.

**Stage 2 — Hydrocyclone:**
The partially cleaned water enters a **hydrocyclone** tangentially. Centrifugal action (up to 1000g) separates remaining oil to the vortex core, which exits at the top. Cleaned water exits the bottom.

**Stage 3 — Adsorption Filter:**
Water passes through a **fine filter** (activated carbon or polymeric fibers) which captures residual oil down to **<15 ppm**. A **monitor** continuously measures oil content; if >15 ppm, a 3-way valve diverts the flow back to the bilge tank.

**MARPOL Annex I Limits:**
- Discharge oil content ≤ **15 ppm**
- Not allowed within 50 nautical miles of land (with some exceptions)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**FWG Construction, Working & Vacuum (7 marks):**

A Fresh Water Generator is a **vacuum multi-stage distillation plant**.

**Working Principle:**
1. Seawater enters the **evaporator** through a spray nozzle at the top.
2. The evaporator shell is maintained at **0.1–0.3 bar abs** by a vacuum pump.
3. **Hot jacket water** (from main engine) at 75–85°C flows through heating coils.
4. At reduced pressure, seawater **boils at 40–45°C**, producing pure water vapor (salt cannot vaporize).
5. The vapor rises and contacts the **condenser tubes**, through which cold seawater flows.
6. Vapor condenses on tube surfaces and drips down as **fresh water**, collected and tested for salinity (<1.5 ppm ideally).
7. Concentrated **brine** exits from the bottom of the evaporator.

**Why vacuum?**
- **Lowers boiling point** (40°C instead of 100°C) → uses waste heat from jacket water.
- **Prevents scale formation** on heating surfaces (low temperature operation).
- **Energy efficient** — uses otherwise wasted engine heat.

**Onboard Applications:**
- Potable water supply for crew
- Boiler feed make-up
- Cooling water make-up
- Dishwashing, laundry

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a)** Explain with diagram the **construction and working of a disc-type fuel oil purifier**. Discuss the role of the gravity disc. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **working of a marine sewage treatment plant (STP)** with reference to **MARPOL Annex IV**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Disc-Type Purifier (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
- A **vertical bowl** rotating at 6,000–9,000 rpm driven by a horizontal shaft through a worm gear.
- Inside the bowl, a **stack of conical discs** (40–150 discs, 0.5–1 mm thick, with spacing ~1 mm) is mounted on a central distributor.
- A **gravity disc** (also called SG disc) regulates the interface position between oil and water.
- Sludge collects at the bowl periphery; clean oil exits at the top via gravity.

**Working:**
1. Fuel oil enters through the central spindle.
2. It passes through the disc stack via holes in the discs.
3. Heavier **water and sludge** are flung to the bowl wall due to centrifugal force.
4. Sludge is ejected periodically through peripheral ports.
5. **Clean oil** moves inward (lower density) and overflows at the top.

**Role of Gravity Disc:**
The gravity disc (specific gravity disc) controls the **diameter of the oil-water interface** inside the bowl. By selecting a disc of correct diameter (corresponding to oil SG), the interface is positioned at the disc stack's outer edge, ensuring optimal separation. If wrong SG disc is selected, water or oil may be carried out with the wrong discharge.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Marine STP (7 marks):**

**Process Flow:**
1. **Collection** — Black water from toilets, galley grey water from sinks/showers collected in a holding tank.
2. **Maceration** — Solid waste is chopped into fine slurry by macerator pumps.
3. **Biological Aeration** — In the aeration chamber, **aerobic bacteria** break down organic waste using dissolved oxygen supplied by a blower. Retention time: 8–24 hours. BOD reduction: 90–95%.
4. **Sedimentation** — Activated sludge settles in a clarifier; supernatant flows to disinfection.
5. **Chlorination** — Effluent is dosed with chlorine tablets or sodium hypochlorite for disinfection. Contact time ≥ 30 minutes.
6. **Discharge** — Treated effluent is pumped overboard through a monitoring port.

**MARPOL Annex IV Standards:**
- **Suspended solids** ≤ 25 mg/L
- **Faecal coliform count** ≤ 100/100 mL
- **BOD₅** ≤ 25 mg/L (in some special areas)
- **pH** between 6 and 9

**Discharge restrictions:**
- Forbidden within **4 nautical miles** of nearest land (using approved STP).
- Between 4 and 12 nm: only with approved comminuted and disinfected sewage.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Purifier vs Clarifier** — Marks lost if you do not mention that purifier handles water + sludge, while clarifier handles only solids. Always state that purifier needs a **specific gravity disc**.
>
> 2. **Omitting MARPOL compliance** — In any question about OWS, STP, or Incinerator, failing to cite the relevant MARPOL annex and numerical limit (15 ppm / 25 mg/L / 850°C) results in 1–2 mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Forgetting intercooling justification** — When asked about two-stage compressors, students often describe the layout but skip the **"why"** (work saving by ~24%, lower discharge temperature). Examiners reward conceptual clarity.
>
> 4. **Boiler types** — Don't confuse *fire-tube* and *water-tube* — clearly mark which fluid is inside the tubes.
>
> 5. **Fresh Water Generator** — Always mention **boiling point elevation, vacuum, and scale prevention** in the same breath.
>
> 6. **Heat Exchanger** — Many students compute heat duty but forget to apply the **LMTD correction factor F** for multi-pass configurations.
>
> 7. **Valves** — When drawing valve cross-sections, always label the **direction of flow arrow** and the **seating direction** for globe vs gate valves.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Marine Air Compressor**: Two-stage reciprocating with intercooler is standard; saves ~24% compression work. Pressure 30 bar for starting air.
- **Marine Pumps**: Centrifugal for high-flow/low-head; Positive displacement for high-pressure/low-flow.
- **Marine Boilers**: Fire-tube for low pressure (<10 bar), water-tube for high pressure (>10 bar); exhaust gas economizer for waste heat recovery.
- **Purifiers**: Disc-stack centrifuge at 6000+ rpm; purifier for 3-phase (oil-water-sludge), clarifier for 2-phase (oil-solids); gravity disc controls interface.
- **Marine Valves**: Gate = isolation, Globe = throttling, Ball = quick shut-off, Check = one-way, Safety = overpressure relief.
- **Heat Exchangers**: Shell & tube most common; use **LMTD** method for sizing; correction factor F for multi-pass.
- **Oil Water Separator**: 3-stage (coalescer → hydrocyclone → adsorber); MARPOL limit **15 ppm**.
- **Incinerator**: Must operate at ≥850°C, ≥2s residence time; burns sludge, oil residues, garbage; per **MARPOL Annex VI**.
- **Sewage Treatment Plant**: Biological aeration + chlorination; discharge limit **25 mg/L SS, 100 coliform/100 mL**; MARPOL Annex IV.
- **Fresh Water Generator**: Vacuum distillation at **0.1 bar** and **40–45°C**; uses jacket water waste heat; produces 30–100 tons/day potable water; salinity monitor mandatory.
- **Safety devices**: All pressure vessels (boilers, receivers) must have **two safety valves**, with one being a dummy for testing.
- **MARPOL annexes**: Annex I (oil), Annex IV (sewage), Annex V (garbage), Annex VI (air pollution).
- **Universal on ships**: All marine auxiliaries are designed for **24/7 continuous operation**, salt-laden air, vibration, and limited maintenance access.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> **Piping Arrangement for Steam** in a marine steam plant refers to the engineered network of pipes, valves, fittings, supports, insulation, and safety devices that transport steam from the boiler/steam generator to its various consumers (main propulsion turbine, auxiliary turbines, deck machinery, oil fuel purifiers, freshwater generators, heating coils, etc.) and safely discharge the resulting condensate/exhaust, all in strict accordance with the relevant classification society rules (IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS) and SOLAS/IMDG/MARPOL statutory codes.

The arrangement is **NOT** a random cluster of pipes — it is a **pre-designed, pressure-rated, thermally-compensated, drainable, and inspectable** engineering system. It is a **high-energy fluid conveyance system** carrying working fluid at temperatures up to **540 °C** and pressures up to **100 bar** in modern marine boilers (sub-critical and super-critical designs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme - PCNST503, Module 3):**
> The student must be able to *describe, sketch, and justify* the steam piping layout on board a merchant ship, identify each fitting and valve, and explain the safety philosophy (redundancy, double isolation, drainability, and expansion compensation) behind the arrangement.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine the human **circulatory system**:

| Human Body | Marine Steam Plant |
|------------|--------------------|
| Heart | Boiler / Steam Generator |
| Arteries (oxygenated blood) | **Main Steam Line** (live steam from boiler) |
| Veins (deoxygenated blood) | **Exhaust Steam Line** (used steam) |
| Capillaries | Branch lines to auxiliaries |
| Valves in heart | **Stop Valves, Safety Valves** |
| Lymphatic drainage | **Drain Line, Steam Trap Line** |

Just as arteries must be **flexible (to accommodate body movement)**, **insulated (to retain heat)**, and **valved (to isolate during surgery)**, the steam piping on a ship must be:
1. **Flexible** – to absorb thermal expansion and ship flexing (hogging/sagging).
2. **Insulated** – to prevent heat loss (efficiency) and burn injuries to crew.
3. **Isolatable** – every consumer must be isolable by at least **two valves** in series (regulatory requirement).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for a Marine Engineer:** "**PIPES**" — **P**ressure-rated, **I**nsulated, **P**itched for drainage, **E**xpansion-compensated, **S**afety-valved.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Visibility)

- **Maximum recommended steam velocity (superheated, main line):** **50 – 60 m/s**
- **Maximum recommended steam velocity (saturated steam, branch lines):** **30 – 40 m/s**
- **Maximum recommended steam velocity (exhaust steam):** **25 – 30 m/s**
- **Standard design pressure for class piping (high-pressure):** **Up to 100 bar**
- **Standard design pressure for auxiliary steam line:** **7 – 15 bar**
- **Standard design pressure for low-pressure heating steam:** **3 – 5 bar**
- **Acceptable condensate accumulation in steam line:** **Zero (lines must be self-draining)**
- **Thermal expansion of carbon steel per 100 °C rise:** **≈ 1.2 mm/m**
- **Heat loss limit for lagged steam pipe:** **< 10 % per 100 m** of pipe run

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These metrics are **non-negotiable for the KTU ESE**. A 14-mark question may simply ask: "Justify the steam velocity in a marine main steam line is limited to 60 m/s." You must know these numbers verbatim.

---

### 1.4 Classification & Categories of Marine Steam Piping

A marine steam piping system is conventionally sub-divided into the following functional categories:

1. **High-Pressure (HP) Steam Line** – from boiler stop valve to HP turbine admission.
2. **Auxiliary Steam Line** – branch from HP line (after pressure reduction) to auxiliaries.
3. **Exhaust Steam Line** – collects steam from auxiliary turbines and feeds it to a feed heating/evaporator system.
4. **Steam Drain Line** – collects condensate from steam traps and leaky glands; returns it to the hot well or condenser.
5. **Safety Valve Escape Pipe** – discharges boiler safety valve lift to the atmosphere (silenced).
6. **Blowdown Pipe** – for boiler surface blowdown to a scum tank.
7. **Gauge Glass / Water Column Pipe** – for boiler water level indication.
8. **Soot Blowing Steam Pipe** – for cleaning boiler tube surfaces.
9. **Steam Smothering Pipe** – for fire-fighting in machinery spaces (CO₂ replaced by steam in some systems, now largely obsolete).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind the Arrangement

The piping arrangement is engineered around **four non-negotiable engineering drivers**, each addressed by a specific design feature:

| Engineering Driver | Consequence if Ignored | Design Feature Adopted |
|--------------------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| **High temperature & pressure** | Burn injuries, pipe rupture, loss of life | Heavy schedule pipes, full-penetration butt welds, flanged joints, lagging |
| **Thermal expansion** | Pipe buckling, support failure, flange leak | Expansion loops (U-bend or L-bend), bellows, sliding supports |
| **Condensate formation (water hammer)** | Catastrophic pipe failure, turbine blade erosion | Continuous gradient (1:200 minimum), drain pockets, steam traps |
| **Need to isolate equipment for maintenance** | Inability to repair, casualty | Double isolation (root valve + local valve), spectacle flanges, bypass |

---

### 2.2 Component-by-Component Functional Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Pipes (The Pressure Boundary)

- **Material:** Seamless carbon steel to **IACS UR S6 / S7** (e.g., ASTM A106 Gr.B, A335 P11/P22 for higher temperatures).
- **Wall thickness:** Determined by the **Barlow formula** (see Section 3).
- **Size (NB):** Determined by the **velocity-based sizing** equation.
- **Joints:**
  - **Butt-welded** for HP main line (permanent, leak-tight).
  - **Flanged** (raised face, tongue-and-groove) at equipment connections for maintenance.
  - **Socket-welded** for small-bore (≤ 50 mm) auxiliaries.
  - **Screwed (NPT/BSP)** for instrument and gauge lines (≤ 25 mm).

#### 2.2.2 Valves (The Control Elements)

| Valve Type | Function | Location |
|------------|----------|----------|
| **Main Steam Stop Valve (MSSV)** | Isolates boiler from HP line | Immediately after boiler neck |
| **Auxiliary Steam Stop Valve (ASSV)** | Isolates aux steam ring main | After pressure-reducing station |
| **Safety Valve (Spring-loaded)** | Over-pressure protection | Boiler shell (≥ 2 in number) |
| **Relief Valve** | Back-up for safety valve | Downstream of reducing valve |
| **Check / Non-Return Valve (NRV)** | Prevents back-flow | Boiler drum, feed line, exhaust line |
| **Globe Valve (Needle)** | Throttling | Steam to oil fuel heater |
| **Gate Valve** | On/Off isolation | Drain pots, separator |
| **Quick-closing Valve** | Emergency shutdown | Funnel uptakes (soot blowers) |
| **Slam-bang valve (automatic)** | Emergency | Steam to bunker station (oil burn-back) |

> [!NOTE]
> **MSSV is a critical regulatory item** under SOLAS Reg. II-1/26–27. It must be operable from outside the boiler casing (remote chain or hydraulic operation) in case of a boiler uptake fire.

#### 2.2.3 Steam Traps (The Condensate Removers)

A **steam trap** is an automatic valve that discharges condensate and air but **not live steam**. Three principal types on board:

1. **Float & Thermostatic (FT) trap** – for heavy condensate loads (main line drains).
2. **Thermodynamic (TD) / Disc trap** – for small loads, superheat, soot blowers.
3. **Inverted Bucket trap** – for moderate loads, heating coils.
4. **Thermostatic (Bellows / Bimetallic) trap** – for tracing, small-bore drains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A failed-open steam trap wastes steam (energy loss, ~5–10 % of boiler output). A failed-closed steam trap causes **water hammer** (mechanical damage). Both are reportable defects under the **Safety Management System (ISM Code)**.

#### 2.2.4 Expansion Devices

- **Expansion loop (U/L)** – preferred for HP main line. Made of long-radius bends.
- **Bellows (axial/lateral)** – used where space is limited (auxiliary machinery spaces).
- **Slip joint** – for low-pressure exhaust line.
- **Flexible hose (metallic corrugated)** – for final connection to vibrating equipment (turbine).

#### 2.2.5 Insulation (Lagging)

- **Material:** Calcium silicate, mineral wool (rockwool), or **aerogel** (modern).
- **Thickness:** Calculated to keep **external surface temperature ≤ 60 °C** (personnel burn protection) and limit heat loss.
- **Cladding:** Aluminium sheet (0.5–0.8 mm) or galvanised iron to make it **vapour-tight** (prevents moisture ingress that destroys insulation).

#### 2.2.6 Supports & Hangers

- **Rigid supports** (shoes, saddles) at fixed points.
- **Spring hangers** at dynamic points.
- **Pipe guides** (lateral restraint) at intermediate points.
- **Anchor points** to direct expansion into the loop.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY** for KTU ESE — at least one numerical problem (typically 7 marks) appears every exam cycle.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Constants | KTU Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | $t = \dfrac{PD}{2SE - 1.2P} + C$ | Barlow's formula (pipe wall thickness) | $t$ = thickness, $P$ = design pressure, $D$ = OD, $S$ = allowable stress, $E$ = joint efficiency (1.0 for seamless), $C$ = corrosion allowance (1.5 mm) | Pipe schedule calculation |
| 2 | $D_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi \rho_{s} V_{s}}}$ | Velocity-based pipe diameter | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow (kg/s), $\rho_s$ = steam density, $V_s$ = steam velocity | Pipe sizing |
| 3 | $V_{s} = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi \rho_s D^{2}}$ | Steam velocity check | – | Verification |
| 4 | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^{2}}{2} + \sum K \dfrac{\rho V^{2}}{2}$ | Pressure drop in pipe (Darcy + fittings) | $f$ = friction factor, $L$ = length, $K$ = loss coefficient | Header / branch line design |
| 5 | $\Delta L = \alpha L \Delta T$ | Free thermal expansion | $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} \; /^{\circ}\text{C}$ for carbon steel, $L$ = length, $\Delta T$ = temperature rise | Expansion loop design |
| 6 | $h = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 E D \Delta L}{10 S}}$ | Expansion loop leg length | $E$ = Young's modulus (200 GPa), $S$ = allowable stress, $D$ = pipe OD | Loop sizing |
| 7 | $\dot{Q} = U A \Delta T_{lm}$ | Heat loss through insulation | $U$ = overall heat transfer coeff, $A$ = surface area, $\Delta T_{lm}$ = log-mean temp. difference | Insulation thickness |
| 8 | $A_{sv} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{max}}{K \cdot P}$ | Safety valve orifice area (API 520) | $K$ = discharge coefficient, $P$ = set pressure | Safety valve selection |
| 9 | $h_{f} = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$ | Frictional head loss | $g = 9.81$ m/s² | Drain line sizing |
| 10 | $C_v = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{N_1 P_1 Y \sqrt{x / T_1 Z}}$ | Steam valve sizing | $C_v$ = valve flow coefficient | Control valve selection |

> [!NOTE]
> In a KTU answer sheet, **always write the formula first, then substitute values with units, then box the final answer with units**. Examiners allocate 1 mark for the correct formula alone.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The same engineering principles are applied (with different fluids) in:

- **Power plant steam piping** (utility boilers, 600 MW + units).
- **Process plant steam networks** (refineries, petrochemical).
- **District heating networks** (condensate return).
- **Nuclear plant secondary loop** (more rigorous seismic + LOCA qualification).
- **Geothermal steam piping** (corrosion-resistant alloys due to H₂S, CO₂).

Hence, a marine engineer trained in steam piping can transition seamlessly to a power-plant or process-plant piping engineer with minimal re-training — a **highly portable skill**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pipe Sizing from First Principles (Continuity)

**Given:** A main steam line carries a mass flow $\dot{m}$ of superheated steam at pressure $P$ and temperature $T$. Determine the minimum pipe internal diameter $D_{min}$ such that the steam velocity does not exceed $V_{max}$ (to avoid noise, erosion, and pressure drop).

**Step 1 — Continuity equation** (conservation of mass, steady-state, 1-D):

$$
\dot{m} = \rho_s A V_s
$$

where $\rho_s$ is the steam specific volume-reciprocal (density), $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the pipe, and $V_s$ is the average steam velocity.

**Step 2 — Express cross-sectional area** in terms of internal diameter $D$:

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $A$ into the continuity equation**:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho_s \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot V_s
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $D$**:

$$
D^{2} = \frac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi \rho_s V_s}
$$

$$
D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi \rho_s V_s}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the design-limit velocity $V_{max}$** to obtain the *minimum* permissible diameter:

$$
\boxed{\,D_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi \rho_s V_{max}}}\,}
$$

> **Why $V_{max}$ matters:** At $V_s > 60$ m/s in superheated steam, vibration, noise, erosion of fittings, and excessive pressure drop occur. Below $V_s = 25$ m/s, the pipe is over-sized — costly, and water droplets may settle out (drainage problem).

**Step 6 — Practical example (KTU-style numerical):**

> A boiler delivers **20 kg/s** of superheated steam at **40 bar, 450 °C**. Using a maximum steam velocity of **55 m/s**, find the required pipe diameter. Given steam specific volume at this state is **0.0613 m³/kg**.

*Substitute:*

$$
\rho_s = \frac{1}{0.0613} = 16.31 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}
$$

$$
D_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 20}{\pi \times 16.31 \times 55}}
$$

$$
D_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{80}{2818.3}} = \sqrt{0.02839}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{min} = 0.1685 \; \text{m} = 168.5 \; \text{mm} \approx 175 \; \text{mm NB (7" NB standard)}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Wall Thickness from Barlow's Formula

A thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel (the pipe) is subjected to internal pressure $P$. The hoop stress $\sigma_h$ in the wall is balanced by the pressure force over the projected area.

**Step 1 — Force balance on a half-cylinder of length $L$ and diameter $D$**:

$$
P \cdot D \cdot L = 2 \cdot t \cdot L \cdot \sigma_h
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for hoop stress**:

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{PD}{2t}
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the safety factor** by replacing $\sigma_h$ with the allowable stress $S$ (with joint efficiency $E$ and corrosion allowance $C$):

$$
S = \sigma_h \cdot E = \frac{PD}{2t} \cdot E
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $t$**:

$$
t = \frac{PD}{2SE}
$$

**Step 5 — Add the corrosion allowance and the mill negative tolerance** (typically 12.5 % of the calculated $t$ is added per ASME B31.1):

$$
\boxed{\,t = \dfrac{PD}{2SE - 1.2P} + C\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Worked example:**

> Pipe OD = **168 mm**, design pressure = **45 bar = 4.5 MPa**, allowable stress $S = 110 \; \text{MPa}$ (carbon steel at 450 °C), $E = 1.0$ (seamless), $C = 1.5$ mm.

$$
t = \frac{4.5 \times 168}{2 \times 110 \times 1.0 - 1.2 \times 4.5} + 1.5
$$

$$
t = \frac{756}{220 - 5.4} + 1.5 = \frac{756}{214.6} + 1.5
$$

$$
t = 3.523 + 1.5 = 5.02 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Select standard schedule:** **Schedule 40 (S40)** gives wall = **7.11 mm** for 6" NB pipe — safely above calculated value. ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Thermal Expansion & Expansion Loop Slegth

**Step 1 — Free thermal expansion** of a pipe of length $L$ when heated by $\Delta T$:

$$
\Delta L = \alpha L \Delta T
$$

with $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} \; /^{\circ}\text{C}$ for carbon steel.

**Step 2 — For an expansion U-loop** of leg length $h$ (both legs = $h$), the loop must absorb the expansion. The flexibility of a U-loop is given by the empirical Kellogg formula:

$$
\boxed{\,h = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 E D \Delta L}{10 S}}\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Worked example:**

> $L = 30$ m, $\Delta T = 420 \; ^{\circ}\text{C}$ (from ambient 30 °C to 450 °C), $D = 0.168$ m, $E = 200 \times 10^{9}$ Pa, $S = 110 \times 10^{6}$ Pa.

$$
\Delta L = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 30 \times 420 = 0.1512 \; \text{m} = 151.2 \; \text{mm}
$$

$$
h = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 200 \times 10^{9} \times 0.168 \times 0.1512}{10 \times 110 \times 10^{6}}}
$$

$$
h = \sqrt{\frac{1.524 \times 10^{10}}{1.1 \times 10^{9}}} = \sqrt{13.85} = 3.72 \; \text{m}
$$

**Select $h = 4$ m** for safe design (each leg of the U-loop). ✓

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | KTU-Mandated Action | Equation / Table | Marks |
|----------|---------------------|------------------|-------|
| Find pipe diameter | Use continuity | $D_{min} = \sqrt{4\dot{m}/(\pi \rho V)}$ | 3–4 |
| Find wall thickness | Use Barlow | $t = PD/(2SE - 1.2P) + C$ | 3–4 |
| Find expansion loop | Use Kellogg | $h = \sqrt{3ED\Delta L/10S}$ | 3–4 |
| Find pressure drop | Use Darcy-Weisbach | $\Delta P = f(L/D)(\rho V^2/2)$ | 4–5 |
| Find steam trap capacity | Use manufacturer's $C_v$ chart | $\dot{m}_{trap} = f(P, \Delta T)$ | 2–3 |
| Find safety valve orifice | Use API 520 | $A = \dot{m}/(K P)$ | 4–5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always show **two** significant figures beyond the precision of input data. A KTU examiner deducts 0.5 marks if a student writes `0.16852` when input is given to 3 s.f.
