## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Examiner's Insight):**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the safety factor / joint efficiency** in the Barlow formula — lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Not specifying which classification society** (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV) the arrangement complies with — lose 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 3. **Drawing a pictorial diagram** (3-D pipe) instead of a **schematic** — examiners deduct 1 mark for "unprofessional drawing style".
> 4. **Ignoring drainage pitch** when listing line layout features — lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Using non-standard abbreviations** (e.g., "SV" instead of "MSSV") — lose 0.5 mark.
> 6. **Not mentioning the remote operation of MSSV** under SOLAS — lose 1 mark.
> 7. **Skipping units in the final numerical answer** — lose 0.5 mark.

---

### 5.2 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a steam trap? State its location and two types used in a marine steam plant.*

**Model Answer (verbatim points an examiner expects):**
- A steam trap is an **automatic valve** that discharges **condensate and non-condensable gases** from a steam space **without** allowing live steam to escape. **(1 mark)**
- Location: at the end of every drain pocket on the main steam line, after heat exchangers, at the bottom of steam separators, and at the end of heating coil supply lines. **(1 mark)**
- Two types: **(i) Float & Thermostatic (FT) trap** — for main line drains with heavy condensate load. **(ii) Thermodynamic (Disc) trap** — for soot-blowing lines and small-bore drains. **(1 mark)**

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is the main steam line pitched towards the boiler? Mention the recommended gradient.*

**Model Answer:**
- The main steam line is pitched **towards the boiler** (or towards drain pots) so that **condensate formed due to heat loss is drained back** and does not travel to the HP turbine. **(1.5 marks)**
- This prevents **water hammer** in the HP line, which can fracture the pipe or damage the turbine blades. **(1 mark)**
- Recommended gradient: **1 in 200 (5 mm per metre run)** as per IACS UR S14. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### 5.3 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Choice 1):**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Dec 2024 (similar)]**
> *(a)* With the aid of a neat **schematic line diagram**, describe the **piping arrangement from the boiler to the HP turbine** in a marine steam plant. Label all major valves and fittings. **(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Explain the **safety philosophy** of the arrangement, with reference to **MSSV, double isolation, drainage, and expansion compensation**. **(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Schematic diagram (3 marks for the diagram, 4 marks for the description):**

Components to be drawn in series from boiler to HP turbine:

1. **Boiler drum** (saturated steam outlet)
2. **Superheater outlet header**
3. **Main Steam Stop Valve (MSSV)** — with remote chain operation
4. **Two safety valves** in parallel (set at 1.03 × working pressure)
5. **Steam separator / moisture remover**
6. **Drain pot with float trap** at the separator
7. **Expansion U-loop** (or bellows)
8. **HP turbine admission valve** (combined stop & control valve)
9. **Gland steam leak-off line** going to gland steam condenser
10. **Auxiliary steam take-off** (after pressure reduction) — fed to the aux ring main
11. **Pressure and temperature transmitters** at the inlet to HP turbine

**Description (4 marks):**
- Live steam at **40–100 bar** and **450–540 °C** leaves the superheater and enters the MSSV, which is the first valve of isolation. The MSSV is **remotely operable from outside the boiler casing** as required by SOLAS Reg. II-1/26.
- The steam then passes through **two spring-loaded safety valves** that lift to the atmosphere (via a silenced escape pipe) if pressure exceeds the set value.
- A **steam separator** removes entrained moisture (carryover) to protect the turbine blades from erosion.
- A **drain pocket + float trap** removes any condensate that forms in the line due to heat loss; without this, water entering the HP turbine would cause **water hammer** and **blade fracture**.
- The **expansion U-loop** absorbs the **151 mm** of free expansion (for a typical 30 m run at 450 °C) without stressing the pipe anchors.
- The steam then enters the **HP turbine admission valve**, which is the emergency stop valve for the turbine and is also remotely operable from the engine control room (ECR).
- An **auxiliary steam take-off** branches off after the pressure-reducing station (typically 7 bar) to feed all auxiliary consumers.

**(b) Safety philosophy (7 marks):**

The arrangement is governed by the principle of **defence-in-depth**:

| # | Safety Element | Engineering Rationale |
|---|----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **MSSV with remote operation** | Allows isolation in case of uptake fire or uncontrolled boiler pressure rise, **without entering the boiler space** |
| 2 | **Two safety valves** | Redundancy — if one is gagged for testing, the other still protects the boiler |
| 3 | **Double isolation** (root valve + local valve) on every consumer | Allows maintenance of the consumer without depressurising the entire ring main |
| 4 | **Drainage pockets + steam traps** | Prevents water hammer — the single biggest cause of pipe rupture casualties on steam ships |
| 5 | **Expansion U-loop** | Prevents pipe buckling and flange leakage caused by thermal growth |
| 6 | **Insulation + cladding** | Personnel protection (burn injury) + thermodynamic efficiency |
| 7 | **Material certification (IACS UR S6)** | Guarantees the pressure boundary can withstand design pressure + corrosion + 200 °C safety margin |
| 8 | **Inspection access (bosses, test points)** | Allows NDT (ultrasonic, radiography) during Class surveys |

> Each row above carries **0.75 mark**; ensure at least 6 distinct points are written.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2):**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, May 2024 (similar)]**
> *(a)* A boiler delivers **20 kg/s of superheated steam at 40 bar, 450 °C** to the main steam line. If the maximum allowable steam velocity is **55 m/s** and the steam specific volume at this state is **0.0613 m³/kg**, calculate the **minimum required internal pipe diameter**. **(7 marks)*
> *(b)* For the same pipe (OD = 175 mm), determine the **minimum wall thickness** using Barlow's formula, given: design pressure = 45 bar, allowable stress = 110 MPa, joint efficiency = 1.0 (seamless), corrosion allowance = 1.5 mm. State the standard pipe schedule to be selected. **(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pipe sizing (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify continuity equation** (1 mark):
$$
\dot{m} = \rho_s A V_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_s V_s}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate density** (1 mark):
$$
\rho_s = \frac{1}{v_s} = \frac{1}{0.0613} = 16.31 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values** (2 marks):
$$
A = \frac{20}{16.31 \times 55} = \frac{20}{897.05} = 0.02230 \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for diameter** (2 marks):
$$
D_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.02230}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.02839} = 0.1685 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mm and standard size** (1 mark):
$$
\boxed{D_{min} = 168.5 \; \text{mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Select 175 mm NB (7 inch standard pipe)}}
$$

**(b) Wall thickness (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Write Barlow's formula** (1 mark):
$$
t = \frac{PD}{2SE - 1.2P} + C
$$

**Step 2 — Convert units to be consistent** (1 mark):

- $P = 45 \; \text{bar} = 4.5 \; \text{MPa}$
- $D = 175 \; \text{mm}$
- $S = 110 \; \text{MPa}$
- $E = 1.0$
- $C = 1.5 \; \text{mm}$

**Step 3 — Substitute and evaluate denominator** (2 marks):
$$
2SE - 1.2P = 2 \times 110 \times 1.0 - 1.2 \times 4.5 = 220 - 5.4 = 214.6
$$

**Step 4 — Compute thickness** (2 marks):
$$
t = \frac{4.5 \times 175}{214.6} + 1.5 = \frac{787.5}{214.6} + 1.5 = 3.670 + 1.5 = 5.17 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Standard schedule selection** (1 mark):
$$
\boxed{\,t_{required} = 5.17 \; \text{mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Select Schedule 40 (wall = 7.11 mm for 7" NB pipe)}\,}
$$

The next higher standard schedule gives a **38 % margin** over the calculated minimum — within the typical design margin of 20–50 % for HP marine steam lines. ✓

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 5-Minute Summary:**

- ☐ Steam piping transports energy from boiler to consumers — it is the **"circulatory system"** of the ship.
- ☐ The arrangement consists of: **pipes, valves, fittings, steam traps, expansion devices, insulation, supports**.
- ☐ Key design drivers: **high T/P, thermal expansion, condensate (water hammer), maintenance isolation**.
- ☐ **MSSV** is the master isolation valve; must be **remotely operable** under SOLAS.
- ☐ **Two safety valves** in parallel on every boiler (redundancy rule).
- ☐ **Pitch the line** towards the boiler at **1 in 200** for drainage.
- ☐ **Steam trap** removes condensate but not live steam — three common types: **FT, TD, Bucket**.
- ☐ **Double isolation** (root + local valve) on every consumer.
- ☐ **Barlow's formula** for wall thickness: $t = PD/(2SE - 1.2P) + C$.
- ☐ **Continuity equation** for pipe diameter: $D = \sqrt{4\dot{m}/(\pi \rho V)}$.
- ☐ **Velocity limits:** Main = 50–60 m/s, Aux = 30–40 m/s, Exhaust = 25–30 m/s.
- ☐ **Expansion loop leg length:** $h = \sqrt{3ED\Delta L/10S}$ (Kellogg).
- ☐ **Thermal expansion of carbon steel:** $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} \; /^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- ☐ Material of HP pipe: **Seamless carbon steel** (ASTM A106 Gr.B / A335).
- ☐ Joints: **Butt-weld** for HP, **flanged** at equipment, **socket-weld** for small bore.
- ☐ All piping must comply with **IACS Unified Requirements (UR S6 to S22)**.
- ☐ In the KTU answer, always draw a **schematic line diagram**, label all valves, and quote **classification society** rules.

---

> **End of Topic — Piping Arrangement for Steam**
> *Module 3 | PCNST503 — Marine Engineering | KTU 2024 Scheme*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bilge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bilge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bilge (Marine Engineering Definition):**
> In marine engineering, the **bilge** refers to the lowest internal region of a ship's hull, situated on each side of the **keel** and the **shell plating**, where water, oil, and other fluids accumulate due to leakage, condensation, wash from tanks, or seepage through hull joints. The **bilge system** is the integrated network of piping, valves, pumps, wells, and separators engineered to collect, transfer, and discharge this accumulated liquid safely overboard or to a holding tank, in compliance with the **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships (MARPOL 73/78)** and **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea)** regulations.

The complete bilge arrangement comprises:

- **Bilge wells (or bilge suction boxes)** – collection sumps fitted with strainers
- **Bilge main line** – main piping running longitudinally in the engine room
- **Bilge branches** – individual suctions from each compartment
- **Bilge pumps** – main, auxiliary, and emergency pumps
- **Oily Water Separator (OWS)** – treatment unit to reduce oil content below **15 ppm**
- **Sludge tank and bilge holding tank** – retention storage
- **Non-return valves and storm valves** – prevention of back-flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The bilge system is one of the most heavily regulated shipboard systems because it directly governs **safety at sea** (flooding prevention), **environmental compliance** (oil pollution control), and **machinery reliability** (preventing water ingress into engine rooms). Questions are frequently drawn from SOLAS Chapter II-1 regulations and MARPOL Annex I.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a multi-story building with a flat roof. During rain, water inevitably seeps into the lowest corners of every floor, creating puddles. Without a drainage system, the building would flood. The bilge is essentially the **"plumbing of the ship's lowest points"** — similar to how a building has floor drains, the ship has bilge wells in each compartment.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of your car's engine sump. Oil collects at the lowest point, and there's a drain plug to remove contaminants. The ship's bilge system works similarly, but on a much larger scale, handling potentially oily water that **cannot be discharged directly to sea** because it could pollute the ocean. Just like you wouldn't pour used engine oil down a storm drain, ships must treat their bilge water through an **Oily Water Separator** before discharging it.

The bilge also serves a **dual safety purpose**:

1. **Active role:** Pumps water out to prevent flooding.
2. **Passive role:** During dry-docking, supports the ship on a **bilge block** (a wooden or concrete support placed under the bilge keel).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key constants and standard metrics used throughout this module:**
> - Permissible oil content in discharged bilge water: **≤ 15 ppm** (MARPOL Annex I, Reg. 16)
> - Standard oil content alarm set-point: **15 ppm**
> - Typical bilge pump suction line velocity: **≤ 2 m/s**
> - Specific gravity of seawater: **1.025**
> - Specific gravity of freshwater: **1.000**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Bilge Water

Understanding *why* water accumulates is the foundation of bilge system design:

- **Condensation:** Humid air in engine rooms and tanks condenses on cooler steel surfaces.
- **Hull joint leakage:** Minor seepage through riveted or welded joints, especially older vessels.
- **Tank washings:** Residual seawater in cargo holds and ballast tanks after deballasting.
- **Machinery leakage:** Cooling water, lube oil, and fuel oil leaks from engines and pumps.
- **Flooding emergencies:** During hull breach, the bilge system becomes a critical life-saving tool.

### 2.2 Functional Requirements of a Bilge System

A properly designed bilge system must:

1. Drain water from **any compartment under any reasonable list or trim condition**.
2. Provide **independent and reliable pumping capacity** — loss of one pump must not cripple the system.
3. Prevent **cross-flooding** between compartments via non-return valves.
4. Comply with **MARPOL Annex I** by ensuring oil content before discharge is ≤ 15 ppm.
5. Operate efficiently when the vessel is **listed up to 22.5°** (heeling angle per SOLAS).

### 2.3 Components Breakdown

| Component | Function | Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Bilge well (sump)** | Collection point with strainer | Lowest point of each compartment |
| **Mud box / strainer** | Filters debris before pump suction | Just before pump suction |
| **Bilge main** | Common suction line | Engine room, fore-aft direction |
| **Branch suction** | Individual compartment connection | Each compartment to bilge main |
| **Self-priming pump** | Generates suction head | Engine room |
| **Ejector** | Uses high-pressure fluid (eductors) | Engine room |
| **OWS (Oily Water Separator)** | Reduces oil to ≤15 ppm | Engine room |
| **15 ppm bilge alarm** | Monitors effluent oil content | Discharge line of OWS |
| **Bilge holding tank** | Stores treated/ untreated bilge | Lower deck |
| **Non-return valve** | Prevents back-flow | Each branch connection |

### 2.4 SOLAS Pumping Capacity Requirements

The **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS)** Chapter II-1, Regulation 35-1, defines the bilge pumping arrangement with mathematical precision:

> A power-driven bilge pump must be capable of pumping from any one space at a rate not less than:
>
> $$Q = \frac{2}{3} \cdot D \cdot B \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/h]}$$

where:

- $D$ = moulded depth of the ship to the bulkhead deck (in **metres**)
- $B$ = breadth of the ship (in **metres**)

This formula ensures adequate pumping capacity scales with ship size. However, this calculated capacity must **not be less than** a regulatory minimum based on ship length per SOLAS tables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum number of bilge pumps per SOLAS:**
> - Ships ≥ 2000 GT: **three independent power bilge pumps** (one may be main engine-driven)
> - Smaller vessels: scaled-down requirements per SOLAS tables.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for the Bilge topic. Memorize these formulas — they appear frequently in Part A and Part B questions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot D \cdot B$ | Minimum bilge pump capacity per SOLAS Reg. II-1/35-1 | m³/h |
| 2 | $Q \geq 4 \cdot L_{wl}$ (smaller vessels) | Alt. SOLAS table-based capacity | m³/h |
| 3 | $A = \dfrac{Q}{v}$ | Cross-section area of bilge pipe ($Q$ = flow, $v$ = velocity) | m² |
| 4 | $d = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q}{\pi v}}$ | Internal diameter of bilge pipe | m |
| 5 | $v_{max} \leq 2$ m/s | Recommended bilge pipe suction velocity | m/s |
| 6 | $P_{discharge} = \rho g H$ | Pressure at pump discharge head | Pa |
| 7 | $\eta_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{P_{input}}$ | Hydraulic efficiency of bilge pump | dimensionless |
| 8 | $h_{f} = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{d} \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | Darcy-Weisbach frictional head loss in pipe | m |
| 9 | $V_{tank} = Q \cdot t$ | Bilge holding tank volume (Q = inflow, t = retention time) | m³ |
| 10 | $\phi_{oil} \leq 15$ ppm | MARPOL effluent oil concentration limit | mg/L |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** When calculating bilge pipe diameter, students often use the discharge velocity ($v \approx 3$ m/s) instead of the **suction velocity ($v \leq 2$ m/s)**. Bilge suctions are **suction-side calculations**, so always use $v \approx 1.5$ to $2$ m/s.

### 2.6 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

Bilge systems are **safety-critical** on every merchant vessel — bulk carriers, tankers, container ships, and passenger vessels. Bilge failures have caused:

- **Catastrophic capsize** (e.g., vessels losing stability due to free surface effect of un-pumped water)
- **Major oil spills** leading to multi-million-dollar penalties under MARPOL
- **Engine room flooding** resulting in total propulsion loss

A marine engineer must therefore understand both the **hydraulic design** (sizing pipes and pumps) and the **regulatory framework** (SOLAS + MARPOL + Class rules).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: SOLAS Bilge Pump Capacity Formula

The SOLAS regulation provides a formula relating bilge pump capacity to ship principal dimensions. Let's derive and apply it step-by-step.

**Given data for a sample vessel:**
- Moulded depth to bulkhead deck: $D = 18$ m
- Breadth of ship: $B = 28$ m
- Length of ship: $L = 180$ m

**Step 1: Apply the SOLAS formula**

The minimum pumping rate per SOLAS Reg. II-1/35-1 is:

$$
Q_{min} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot D \cdot B
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the numerical values**

$$
Q_{min} = \frac{2}{3} \times 18 \times 28
$$

We multiply $18 \times 28$ first:

$$
18 \times 28 = 504
$$

Then divide by 3:

$$
\frac{504}{3} = 168
$$

Multiply by 2:

$$
2 \times 168 = 336
$$

**Step 3: Final answer**

$$
Q_{min} = 336 \ \text{m}^3/\text{h}
$$

This is the minimum capacity per pump when pumping from any single compartment. Since SOLAS also requires multiple independent pumps, the **total installed capacity** is usually $3 \times 336 = 1008$ m³/h, but the regulation speaks to per-pump minimum.

### 3.2 Derivation: Sizing a Bilge Suction Pipe

**Given:**
- Flow rate to be carried: $Q = 336$ m³/h (from previous calculation)
- Maximum allowed suction velocity: $v = 2$ m/s (per marine practice)
- Fluid: seawater at SG = **1.025**

**Step 1: Convert flow rate to SI units (m³/s)**

$$
Q_{SI} = \frac{336}{3600}
$$

Performing the division:

$$
336 \div 3600 = 0.09333 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the continuity equation**

The volumetric flow through a pipe of internal diameter $d$ at velocity $v$ is:

$$
Q = A \cdot v
$$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional area. For a circular pipe:

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

Substituting:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v
$$

**Step 3: Solve for diameter $d$**

Rearranging:

$$
d^2 = \frac{4Q}{\pi v}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi v}}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute values**

$$
d = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.09333}{\pi \times 2}}
$$

Computing numerator:

$$
4 \times 0.09333 = 0.37333
$$

Computing denominator:

$$
\pi \times 2 = 6.2832
$$

Division:

$$
\frac{0.37333}{6.2832} = 0.05942
$$

Square root:

$$
d = \sqrt{0.05942} = 0.2438 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to millimetres (practical pipe sizing)**

$$
d = 0.2438 \times 1000 = 243.8 \ \text{mm}
$$

Selecting the **next standard pipe size**: $d \approx 250$ mm (or 10 inches).

**Step 6: Calculate the Reynolds number for verification**

$$
Re = \frac{v d}{\nu}
$$

Using kinematic viscosity of seawater $\nu \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s:

$$
Re = \frac{2 \times 0.2438}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
Re = \frac{0.4876}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.64 \times 10^{5}
$$

This is fully turbulent flow, confirming the velocity selection is reasonable.

### 3.3 Derivation: Head Loss in Bilge Suction Line

To size the bilge pump motor, we need total dynamic head (TDH):

$$
TDH = H_{static} + h_{f,suction} + h_{f,discharge} + h_{fittings}
$$

**Given:**
- Vertical suction lift: $H_{static} = 4$ m
- Pipe length suction: $L_{s} = 25$ m
- Pipe length discharge: $L_{d} = 35$ m
- Diameter: $d = 0.25$ m
- Velocity: $v = 2$ m/s
- Darcy friction factor (turbulent steel pipe): $f = 0.02$

**Step 1: Friction loss in suction line**

$$
h_{f,s} = f \cdot \frac{L_s}{d} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g}
$$

Substituting:

$$
h_{f,s} = 0.02 \cdot \frac{25}{0.25} \cdot \frac{2^2}{2 \times 9.81}
$$

Calculating the ratio $L/d$:

$$
\frac{25}{0.25} = 100
$$

Calculating the velocity-head term:

$$
\frac{v^2}{2g} = \frac{4}{19.62} = 0.2039 \ \text{m}
$$

Final multiplication:

$$
h_{f,s} = 0.02 \times 100 \times 0.2039 = 0.4078 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 2: Friction loss in discharge line**

$$
h_{f,d} = 0.02 \cdot \frac{35}{0.25} \cdot 0.2039
$$

$$
\frac{35}{0.25} = 140
$$

$$
h_{f,d} = 0.02 \times 140 \times 0.2039 = 0.5709 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3: Fittings allowance (standard 30% margin)**

$$
h_{fittings} = 0.30 \times (h_{f,s} + h_{f,d})
$$

$$
h_{fittings} = 0.30 \times (0.4078 + 0.5709) = 0.30 \times 0.9787 = 0.2936 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 4: Total Dynamic Head**

$$
TDH = 4 + 0.4078 + 0.5709 + 0.2936
$$

$$
TDH = 5.272 \ \text{m of seawater}
$$

**Step 5: Required pump power**

$$
P = \frac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{pump}}
$$

Using $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $Q = 0.09333$ m³/s, $H = 5.272$ m, $\eta_{pump} = 0.65$:

$$
P = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.09333 \times 5.272}{0.65}
$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$
1025 \times 9.81 = 10055.25
$$

$$
10055.25 \times 0.09333 = 938.45
$$

$$
938.45 \times 5.272 = 4947.85
$$

$$
P = \frac{4947.85}{0.65} = 7612 \ \text{W}
$$

$$
P \approx 7.6 \ \text{kW}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pump Type Selection

| Parameter | Centrifugal | Positive Displacement (PD) | Eductor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Best for** | Large flow, low viscosity | Sludge, viscous fluids | Stripping residual bilge |
| **Priming** | Needs priming system | Self-priming | No priming needed |
| **Head capability** | Limited (low head) | High head | Very low head |
| **Solids handling** | Poor | Excellent | Limited |
| **Efficiency** | High at design point | Moderate | Low (~20–30%) |
| **Typical duty** | Main bilge pumping | Sludge transfer | Final stripping |

### 3.5 Oily Water Separator (OWS) — Working Principle

The OWS uses **gravity separation** combined with **coalescer filters**:

**Stage 1 — Gravity separator:** Oil droplets rise due to density difference. Rising velocity follows **Stokes' Law**:

$$
v_{rise} = \frac{g \cdot d^2 \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_o)}{18 \mu}
$$

where:
- $d$ = droplet diameter
- $\rho_w$ = water density (≈ 1025 kg/m³)
- $\rho_o$ = oil density (≈ 900 kg/m³)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of water

**Stage 2 — Coalescer:** Small droplets merge into larger ones for easier separation.

**Stage 3 — 15 ppm monitor:** Optical or capacitive sensor continuously samples effluent. If oil > 15 ppm, **3-way valve diverts flow back to holding tank**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bilge System Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Compartment A Bilge Well] --> B1[Bilge Branch Valve]
    A2[Compartment B Bilge Well] --> B2[Bilge Branch Valve]
    A3[Engine Room Bilge Well] --> B3[Bilge Branch Valve]
    B1 --> C[Bilge Main Line]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Mud Box Strainer]
    D --> E[Main Bilge Pump]
    E --> F[Oily Water Separator OWS]
    F --> G{15 ppm Monitor}
    G -- Below 15 ppm --> H[Overboard Discharge]
    G -- Above 15 ppm --> I[Bilge Holding Tank]
    I --> J[Sludge Disposal Port]
    E --> K[Eductor Driven by Cooling Water]
    K --> C
```

### 4.2 OWS Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Oily Bilge Inlet] --> P2[Coarse Strainer]
    P2 --> P3[Gravity Separator Stage 1]
    P3 --> P4[Coalescer Module]
    P4 --> P5[Second Stage Separator]
    P5 --> P6[15 ppm Oil Content Sensor]
    P6 --> P7{Concentration Decision}
    P7 -- Less than 15 ppm --> P8[Discharge Overboard]
    P7 -- Greater than 15 ppm --> P9[Return to Holding Tank]
    P9 --> P3
```

### 4.3 SOLAS Pump Capacity Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[Calculate Q using 2 over 3 D times B] --> Q1{Is Q greater than SOLAS Table Minimum}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[Use Calculated Q]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Use SOLAS Table Minimum]
    Q2 --> Q4[Check Number of Pumps]
    Q3 --> Q4
    Q4 --> Q5{Is GT greater than 2000}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Require 3 independent pumps]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[Apply SOLAS Table for smaller vessels]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bilge System Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CollectionTier
        S1[Bilge Well Fore Peak]
        S2[Bilge Well Cargo Hold 1]
        S3[Bilge Well Cargo Hold 2]
        S4[Bilge Well Engine Room]
        S5[Bilge Well Aft Peak]
    end
    subgraph TransferTier
        T1[Bilge Branch Line]
        T2[Bilge Main Line]
        T3[Cross Connection]
    end
    subgraph TreatmentTier
        R1[Mud Box]
        R2[Main Bilge Pump]
        R3[Oily Water Separator]
        R4[15 ppm Alarm]
    end
    subgraph DischargeTier
        D1[Overboard Discharge]
        D2[Holding Tank]
        D3[Sludge Port Reception]
    end
    S1 --> T1
    S2 --> T1
    S3 --> T1
    S4 --> T1
    S5 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> D1
    R4 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define the term "bilge" as used in marine engineering. List any four components of a shipboard bilge system.**

**Model Answer:**

> In marine engineering, the **bilge** is the lowest internal section of a ship's hull on either side of the keel, where water and oil accumulate from various sources such as condensation, hull leakage, and machinery drippings.

**Four components of the bilge system:**

1. **Bilge wells (sumps)** with strainers — collection points
2. **Bilge main line** — common suction piping
3. **Bilge pumps** — main and emergency (centrifugal/PD/eductor)
4. **Oily Water Separator (OWS)** with 15 ppm monitor
5. *(Any other valid: non-return valves, storm valves, holding tank)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**State the MARPOL limit for oil content in bilge water discharge and explain the function of the 15 ppm bilge alarm.**

**Model Answer:**

> As per **MARPOL Annex I, Regulation 16**, the oil content in bilge water discharged overboard must not exceed **15 ppm** (parts per million, equivalent to 15 mg/L).

**Function of the 15 ppm bilge alarm:**

- Continuously samples the OWS effluent using an optical or capacitive sensor.
- If oil concentration exceeds 15 ppm, the alarm **automatically diverts** the discharge flow back to the bilge holding tank via a 3-way valve.
- Prevents illegal discharge of oily bilge and protects the marine environment.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

> **(a)** Derive the SOLAS minimum bilge pumping capacity formula. Explain how the formula is applied to determine pump capacity for a vessel with moulded depth $D = 16$ m and breadth $B = 24$ m. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the working principle of an Oily Water Separator (OWS). Mention the role of the 15 ppm monitor. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation and Application:**

The SOLAS Regulation II-1/35-1 prescribes the minimum bilge pumping capacity based on ship principal dimensions to ensure that any one flooded compartment can be emptied within a reasonable time.

The formula is:

$$
Q_{min} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot D \cdot B
$$

where:
- $D$ = moulded depth (m)
- $B$ = moulded breadth (m)
- $Q_{min}$ = minimum pumping rate per pump (m³/h)

**Application for $D = 16$ m, $B = 24$ m:**

$$
Q_{min} = \frac{2}{3} \times 16 \times 24
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
16 \times 24 = 384
$$

$$
\frac{384}{3} = 128
$$

$$
2 \times 128 = 256
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{min} = 256 \ \text{m}^3/\text{h}}
$$

This is the minimum capacity each bilge pump must deliver when pumping from any single compartment. The total installed capacity will be $3 \times 256 = 768$ m³/h for vessels ≥ 2000 GT.

**(b) OWS Working Principle:**

```
Bilge Inlet → Coarse Strainer → Stage 1 Gravity Separator
   → Coalescer Module → Stage 2 Separator
   → 15 ppm Monitor → Overboard OR Holding Tank (divert)
```

- **Stage 1:** Crude separation using gravity; oil rises due to density difference (oil lighter than water).
- **Coalescer:** Small oil droplets merge into larger ones on coalescing plates/fibres.
- **Stage 2:** Final polishing to reduce residual oil.
- **15 ppm Monitor:** Continuously measures oil content; if > 15 ppm, signals 3-way valve to divert flow back to holding tank.

**Block diagram reference:** See SECTION 4.2.

---

**Question B [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> **(a)** A vessel has the following data: $Q = 200$ m³/h bilge flow, maximum suction velocity $v = 2$ m/s. Calculate the required internal diameter of the bilge suction pipe. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain with a sketch the typical layout of a ship's bilge system showing wells, mains, pumps, and OWS. Discuss briefly how non-return valves prevent cross-flooding. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pipe Diameter Calculation:**

Given:
- $Q = 200$ m³/h
- $v = 2$ m/s

**Step 1 — Convert flow rate:**

$$
Q_{SI} = \frac{200}{3600} = 0.05556 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply continuity equation:**

$$
Q = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi v}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
d = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.05556}{\pi \times 2}}
$$

Numerator: $4 \times 0.05556 = 0.22222$

Denominator: $\pi \times 2 = 6.2832$

Division:

$$
\frac{0.22222}{6.2832} = 0.03537
$$

Square root:

$$
d = \sqrt{0.03537} = 0.1881 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{d \approx 188 \ \text{mm}}
$$

Select next standard size: **200 mm (≈ 8 inches)**.

**(b) Layout and Non-Return Valves:**

The bilge system layout consists of:

1. **Bilge wells** in each compartment (lowest points) fitted with **mud boxes/strainers**.
2. **Branch suction lines** with **screw-down non-return valves** connecting each compartment to the bilge main.
3. **Bilge main line** running longitudinally through the engine room.
4. **Main bilge pump** + **auxiliary pump** + **eductor**.
5. **Oily Water Separator** with 15 ppm monitor.
6. **Discharge overboard** via sea valve or to **holding tank**.

**Role of non-return valves (NRVs):**

- Each branch line is fitted with an NRV that allows flow **only towards the pump**, not back to the compartment.
- In a flooding scenario, if one compartment is breached, the NRVs in *other* branches prevent water from flowing from the flooded compartment into adjacent dry compartments via the bilge main.
- This is critical to **prevent cross-flooding** and loss of stability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong velocity:** Using discharge velocity ($v = 3$ m/s) instead of suction ($v \leq 2$ m/s) in pipe sizing — costs ~2 marks.
> 2. **Missing conversion:** Forgetting to convert m³/h → m³/s before applying the continuity equation — costs ~1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting NRV explanation:** In Part (b), many students draw the layout but forget to explain *why* non-return valves are essential — costs ~2 marks.
> 4. **MARPOL confusion:** Confusing the 15 ppm limit with total oil volume or tank capacity — costs ~1 mark.
> 5. **SOLAS formula units:** Writing $D \cdot B$ without specifying they are in **metres** — costs ~1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bilge Module**

- **Bilge** = lowest internal region of the hull + entire drainage system.
- **Three independent pumps** required for ships ≥ 2000 GT per SOLAS.
- **SOLAS capacity formula:** $Q_{min} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot D \cdot B$ (m³/h, D & B in metres).
- **Suction velocity** must be ≤ 2 m/s (to avoid cavitation in centrifugal pumps).
- **Pipe sizing formula:** $d = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q}{\pi v}}$ — remember Q in m³/s.
- **MARPOL Annex I Reg. 16:** oil discharge limit = **15 ppm**.
- **15 ppm alarm** is mandatory on all OWS fitted to ships > 400 GT.
- **Non-return valves** on every bilge branch prevent cross-flooding.
- **Bilge wells** must be located to drain the compartment when listed up to **22.5°**.
- **OWS stages:** strainer → gravity separator → coalescer → monitor.
- **Eductors** use high-pressure seawater or cooling water to create vacuum — no priming needed.
- **Bilge keels** (external plates on hull sides) reduce rolling — *passive* bilge feature, not part of drainage.
- **Power calculation:** $P = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta}$ — remember to divide by pump efficiency (≈ 0.60–0.70).
- **Free surface effect:** Un-pumped bilge water reduces ship stability — pumping must be regular.
- **Holding tank** is mandatory for vessels that cannot discharge at sea.
- **Class society rules** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, IRS) supplement SOLAS with additional construction details.
- **Emergency bilge suction** from the main engine-driven pump is permitted as one of the three required pumps.
- **Cross-flooding prevention** is the *single most important safety function* of NRVs.
- **Pump types:** centrifugal (high flow, low head), positive displacement (sludge), eductor (final stripping).
- **Sludge** from OWS is discharged ashore at port reception facilities — never at sea.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ballast and Oil Fuel Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ballast and Oil Fuel Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ballast System — Formal Definition

The **ballast system** on a marine vessel is an integrated network of tanks, pipelines, pumps, valves, and control mechanisms designed to take in, transfer, hold, and discharge seawater (ballast water) in order to control the vessel's **draft, trim, list, stability, and structural stress distribution** during various operational conditions.

According to the **International Convention on Ballast Water Management (BWM Convention) – IMO Resolution A.868(20)** and the **MARPOL 73/78 Convention**, the ballast system is a mandatory auxiliary system fitted on virtually all seagoing ships to ensure safe navigation when cargo is partially or fully unloaded.

### Key Functions of the Ballast System

| Function | Engineering Purpose |
|----------|--------------------|
| **Draft Control** | Maintain sufficient propeller and rudder immersion |
| **Trim Correction** | Adjust fore-aft inclination for optimal hull efficiency |
| **Stability** | Lower the metacenter / shift G to maintain GM ≥ 0.15 m |
| **Stress Distribution** | Equalize hogging and sagging bending moments along hull girder |
| **Counter-flooding** | Emergency stability recovery after damage (SOLAS requirement) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The ballast system is examined in conjunction with the **SOLAS Chapter II-1** regulations and the **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL 1966)**. Students must understand the relationship between **displacement (Δ)**, **draft (T)**, and **ballast quantity (W_b)**.

## 1.2 Oil Fuel System — Formal Definition

The **oil fuel system** (or marine fuel oil system) is the complete arrangement of storage tanks, transfer pumps, purifiers, heaters, viscosity controllers, mixing tanks, supply pumps, and associated safety devices that deliver **fuel oil of specified quality, temperature, and viscosity** to the main propulsion engine, auxiliary engines, and auxiliary boilers.

This system must comply with **SOLAS Chapter II-2**, **MARPOL Annex VI** (air pollution), **ISO 8217:2017** (fuel standard), and classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Fuel Grades (ISO 8217:2017):**
> - **HFO (Heavy Fuel Oil)** – Residual grades: RMG 180, RMG 380, RMG 500
> - **MGO/MDO (Marine Gas Oil / Marine Diesel Oil)** – Distillate grades: DMA, DMB, DMZ
> - **ULSFO (Ultra-Low Sulphur Fuel Oil)** – 0.10% S (ECA compliant)
> - **VLSFO (Very Low Sulphur Fuel Oil)** – 0.50% S (post-2020 global cap)

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

### 🎈 Ballast System Analogy
Imagine riding a bicycle with a heavy backpack. When you remove the backpack (unload cargo), the bicycle becomes unstable and the front wheel lifts. To keep balance, you would add small weights in a saddlebag near the bottom — the **lower you place the weight, the more stable** you become. A ship experiences the exact same phenomenon. When cargo is discharged at port, the ship must "fill its belly" with seawater in dedicated **double bottom** and **side ballast tanks** to stay stable and submerged to a safe draft.

### ⛽ Oil Fuel System Analogy
Think of the fuel system as a hospital's blood circulation network. Fuel is "blood" stored in reservoirs (settling/service tanks), filtered by kidneys (purifiers and filters), heated for proper viscosity (like warming IV fluid), and pumped at controlled pressure to "vital organs" (engines and boilers). The heart of this system is the **fuel oil supply pump** and the brain is the **viscosity controller**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Ballast Tank Types: "DBS WPT"**
> - **D**ouble Bottom (DB)
> - **B**ilge (used for emergency)
> - **S**ide (Wing)
> - **W**ing (alternative term for side)
> - **P**eak (Fore/Aft Peak)
> - **T**opside (Upper wing or hopper)

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Design Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|--------|--------|---------------|------|
| Minimum safe forward draft | T_F | **0.02 × L** + 0.04 | m |
| Minimum safe aft draft | T_A | ≥ propeller diameter + 0.04 m | m |
| Metacentric height (GM) | GM | ≥ **0.15 m** (intact), ≥ 0.05 m (damaged) | m |
| Fuel oil supply pressure | P_s | **4–6 bar** | bar |
| Fuel oil supply temperature | T_s | **135–150 °C** (HFO) | °C |
| Fuel oil supply viscosity | ν | **10–14 cSt** at 100 °C | cSt |
| Centrifuge bowl speed | N | **6,000–9,000 rpm** | rpm |
| Seawater ballast pump head | H | **15–30 m** | m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ballast System — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Fundamental Principle: Archimedes' Law Applied to Floating Bodies

The ship floats in equilibrium when:

$$\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla = W_{total}$$

where:
- $\nabla$ = displaced volume (m³)
- $\rho_{sw}$ = density of seawater (**1025 kg/m³** standard, **1000 kg/m³** fresh)
- $W_{total}$ = total weight of ship + cargo + fuel + ballast (N)

When cargo is discharged, $W_{total}$ decreases, so $\nabla$ must decrease, which means the ship **rises out of the water**. To maintain a safe draft, we add ballast water of weight $W_b$:

$$W_{total,new} = W_{ship+lighter+cargo} + W_{ballast}$$

### 2.1.2 Ballast System Operational Sequence

1. **Ballasting (Loading)** — Open sea or designated area
   - Open sea overboard inlet valve
   - Start ballast pump
   - Fill designated tanks sequentially
   - Monitor draft gauges and stress monitoring system

2. **Ballast Water Exchange** (BWE)
   - **Sequential method** — empty then fill one tank at a time
   - **Flow-through method** — pump in ≥ 200% tank volume
   - **Dilution method** — continuous overtopping

3. **Deballasting (Discharge)**
   - Discharge to port reception facility (PRF) per **BWM Convention**
   - Treat via **Ballast Water Treatment System (BWTS)** if fitted

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **D-2 Standard (BWM Convention):** Vessels must discharge ballast with < **10 viable organisms per m³** for organisms ≥ 50 μm, and < **10 viable organisms per mL** for organisms < 50 μm and ≥ 10 μm.

### 2.1.3 Ballast Tank Arrangement

**Common Tank Locations (refer to SECTION 4 diagram):**
- **Double Bottom Tanks (DBT)** — provide transverse stability
- **Wing/Side Ballast Tanks** — provide transverse stability and roll damping
- **Fore Peak Tank (FPT)** — adjusted for trim by bow
- **Aft Peak Tank (APT)** — adjusted for trim by stern
- **Topside Tanks** — used when double bottom is full
- **Hopper Tanks** — bulk carrier specific (lower hold corners)

### 2.1.4 Stress Management

Loading patterns must control **shear forces and bending moments** along the hull girder. The **still water bending moment (SWBM)** must be kept within allowable limits defined by classification societies.

> [!WARNING]
> **Hogging and Sagging:**
> - **Hogging** — U-shaped bending (bow & stern lower than midship). Caused by heavy ends (e.g., engine aft + heavy bow ballast). Must keep SWBM ≤ M_s,allowable.
> - **Sagging** — Inverted U (midship lower than ends). Caused by heavy middle cargo (bulk carriers in alternate hold loading).

## 2.2 Oil Fuel System — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 System Layout & Flow Path

The fuel system operates in **three distinct circuits:**

**Circuit 1 — Transfer Circuit (Low Pressure, Long Distance)**
- Storage tanks → Transfer pump → Settling tank
- Pressure: 3–5 bar
- Flow: gravity + pump assisted

**Circuit 2 — Purification Circuit (Medium Pressure)**
- Settling tank → Purifier/Clarifier → Service/Buffer tank
- Centrifuge separation of water and solids
- Heating to **85–95 °C** to reduce viscosity for separation

**Circuit 3 — Supply Circuit (High Pressure, High Temperature)**
- Service tank → Supply pump → Heater (steam) → Viscosity controller → Engine
- Final viscosity: **10–14 cSt** (HFO) or 2–6 cSt (diesel)

### 2.2.2 Viscosity-Temperature Relationship

For HFO, the relationship is governed by the **ASTM viscosity-temperature equation (D341)**:

$$\log \log (\nu + 0.7) = A - B \cdot \log T$$

where:
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity (cSt)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (Kelvin)
- $A, B$ = oil-specific constants

For simplified manual calculations in the engine room, a **linear approximation** is often used:

$$\frac{\nu_1}{\nu_2} = e^{\left( \frac{E}{R} \right) \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)}$$

where:
- $E$ = activation energy for viscous flow (typical HFO: **15,000–25,000 J/mol**)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**

### 2.2.3 Purifier Theory

A **disc-stack centrifugal separator** works on the principle of sedimentation under high centripetal acceleration:

$$a = \omega^2 r = \frac{\pi^2 N^2}{900} \cdot r$$

The equivalent settling area of a centrifuge is:

$$\Sigma = \frac{2\pi \omega^2 (r_2^3 - r_1^3) \cdot Z}{3g}$$

where:
- $Z$ = number of discs
- $r_1, r_2$ = inner and outer disc radii
- $N$ = rotational speed (rpm)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)

The **separation efficiency** is given by the **cut size** $d_c$:

$$d_c = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu Q}{\Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot \Sigma}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Higher Σ = better separation.** A purifier with Σ = 50,000 m² can replace a settling tank of 50,000 m² plan area — that's why one small centrifuge replaces huge gravity settling tanks.

### 2.2.4 Fuel Oil Heating Stages

| Stage | Location | Temperature | Purpose |
|-------|----------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | Storage tank | 30–45 °C | Pump-ability |
| 2 | Pre-heater (before purifier) | 85–95 °C | Separation viscosity (~25 cSt) |
| 3 | Final heater | 135–150 °C | Injection viscosity (10–14 cSt) |
| 4 | Cooler (for MGO only) | 30–40 °C | Engine protection |

### 2.2.5 Safety Devices

- **High-temperature alarm** — at outlet of final heater (~160 °C trip)
- **Low-pressure alarm** — supply line (~3 bar)
- **High-pressure alarm** — relief valve setting (~10 bar)
- **Viscosity controller failure alarm** — backup steam tracing
- **Oil mist detector** — in crankcase (L.O. system tie-in)
- **Quick-closing valves** — per SOLAS Reg. II-2/4.2.2

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Application | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | Displacement | $\Delta$ (t), $\rho$ (t/m³), $\nabla$ (m³) |
| 2 | $T_{mean} = \frac{T_F + T_A}{2}$ | Mean draft | $T$ (m) |
| 3 | $Trim = T_A - T_F$ | Fore-aft inclination | m (positive = trim by stern) |
| 4 | $W_b = \Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{light}$ | Required ballast weight | t |
| 5 | $V_b = \frac{W_b}{\rho_{sw}}$ | Required ballast volume | m³ |
| 6 | $GM = KM - KG$ | Metacentric height | m |
| 7 | $M_{hogging} = \frac{W \cdot L}{32}$ (uniform) | Bending moment | kN·m |
| 8 | $P_{pump} = \rho g Q H$ | Pump power input | W |
| 9 | $\eta_{pump} = \frac{\rho g Q H}{P_{shaft}}$ | Pump efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\nu = \nu_0 \cdot e^{E/R \cdot (1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | Viscosity-temperature | cSt, K |
| 11 | $\Sigma = \frac{2\pi \omega^2 (r_2^3 - r_1^3) Z}{3g}$ | Centrifuge equiv. area | m² |
| 12 | $d_c = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu Q}{\Delta \rho g \Sigma}}$ | Cut size (separation) | m |
| 13 | $F = \rho Q V_{rel}$ | Momentum flux in pumps | N |
| 14 | $P_{heater} = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ | Heater capacity | kW |
| 15 | $Q_{pump} = \frac{V_{tank}}{t_{fill}}$ | Pump flow rate | m³/s |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| System | Real-World Application |
|--------|------------------------|
| Ballast | Cross-channel ferries adjust ballast every 1–2 hours |
| Ballast | VLCCs may carry 30–40% of DWT as ballast |
| Ballast | LNG carriers use ballast for partial filling during cool-down |
| Fuel | Two-stroke MAN B&W engines require HFO at 130 °C, 5 bar |
| Fuel | ECA zones (Baltic, North Sea, North American) demand 0.10% S fuel |
| Fuel | Hybrid scrubbers (open/closed/hybrid) consume fuel to run |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CO Mapping (KTU 2024):** This topic addresses **CO3 — Describe shipboard auxiliary systems** at Bloom's levels **Remember, Understand, Apply**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ballast Volume Required to Maintain Draft

**Problem Context:** A bulk carrier of deadweight 50,000 tonnes sails from a fully loaded departure condition (mean draft 12.0 m, seawater density 1.025 t/m³) to a port where it must discharge all cargo. The lightship weight is 12,000 t. To maintain a safe minimum draft of 8.0 m at the port, find:
- (a) Light displacement
- (b) Total ballast weight needed
- (c) Volume of ballast water

### Step 1: Identify Light Displacement

Light displacement = Light ship weight + Constant (lub oil, fresh water, crew, stores, etc.)

Assume constant = 800 t

$$W_{light,ship} = 12{,}000 + 800 = 12{,}800 \text{ t}$$

### Step 2: Loaded Displacement at Departure

$$W_{loaded} = W_{light} + W_{cargo} + W_{fuel} + W_{freshwater} + W_{stores}$$

Given loaded mean draft = 12.0 m, from hydrostatic data the displacement is **62,000 t** (from ship's hydrostatic curves).

### Step 3: Hydrostatic Relationship (Per Ton of Draft Change)

From the **Deadweight / Draft Curve** at T = 8.0 m:
- Displacement at 8.0 m = **30,000 t** (read from hydrostatic table)
- Therefore, lightship + constant ≈ 30,000 − $W_{ballast}$

### Step 4: Solve for Ballast Weight

At the port in ballast condition:

$$W_{port} = W_{light,ship} + W_{ballast}$$

$$W_{port} = 30{,}000 \text{ t}$$

$$W_{ballast} = 30{,}000 - 12{,}800 = 17{,}200 \text{ t}$$

### Step 5: Convert to Ballast Volume

$$V_{ballast} = \frac{W_{ballast}}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{17{,}200}{1.025} = 16{,}780.5 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 6: Cross-Check with Pump Time

If ballast pump capacity = 1,500 m³/h:

$$t_{ballast} = \frac{16{,}780.5}{1{,}500} = 11.19 \text{ hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **directly testable** in KTU 2024 numerical problems. Students often confuse **displacement (Δ)** with **deadweight (DWT)** — remember: Δ = W_total, and DWT = W_total − W_lightship.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Centrifuge Σ-Number and Separation Cut Size

**Problem Context:** A disc-stack purifier has the following specifications:
- Rotational speed N = 7,500 rpm
- Outer disc radius r₂ = 0.1075 m
- Inner disc radius r₁ = 0.0400 m
- Number of discs Z = 145
- Throughput Q = 3.33 × 10⁻³ m³/s
- Viscosity of oil at separation temperature μ = 0.0025 Pa·s
- Density difference Δρ = 75 kg/m³

Find: (a) Σ-number, (b) Cut size d_c

### Step 1: Calculate Angular Velocity

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 7{,}500}{60} = 785.4 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Σ-Number

$$\Sigma = \frac{2\pi \omega^2 (r_2^3 - r_1^3) Z}{3g}$$

Compute intermediate values:

$$r_2^3 = (0.1075)^3 = 1.2424 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$r_1^3 = (0.0400)^3 = 6.400 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3$$

$$r_2^3 - r_1^3 = 1.2424 \times 10^{-3} - 0.0640 \times 10^{-3} = 1.1784 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$\omega^2 = (785.4)^2 = 6.169 \times 10^5 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\Sigma = \frac{2\pi \times 6.169 \times 10^5 \times 1.1784 \times 10^{-3} \times 145}{3 \times 9.81}$$

$$\Sigma = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 6.169 \times 10^5 \times 1.1784 \times 10^{-3} \times 145}{29.43}$$

Compute numerator:

$$2 \times 3.1416 = 6.2832$$

$$6.2832 \times 6.169 \times 10^5 = 3.876 \times 10^6$$

$$3.876 \times 10^6 \times 1.1784 \times 10^{-3} = 4.567 \times 10^3$$

$$4.567 \times 10^3 \times 145 = 6.622 \times 10^5$$

$$\Sigma = \frac{6.622 \times 10^5}{29.43} = 22{,}502 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 3: Calculate Cut Size

$$d_c = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu Q}{\Delta\rho \cdot g \cdot \Sigma}}$$

$$d_c = \sqrt{\frac{18 \times 0.0025 \times 3.33 \times 10^{-3}}{75 \times 9.81 \times 22{,}502}}$$

Numerator:

$$18 \times 0.0025 = 0.045$$

$$0.045 \times 3.33 \times 10^{-3} = 1.4985 \times 10^{-4}$$

Denominator:

$$75 \times 9.81 = 735.75$$

$$735.75 \times 22{,}502 = 1.6559 \times 10^7$$

$$d_c = \sqrt{\frac{1.4985 \times 10^{-4}}{1.6559 \times 10^7}} = \sqrt{9.049 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$d_c = 3.008 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \approx 3 \text{ μm}$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

A **3 μm cut size** means particles ≥ 3 μm will be removed with >50% probability. This is sufficient for marine HFO purification (typically target 5–15 μm cut).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Ballast System Decision Table

| Ship Condition | Required Action | Tank Priority | Risk if Ignored |
|----------------|-----------------|---------------|------------------|
| Fully loaded, sailing | Deballast if any residual | DBT first | Excessive stress |
| Partially loaded, sailing | Adjust fore-aft trim | FPT/APT | Propeller emergence |
| In ballast, sailing | Maintain stability | DBT + Side | Capsize risk (low GM) |
| Loading cargo | Deballast progressively | Match load sequence | Hull damage, slamming |
| Damage, flooding | Counter-flood | Symmetric side | Loss of stability |
| Heavy weather | Sloshing management | Partially fill | Free surface effect |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Fuel System Decision Table

| Operating Mode | Fuel Type | Heater ON? | Purifier Status | Viscosity Setpoint |
|----------------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Main engine – Maneuvering | MGO | No | Bypass | 6 cSt @ 40 °C |
| Main engine – Sea passage | HFO | Yes (final) | Running | 12 cSt @ 130 °C |
| Aux. engine – Harbor | MGO | No | Bypass | 4 cSt @ 40 °C |
| Aux. engine – Sea | HFO (or MGO) | Yes | Running | 12 cSt @ 130 °C |
| Boiler – Cold start | MGO | No | N/A | Atomization OK |
| ECA zone | ULSFO / MGO | Yes (mild) | Running | 10 cSt @ 100 °C |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ballast System — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Vessel Arrives Light or Partially Loaded]) --> Decision{Safe Draft?}
    Decision -- No --> FillDBT[Fill Double Bottom Tanks First]
    FillDBT --> Check1{DBT Full?}
    Check1 -- No --> PumpOp[Run Ballast Pump]
    PumpOp --> SeaInlet[Open Sea Inlet Valve]
    SeaInlet --> FillDBT
    Check1 -- Yes --> FillSide[Fill Wing/Side Ballast Tanks]
    FillSide --> Check2{Side Tanks Full?}
    Check2 -- No --> FillSide
    Check2 -- Yes --> FillTop[Fill Topside Tanks]
    FillTop --> Check3{Desired Draft Achieved?}
    Check3 -- No --> FillTop
    Check3 -- Yes --> StopPump[Stop Pump, Close Sea Inlet]
    StopPump --> Record1[Log in Ballast Water Record Book]
    Record1 --> Decision
    Decision -- Yes --> Deballast[Proceed with Operations]

    subgraph TreatmentPath[Ballast Water Treatment Route]
        T1[Ballast Tank] --> T2[BWMS Filtration]
        T2 --> T3[UV or Chemical Treatment]
        T3 --> T4[Compliance with D-2 Standard]
        T4 --> T5[Discharge to Port Reception Facility]
    end

    style Start fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style Decision fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
    style StopPump fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style TreatmentPath fill:#E0F4FF,color:#000
```
