# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define the term "Ballast" and explain its primary purpose on a ship.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Ballast refers to the **sea water** taken into specially designated compartments (ballast tanks) of a ship to control its draft, trim, and stability when the ship is sailing without full cargo.

**Primary Purposes (2 marks):**
1. To maintain safe **forward and aft drafts**, ensuring proper propeller immersion and steerage.
2. To provide adequate **transverse and longitudinal stability** by lowering the center of gravity (G) and ensuring GM ≥ 0.15 m.
3. To control **hull girder stresses** (hogging and sagging) by distributing weight along the length.
4. To comply with the **Load Line Convention** requirements.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: List any three (3) essential components of a marine oil fuel supply system and state their functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **1. Settling/Service Tank** (1 mark) | Provides residence time (~24 hr) for water and sludge separation by gravity. |
| **2. Centrifugal Purifier** (1 mark) | Removes emulsified water and solid contaminants using high centripetal acceleration; increases fuel quality. |
| **3. Viscosity Controller** (1 mark) | Maintains fuel viscosity at 10–14 cSt at engine inlet by regulating steam flow to final heater. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q: (a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **ballast system** of a modern bulk carrier. List the various types of ballast tanks and explain the procedure of ballasting/deballasting with reference to SOLAS regulations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier has the following particulars:
- Light ship weight = 11,000 t
- Constant (L.O, FW, Stores) = 600 t
- Loaded displacement at departure = 58,000 t at draft 11.5 m
- Hydrostatic data: T = 7.0 m → Δ = 28,000 t
- Density of seawater = 1.025 t/m³

**Calculate:**
- (i) The cargo weight carried at departure
- (ii) The weight and volume of ballast water required at the discharge port to maintain a draft of 7.0 m
- (iii) The time required to ballast using a pump of capacity 1,200 m³/h **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Ballast System of a Bulk Carrier (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** A ballast system is mandatory under **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Reg. 20–22** for all vessels > 100 GT. It enables safe navigation in various load conditions.

**Types of Ballast Tanks (2 marks):**
- **Double Bottom Tanks (DBT)** — extend across ship bottom; primary transverse stability provider
- **Wing/Side Ballast Tanks (WBT)** — vertical tanks along the side shell in double-skin ships
- **Fore Peak Tank (FPT)** — small tank at extreme forward; controls trim by bow
- **Aft Peak Tank (APT)** — small tank at extreme aft; controls trim by stern
- **Topside Tanks** — used when double bottoms are full
- **Hopper Tanks** — angled tanks in cargo hold corners (bulk carriers)
- **Dedicated Ballast Tanks (DBT in tankers)** — segregated, never carry oil

**Ballasting Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Vessel enters designated ballast water exchange area (open sea, ≥ 200 nm from shore, ≥ 200 m depth).
2. Close all overboard discharge valves.
3. Open sea chest inlet valve.
4. Start ballast pump; ballast tanks filled sequentially — DBT first (lowest), then wing tanks, then topside.
5. Monitor via draft gauges, tank level sensors, and stress monitoring system.
6. Stop pump at target draft; close valves.
7. Log operation in **Ballast Water Record Book**.

**Deballasting Procedure (1 mark):**
1. Discharge via **port reception facility** or **BWMS** (treatment system).
2. Compliance with **D-2 standard** (BWM Convention) verified.
3. Maintain minimum operational draft (~0.02L + 0.04 m forward).

**Reference to SOLAS (1 mark):** SOLAS Reg. II-1/20–22 prescribes pumping capacity, piping redundancy, and stability after flooding.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**(i) Cargo weight at departure (2 marks):**

$$W_{departure} = \Delta_{loaded} - W_{lightship} - W_{constant}$$

$$W_{departure} = 58{,}000 - 11{,}000 - 600 = 46{,}400 \text{ t}$$

**(ii) Ballast weight and volume at port (3 marks):**

At the discharge port, all cargo is unloaded, so:

$$W_{port} = W_{lightship} + W_{constant} + W_{ballast}$$

From hydrostatic data at T = 7.0 m:

$$\Delta_{port} = W_{port} = 28{,}000 \text{ t}$$

$$W_{ballast} = 28{,}000 - 11{,}000 - 600 = 16{,}400 \text{ t}$$

$$V_{ballast} = \frac{W_{ballast}}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{16{,}400}{1.025} = 16{,}000 \text{ m}^3$$

**(iii) Ballasting time (2 marks):**

$$t = \frac{V_{ballast}}{Q_{pump}} = \frac{16{,}000}{1{,}200} = 13.33 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{t = 13.33 \text{ h} \approx 13 \text{ h } 20 \text{ min}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of a line diagram, explain the **fuel oil system** of a marine main propulsion engine. Describe the function of each major component in the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A marine centrifugal purifier operates with the following data:
- Number of discs Z = 130
- Disc outer radius r₂ = 0.105 m
- Disc inner radius r₁ = 0.040 m
- Rotational speed N = 7,200 rpm
- Viscosity of oil μ = 0.003 Pa·s
- Density difference Δρ = 80 kg/m³
- Throughput Q = 2.5 × 10⁻³ m³/s

**Calculate:**
- (i) The Σ-number of the centrifuge
- (ii) The cut size d_c
- (iii) Comment on the effectiveness of separation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Fuel Oil System (7 marks)

**System Layout (1 mark):** The fuel oil system has three circuits: (1) Storage/Transfer, (2) Purification, (3) Supply.

**Components and Functions (5 marks):**

1. **Storage Tanks** (0.5 mark): Hold HFO/MGO bunkers. Steam coils maintain HFO at 30–45 °C for pump-ability.
2. **Transfer Pump** (0.5 mark): Centrifugal pump (3–5 bar) moves fuel from storage to settling tank.
3. **Settling Tank** (0.5 mark): Gravity separation tank; water and sludge settle to bottom; residence time ~24 h.
4. **Pre-Heater** (0.5 mark): Steam heating to 85–95 °C reduces viscosity to ~25 cSt for purifier efficiency.
5. **Centrifugal Purifier** (1 mark): Disc-stack centrifuge separates emulsified water and solids; ~6,000–9,000 rpm.
6. **Service/Buffer Tank** (0.5 mark): Holds purified fuel; sized for at least 8 h of engine consumption.
7. **Supply Pump** (0.5 mark): Positive displacement screw pump delivers 4–6 bar pressure to engine.
8. **Final Heater** (0.5 mark): Steam heating to 135–150 °C; reduces viscosity to 10–14 cSt.
9. **Viscosity Controller** (0.5 mark): Closed-loop control — adjusts steam flow to maintain viscosity setpoint.

**Safety Devices (1 mark):** High-temperature alarm, low/high pressure alarms, oil mist detector, quick-closing valves (SOLAS Reg. II-2/4.2.2).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**(i) Σ-number (3 marks):**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 7{,}200}{60} = 753.98 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$r_2^3 = (0.105)^3 = 1.158 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$r_1^3 = (0.040)^3 = 6.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3$$

$$r_2^3 - r_1^3 = 1.094 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$\omega^2 = (753.98)^2 = 5.685 \times 10^5 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\Sigma = \frac{2\pi \times 5.685 \times 10^5 \times 1.094 \times 10^{-3} \times 130}{3 \times 9.81}$$

$$\Sigma = \frac{508{,}229}{29.43} = 17{,}271 \text{ m}^2$$

**(ii) Cut size (2 marks):**

$$d_c = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu Q}{\Delta\rho \cdot g \cdot \Sigma}}$$

$$d_c = \sqrt{\frac{18 \times 0.003 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{80 \times 9.81 \times 17{,}271}}$$

$$d_c = \sqrt{\frac{1.35 \times 10^{-4}}{1.355 \times 10^7}} = \sqrt{9.96 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$\boxed{d_c = 3.16 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \approx 3.16 \text{ μm}}$$

**(iii) Effectiveness comment (2 marks):**

A cut size of **3.16 μm** indicates that particles ≥ 3.16 μm are removed with >50% probability. For marine HFO purification, the target cut size is typically **5–15 μm**. Therefore, this purifier is **highly effective** for marine residual fuel applications and well within the design specification for delivering fuel with cleanliness to ISO 4406 code 18/16/13 or better.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> **Ballast System Numerical Questions:**
> - Forgetting to **add constant weight** (L.O, FW, stores, crew) to lightship weight
> - Confusing **displacement (Δ)** with **deadweight (DWT)** — Δ = lightship + everything else; DWT = Δ − lightship
> - Using **fresh water density (1.000 t/m³)** instead of seawater (**1.025 t/m³**)
> - Forgetting unit conversion: m³/h → m³/s for time calculation
> 
> **Fuel System Numerical Questions:**
> - Using **N (rpm)** directly instead of converting to **ω (rad/s)**: $\omega = 2\pi N/60$
> - Mixing **dynamic viscosity (μ, Pa·s)** with **kinematic viscosity (ν, cSt or m²/s)**
> - Forgetting to **square the radii difference** correctly
> - Forgetting that **Σ has units of m²** and is essentially an "equivalent settling area"
> 
> **Diagram Questions:**
> - Sketches MUST include **all safety devices** (alarms, relief valves, quick-closing valves)
> - Missing the **viscosity controller** in the supply circuit diagram (a common partial-mark deduction)
> - Not showing **tank sequential filling priority** in ballasting diagrams

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **🔑 HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — Read These the Night Before Exam**

### Ballast System
- ✅ **Archimedes' principle**: Ship floats when buoyancy = weight
- ✅ **Minimum forward draft** = **0.02L + 0.04 m** (in meters, L in meters)
- ✅ **Minimum GM**: ≥ 0.15 m (intact), ≥ 0.05 m (damaged)
- ✅ **Tank types**: DBT, Side/Wing, FPT, APT, Topside, Hopper
- ✅ **Stress types**: **Hogging** (ends down, middle up) vs **Sagging** (middle down, ends up)
- ✅ **BWM Convention** D-2: < 10 viable organisms/m³ (≥50 μm), < 10/mL (<50 μm, ≥10 μm)
- ✅ **BWMS types**: Filtration + UV, Filtration + Chemical, Electro-chlorination
- ✅ **Discharge** to **Port Reception Facility (PRF)** mandatory in many ports
- ✅ **Ballast Water Record Book** must be maintained
- ✅ **Pump types**: Centrifugal (large flow), positive displacement (high pressure)
- ✅ **Sequential filling**: DBT → Side → Topside (lowest to highest)
- ✅ **Free surface effect**: partially filled tanks reduce GM (always fill ballast tanks either ≤10% or ≥98%)

### Oil Fuel System
- ✅ **Three circuits**: Transfer (LP), Purification (MP), Supply (HP)
- ✅ **Heating stages**: Storage (30–45 °C) → Pre-heat (85–95 °C) → Final (135–150 °C)
- ✅ **Viscosity setpoint**: **10–14 cSt at 100 °C** for HFO injection
- ✅ **Purifier type**: Disc-stack centrifuge, Σ-number is key metric
- ✅ **Σ formula**: $\Sigma = \frac{2\pi \omega^2 (r_2^3 - r_1^3) Z}{3g}$
- ✅ **Cut size formula**: $d_c = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu Q}{\Delta\rho g \Sigma}}$
- ✅ **ASTM D341** governs viscosity-temperature relationship
- ✅ **ISO 8217:2017** is the standard for marine fuel quality
- ✅ **ECA zones** require ≤ **0.10% S** fuel (ULSFO or MGO)
- ✅ **MARPOL Annex VI** caps global sulphur at 0.50% (since 2020)
- ✅ **SOLAS Reg. II-2/4.2.2** mandates quick-closing valves for fuel systems
- ✅ **Common fuel pumps**: Gear pump, Screw pump (Suhas, MAN B&W use steam-driven)
- ✅ **Viscosity controller**: Pneumatic or electronic; typically 2-element PID control

### Cross-Cutting Concepts
- ✅ **Both systems** are SOLAS-mandated and subject to **classification society survey** (annual, special, continuous)
- ✅ **Both require** redundancy: backup pumps, alternative power supply
- ✅ **Alarms and trips** for safety are mandatory in both systems
- ✅ **Energy efficiency** is the modern focus — variable speed drives (VSD) on pumps, fuel flow meters
- ✅ **Environmental compliance** drives design: BWT for ballast, scrubbers/EGR for fuel
- ✅ **Watch-keeping** duties require continuous monitoring of both systems in engine room

### KTU 2024 Exam Quick Tips
- 📝 For 3-mark definitions: **define + list 2–3 key points**
- 📝 For 14-mark questions: **always include a sketch** even if not explicitly asked
- 📝 For numerical: **show all 5–7 working steps** with units
- 📝 For 7+7 questions: allocate **roughly equal time** to both sub-parts
- 📝 Use **standard symbols** (P, T, V, Q, η) and **tabulate** when comparing components

---

### END OF NOTES — MODULE 3: BALLAST AND OIL FUEL SYSTEMS

*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 guidelines • Bloom's Levels: Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyze*
*Mapped to Course Outcomes: CO3, CO4 • Aligned with SOLAS, MARPOL, BWM Convention, and ISO 8217:2017*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lube Oil and Cooling System with Various Fittings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Lube Oil and Cooling System with Various Fittings ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Lubricating Oil (LO) System — Formal Definition The **Lubricating Oil System** of a marine diesel engine is a closed, pressurized, temperature-regulated hydraulic network that delivers clean, cooled oil at a controlled viscosity and pressure to all sliding and rolling contact surfaces — main bearings, big-end bearings, small-end bearings, crosshead bearings, camshaft bearings, cylinder walls (in crosshead engines), turbocharger bearings, gear trains, and governor linkages — to provide **boundary/hydrodynamic lubrication, cooling, sealing, and flushing of wear debris**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > The LO system in a marine engine is designed to ensure continuous circulation of lubricating oil at the correct pressure, temperature, and cleanliness level to all bearing surfaces, thereby reducing friction, removing frictional heat, neutralizing acidic combustion products, and removing contaminants. ### 1.2 Cooling System — Formal Definition The **Marine Engine Cooling System** is a multi-loop, heat-rejection network that extracts surplus heat from the engine's working components — cylinder liners (jacket cooling), cylinder heads, pistons (piston cooling), exhaust valves, turbochargers, lubricating oil, and charge air — and dissipates it ultimately to the sea (the ultimate heat sink) using **Sea Water (SW)** as the final cooling medium, with **Fresh Water (FW)** acting as an intermediate coolant in modern two-stroke / large four-stroke marine engines. > [!NOTE] > **Classification of Cooling Loops in a Modern Marine Diesel Engine:** > - **HT (High Temperature) Fresh Water Circuit** — cools cylinder jackets, cylinder heads, exhaust valves > - **LT (Low Temperature) Fresh Water Circuit** — cools charge air, LO, sometimes piston cooling > - **Sea Water (SW) Circuit** — final heat sink; cools LT fresh water via central coolers, plus direct cooling of pistons, air coolers in some configurations ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition **Think of the Lube Oil System as the "Bloodstream" of the Engine:** Just as blood in a human body: - **Carries nutrients** (oil carries anti-wear additives, detergents, dispersants) - **Removes waste** (oil carries away metal wear particles, combustion soot, sludge) - **Regulates temperature** (oil carries away frictional heat) - **Operates under pressure** from a "heart" (the LO pump) - **Is filtered by "kidneys"** (LO filters and purifiers) - **Is cooled by the "skin"** (LO cooler rejecting heat to LT freshwater) **Think of the Cooling System as the Engine's "Sweating and Panting" Mechanism:** When you exercise: - Your body pumps blood closer to the skin to radiate heat (LO pump pushing hot oil to the cooler) - You sweat — water evaporates and carries heat away (sea water absorbing heat in central coolers) - You breathe faster to exhale hot air (air cooler cooling the charge air) Without these systems, an engine (like a human body) would "fever" itself to destruction within minutes. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Operational Metrics in KTU Exams:** > - Main LO Pressure: **2.5 – 4.5 bar** (typical for large 2-stroke MAN/B&W) > - LO Inlet Temperature: **45 – 55 °C** > - LO Outlet Temperature: **55 – 65 °C** > - HT FW Outlet (Jacket): **80 – 90 °C** > - LT FW Temperature: **32 – 38 °C** > - SW Inlet: **typically < 32 °C** (tropical up to 36 °C) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Lubricating Oil System — Functional Components A modern marine engine LO system consists of the following components arranged in a specific flow sequence: #### 2.1.1 Oil Sump / Drain Tank / Service Tank - **Wet sump engines** (most 4-stroke medium-speed): oil is stored in the oil pan directly under the crankshaft. - **Dry sump engines** (large 2-stroke MAN B&W, Wärtsilä): oil drains by gravity to a **drain tank** located below the engine, then is pumped to a **service tank** located high up in the engine room. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Service Tank Volume** is typically sized to hold **100% of the engine's full oil charge**, ensuring sufficient reserve for topping up after purifier sludge discharge and filter cleaning. #### 2.1.2 LO Pumps | Pump Type | Function | Drive | Location | |-----------|----------|-------|----------| | **Main LO Pump** | Circulates oil through engine | Geared from engine | Engine room | | **Standby/Pre-lubricating Pump** | Pre-lube before start, backup | Electric motor | Engine room | | **Emergency LO Pump** | Starts on low oil pressure | Battery/DC motor | Engine room | | **Transfer/Priming Pump** | Transfers oil from drain to service tank | Electric | Engine room | | **Piston Cooling LO Pump** | For crosshead engines only | Geared | Engine | #### 2.1.3 LO Coolers - **Plate-type heat exchangers** (most common today) — brazed or gasketed plates - **Shell-and-tube type** (older designs) - Uses **LT fresh water** as the cooling medium (in central cooling systems) or **sea water** (in direct cooling) #### 2.1.4 LO Filters - **Duplex Filters** — manual changeover with two baskets; one in service, one standby - **Auto-Backflush Filters** — self-cleaning, automatic, with differential pressure switch - **Magnetic plugs/strainers** — for ferrous particle capture #### 2.1.5 LO Purifier / Clarifier - Disc-stack centrifugal separator - Removes water, sludge, combustion by-products, soot - **Purifier mode** (water + dirt removal) vs **Clarifier mode** (only dirt removal) - Standard: **two purifiers** in parallel — one running, one standby #### 2.1.6 LO Piping, Valves, and Fittings - **Pressure regulating valve (PRV)** — maintains 4.5 bar system pressure - **Thermostatic 3-way valve** — bypasses LO cooler during cold start - **Thermometer pockets, pressure gauges, flow switches** - **Safety relief valve** — opens at ~6 bar to protect system ### 2.2 Cooling System — Functional Architecture #### 2.2.1 Three-Circuit Central Cooling System (Modern Standard) **Circuit 1 — Sea Water (SW) Circuit (Open Loop)** - Sea chest → sea water strainer → SW pump(s) → central cooler(s) → overboard discharge - This is the ONLY seawater circuit, minimizing corrosion & biofouling - Self-cleaning filters (e.g., Koyo, Westfalia) or conventional basket strainers **Circuit 2 — LT (Low Temperature) Fresh Water Circuit** - LT FW pump(s) → cools LO cooler, charge air cooler, piston cooling FW cooler - Temperature maintained at **32–38 °C** by thermostatic 3-way valve modulating SW flow **Circuit 3 — HT (High Temperature) Fresh Water Circuit** - HT FW pump(s) → cools cylinder jackets, cylinder heads, exhaust valve seats - Temperature maintained at **80–90 °C** by thermostatic 3-way valve #### 2.2.2 Direct Sea Water Cooling (Older / Smaller Engines) - Sea water directly cools cylinder jackets via individual seawater pumps per cylinder - Higher corrosion risk, hence now largely replaced by central cooling #### 2.2.3 Piston Cooling System - **In crosshead engines (2-stroke)**: cooled by fresh water or oil via telescopic pipes from the crosshead - **In trunk-piston engines (4-stroke)**: cooled by LO splash from bearings or oil jets directed at the piston underside ### 2.3 Marine Pipe Fittings — Classification | Fitting | Function | Application in LO/Cooling | |---------|----------|---------------------------| | **Gate Valve** | Full open/close, low pressure drop | Suction/discharge isolation | | **Globe Valve** | Throttling, flow regulation | LO cooler bypass | | **Butterfly Valve** | Quick shut-off, compact | Sea water overboard | | **Check (Non-Return) Valve** | Prevent backflow | Pump discharge | | **Y-Strainer** | Remove coarse particles | Pump suction | | **Basket Strainer** | Heavy debris | Sea chest inlet | | **Sight Flow Indicator** | Visual flow check | LO circulation line | | **Expansion Joint** | Absorb thermal expansion | Long SW piping | | **Quick-Closing Valve** | Emergency shutoff | Fuel, LO lines | | **Pressure Reducing Valve (PRV)** | Downstream pressure control | LO system pressure | | **Thermostatic 3-Way Valve** | Temperature-controlled mixing | LO & FW coolers | | **Safety/Relief Valve** | Overpressure protection | LO system header | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **THE FOLLOWING EQUATIONS ARE HIGH-FREQUENCY IN KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATIONS** | # | Formula | Description | Standard Units | |---|---------|-------------|----------------| | 1 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat transfer rate by fluid | $W$ | | 2 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD}$ | Heat exchanger rating | $W$ | | 3 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q_v$ | Mass flow from volumetric flow | $kg/s$ | | 4 | $\Delta P_{\text{pump}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H$ | Pump head pressure | $Pa$ | | 5 | $P_{\text{pump}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q_v \cdot H}{\eta_{\text{pump}}}$ | Pump shaft power | $W$ | | 6 | $Q_{\text{LO}} = \dfrac{f \cdot P_{\text{fric}} \cdot v}{J}$ | Heat to be removed by oil | $W$ | | 7 | $T_{\text{Kinematic}} = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity | $m^2/s$ | | 8 | $\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temp Difference | $K$ | | 9 | $C_r = \dfrac{C_{\min}}{C_{\max}}$ | Heat capacity ratio (for NTU) | dimensionless | | 10 | $V_{\text{tank}} = 1.25 \cdot V_{\text{circ}}$ | LO service tank sizing | $m^3$ | ### 2.5 Properties of Lubricating Oil (Engineering Utility) | Property | Typical Range (Marine System Oil) | Significance | |----------|----------------------------------|--------------| | Kinematic Viscosity @ 40 °C | **180 – 220 cSt** (SAE 50) | Flow through bearings | | Viscosity Index (VI) | **> 95** | Low viscosity variation with T | | Flash Point (COC) | **> 220 °C** | Fire safety | | Pour Point | **< -10 °C** | Cold start capability | | TBN (Total Base Number) | **5 – 12 mg KOH/g** | Acid neutralization | | Water content | **< 0.2 %** | Prevents emulsification | | TAN (Total Acid Number) | **< 2.0 mg KOH/g** | Oil degradation indicator | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — LO Cooler Heat Load **Problem Statement:** A marine trunk-piston diesel engine has a brake power of **6000 kW** at 100 rpm. The mechanical efficiency is **0.88**, and the heat lost to the lubricating oil is approximately **6% of the indicated power**. The LO cooler is fed with oil at **65 °C** and returns it at **52 °C**. The specific heat of oil is **2.0 kJ/kg·K**. Calculate the mass flow rate of oil required through the cooler. #### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Compute Indicated Power** $$P_i = \dfrac{P_b}{\eta_m} = \dfrac{6000}{0.88}$$ $$P_i = 6818.18 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Heat Rejected to LO** $$Q_{\text{LO}} = 0.06 \times P_i = 0.06 \times 6818.18$$ $$Q_{\text{LO}} = 409.09 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Temperature Rise Across Cooler** $$\Delta T = T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}} = 65 - 52$$ $$\Delta T = 13 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Energy Balance** $$Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$ $$\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{c_p \cdot \Delta T} = \dfrac{409.09 \text{ kW}}{2.0 \text{ kJ/kg·K} \times 13 \text{ K}}$$ $$\dot{m} = \dfrac{409.09}{26.0}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m} = 15.73 \text{ kg/s} \approx 56.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Pump Power for Central Cooler **Problem Statement:** A sea water pump delivers **150 m³/hr** of seawater to the central cooler. The total head developed is **18 m of water column**. The pump efficiency is **72%**. Calculate: - (a) The mass flow rate of seawater - (b) The hydraulic power delivered - (c) The shaft power input to the pump - (d) The discharge pressure Take $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s². #### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Volumetric Flow Conversion** $$Q_v = 150 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} = \dfrac{150}{3600} = 0.04167 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 2 — Mass Flow Rate (Part a)** $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q_v = 1025 \times 0.04167$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m} = 42.71 \text{ kg/s}}$$ **Step 3 — Hydraulic Power (Part b)** $$P_{\text{hyd}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_v \cdot H = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.04167 \times 18$$ $$P_{\text{hyd}} = 7543.6 \text{ W}$$ $$\boxed{P_{\text{hyd}} \approx 7.54 \text{ kW}}$$ **Step 4 — Shaft Power (Part c)** $$P_{\text{shaft}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{hyd}}}{\eta_{\text{pump}}} = \dfrac{7.544}{0.72}$$ $$\boxed{P_{\text{shaft}} = 10.48 \text{ kW}}$$ **Step 5 — Discharge Pressure (Part d)** $$\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot H = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 18$$ $$\Delta P = 180\,949.5 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta P \approx 1.81 \text{ bar (gauge)}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 3 — LMTD for LO Cooler **Problem Statement:** A shell-and-tube LO cooler has the following operating data: - LO enters at **60 °C**, leaves at **50 °C** - Sea water enters at **28 °C**, leaves at **38 °C** Calculate the LMTD. #### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify End Temperature Differences** $$\Delta T_1 = T_{\text{hot,in}} - T_{\text{cold,out}} = 60 - 38 = 22 \text{ °C}$$ $$\Delta T_2 = T_{\text{hot,out}} - T_{\text{cold,in}} = 50 - 28 = 22 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 2 — Apply LMTD Formula** Since $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = 22$ °C, the temperature profile is constant — therefore: $$\text{LMTD} = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = 22 \text{ °C}$$ If the values were not equal, we would use: $$\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Insight:** When the temperature difference is constant at both ends, the temperature profile is **linear**, meaning the cooler behaves like a simple counter-flow heat exchanger with no efficiency loss. This is the IDEAL design condition. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — LO & Cooling System Design | Parameter | LO System (HT) | LT Fresh Water | HT Fresh Water | Sea Water | |-----------|----------------|----------------|----------------|-----------| | Typical T_out (°C) | 55–65 | 38–42 | 80–90 | 32–45 | | Typical Pressure (bar) | 2.5–5.0 | 1.5–3.0 | 2.5–4.0 | 2.0–4.0 | | Main Pump Type | Screw / Gear | Centrifugal | Centrifugal | Centrifugal | | Cooler Type | Plate | Plate | Plate | Shell-tube / Plate | | Expansion Tank | Optional | Yes | Yes | N/A | | Vol. Reserve | 100% in service tank | 110% loop | 110% loop | N/A | | Treatment | Centrifuge + Filter | Chemical inhibitor | Chemical inhibitor | Cathodic + Anodes | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Marine Engine Lubricating Oil System — Block Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Drain Tank
Under Engine] --> B[Transfer Pump] B --> C[Service Tank
Elevated Storage] C --> D[Suction Strainer] D --> E[Main LO Pump
Driven by Engine] E --> F[Pressure Regulating Valve
Set at 4.5 bar] F --> G[Auto Backflush Filter] G --> H[LO Cooler
Plate Type LT FW] H --> I[Thermostatic 3-Way Valve] I --> J[Engine Oil Manifold
Main Bearings] J --> J1[Crosshead Bearings] J --> J2[Camshaft Bearings] J --> J3[Turbocharger] J --> J4[Governor Linkage] J1 --> K[Drain Back to Drain Tank] J2 --> K J3 --> K J4 --> K C --> L[Standby LO Pump
Electric Motor] L --> E E --> M[Emergency LO Pump
DC Motor Battery] C --> N[LO Purifier No 1] C --> O[LO Purifier No 2 Standby] N --> P[Sludge Tank] O --> P K --> A classDef tank fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pump fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cooler fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef safety fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef engine fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,C tank class B,E,L,M pump class H,I cooler class F,G safety class J,J1,J2,J3,J4 engine ``` ### 4.2 Marine Engine Three-Circuit Central Cooling System — Block Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sea Chest
Hull Bottom] --> B[Sea Water Strainer
Basket Type] B --> C[Sea Water Pump No 1] B --> D[Sea Water Pump No 2 Standby] C --> E[Central Cooler No 1
HT FW to SW] C --> F[Central Cooler No 2
LT FW to SW] E --> G[Sea Water Overboard Discharge] F --> G H[HT Fresh Water
Expansion Tank] --> I[HT FW Pump] I --> J[HT FW Thermostatic
3-Way Valve] J --> E J --> K[Engine Cylinder Jackets] J --> L[Cylinder Heads] J --> M[Exhaust Valve Seats] K --> N[HT FW Return to Cooler] L --> N M --> N N --> E O[LT Fresh Water
Expansion Tank] --> P[LT FW Pump] P --> Q[LT FW Thermostatic
3-Way Valve] Q --> F Q --> R[LO Cooler] Q --> S[Charge Air Cooler] Q --> T[Piston Cooling Cooler] R --> U[LT FW Return to Cooler] S --> U T --> U U --> F classDef sea fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef hot fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cool fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef valve fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,B,C,D,E,F,G sea class H,I,J,K,L,M,N hot class O,P,Q,R,S,T,U cool ``` ### 4.3 Marine Engine Lubricant & Coolant Classification — Functional Block ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Marine Engine
Lubricants] --> B[System Oil] A --> C[Cylinder Oil] A --> D[Crankcase Oil] B --> B1[Main Bearings] B --> B2[Camshaft] C --> C1[Cylinder Liner
via Quills] C --> C2[Piston Rings] D --> D1[4-stroke engines
unified] E[Marine Engine
Coolants] --> F[Fresh Water
with Inhibitor] E --> G[Sea Water
Open Loop] F --> F1[HT Circuit 80-90C] F --> F2[LT Circuit 32-38C] G --> G1[Central Cooler Only
Modern] G --> G2[Direct Cooling
Older] classDef oil fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cool fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,B,C,D,B1,B2,C1,C2,D1 oil class E,F,G,F1,F2,G1,G2 cool ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model 2024]** *List the main functions of the lubricating oil in a marine diesel engine. Why is the system oil different from cylinder oil in a 2-stroke crosshead engine?* **Model Answer:** The lubricating oil in a marine diesel engine performs the following functions: 1. **Lubrication** — forms a hydrodynamic film between sliding surfaces, reducing friction and wear 2. **Cooling** — carries away heat from bearings and pistons 3. **Sealing** — assists piston rings in sealing combustion gases 4. **Cleaning** — detergents disperse soot and sludge 5. **Corrosion protection** — alkaline additives neutralize acidic combustion products **Difference between System Oil and Cylinder Oil:** | Aspect | System Oil | Cylinder Oil | |--------|-----------|--------------| | **Application** | Bearings, camshaft, turbocharger | Cylinder liner, piston rings | | **Location** | Crankcase, separate from combustion zone | In contact with hot combustion gases | | **TBN** | Low (5–8 mg KOH/g) | High (25–100 mg KOH/g) | | **Detergents** | Lower concentration | Very high to neutralize acids | | **Additives** | Anti-wear, anti-foam | High alkalinity, dispersant | In a 2-stroke crosshead engine, the **crosshead** acts as a **gas-tight barrier** between the crankcase (system oil) and the cylinder (cylinder oil), preventing contamination between them. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Explain the term "Central Cooling System" as used in modern marine engines. Why is it preferred over direct sea water cooling?* **Model Answer:** A **Central Cooling System** is a closed-loop cooling arrangement where sea water is used in **only ONE heat exchanger (the central cooler)**, and fresh water is used as an intermediate coolant in two separate circuits — a **High Temperature (HT)** circuit for cylinder jackets/heads, and a **Low Temperature (LT)** circuit for LO coolers, charge air coolers, and piston cooling. **Why Central Cooling is Preferred:** 1. **Reduced corrosion** — Sea water contacts only the central cooler (corrosion-resistant material like Cu-Ni or titanium), protecting the cylinder jacket, head, and other critical components 2. **Easy maintenance** — Sea water strainers and pumps are accessible; no need to disassemble engine 3. **No salt deposits** — Fresh water doesn't scale jackets, improving heat transfer 4. **Flexibility** — Engine can operate in any ambient sea condition 5. **Regulated temperature** — Thermostatic 3-way valves provide precise temperature control 6. **Higher reliability** — Multiple pumps (HT, LT, SW) with standby units --- ### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **🛑 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALL ALERT — READ BEFORE ATTEMPTING:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse** "main LO pump" with "emergency LO pump" — they have different drives and starting conditions > 2. **Do NOT write** "LO cools the engine" generically — always specify *which heat source* (bearings, pistons, turbo) > 3. Always **label the diagram** (KTU 2024 emphasizes visual understanding — missing arrows = lose 1–2 marks) > 4. In heat load problems, **state assumptions** (specific heat value, heat loss %) explicitly or you lose a mark > 5. For crosshead vs trunk-piston engine comparisons, **mention the crosshead** as a separator — common omission --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 / Past Paper Pattern]** **(a)** With the help of a neat line diagram, describe the **lubricating oil system of a marine diesel engine**. Explain the functions of the main components. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A marine engine of **9000 kW** brake power, running at **120 rpm**, has a mechanical efficiency of **0.87**. The heat lost to the lubricating oil is **5.5% of the indicated power**. The LO cooler receives oil at **62 °C** and discharges it at **50 °C**. Specific heat of oil is **2.05 kJ/kg·K**. Calculate: - (i) Heat load on the LO cooler in kW - (ii) Mass flow of oil in kg/s and m³/hr (take $\rho_{\text{LO}} = 920$ kg/m³) - (iii) Volumetric flow in litres per minute **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) — LO System Description (7 marks)** The lubricating oil system of a modern marine diesel engine consists of the following components (refer to Section 4.1 block diagram): 1. **Drain Tank** (capacity ~1.0 × engine oil charge): receives oil draining from the engine by gravity; located below the engine. 2. **Transfer Pump** (electric, centrifugal): pumps oil from drain tank to service tank. 3. **Service Tank** (elevated, capacity ~1.0–1.25 × full oil charge): stores ready-to-use oil; provides gravity feed suction head to the main pump. 4. **Main LO Pump** (screw or gear type, driven by engine): provides the pressure (4.0–4.5 bar) for oil circulation through the engine. 5. **Standby LO Pump** (electric, centrifugal): starts automatically on low LO pressure; pre-lubricates engine before start. 6. **Emergency LO Pump** (battery/DC operated): starts on extremely low LO pressure (typically <1.5 bar) to protect bearings. 7. **Pressure Regulating Valve (PRV)**: maintains constant system pressure by bypassing excess oil back to the suction side. 8. **Duplex / Auto-Backflush Filter**: removes particles; the auto-backflush type uses differential pressure (typically 0.8–1.0 bar) to trigger cleaning. 9. **LO Cooler** (plate-type, LT fresh water cooled): cools oil from ~65 °C to ~50 °C. 10. **Thermostatic 3-Way Valve**: bypasses cooler during cold start; gradually opens cooler as oil warms. 11. **LO Purifier(s)** (disc-stack centrifugal): remove water and sludge. Two units, one running, one standby. 12. **Engine Internal Distribution**: oil flows to main bearings → crosshead bearings → camshaft → turbocharger → drains back to drain tank. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Indicated Power** $$P_i = \dfrac{P_b}{\eta_m} = \dfrac{9000}{0.87} = 10344.83 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Heat Load on LO Cooler (i)** $$Q_{\text{LO}} = 0.055 \times P_i = 0.055 \times 10344.83$$ $$\boxed{Q_{\text{LO}} = 568.97 \text{ kW} \approx 569 \text{ kW}}$$ **Step 3 — Temperature Difference** $$\Delta T = 62 - 50 = 12 \text{ °C} = 12 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4 — Mass Flow Rate (ii)** $$\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{c_p \cdot \Delta T} = \dfrac{568.97}{2.05 \times 12} = \dfrac{568.97}{24.6}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m} = 23.13 \text{ kg/s}}$$ **Step 5 — Volumetric Flow Rate (ii & iii)** $$Q_v = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{23.13}{920} = 0.02514 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$Q_v = 0.02514 \times 3600 = 90.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$ $$Q_v = 0.02514 \times 60000 \text{ L/min}$$ $$\boxed{Q_v \approx 1508 \text{ L/min}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024]** **(a)** Describe the **central cooling system** of a modern marine engine with a neat block diagram. Compare HT and LT circuits. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A centrifugal sea water pump delivers **200 m³/hr** against a total head of **22 m**. The pump efficiency is **70%**. Calculate the **shaft power required** in kW. Also calculate the **discharge pressure** in bar. Take $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) — Central Cooling System Description (7 marks)** A modern marine engine uses a **three-circuit central cooling system** (refer to Section 4.2 block diagram): **Circuit 1: Sea Water Circuit (Open)** - Sea chest → basket strainer → SW pump (one of two, with standby) → central coolers (HT & LT) → overboard discharge - Sea water does NOT enter the engine directly **Circuit 2: HT Fresh Water Circuit** - Expansion tank (with header tank level alarm) → HT FW pump → thermostatic 3-way valve → cylinder jackets, cylinder heads, exhaust valve seats → central cooler (HT) → return to pump suction - Temperature controlled at **80–90 °C** to prevent cold corrosion (low-load acid dew) and thermal stress **Circuit 3: LT Fresh Water Circuit** - LT expansion tank → LT FW pump → thermostatic 3-way valve → LO cooler, charge air cooler, piston cooling cooler → central cooler (LT) → return to pump suction - Temperature controlled at **32–38 °C** **Comparison Table — HT vs LT** | Aspect | HT Circuit | LT Circuit | |--------|------------|------------| | **Heat Sources** | Cylinder jacket, head, exhaust valves | Charge air, LO, piston cooling | | **Operating T** | 80–90 °C | 32–38 °C | | **Function** | Prevents cold corrosion, low thermal stress | Maximizes charge air density | | **Cooler Duty** | Largest heat duty (~50% of total) | Smaller heat duty | | **Add to FW** | Inhibitor (Na-nitrite/borate) | Same inhibitor | **Part (b) — Pump Power Calculation (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Convert Flow to SI Units** $$Q_v = 200 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} = \dfrac{200}{3600} = 0.05556 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 2 — Hydraulic Power (Water Power)** $$P_{\text{hyd}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_v \cdot H$$ $$P_{\text{hyd}} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.05556 \times 22$$ $$P_{\text{hyd}} = 12\,471.7 \text{ W} = 12.47 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Shaft Power (Brake Power)** $$P_{\text{shaft}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{hyd}}}{\eta_{\text{pump}}} = \dfrac{12.47}{0.70}$$ $$\boxed{P_{\text{shaft}} = 17.82 \text{ kW}}$$ **Step 4 — Discharge Pressure** $$\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot H = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 22 = 221\,125.5 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta P \approx 2.21 \text{ bar (gauge)}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert **m³/hr → m³/s** and end up with a shaft power 3600× larger. Always check units first! --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAM** - [x] **LO system** is the **bloodstream** of the engine — delivers pressurized, cooled, clean oil to all bearing surfaces - [x] **Main LO pressure** is regulated at **2.5 – 4.5 bar** by the **Pressure Regulating Valve (PRV)** - [x] **Three pumps** in LO system: **Main** (engine-driven), **Standby** (electric), **Emergency** (DC/battery) - [x] **Central cooling** uses **sea water only once** — in the central cooler — to minimize corrosion - [x] **HT FW circuit** operates at **80–90 °C** for cylinder jackets/heads - [x] **LT FW circuit** operates at **32–38 °C** for LO cooler & charge air - [x] **Thermostatic 3-way valves** regulate temperature in both FW circuits - [x] **System oil** (low TBN) ≠ **Cylinder oil** (high TBN) in 2-stroke crosshead engines - [x] **Crosshead** in 2-stroke engines separates crankcase (system oil) from cylinder (cylinder oil) - [x] **LO Purifier** uses centrifugal force to remove water and sludge - [x] **Key formula:** $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ — used for ALL heat load calculations - [x] **Key formula:** $P_{\text{shaft}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q_v \cdot H}{\eta}$ — for all pump power problems - [x] **LMTD** is used for **heat exchanger design/rating** - [x] **Common fittings** to remember: gate valve, globe valve, butterfly valve, Y-strainer, basket strainer, sight flow indicator, quick-closing valve, PRV, thermostatic 3-way valve - [x] **Expansion tanks** in HT & LT circuits maintain system pressure and provide water reserve - [x] **SW circuit** must have **dual strainers** (one in service, one standby) and **dual pumps**

topics.title: Domestic Fresh Water and Sea Water Hydrophore System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Domestic Fresh Water and Sea Water Hydrophore System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🏗️ Module 3 — Domestic Fresh Water & Sea Water Hydrophore System

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Hydrophore System** is a centralized pressurized water supply installation fitted on board ships, designed to deliver fresh water (for drinking, washing, cooking) and sea water (for sanitary flushing, deck washing, ballast bilge services) at a constant pressure to all consumer points — independent of pump cycling — by employing an air-cushion pressurised storage tank, automatic pressure switches, and dedicated duty/standby pumps.

### 🔑 Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Hydrophore Tank** | A sealed cylindrical pressure vessel partially filled with water and partially with compressed air acting as a pressure cushion. |
| **Air Cushion** | The compressed air volume above water inside the hydrophore tank that maintains system pressure when pumps are off. |
| **Starting Pressure (P₁)** | The lower pressure set-point at which the pump automatically cuts **IN**. |
| **Stopping Pressure (P₂)** | The upper pressure set-point at which the pump automatically cuts **OUT**. |
| **Pre-charge Pressure (P₀)** | The dry nitrogen/air pressure sealed in the tank before water is admitted (typically **0.5 bar – 1.0 bar** above atmospheric). |
| **Cut-in / Cut-out Pressure** | Alternate KTU-accepted terms for P₁ and P₂. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Operating Pressures on a Modern Vessel:**
> • Domestic Fresh Water Hydrophore : **3.0 bar – 4.5 bar**
> • Sea Water Hydrophore (Sanitary) : **2.5 bar – 4.0 bar**
> • Fire / Deck Wash Hydrophore    : **4.0 bar – 6.0 bar**

### 🌊 Intuitive Analogy — "The Floating Balloon in a Bucket"

Imagine a closed bucket half-filled with water, with a balloon trapped inside the air-space at the top.

- When you open a tap, water flows out, the balloon expands → pressure drops slightly.
- When pressure drops below the lower limit, an automatic sensor (the pressure switch) shouts "Pump ON!" → a pump pushes more water in.
- Water re-enters → balloon compresses → pressure rises back up.
- When the upper pressure is reached, the switch shouts "Pump OFF!" → pumping stops, balloon again quietly maintains pressure.

**That balloon is the "air cushion".** The hydrophore tank is your bucket, and the pumps + pressure switch form the brain-and-muscle system. Without the air cushion, every time someone opened a galley tap, the pump would have to instantly start — which would burn out the motor within weeks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 1.2 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔬 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The hydrophore system works on the **Compressed Air–Water Interface Principle**. Let us break it down into a sequenced engineering logic:

1. **Initial Charging (One-time Setup)**
   The tank is pre-charged with **dry nitrogen** or oil-free compressed air at a pressure **P₀** ≈ **0.5 – 1.0 bar gauge** through a dedicated air-charging valve. This ensures the bladder/space is never fully waterlogged.

2. **Pump Activation (Cut-In)**
   As consumers draw water, the trapped air expands (Boyle's Law). Pressure falls gradually. When pressure drops to the **cut-in pressure P₁** (e.g., **3.0 bar**), the **pressure switch** closes its lower contact and energises the pump contactor. The pump starts.

3. **Pressurisation Phase**
   The duty pump forces water into the tank against the rising air pressure. The pump flow rate **Q** (m³/h) compresses the air volume **V_air** from pressure P₁ to P₂.

4. **Pump Deactivation (Cut-Out)**
   When pressure reaches **cut-out pressure P₂** (e.g., **4.5 bar**), the upper contact of the pressure switch opens and de-energises the pump contactor. The pump stops. Water is now stored under pressure.

5. **Distribution Phase**
   Consumers draw water from the tank. Pressure drops from P₂ → P₁. During this entire phase the pump remains OFF, and the air cushion alone services the demand. This is called the **"Idle Phase"** — the period that gives the pump its rest and long life.

6. **Cycling Repeats**
   Cycle continues indefinitely. A standby pump is automatically selected if duty pump fails (auto-changeover every 24 hours on most modern vessels).

> [!TIP]
> **Why is the Air Cushion Critical?**
> Without it, the system would require a **variable-speed pump or accumulator**. The cushion converts an **intermittent pump duty** into a **continuous-pressure supply** — the simplest, most reliable solution possible at sea, where vibration, salt corrosion and reliability matter most.

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic + air pressure at tank bottom | Pa or bar |
| 2 | $P_1 \cdot V_1 = P_2 \cdot V_2$ | Boyle's Law for the air cushion (isothermal) | Pa·m³ |
| 3 | $V_{tank} = \dfrac{Q_{pump} \cdot t_{cycle}}{k}$ | Tank volume sizing (where k is a utilization factor, **0.5 – 0.7**) | m³ |
| 4 | $P_{precharge} = 0.85 \cdot P_1$ (approx.) | Recommended nitrogen pre-charge | bar |
| 5 | $Q_{pump} \geq 1.5 \times Q_{max,demand}$ | Pump capacity rule-of-thumb | m³/h |
| 6 | $P_{max} = P_{cut\text{-}out} + \Delta P_{line}$ | Max system pressure including pipe friction | bar |
| 7 | $W_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{pump}}$ | Pump hydraulic power | kW |
| 8 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{4 f L \rho v^2}{2 D}$ | Darcy-Weisbach pipe friction loss | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Valuation:** When writing Boyle's Law, KTU examiners expect the absolute pressures (not gauge). Convert correctly: $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Cargo Ships / Tankers** | Drinking water to cabins, galleys, pantries |
| **Naval Warships** | High-pressure seawater for magazine flooding, fire-fighting |
| **Offshore Platforms** | Potable water supply for living quarters |
| **Yachts / Cruise Liners** | Silent, vibration-free water supply to luxury cabins |
| **Land-based Industry** | Same principle used in apartment building booster systems |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 1.3 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 📘 DERIVATION 1 — Air Cushion Volume using Boyle's Law

**Statement:** During the idle phase (pump OFF), water leaves the tank, the trapped air expands from pressure P₂ to P₁, and its volume increases. Derive the relation between the delivered water volume and the air volume change.

**Given:**
- Initial air pressure (cut-out) = $P_2$ (absolute)
- Final air pressure (cut-in) = $P_1$ (absolute)
- Initial air volume (when pump just stopped) = $V_{a2}$
- Final air volume (when pump about to start) = $V_{a1}$
- Volume of water delivered to consumers = $\Delta V_w = V_{a1} - V_{a2}$
- Process is **isothermal** (slow, room-temperature air).

**Step 1 — Apply Boyle's Law for the trapped air:**

$$
P_2 \cdot V_{a2} = P_1 \cdot V_{a1}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* Since temperature is constant and the air mass is sealed inside the tank, the product of pressure and volume is conserved.

**Step 2 — Solve for $V_{a1}$:**

$$
V_{a1} = \dfrac{P_2 \cdot V_{a2}}{P_1}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the volume of water drawn out ($\Delta V_w$):**

$$
\Delta V_w = V_{a1} - V_{a2} = \dfrac{P_2 \cdot V_{a2}}{P_1} - V_{a2}
$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $V_{a2}$:**

$$
\Delta V_w = V_{a2} \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Final Compact Form (KTU Standard Answer):**

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta V_w = V_{a2} \cdot \dfrac{(P_2 - P_1)}{P_1}\;}
$$

> *Interpretation:* The amount of water that can be delivered without the pump running is directly proportional to the air volume at cut-out pressure and the **pressure differential $(P_2 - P_1)$** between cut-out and cut-in.

---

### 📘 DERIVATION 2 — Optimum Pre-charge Pressure

**Statement:** Derive the pre-charge pressure $P_0$ that prevents the air from being completely dissolved into the water and ensures the tank never empties of air.

**Step 1 — Assume the water volume is constant $V_w$.** The total tank volume $V_T$ is:

$$
V_T = V_w + V_a
$$

**Step 2 — When pressure falls from $P_2$ to $P_1$, air expands.** To guarantee that some air always remains, the pre-charge pressure should be just below the cut-in pressure $P_1$:

$$
P_0 \approx 0.9 \cdot P_1
$$

**Step 3 — Safety Adjustment (used by class societies like DNV / Lloyd's):**

$$
\boxed{\;P_0 = P_1 - 0.2 \text{ bar (typically)}\;}
$$

> *Engineering Reason:* If $P_0 > P_1$, water cannot enter at all at cut-in. If $P_0 \ll P_1$, the bladder collapses completely (waterlogging).

---

### 📘 DERIVATION 3 — Tank Sizing Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 style):**
A ship requires $Q_{demand} = 12 \, m^3/h$ of domestic fresh water. Pump capacity is $Q_{pump} = 18 \, m^3/h$. The pressure switch is set at $P_1 = 3.0 \, bar$ (cut-in) and $P_2 = 4.5 \, bar$ (cut-out). The pump takes 45 seconds to go from $P_1$ to $P_2$. Find the required hydrophore tank volume.

**Step 1 — Volume of water delivered by pump in 45 s:**

$$
V_{delivered} = Q_{pump} \times t = 18 \, \dfrac{m^3}{h} \times \dfrac{45}{3600} \, h = 0.225 \, m^3
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the air-cushion equation (from Derivation 1).** Rearrange to find $V_{a2}$:

$$
V_{a2} = \dfrac{\Delta V_w \cdot P_1}{P_2 - P_1}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values (use absolute pressures: $P_1 = 4.0 \, bar_{abs}$, $P_2 = 5.5 \, bar_{abs}$):**

$$
V_{a2} = \dfrac{0.225 \times 4.0}{5.5 - 4.0} = \dfrac{0.900}{1.5} = 0.600 \, m^3
$$

**Step 4 — The air volume at cut-out occupies $V_{a2} = 0.600 \, m^3$.** Assume water occupies ~50% of tank → Total tank volume:

$$
V_{tank} \approx 2 \times 0.600 = 1.20 \, m^3
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\;V_{tank} \approx 1.20 \, m^3 \quad (\text{rounded up to standard size } 1.5 \, m^3)\;}
$$

> *KTU Valuation Tip:* Always state assumptions clearly (e.g., 50% water fill, absolute pressure used, isothermal process). Examiners award marks for these.

---

### 📘 DERIVATION 4 — Pump Power Calculation

**Problem:** Find the motor power required for the fresh water hydrophore pump.

$$
W_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta_{pump} \cdot 1000}
$$

**Substituting:** $\rho = 1000 \, kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$, $Q = 18/3600 = 0.005 \, m^3/s$, $H = 45 \, m$ (head equivalent of 4.5 bar), $\eta_{pump} = 0.65$:

$$
W_{pump} = \dfrac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.005 \times 45}{0.65 \times 1000} = \dfrac{2207.25}{650} \approx 3.4 \, kW
$$

**Select next standard motor size: 4.0 kW (with ~15% service margin).**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 1.5 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a hydrophore system. Why is an air cushion maintained inside the tank?*

**Model Answer:**
A hydrophore system is a pressurized water supply arrangement used on ships to deliver fresh water (domestic) or sea water (sanitary) at constant pressure to multiple consumer points. It consists of a pressure tank, automatic pressure switch-controlled pumps, safety devices and a distribution network.

**The air cushion is essential because:**
1. It stores potential energy in compressed form, providing water flow even when the pump is OFF.
2. It absorbs pressure fluctuations, ensuring smooth delivery.
3. It reduces pump cycling frequency, prolonging motor life.
4. It eliminates the need for a pressure-reducing valve at every outlet.

*(3 marks — full credit for stating all four points.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the difference between a fresh water hydrophore and a sea water hydrophore on a ship.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Fresh Water Hydrophore | Sea Water Hydrophore |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Fresh water tank / FW generator | Sea chest (via sea suction) |
| **Pump Material** | Bronze / stainless steel impeller | Cupro-nickel / bronze for corrosion resistance |
| **Typical Use** | Drinking, cooking, bathing, laundry | WC flushing, deck wash, sanitary, fire-fighting |
| **Pressure Range** | 3.0 – 4.5 bar | 2.5 – 4.0 bar |
| **Water Quality Concern** | Potability, TDS control | Salinity, marine growth |

*(3 marks — comparison table format preferred in KTU valuation.)*

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> • Many students **forget to convert gauge to absolute pressure** when applying Boyle's Law. This alone costs 2–3 marks.
> • Do **NOT confuse the cut-in and cut-out pressures** during derivations — examiners specifically check this.
> • Always **label the schematic** with pump, NRV, pressure switch, safety valve, tank — unlabeled diagrams lose 2 marks.
> • In numericals, **state assumptions** (isothermal, 50% fill, absolute pressures). Skipping them loses 1 mark.
> • **Do not write Python or coding snippets** in Marine Engineering theory papers — KTU examiners reject such answers.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Fresh Water Hydrophore Design

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a domestic fresh water hydrophore system on board a ship. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fresh water hydrophore tank has a total volume of 2.0 m³. It is pre-charged with nitrogen at 2.0 bar (absolute). The pump cut-in pressure is 3.0 bar (gauge) and cut-out pressure is 4.5 bar (gauge). Atmospheric pressure = 1.013 bar. Calculate:
   (i) The volume of water that can be delivered between two pump cycles. **(4 marks)**
   (ii) The pre-charge pressure in bar (gauge) recommended. **(3 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**(a) Construction & Working (7 Marks)**

**Construction:**
A typical fresh water hydrophore system on a ship consists of:

1. **Pressure Tank (Hydrophore Tank)** — A horizontal or vertical cylindrical pressure vessel, capacity typically **1.0 – 3.0 m³**, made of carbon steel with epoxy coating or stainless steel. Internally it may be fitted with a **rubber bladder** (membrane type) or operates as a direct air-water contact type.

2. **Pre-Charge System** — A nitrogen cylinder with pressure-reducing valve connected via a non-return valve to the top of the tank for initial air charging.

3. **Pumps (1 Duty + 1 Standby)** — Centrifugal pumps with mechanical seals, rated for **1.5× maximum demand**.

4. **Pressure Switch** — Adjustable differential type, typically set at **P₁ = 3.0 bar** (cut-in) and **P₂ = 4.5 bar** (cut-out).

5. **Safety Relief Valve** — Set at **5.5 – 6.0 bar** to protect against over-pressurisation.

6. **Pressure Gauge** — Local and remote indication on engine control room (ECR) mimic.

7. **Low Air Pressure Switch** — Triggers an alarm in the ECR if the air cushion drops below safe limit.

8. **Non-Return Valves** — On each pump discharge to prevent back-flow.

9. **Distribution Piping** — Stainless steel 316L or copper-nickel to all consumer points.

**Working:**
- The tank is pre-charged with nitrogen to ~**2.0 bar abs**.
- As consumers draw water, pressure drops. At **3.0 bar gauge (cut-in)**, the pressure switch energises the duty pump.
- The pump forces water into the tank, compressing the air cushion from 3.0 to 4.5 bar gauge.
- At **4.5 bar gauge (cut-out)**, the pump is stopped.
- The compressed air cushion now drives water to all outlets.
- When pressure again falls to 3.0 bar, the cycle repeats.
- An auto-changeover relay rotates duty and standby pumps every 24 hours for equal wear.

*(7 marks — block diagram 3 marks + description 4 marks.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $V_{tank} = 2.0 \, m^3$
- $P_0 = 2.0 \, bar_{abs}$ (pre-charge)
- $P_1 = 3.0 \, bar_{gauge}$ → $P_{1,abs} = 3.0 + 1.013 = 4.013 \, bar_{abs}$
- $P_2 = 4.5 \, bar_{gauge}$ → $P_{2,abs} = 4.5 + 1.013 = 5.513 \, bar_{abs}$
- $P_{atm} = 1.013 \, bar$

**Step 1 — Assume 50% initial water filling.**
So initial air volume at pre-charge:
$$
V_{a0} = 0.5 \times V_{tank} = 1.0 \, m^3
$$

**(i) Volume of water delivered between cycles (4 marks):**

**Step 2 — First, find air volume at cut-out pressure $P_{2,abs}$ using Boyle's Law (pre-charge to cut-out):**

$$
P_0 \cdot V_{a0} = P_{2,abs} \cdot V_{a2}
$$

$$
V_{a2} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 1.0}{5.513} = 0.3627 \, m^3
$$

**Step 3 — Now find air volume at cut-in pressure $P_{1,abs}$ (cut-out to cut-in expansion):**

$$
P_{2,abs} \cdot V_{a2} = P_{1,abs} \cdot V_{a1}
$$

$$
V_{a1} = \dfrac{5.513 \times 0.3627}{4.013} = 0.4983 \, m^3
$$

**Step 4 — Volume of water delivered = increase in air volume:**

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta V_w = V_{a1} - V_{a2} = 0.4983 - 0.3627 = 0.1356 \, m^3 \approx 0.136 \, m^3\;}
$$

*(Equivalent to 136 litres per pump cycle.)*

**(ii) Recommended pre-charge pressure (gauge) (3 marks):**

Using the standard design rule:
$$
P_{0,gauge} = P_{1,gauge} - 0.2 \, bar = 3.0 - 0.2 = 2.8 \, bar_{gauge}
$$

Converting to absolute for cross-check:
$$
P_{0,abs} = 2.8 + 1.013 = 3.813 \, bar_{abs}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;P_{0,gauge} \approx 2.8 \, bar\;}
$$

> *Conclusion:* The 2.0 bar absolute pre-charge in the problem is **too low** — it would cause waterlogging of the tank. The recommended value is ~**2.8 bar gauge** to prevent the air from being entirely absorbed/dissolved.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Sea Water Hydrophore & Comparison

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a sea water hydrophore system used for sanitary flushing and deck wash services. State the materials of construction and explain why these are chosen. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the fresh water hydrophore system and the sea water hydrophore system with respect to: source, pump material, pressure range, typical consumers, maintenance requirement, and class-society requirements. Draw a single-line schematic showing the common components. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**(a) Sea Water Hydrophore — Working, Construction & Materials (7 Marks)**

**Source:** Sea water is drawn from the **sea chest** through a **storm valve** and a **suction strainer** (to block marine growth, debris and fish). The suction line leads to the suction manifold serving the hydrophore pumps.

**Pumping:** Two centrifugal pumps (1 duty + 1 standby) take suction from the sea chest. The discharge is fed through non-return valves into the **sea water hydrophore tank**.

**Storage:** The tank is similar in construction to the fresh water tank but uses **epoxy-coated carbon steel** or **GRP lining** to resist chloride corrosion.

**Working Cycle:** Identical to the fresh water system — cut-in ~2.5 bar, cut-out ~4.0 bar, with auto-changeover every 24 hours.

**Distribution:** Sea water is delivered to:
- **WC Flushing** (vacuum or gravity type)
- **Deck Wash Hydrants** (international shore connection-style)
- **Sanitary Spaces** (hospital, galley floor drains)
- **Fire Stations** (on certain vessel classes)

**Materials of Construction & Justification:**

| Component | Material | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Pump body & impeller | **Cupro-nickel (90/10)** or **Bronze** | Resists corrosion in saline environment |
| Tank shell | Carbon steel + **rubber lining** or **glass-flake epoxy coating** | Prevents chloride pitting |
| Piping | **Cu-Ni 90/10** or **GRP** | Salt-water compatible |
| Valves | **Bronze / DZR brass** | Galvanic corrosion resistance |
| Anodes | **Zinc or aluminium sacrificial anodes** | Cathodic protection of tank internals |

*(7 marks — 4 marks for working + 3 marks for materials justification.)*

---

**(b) Comparative Study + Single-Line Schematic (7 Marks)**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Fresh Water Hydrophore | Sea Water Hydrophore |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Fresh water tank / FW generator | Sea chest (sea suction) |
| **Pump material** | Bronze / SS impeller | Cu-Ni / Bronze body, SS shaft |
| **Tank lining** | Epoxy coated or SS | Rubber-lined or GRP |
| **Cut-in / Cut-out** | 3.0 / 4.5 bar | 2.5 / 4.0 bar |
| **Typical consumers** | Cabins, galley, hospital, laundry | WCs, deck wash, sanitary, fire |
| **Maintenance** | Annual inspection, infrequent anode check | Quarterly anode inspection, frequent strainer cleaning |
| **Class requirement** | IACS S7 / Lloyds SSC | IACS S7 + MARPOL Annex IV compliance |
| **Pre-charge medium** | Dry nitrogen or oil-free air | Dry nitrogen preferred |

**Single-Line Schematic (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FW Tank] --> P1[FW Pump 1]
    A --> P2[FW Pump 2]
    P1 --> NRV1[NRV]
    P2 --> NRV2[NRV]
    NRV1 --> T1[FW Hydrophore Tank]
    NRV2 --> T1
    T1 --> PS1[Pressure Switch FW]
    T1 --> SV1[Safety Valve FW]
    B[Sea Chest] --> ST[Strainer]
    ST --> P3[SW Pump 1]
    ST --> P4[SW Pump 2]
    P3 --> NRV3[NRV]
    P4 --> NRV4[NRV]
    NRV3 --> T2[SW Hydrophore Tank]
    NRV4 --> T2
    T2 --> PS2[Pressure Switch SW]
    T2 --> SV2[Safety Valve SW]
    T1 --> D1[FW Distribution]
    T2 --> D2[SW Distribution]
```

*(7 marks — table 5 marks + labelled schematic 2 marks.)*

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Hydrophore = Pressurised water tank + Air cushion + Automatic pressure switch + Pumps.**
- ✅ **Two systems exist on most ships:** Fresh Water (FW) for domestic use, Sea Water (SW) for sanitary/flushing.
- ✅ **Standard pressures:** FW = 3.0/4.5 bar ; SW = 2.5/4.0 bar.
- ✅ **Air cushion acts as energy storage** — provides flow when pump is OFF.
- ✅ **Boyle's Law governs the air-cushion behaviour:** Always use **absolute pressures**.
- ✅ **Pre-charge pressure ≈ Cut-in pressure − 0.2 bar** (gauge) is the standard design rule.
- ✅ **One duty + one standby pump** with auto-changeover every 24 hours.
- ✅ **Cycle time** = Pump ON time + Pump OFF time (idle time); longer idle time = better pump life.
- ✅ **Sea water systems** use Cu-Ni 90/10, bronze, GRP lining — chloride-resistant materials.
- ✅ **Fresh water systems** must protect potability — use stainless steel 316L or food-grade epoxy.
- ✅ **Safety devices** on every hydrophore: Relief valve (set ~5.5–6.0 bar), pressure gauge, low-air alarm, NRVs.
- ✅ **Common KTU mistakes:** Forgetting absolute vs gauge, confusing P₁ and P₂, omitting the schematic label, no assumptions stated.
- ✅ **Energy equation:** Pump power $W = \rho g Q H / \eta$ — always state density (1000 for FW, 1025 for SW).
- ✅ **Class society rules:** IACS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, BV — all require redundancy and relief valves.
- ✅ **Modern trend:** Membrane (bladder) type hydrophore tanks are replacing direct air-water contact tanks to reduce air dissolution losses.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deck Cranes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deck Cranes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Deck Cranes — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deck Crane (Marine):** A pedestal-mounted, slewing, luffing or fixed-boom cargo handling machine installed on the weather deck of a merchant vessel, used for loading, discharging, and self-handling of cargo, ship stores, provisions, and bunker fuel between the ship and the shore or barges.

A marine deck crane is fundamentally a **rotating, articulated lifting appliance** designed to operate in harsh marine environments characterized by salt spray, heeling, pitching, rolling motions, dynamic wind loading, and confined deck spaces. According to classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV) and the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** Code of Safe Working Practice for Merchant Seafarers, every shipboard crane must comply with the standards of a recognized classification society and be subjected to **Prototype Testing, Load Testing, and Periodic Thorough Examinations**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology you must memorize:**
> - **SWL (Safe Working Load):** The maximum load that a crane is authorized to lift under specified conditions, clearly marked on the crane structure.
> - **SLE (Single Lift Equivalent):** A load considered to apply the same dynamic forces as a single lift event.
> - **CE (Characteristic Effort):** The rated effort at which the hoisting mechanism is designed to operate.
> - **AHC (Active Heave Compensation):** A system that counteracts vessel motion, used in offshore cranes for subsea operations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in the middle of a giant LEGO turntable, holding a fishing rod with a heavy fish on the line. The turntable lets you swing left or right to face the fish landing spot (**slewing motion**), you can raise or lower the rod tip to control the height (**luffing motion**), and the winch on the rod lets you pull the fish up and down (**hoisting motion**). Now imagine the turntable is on a small boat that is rocking side-to-side and up-and-down, and a strong wind keeps trying to push the rod. That is essentially what a deck crane does — it performs **three coordinated motions** (slewing, luffing, hoisting) while compensating for ship motion and wind to safely move cargo from pier to ship or vice versa.

The **pedestal** of the crane is the structural column that rises from the ship's deck and houses the slewing ring bearing — analogous to the central pivot of the turntable. The **boom** is the long arm (fishing rod), the **jib** is the optional extension tip of the boom, the **hoist winch** does the lifting, and the **hook block** is the heavy fish at the end.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use shore cranes?** A vessel calling at a small, undeveloped port (with no port-side gantry crane) must **self-discharge**. The deck crane is the ship's own portable cargo handling solution. This is the entire rationale for self-sustaining cargo operations on **multipurpose vessels, general cargo ships, bulk carriers with deck cranes, and supply vessels**.

## 1.3 Operational Context on a Modern Vessel

Modern merchant ships that employ deck cranes as primary cargo gear include:

- **General Cargo Ships** (single or multiple cranes per side)
- **Multi-Purpose Vessels (MPV)** with heavy-lift cranes
- **Bulk Carriers** equipped with deck cranes for self-loading/discharging
- **Reefer Ships** (for refrigerated cargo handling)
- **Container Feeder Vessels** (smaller port reach)
- **OSV (Offshore Supply Vessels)** with Active Heave Compensated (AHC) cranes
- **Dredgers** with trailing pipe hoists

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Design Metric:** A typical merchant vessel deck crane has an **SWL between 5 tonnes and 40 tonnes** at standard outreach. Heavy-lift cranes on multi-purpose vessels can have SWL of **120 tonnes, 300 tonnes, or higher** with tandem (two-crane) lift capability up to **1,200 tonnes** combined.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Deck Cranes

Deck cranes are broadly classified by their **power source, structural type, and operational mode**.

### A. By Power Source
| Type | Drive | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Electric-Hydraulic Crane** | Electric motor → Hydraulic pump → Hydraulic motors/cylinders | Most common on modern vessels |
| **Electric Crane** | Electric motor → Wire rope drum | Older designs, lower maintenance |
| **Electro-Mechanical Crane** | Squirrel cage motor with mechanical gearboxes | Simplified design, fixed mounting |
| **Diesel-Hydraulic Crane** | Ship's auxiliary diesel or independent engine | Emergency or non-electrified vessels |

### B. By Structural / Operational Type
| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Pedestal / King-Post Crane** | Single column supporting slewing platform; most common type |
| **Gantry Crane** | Travels on rails across the deck; used in heavy lift |
| **Jib Crane** | Fixed jib with limited slewing; used for stores handling |
| **Provision Crane** | Small SWL (1–3 t) for ship's stores; often on monkey island |
| **Heavy-Lift Crane** | SWL >100 t, twin pedestal or twin boom |
| **Knuckle-Boom Crane** | Articulated boom with hydraulic folding; used in offshore |
| **Telescopic Boom Crane** | Extending boom for variable outreach |

### C. By Duty Cycle
- **Single-Purpose / One-Off Lift** (project cargo)
- **Continuous Duty** (grabbing/discharging bulk)
- **Intermittent Duty** (general cargo on/off)

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics to Memorize

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The numbers below are board-favorite constants — write them down!**
> - **Prototype Test Load:** **1.25 × SWL** (25% overload) — applied before a new crane enters service.
> - **Annual Thorough Examination Test Load:** **1.25 × SWL** (Class survey requirement).
> - **10-Yearly Overhaul Load Test:** **1.25 × SWL** — full functional test after major refit.
> - **Standard Proof Load Factor (per ISO 12482, ISO 12480):** **1.25 to 1.5 × SWL** depending on crane class.
> - **Design Wind Pressure (operational):** typically **150 N/m²** for shipboard cranes; **250 N/m²** for storm condition.
> - **Maximum Crane List Angle during operation:** **5° static, 7° combined (static + dynamic)** per most class rules.
> - **Maximum Trim Angle during operation:** **2° to 3°** per class rules.
> - **Slewing speed range:** **0.5 to 1.5 rpm**.
> - **Hoisting speed range:** typically **10 to 30 m/min** at full load; faster at light load.
