# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of an Electro-Hydraulic Deck Crane

The vast majority of modern deck cranes on KTU-syllabus-relevant vessels are **electro-hydraulic pedestal cranes**. The energy conversion chain is:

**Electrical Energy → Mechanical Energy (Electric Motor) → Hydraulic Pressure Energy (Pump) → Mechanical Motion (Hydraulic Motor / Cylinder) → Linear or Rotary Motion (Load)**

> [!NOTE]
> **The "why hydraulic?" question is a KTU favorite.** Hydraulic systems are preferred on ships because:
> 1. They provide smooth, infinitely variable speed control.
> 2. They develop high torque at low RPM without complex gear trains.
> 3. They incorporate **overload protection** via relief valves.
> 4. They self-lubricate and resist marine corrosion with proper sealing.
> 5. They allow compact, modular installation in confined deck spaces.

## 2.2 The Four Functional Subsystems of a Deck Crane

Every deck crane, regardless of type, can be broken down into **four functional subsystems** — this is the KTU exam-style analytical framework.

### Subsystem 1: The **Hoisting System** (Luffing winch + Hoist winch)
The **hoist winch** raises and lowers the load via the wire rope wound on a grooved drum. The **luffing winch** (or **luffing cylinder** in hydraulic cranes) raises and lowers the boom, changing the working radius.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hook Velocity Relation:** The load lifts faster on a single-part reeving than on a multi-part reeving because of mechanical advantage (load × speed = power, constant for ideal systems).

### Subsystem 2: The **Slewing System** (Slewing ring + Slewing motor)
The **slewing ring** is a large diameter, pre-loaded, anti-friction (ball or roller) bearing connecting the rotating superstructure to the fixed pedestal. A **slewing motor** (hydraulic or electric) drives a pinion gear that engages the internal or external teeth of the slewing ring, allowing the entire upper crane to rotate through 360° (or 370° with end-limit stops for cable management).

### Subsystem 3: The **Power Pack / Hydraulic System** (Pump + Valves + Tank)
The **hydraulic power pack** consists of an electric motor, a hydraulic pump (typically axial piston, variable displacement), a reservoir tank, control valves (directional, flow, pressure), filters, and accumulators. It generates the high-pressure hydraulic fluid (typically **250 to 350 bar** working pressure) that powers the crane's actuators.

### Subsystem 4: The **Structural System** (Pedestal, Boom, Jib, A-frame)
The **pedestal** is the welded column fixed to the ship's deck with a base ring and bolted connection (or welded for permanent installation). The **boom** is the primary lifting arm, often of **box-girder construction** for high stiffness-to-weight ratio. The **A-frame** (or gantry) at the top supports the boom pendant ropes and provides the structural tie that converts boom weight and load forces into vertical and horizontal reactions at the pedestal.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes every formula you may need for deck crane numerical problems in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_{SWL} = m_{load} \times g$ | Conversion of load mass to weight | $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$ |
| 2 | $M_{overturn} = W_{SWL} \times R$ | Overturning moment at pedestal base | $M$ in $\text{t·m}$ or $\text{kN·m}$ |
| 3 | $T_{hoist} = \dfrac{W_{SWL} \times v_{hoist}}{\eta_{total}}$ | Hoisting power requirement | $T$ in $\text{kW}$, $\eta$ = efficiency |
| 4 | $P_{hyd}} = \dfrac{Q \times p}{600}$ | Hydraulic power (Q in L/min, p in bar) | $P$ in $\text{kW}$ |
| 5 | $F_{rope, single-part} = W_{SWL}$ | Single-part line tension | Equal to load weight |
| 6 | $F_{rope, n-part} = \dfrac{W_{SWL}}{n \times \eta_{reeve}}$ | Tension in n-part reeving | $\eta_{reeve}$ per sheave ≈ 0.97 |
| 7 | $F_{wind} = p_{wind} \times A_{boom}$ | Wind force on boom | $p$ in $\text{N/m}^2$, $A$ projected area |
| 8 | $\sigma_{boom} = \dfrac{M_{bend}}{Z_{section}}$ | Bending stress in boom | $Z$ = section modulus |
| 9 | $n_{factor} = \dfrac{T_{yield, rope}}{F_{rope, max}}$ | Factor of safety on wire rope | Class rules: $n \ge 3$ to $5$ |
| 10 | $T_{test} = 1.25 \times SWL$ | Proof load for testing | Per ISO & Class rules |
| 11 | $R_{min} \times W_{max} = R_{max} \times W_{min}$ | Load-radius chart (hyperbola) | Constant for constant moment cranes |
| 12 | $D_{drum} \ge (18 \text{ to } 25) \times d_{rope}$ | Minimum drum/sheave diameter | $D$ = drum diameter, $d$ = rope diameter |
| 13 | $M_{stab} \ge 1.3 \times M_{overturn}$ | Stability moment for class approval | Class requirement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to express absolute value** when writing such formulas on the exam, never the vertical bar `|`, as the table parser can break. Example: $\vert M_{overturn} \vert$.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters on Board

The formulas above are not academic curiosities; they are applied every day:

- **Chief Officer / Chief Mate** uses formula 11 (load-radius chart) to determine the **maximum load that may be lifted at a given radius** before each lift. This chart is permanently posted in the crane cab.
- **Chief Engineer** uses formula 4 to size or verify the hydraulic power pack and to estimate fuel consumption.
- **Master** uses formula 9 to verify the **wire rope certificate's factor of safety** before each job.
- **Surveyor** uses formula 10 during the annual load test.

## 2.5 The Load-Radius Chart (A KTU Hot Topic)

The **load-radius chart** is a plot of the **maximum permitted SWL (in tonnes) versus the radius from the crane centerline (in metres)**. Two design philosophies exist:

### (a) Constant-Moment Design (Hyperbolic SWL)
$$SWL(R) = \dfrac{M_{max}}{R}$$

The maximum allowable load decreases as the radius increases, so that the product $SWL \times R$ remains bounded by a maximum permissible moment. **Most shipboard cranes use this design** because the pedestal, slewing ring, and structural base are sized for a fixed moment.

### (b) Constant-Power Design
$$SWL(R) = \dfrac{P_{max}}{R \times \omega_{slew}}$$

The hoist motor power limits the achievable load at high radius. This is less common on merchant ship cranes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When asked "If the SWL at 10 m radius is 30 tonnes, what is the SWL at 20 m radius for a constant-moment crane?", students often forget the units and use raw tonnes. **Always convert SWL to force (kN or t-force) first if mixing with moment values.**

**Worked example (mental check):**
- $SWL_{10} = 30 \; t$ at $R = 10 \; m \Rightarrow M_{max} = 30 \times 10 = 300 \; \text{t·m}$
- $SWL_{20} = \dfrac{300}{20} = 15 \; t$ at $R = 20 \; m$. ✓

---

## 2.6 Wire Rope Reeving Systems

The **reeving arrangement** determines the mechanical advantage and the speed of the hook. Common configurations:

| Reeving | Mechanical Advantage | Hook Speed | Hoist Force |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single-part (no sheave) | 1 | Fast | $W_{SWL}$ |
| 2-part | 2 | Half | $W_{SWL}/2$ |
| 4-part | 4 | Quarter | $W_{SWL}/4$ |
| 6-part | 6 | Sixth | $W_{SWL}/6$ |

The **dead end** of the wire rope is attached to either the boom tip (top) or the hook block (bottom). The **live end** is wound on the hoist drum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use multi-part reeving?** It allows a smaller, lighter hoist motor and drum to lift a heavier load, at the cost of slower hook speed. For heavy SWL cranes (40 t+), 4-part or 6-part reeving is standard.

---

## 2.7 Structural Mechanics of the Pedestal Crane

A free-body diagram of a pedestal crane shows that the **load at the hook** creates:

- A **vertical downward force** on the pedestal base.
- An **overturning moment** about the pedestal base (when boom is off-center).
- A **radial horizontal force** that tends to topple the crane in the direction of the boom.

This is resisted by:

- The **pedestal base moment** (welded/bolted to deck).
- The **A-frame pendant system** (backstay tension) which transfers part of the overturning moment into the A-frame and back to the pedestal.
- The **ship's GM (metacentric height)** and **ballast system**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Class Rule (DNV / Lloyd's):** The maximum overturning moment on the pedestal base under a load of 1.25 × SWL must not exceed **75% of the moment capacity of the pedestal base connection** to maintain a 1.3 factor of safety.

---

## 2.8 Safety Devices and Limit Switches

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically tests safety device knowledge. Every modern deck crane must have:

| # | Safety Device | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hoist Upper Limit Switch** | Stops hoist before block-to-boom collision |
| 2 | **Hoist Lower Limit Switch** | Prevents over-unwinding of rope from drum |
| 3 | **Anti-Two-Block (A2B) Device** | Detects hook block approaching boom tip; cuts hoist power |
| 4 | **Luffing Up Limit Switch** | Prevents boom over-luffing past maximum angle |
| 5 | **Luffing Down Limit Switch** | Prevents boom hitting the deck or structure |
| 6 | **Slewing End Limit** | Stops slewing before cable damage (2 end positions) |
| 7 | **Load Moment Indicator (LMI)** | Displays actual load and warns when approaching SWL |
| 8 | **Safe Load Indicator (SLI)** | Cuts hoist/luffing power at 90% SWL; full stop at 110% SWL |
| 9 | **Anemometer (Wind Speed Indicator)** | Visual/audible warning if wind > operational limit (typically 20 m/s) |
| 10 | **Emergency Stop (E-Stop)** | Cuts all power to crane immediately |
| 11 | **Overload Relief Valve** | Hydraulic circuit protection |
| 12 | **Outriggers / Pad-eyes** | Crane pedestal may have deck-tie pad-eyes for storm securing |
| 13 | **Limit Switch on Slewing Ring Bolt** | Indicates loose slewing ring bolts |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** The terms **LMI** (Load Moment Indicator) and **SLI** (Safe Load Indicator) are NOT synonymous in marine classification. **LMI** measures moment (load × radius) and is the more sophisticated device. **SLI** measures pure load. Many students incorrectly use them interchangeably in answers and lose **1–2 marks** per question.

---

## 2.9 Operational Limits and Environmental Constraints

The KTU syllabus emphasizes the environmental envelope in which the crane may operate:

- **Maximum Wind Speed (operational):** typically **20 m/s** (≈ 8 Beaufort, gale)
- **Maximum Wind Speed (out of service, crane secured):** typically **55 m/s**
- **Maximum List (operational):** **5° static, 7° combined**
- **Maximum Trim (operational):** **2° to 3°**
- **Minimum Ambient Temperature (operational):** **−20 °C** for most class-approved cranes
- **Sea State Limit (operational):** typically up to **SS-3** for cargo handling
- **Vessel Motion Compensation (AHC only):** required for offshore supply and subsea cranes.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Permissible Load on a Constant-Moment Crane

A KTU 2024 module favorite — derive the SWL at any radius from a given maximum design moment.

**Problem Statement (typical KTU problem):**
> A pedestal crane is rated for a maximum overturning moment of **300 t·m** at the pedestal base. The SWL at **8 m** radius is **30 t**. Determine: (a) The SWL at **20 m** radius. (b) The maximum allowable radius for an SWL of **10 t**. (c) The required minimum counterweight moment to maintain stability when lifting 30 t at 8 m on a 5° list.

### Step 1: Convert the given SWL to a working load force (weight).
The mass in tonnes is conventionally treated as a weight in tonne-force (1 t ≈ 9.81 kN, but for marine simplicity, 1 t mass = 1 t-force under Earth's gravity). We use tonnes-force throughout for clarity.

$$W_{SWL, 8m} = 30 \; \text{tf}$$

### Step 2: Compute the design moment at the given point.
The maximum overturning moment about the pedestal base equals the SWL times the working radius:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{max} &= W_{SWL, 8m} \times R_{8m} \\
M_{max} &= 30 \; \text{tf} \times 8 \; \text{m} \\
M_{max} &= 240 \; \text{tf·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Note:** The KTU question states 300 t·m, but the SWL of 30 t at 8 m only generates 240 t·m. This means the **actual working moment** is 240 t·m, but the design allows up to 300 t·m. We use 300 t·m as the **rated maximum** for the design (this represents the worst-case combination of full SWL plus dynamic factors, per class rules).

### Step 3: Compute the SWL at 20 m radius using the constant-moment relation.
For a constant-moment crane, the load-radius product must not exceed the rated moment:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{max} &= W_{SWL, 20m} \times R_{20m} \\
W_{SWL, 20m} &= \dfrac{M_{max}}{R_{20m}} \\
W_{SWL, 20m} &= \dfrac{300 \; \text{tf·m}}{20 \; \text{m}} \\
W_{SWL, 20m} &= 15 \; \text{tf}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer to part (a):** SWL at 20 m radius = **15 tonnes**.

### Step 4: Compute the maximum allowable radius for a 10 t load.
Using the same constant-moment relation:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{max} &= W_{SWL, target} \times R_{max} \\
R_{max} &= \dfrac{M_{max}}{W_{SWL, target}} \\
R_{max} &= \dfrac{300 \; \text{tf·m}}{10 \; \text{tf}} \\
R_{max} &= 30 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer to part (b):** Maximum allowable radius for 10 t lift = **30 m**.

### Step 5: Compute the moment on a 5° list.
When the vessel is listed by angle $\theta$, the effective radius and the gravitational vector produce a **list-induced moment** that adds vectorially to the load moment. For small angles, the additional moment is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta M_{list} &= W_{SWL} \times h_{hook} \times \sin\theta
\end{aligned}$$

Where $h_{hook}$ is the vertical height of the hook above the pedestal base. If we assume the hook height is approximately equal to the working radius (boom is at 45° on average):

$$\begin{aligned}
h_{hook} &\approx R = 8 \; \text{m} \\
\Delta M_{list} &= 30 \; \text{tf} \times 8 \; \text{m} \times \sin(5°) \\
\Delta M_{list} &= 30 \times 8 \times 0.0872 \\
\Delta M_{list} &= 20.93 \; \text{tf·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Total effective moment:**
$$M_{total} = M_{SWL} + \Delta M_{list} = 240 + 20.93 = 260.93 \; \text{tf·m}$$

This is still below the 300 t·m rated maximum, so the lift is permitted. **Counterweight moment** would be required only if $M_{total} > M_{max}$.

### Step 6: Condition for counterweight requirement.
A counterweight on the opposite side of the pedestal must produce a moment that balances the excess. The required counterweight moment is:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{counterweight, required} &= M_{total} - M_{max} \\
M_{counterweight, required} &= 260.93 - 300 \\
M_{counterweight, required} &= -39.07 \; \text{tf·m}
\end{aligned}$$

The negative sign means **no counterweight is required** for this scenario. If a stronger list or heavier load were imposed, the counterweight moment would be positive and must be provided.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hoist Drum Power Requirement

**Problem:** A deck crane must lift a 20 t load at 12 m/min hook speed. The total efficiency of the hoist mechanism (motor, gearbox, sheaves, drum) is 78%. Determine: (a) The required hoist motor power. (b) The hydraulic motor power if the system pressure is 280 bar and the pump flow rate is 90 L/min.

### Step 1: Convert the load to force.
$$F_{load} = m \times g = 20,000 \; \text{kg} \times 9.81 \; m/s^2 = 196,200 \; \text{N} = 196.2 \; \text{kN}$$

### Step 2: Convert the hook speed to m/s.
$$v_{hook} = \dfrac{12 \; \text{m/min}}{60} = 0.2 \; \text{m/s}$$

### Step 3: Compute the ideal (100% efficient) power required.
$$P_{ideal} = F_{load} \times v_{hook} = 196,200 \; \text{N} \times 0.2 \; \text{m/s} = 39,240 \; \text{W} = 39.24 \; \text{kW}$$

### Step 4: Apply the mechanical efficiency to get the motor input power.
$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{ideal}}{\eta_{total}} = \dfrac{39.24}{0.78} = 50.31 \; \text{kW}$$

**Answer to part (a):** Required motor power ≈ **50.3 kW** (so a 55 kW motor is selected for safety margin).

### Step 5: Compute the hydraulic power using the alternate formula.
$$P_{hyd} = \dfrac{Q \times p}{600}$$

Where $Q$ is in L/min and $p$ is in bar, giving $P$ in kW.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{hyd} &= \dfrac{90 \; \text{L/min} \times 280 \; \text{bar}}{600} \\
P_{hyd} &= \dfrac{25,200}{600} \\
P_{hyd} &= 42.0 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer to part (b):** Hydraulic power available = **42 kW**, which is sufficient for the 39.24 kW ideal requirement (with some margin for the 78% efficiency of the hoist sheave system, but the hydraulic power is the input to the hoist motors, not the output at the hook).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Wire Rope Selection — Factor of Safety

**Problem:** A hoist drum lifts a 25 t SWL using a 4-part reeving. The sheave efficiency is 0.97 per sheave, and 3 sheaves are used in the system. The wire rope is a 6×36 IWRC construction with a minimum breaking load of **550 kN** at 28 mm diameter. Determine: (a) The maximum tension in any rope part. (b) The factor of safety. (c) Whether the rope is acceptable per class rules requiring $n \ge 5$.

### Step 1: Total reeving efficiency.
$$\eta_{total, reeve} = 0.97^3 = 0.9127$$

### Step 2: Load per rope part.
$$F_{rope} = \dfrac{W_{SWL}}{n \times \eta_{total, reeve}} = \dfrac{25 \times 9.81 \; \text{kN}}{4 \times 0.9127} = \dfrac{245.25}{3.651} = 67.16 \; \text{kN}$$

**Answer to part (a):** Maximum rope tension ≈ **67.2 kN**.

### Step 3: Factor of safety.
$$n_{factor} = \dfrac{T_{breaking}}{F_{rope, max}} = \dfrac{550 \; \text{kN}}{67.16 \; \text{kN}} = 8.19$$

**Answer to part (b):** Factor of safety ≈ **8.2**.

### Step 4: Class rule compliance.
Class rules for shipboard crane hoisting ropes require a minimum factor of safety of **5.0 for general duty, 6.0 for personnel handling, 4.0 for grab duty**.

Since $n = 8.2 > 5.0$, the rope is **acceptable for general cargo duty**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The student should always convert the SWL from tonnes to kN using $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$ before mixing with rope breaking loads given in kN. A common error is to treat 25 t as 25 kN, which is off by a factor of 9.81 — a guaranteed **2–3 mark loss** in any ESE numerical.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Crane Selection Decision Table

The following matrix summarizes how a Chief Officer would select a deck crane configuration for a given cargo task. This is useful for KTU design-oriented questions.

| Cargo Task | Recommended SWL | Recommended Outreach | Reeving | Boom Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| General palletized cargo | 10–20 t | 15–25 m | 2-part | Telescopic or lattice |
| Heavy machinery | 30–50 t | 10–20 m | 4-part | Lattice boom with A-frame |
| Bulk grabs | 15–25 t | 20–30 m | 4-part | Single rope grab |
| Containers (20 ft) | 25–30 t | 20–25 m | 4-part | Telescopic |
| Project cargo | 100–500 t | 10–15 m | 8–12 part | Heavy-lift, twin pedestal |
| Ship's stores (provision) | 1–3 t | 5–10 m | Single-part | Small pedestal |

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Annual Thorough Examination Load Test Schedule

**Problem:** A crane with SWL = 30 t must undergo a Class-approved load test. Determine: (a) The required test load for the **annual thorough examination**. (b) The test sequence (lifting, lowering, slewing, luffing). (c) The criterion for passing.

### Step 1: Calculate the test load.
Per **ISO 12482 / MLC 134 / Class rules**, the annual test load is **1.25 × SWL** for general cargo cranes:

$$W_{test} = 1.25 \times 30 \; \text{t} = 37.5 \; \text{t}$$

### Step 2: Test sequence.
1. Apply test load statically at minimum radius. Hold for **5 minutes**. Check for permanent deformation.
2. Hoist test load at full hoist speed through full lift range. Check hoist motor and brake.
3. Lower test load with full load on the brake (lowering test).
4. Slew test load through 360° (or ±185° from centerline) at full slewing speed. Check slewing ring.
5. Luff boom through full range with test load. Check luffing system.
6. Lower test load and check for residual deflection in boom.

### Step 3: Pass criterion.
- **Permanent deformation:** None permitted.
- **Wire rope:** No broken strands, no bird-caging.
- **Hydraulic system:** No leaks, no pressure drop > 5% during hold.
- **Brakes:** Must hold 1.25 × SWL statically with no drift for 5 minutes.
- **Electrical:** All limit switches must function correctly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Test load is removed after the test.** The crane returns to service at its rated SWL, not the test load. This is a frequently confused point.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Marine Deck Crane

The following block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a typical electro-hydraulic pedestal deck crane, showing how power flows from the ship's electrical bus to the lifted cargo.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ship Main Switchboard 440V 60Hz] --> B[Crane Main Isolator]
    B --> C[Slip Ring Assembly on Pedestal]
    C --> D[Cabin Control Console]
    C --> E[Power Pack Motor Starter]
    E --> F[Hydraulic Power Pack]
    F --> G[Hoist Hydraulic Motor]
    F --> H[Luffing Hydraulic Motor]
    F --> I[Slewing Hydraulic Motor]
    G --> J[Hoist Drum and Wire Rope]
    H --> K[Luffing Winch and Boom Pendant]
    I --> L[Slewing Ring and Pinion]
    J --> M[Hook Block and Cargo]
    K --> N[Boom and Jib]
    L --> O[Crane Superstructure]
    F --> P[Safety Relief and Control Valves]
    P --> G
    P --> H
    P --> I
    D --> Q[Load Moment Indicator LMI]
    D --> R[Anemometer and Wind Speed Display]
    D --> S[Limit Switch Array]
    S --> G
    S --> H
    S --> I
    M --> T[Lifted Cargo]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **ship's electrical bus** feeds the **crane isolator**, which is the main circuit breaker for the crane.
- Power is transferred across the rotating interface via a **slip ring assembly** (so that the cab and motors can rotate without cable tangling).
- Inside the cab, the **operator** uses the **control console** to command all motions.
- The **power pack** converts electrical energy to hydraulic pressure, which drives the three motion motors.
- The **safety system** (relief valves, LMI, limit switches, anemometer) constantly monitors the system and cuts power if any parameter exceeds its limit.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Hydraulic Circuit Schematic (Conceptual)

The following diagram represents a simplified **open-center hydraulic circuit** for a deck crane. Note that real cranes use load-sensing (LS) closed-center systems, but the conceptual model is sufficient for KTU theory.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TANK[Reservoir Tank] --> PUMP[Variable Displacement Pump]
    PUMP --> FILT[High Pressure Filter]
    FILT --> DIV[Main Divider Combiner Valve]
    DIV --> H1[Hoist Motor A]
    DIV --> H2[Hoist Motor B]
    DIV --> L[Luffing Motor]
    DIV --> S1[Slewing Motor A]
    DIV --> S2[Slewing Motor B]
    DIV --> RELIEF[Main Relief Valve 350 bar]
    RELIEF --> TANK
    H1 --> BRK1[Hoist Brake]
    H2 --> BRK1
    BRK1 --> DRUM[Hoist Drum]
    L --> BRK2[Luffing Brake]
    BRK2 --> BOOM[Boom Luffing Gear]
    S1 --> BRK3[Slewing Brake]
    S2 --> BRK3
    BRK3 --> RING[Slewing Ring Drive Pinion]
    ACC[Accumulator 20L 330 bar] --> DIV
    ACC --- CHG[Charging Check Valve]
    CHG --> PUMP
```

**Key elements:**
- **Pump:** Provides flow at system pressure.
- **Main Relief Valve:** Limits maximum system pressure to prevent hose/motor damage.
- **Divider-Combiner:** Allows simultaneous operation of multiple motions (e.g., hoist + slew) while maintaining proportional flow sharing.
- **Brakes:** Spring-applied, hydraulically released fail-safe brakes on each motion.
- **Accumulator:** Stores hydraulic energy for emergency braking and absorbs pressure spikes.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Operational Sequence of a Single Lift

The following state diagram describes the **lifecycle of a single cargo lift operation** from start to finish.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PreLiftCheck
    PreLiftCheck --> CraneSelected: SWL and radius verified
    CraneSelected --> BoomPositioned: Luff and slew to pick point
    BoomPositioned --> HookLowered: Lower hook to cargo
    HookLowered --> CargoAttached: Slings or hook engaged
    CargoAttached --> SlackTaken: Hoist until slack removed
    SlackTaken --> TrialLift: Lift 0.2 m and hold 60 seconds
    TrialLift --> FullLift: Confirm balance, proceed
    FullLift --> SlewToDestination: Slew 360 as needed
    SlewToDestination --> LowerToReceiver: Lower to shore or hatch
    LowerToReceiver --> CargoReleased: Cargo unhooked
    CargoReleased --> HookRecovered: Hoist to clear position
    HookRecovered --> BoomReturned: Slew and luff to park position
    BoomReturned --> ParkedAndSecured: Power off, storm pins engaged
    ParkedAndSecured --> [*]
    TrialLift --> Aborted: Imbalance or swing detected
    Aborted --> HookRecovered
    FullLift --> Aborted: Wind shift or overload
    Aborted --> LowerToReceiver: Emergency lower
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Every lift must begin with a **pre-lift check** (SWL vs. radius, weather, list, trim).
- The **trial lift** (hoisting 0.2 m off the deck and holding for 60 seconds) is a mandatory step to verify the load is balanced and the slings are correctly engaged.
- The system can transition to an **aborted** state at any point where safety is in question.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Safety Device Interlock Hierarchy

The following block diagram shows the **layered safety architecture** of a modern deck crane, illustrating the priority of safety devices.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Level 1: Operator Cabin E-Stop] --> L2
    L2[Level 2: Anemometer Trip at 20 m/s] --> L3
    L3[Level 3: Safe Load Indicator SLI at 110 percent SWL] --> L4
    L4[Level 4: Load Moment Indicator LMI] --> L5
    L5[Level 5: Anti Two Block A2B Device] --> L6
    L6[Level 6: Hoist Upper and Lower Limits] --> L7
    L7[Level 7: Luffing Up and Down Limits] --> L8
    L8[Level 8: Slewing End Limits] --> L9
    L9[Level 9: Overload Relief Valve 350 bar] --> L10
    L10[Level 10: Slewing Ring Bolt Monitor] --> L11
    L11[Action: Cut Power or Sound Alarm]:::action
    classDef action fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The hierarchy is intentionally **redundant** — if the operator fails to stop a motion, the LMI/SLI does; if the LMI fails, the relief valve does; and so on.
- This is a **defense-in-depth** design philosophy mandated by SOLAS and class rules.

## 4.5 Mermaid Block — Wire Rope Reeving (4-Part System)

The following diagram traces the path of a wire rope in a 4-part reeving system for a heavy-lift deck crane.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D[Hoist Drum on Crane Machinery House] -->|Live end wraps on drum| RP1[Sheave 1 at Boom Tip]
    RP1 --> RP2[Sheave 2 in Hook Block]
    RP2 --> RP3[Sheave 3 at Boom Tip]
    RP3 --> RP4[Sheave 4 in Hook Block]
    RP4 --> DEAD[Dead End Secured to Machinery House or Boom Base]
    HB[Hook Block with 4 Supporting Sheaves]:::hook
    RP2 --- HB
    RP4 --- HB
    classDef hook fill:#C0E0FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **live end** is wound onto the drum.
- The rope alternates between **boom tip sheaves** and **hook block sheaves**, creating 4 supporting parts to the load.
- The **dead end** is fixed and bears the entire dynamic load if the live end breaks (it is also a critical safety feature).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define Safe Working Load (SWL) of a marine deck crane. What is the standard proof load factor for the prototype test of a new deck crane as per classification society rules?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition of SWL (2 marks):** The Safe Working Load (SWL) of a deck crane is the maximum load (in tonnes or kilograms) that the crane is authorized to lift in service under the specified operating conditions, with the load, radius, and environmental factors (wind, list, trim) all within their design envelopes. It is permanently marked on the crane structure, typically on the boom and the operator's cab.
- **Standard proof load factor (1 mark):** The standard prototype proof load for a new deck crane is **1.25 × SWL** (i.e., 25% overload) as required by ISO 12482, ISO 12480, and the major classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV).

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **List any four (4) essential safety devices fitted on a modern electro-hydraulic marine deck crane. Briefly state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 marks per device):**
1. **Safe Load Indicator (SLI):** Measures the actual suspended load and compares it to the SWL; cuts hoist power and sounds alarm at 110% SWL.
2. **Anti-Two-Block (A2B) Device:** Detects when the hook block approaches the boom tip; cuts hoist power to prevent rope-on-rope damage.
3. **Hoist Upper Limit Switch:** Stops the hoist motor before the hook block collides with the boom tip.
4. **Luffing Up Limit Switch:** Prevents the boom from over-luffing past the maximum permissible angle, which would overload the A-frame and backstay.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3A.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024 — Internal Choice Set 1] **(a)** With the aid of a suitable sketch, describe the **four functional subsystems** of a typical electro-hydraulic pedestal deck crane installed on a general cargo vessel. **(b)** A deck crane has a maximum overturning moment capacity of **240 t·m** at the pedestal base. Calculate (i) the SWL at a working radius of **15 m**, (ii) the maximum permissible working radius for lifting a load of **8 tonnes**, and (iii) the additional list-induced moment when the vessel is listed **5°** with the hook at a vertical height of **10 m** above the pedestal base while lifting the full 15-m radius load.

### Part (a) Model Answer (7 marks):

The four functional subsystems are:

**1. Hoisting and Luffing System (2 marks):** The hoisting system comprises the **hoist winch** (with wire rope drum, hoist motor, and brake) that raises and lowers the load. The **luffing system** (luffing winch with boom pendant ropes, or luffing hydraulic cylinder) raises and lowers the boom, varying the working radius. The wire rope is reeved through sheaves at the boom tip and hook block.

**2. Slewing System (2 marks):** The slewing system consists of a **slewing ring** (large-diameter anti-friction bearing) mounted between the rotating superstructure and the fixed pedestal, and a **slewing drive** (hydraulic motor + pinion, or electric slew motor with gear reducer). It rotates the entire upper crane through 360° continuously (or 370° with cable management end-stops).

**3. Hydraulic Power Pack (1.5 marks):** The power pack comprises an **electric drive motor**, a **variable-displacement axial piston pump**, a **hydraulic oil reservoir**, **filters**, **heat exchangers**, and a **control valve bank**. It generates hydraulic fluid at working pressures of **250 to 350 bar** to power the crane's actuators.

**4. Structural System (1.5 marks):** The structural system includes the **pedestal** (welded column fixed to the ship's deck with a base ring), the **boom** (lattice or box-girder construction), the **A-frame** (or gantry) at the top that supports the boom pendant ropes, and the **operator's cab** mounted on the slewing superstructure.

### Part (b) Model Solution (7 marks):

**Given:**
- $M_{max} = 240 \; \text{t·m}$
- $R = 15 \; \text{m}$ (for part i)
- $W = 8 \; \text{t}$ (for part ii)
- $\theta = 5°$, $h_{hook} = 10 \; \text{m}$ (for part iii)

**(i) SWL at 15 m radius (3 marks):**
For a constant-moment design:
$$\begin{aligned}
W_{SWL, 15m} &= \dfrac{M_{max}}{R} \\
&= \dfrac{240 \; \text{t·m}}{15 \; \text{m}} \\
&= 16 \; \text{t}
\end{aligned}$$
**Answer:** SWL at 15 m radius = **16 tonnes**.

**(ii) Maximum radius for 8 t lift (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
R_{max, 8t} &= \dfrac{M_{max}}{W} \\
&= \dfrac{240 \; \text{t·m}}{8 \; \text{t}} \\
&= 30 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$
**Answer:** Maximum radius for 8 t = **30 m**.

**(iii) List-induced moment (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta M_{list} &= W_{SWL, 15m} \times h_{hook} \times \sin\theta \\
&= 16 \; \text{t} \times 10 \; \text{m} \times \sin(5°) \\
&= 16 \times 10 \times 0.0872 \\
&= 13.95 \; \text{t·m}
\end{aligned}$$
**Answer:** Additional list-induced moment ≈ **13.95 t·m**.

**Verification of safety:**
$$\begin{aligned}
M_{total} &= M_{SWL} + \Delta M_{list} \\
&= (16 \times 15) + 13.95 \\
&= 240 + 13.95 \\
&= 253.95 \; \text{t·m}
\end{aligned}$$
Since $M_{total} = 253.95 > M_{max} = 240 \; \text{t·m}$, the lift **exceeds the rated moment** by ~5.8% and is **NOT permitted** without reducing the load, lowering the hook height, or waiting for the list to subside. This conclusion is worth 1 mark and is the hallmark of a complete engineering answer.

---

> **Q3B.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024 — Internal Choice Set 1 — Alternative] **(a)** Explain the **wire rope reeving system** used in a marine deck crane. With the help of a diagram, describe **4-part reeving** and discuss its advantages. **(b)** A 25 t SWL deck crane uses a 4-part reeving system with 3 sheaves (efficiency 0.97 each) and a wire rope of 28 mm diameter. The wire rope's minimum breaking load is **550 kN**. Calculate: (i) the maximum tension in any single rope part, (ii) the actual factor of safety, and (iii) state with reasons whether the rope is acceptable for general cargo duty per class rules.

### Part (a) Model Answer (7 marks):

**Wire Rope Reeving — Concept (2 marks):** A **reeving system** is the arrangement by which the wire rope is threaded between the hoist drum, the boom tip sheaves, and the hook block sheaves. The number of rope parts supporting the load determines the **mechanical advantage** and hence the hoist force and speed.

**4-Part Reeving — Description (3 marks):**
- The **live end** of the rope is wound on the hoist drum.
- The rope passes up to **Sheave 1** at the boom tip, down to **Sheave A** in the hook block, up to **Sheave 2** at the boom tip, and down to **Sheave B** in the hook block.
- The **dead end** is secured to either the boom tip or the machinery house structure.
- The load is supported by **4 parallel rope parts**, each carrying approximately 1/4 of the total load.

**Advantages of 4-Part Reeving (2 marks):**
1. **Reduced hoist motor torque** — only 1/4 of the load on the drum, allowing a smaller, lighter motor.
2. **Reduced drum size and rope diameter** for the same SWL.
3. **Increased safety factor** on each rope part, since the load is distributed.
4. **Slower hook speed**, which is desirable for heavy, controlled lifts.
5. **Improved load control** during lowering, as the brake acts on a smaller effective force.

### Part (b) Model Solution (7 marks):

**Given:**
- $W_{SWL} = 25 \; \text{t}$
- $n_{parts} = 4$
- Sheaves = 3, $\eta_{sheave} = 0.97$
- $d_{rope} = 28 \; \text{mm}$
- $T_{MBL} = 550 \; \text{kN}$

**(i) Maximum tension in a single rope part (3 marks):**
Total reeving efficiency:
$$\eta_{total} = 0.97^3 = 0.9127$$

Load on the load hook:
$$F_{load} = 25,000 \; \text{kg} \times 9.81 \; m/s^2 = 245,250 \; \text{N} = 245.25 \; \text{kN}$$

Maximum tension per rope part (worst case, accounting for friction losses in the highest-friction sheave):
$$F_{rope, max} = \dfrac{F_{load}}{n \times \eta_{total}} = \dfrac{245.25}{4 \times 0.9127} = \dfrac{245.25}{3.651} = 67.17 \; \text{kN}$$

**Answer:** Maximum tension = **67.17 kN**.

**(ii) Actual factor of safety (2 marks):**
$$n_{actual} = \dfrac{T_{MBL}}{F_{rope, max}} = \dfrac{550 \; \text{kN}}{67.17 \; \text{kN}} = 8.19$$

**Answer:** Factor of safety = **8.19**.

**(iii) Class rule compliance (2 marks):**
- **Class rule minimum factor of safety for general cargo duty:** $n \ge 5.0$
- **Personnel handling:** $n \ge 6.0$
- **Grab duty:** $n \ge 4.0$
- Since $n_{actual} = 8.19 > 5.0$, the rope is **acceptable for general cargo duty** with a comfortable safety margin.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always convert tonnes to kN** using $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$ when mixing with rope breaking loads. Treating 1 t = 1 kN is the **#1 reason for full-mark loss** in crane numericals.
> 2. **Forgetting the list-induced moment** in stability calculations is the **#2 reason for partial loss** in lift-analysis questions. The question always contains the angle and hook height for a reason.
> 3. **Confusing SLI with LMI** loses **1–2 marks per occurrence** in safety device questions.
> 4. **Forgetting the "constant-moment" assumption** when computing SWL at a different radius leads to wrong answers; always state the assumption.
> 5. **Not stating the units** (tonnes, kN, t·m) in numerical answers will cost **0.5 to 1 mark per question** per occurrence.
> 6. In reeving calculations, **forgetting the sheave efficiency** (and using 100% efficiency) results in a too-low tension estimate, which can falsely pass a rope that is actually undersize. This is a **safety-critical error** in real life.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

### A. Definitions
- **SWL (Safe Working Load):** Maximum authorized lift; clearly marked on the crane.
- **Proof Load:** 1.25 × SWL for prototype and annual tests.
- **SLE (Single Lift Equivalent):** A standard reference load for fatigue assessment.
- **AHC (Active Heave Compensation):** Offshore crane feature counteracting vessel motion.

### B. Numerical / Formula
- $W_{SWL}(R) = \dfrac{M_{max}}{R}$ (constant-moment crane)
- $P_{hoist} = \dfrac{W_{SWL} \times v_{hook}}{\eta_{total}}$
- $P_{hyd} = \dfrac{Q \times p}{600}$ ($Q$ in L/min, $p$ in bar, $P$ in kW)
- $F_{rope, n-part} = \dfrac{W_{SWL}}{n \times \eta_{reeve}}$
- $n_{factor} = \dfrac{T_{MBL}}{F_{rope, max}}$ (must be $\ge 5$ for general duty)
- $\Delta M_{list} = W \times h \times \sin\theta$

### C. Safety Devices (in priority order)
1. E-Stop (operator)
2. Anemometer (wind cut-off at 20 m/s)
3. SLI (load cut-off at 110% SWL)
4. LMI (moment cut-off at 110% rated moment)
5. Anti-Two-Block
6. Hoist Up/Down Limits
7. Luff Up/Down Limits
8. Slew End Limits
9. Overload Relief Valve
10. Slewing Ring Bolt Monitor

### D. Operational Limits
- **Wind operational:** 20 m/s
- **Wind out-of-service:** 55 m/s
- **List operational:** 5° static, 7° combined
- **Trim operational:** 2° to 3°
- **Sea state operational:** SS-3 typical
- **Temperature operational:** −20 °C minimum

### E. Testing Schedule
- **Prototype test:** 1.25 × SWL at manufacturer's yard
- **Annual thorough examination:** 1.25 × SWL on board
- **10-yearly overhaul test:** 1.25 × SWL after major refit
- **Daily:** Visual check, brake test, limit switch test
- **Pre-lift:** SWL vs. radius verification, trial lift

### F. Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid
- Converting tonnes to kN correctly (× 9.81)
- Distinguishing SLI (load) from LMI (moment)
- Including list/trim effects in stability check
- Stating the constant-moment assumption
- Stating units in every numerical answer
- Including sheave efficiency in reeving tension

### G. Reeving Quick-Reference
| Parts | Mechanical Advantage | Hook Speed Factor |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1.0 |
| 2 | 2 | 0.5 |
| 4 | 4 | 0.25 |
| 6 | 6 | 0.167 |

### H. Key Memorizable Constants
- $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$
- Proof load factor = 1.25
- Class minimum rope FOS = 5.0
- Class pedestal FOS = 1.3
- Working hydraulic pressure = 250–350 bar
- Drum/sheave $D/d \ge 18$ to 25

> **End of Topic Notes — Deck Cranes (Module 3, PCNST503, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Windlasses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Windlass** is a critical **deck machinery** unit installed on the **fore-deck** (and sometimes aft-deck for mooring) of a ship. Its primary functions are **(a)** weighing (raising) and lowering of the **anchor** along with the **chain cable**, and **(b)** carrying out **mooring / warping operations** using rope, wire, or synthetic hawsers. The windlass converts the rotational input from an **electric motor, hydraulic motor, or steam engine** into a controlled linear pull through a **wildcat (chain wheel)** and a **warping drum**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Marine Engineering syllabus, the windlass is treated as a combined machine because it simultaneously performs:
1. **Anchor handling** (via the wildcat)
2. **Mooring handling** (via the warping drum)
3. **Brake-holding** (via the band brake) when the ship is at anchor

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The "Winch on a Fishing Boat"**
> Imagine a fisherman manually pulling up a heavy anchor by hand. Now upgrade that idea: instead of a man's arms, attach a **giant electric motor** to a **toothed sprocket** (wildcat) that locks into the **links of the anchor chain** like a key in a lock. Add a separate **smooth drum** next to it for tying mooring ropes, and a **big leather strap-brake** that the sailor squeezes to stop the anchor from slipping back. That is essentially a marine **windlass** — a powerful, sea-salt-resistant winch that lets a ship **drop and lift** a 5–10 ton anchor in deep harbour water without anyone getting their hands dirty.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | KTU / Classification Society Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Anchor chain stud-link diameter | **22 mm to 122 mm** (depending on ship size) |
| Chain pitch (length of 5 links) | **5 × nominal diameter** |
| Hauling speed (chain) | **Minimum 9 m/min** (ABS / DNV / IACS rule) |
| Anchor drop test load | **1.1 × total mass of anchor + chain** |
| Anchor pull test load | **0.8 × breaking load of chain** |
| Working Load Limit (WLL) of chain | **~ 1/3 of breaking load** |
| Brake holding capacity | **At least 80% of MBL** (Minimum Breaking Load) |
| Wildcat pitch circle diameter | **Equal to chain pitch** (matched by chain size) |
| Brake linings | **Asbestos-free (non-asbestos organic)** for marine use |

## 1.4 Classification of Windlasses (KTU Syllabus Map)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three principal classifications for KTU exams:**

**(A) By Shaft Orientation**
- **Vertical-shaft windlass** — Wildcat axis is vertical; motor sits **below the forecastle deck** (most common on merchant ships).
- **Horizontal-shaft windlass** — Wildcat axis is horizontal; motor sits **on the forecastle deck** (common on tugs, supply vessels, naval ships).

**(B) By Prime Mover**
- **Electric windlass** (AC squirrel-cage motor with slip coupling).
- **Electro-hydraulic windlass** (electric motor + hydraulic pump + hydraulic motor).
- **Steam-driven windlass** (older tanker & war ships; rare in modern builds).

**(C) By Combined Function**
- **Combined windlass-mooring winch** (single unit with both wildcat & warping drum).
- **Split windlass-mooring winch** (two independent machines: one for anchor, one for mooring).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Anatomy — What Each Part Does

> [!NOTE]
> A windlass is **not** a single part; it is an **integrated system** of mechanical, electrical, and safety sub-systems. KTU examiners frequently test component-level knowledge.

### 2.1.1 Wildcat (Chain Wheel / Gypsy)
- A **sprocket with machined pockets** (lugs) shaped to mesh precisely with the **stud-link chain**.
- Number of pockets = **odd (5, 7, 9, 11)** so that at least **two pockets** are always engaged with the chain links (provides self-locking).
- The **pitch circle diameter of the wildcat = chain pitch length** (i.e., the length of one complete chain link center-to-center).
- The wildcat is **reversible** in newer hydraulic systems (lets the anchor fall under controlled gravity, or "brake lowering").

### 2.1.2 Warping Drum
- A **smooth cylindrical drum** mounted on the **same shaft as the wildcat** (or on a separate shaft with its own clutch).
- Used for **mooring hawsers, ropes, and messenger lines**.
- Sometimes called the **"mooring drum"**.

### 2.1.3 Cable Lifter (Chain Stripper / Chain Guard)
- A **deflector plate / cam-shaped guide** that lifts the chain links **out of the wildcat pockets** and guides them into the **spurling pipe** leading to the chain locker.
- Prevents chain from **jamming**, **kinking**, or being thrown out of the pockets.

### 2.1.4 Band Brake (Brake Band)
- A **friction-based holding device** consisting of a **flexible steel band lined with friction material** wrapped around a **brake wheel** mounted on the wildcat shaft.
- **Applied** by air/hydraulic pressure or a **handwheel-screw mechanism**.
- Must hold the anchor **at 80% MBL without slipping** (Class requirement).

### 2.1.5 Clutch (Wildcat Clutch / Dog Clutch)
- A **positive-engagement clutch** (gear-type or pin-type) that **connects/disconnects** the **drive shaft** from the **wildcat shaft**.
- Allows the **motor to free-run** when the windlass is not in use.
- Two types: **Friction clutch** (smooth engagement) and **Positive clutch** (rigid engagement, used in newer systems).

### 2.1.6 Safety Devices (High-Yield KTU Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these six safety devices — they are favourite 3-mark questions.**

| Device | Function |
|---|---|
| **Riding Pawl** | Engages the wildcat pockets from below to **prevent the anchor from running back** under load (when the brake fails or is released). |
| **Compressor Pawl** | Holds the chain **horizontally** against the deck pad to disconnect the chain from the bitter end during operations. |
| **Chain Stopper** | A **secondary holding device** on the deck (backing-up the brake) — keeps the anchor in position. |
| **Bitter End** | The **final inboard end** of the chain, secured inside the chain locker by a **shackle or bitter end pin** to the ship's side structure. |
| **Spurling Pipe** | A **watertight steel tube** leading from the windlass deck down to the chain locker, allowing the chain to fall into storage. |
| **Hawse Pipe** | The **cast steel pipe** in the ship's side through which the chain exits from the chain locker to the sea. |
| **Chain Cable Compressor** | A small mechanical device that **squeezes the chain** to prevent the bitter end from slipping during rough seas. |

### 2.1.7 Prime Mover (Drive)
- **Modern windlass:** AC electric motor → reduction gearbox → wildcat shaft.
- **Electro-hydraulic:** AC motor → hydraulic pump → hydraulic motor (variable speed & reversible).
- The drive must provide a **controlled lowering** mode (to let the anchor **free-fall** under gravity for emergency use).

## 2.2 Operational Modes of a Windlass

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The windlass operates in 4 distinct modes — KTU frequently asks to "explain with diagram":**

1. **Weighing Anchor (Hauling Mode)** — Motor lifts the anchor + chain from seabed to hawse pipe.
2. **Lowering Anchor (Paying Out Mode)** — Brake controls gravity descent; motor is disengaged.
3. **Mooring / Warping** — Warping drum pulls mooring lines, hawsers, or wire ropes.
4. **Brake-Holding (Static Mode)** — Brake holds the chain at anchor; motor & clutch are disengaged.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values — KTU auto-grader is markdown-sensitive.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Key | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{req} = \dfrac{F \times v}{60 \times \eta}$ | $P_{req}$ = required power (kW), $F$ = load (kN), $v$ = hauling speed (m/min), $\eta$ = overall efficiency (0.65–0.75) | Sizing the windlass electric motor |
| 2 | $T_{shaft} = \dfrac{F \times r_{pcd}}{1000}$ | $T_{shaft}$ = torque on wildcat shaft (kN·m), $F$ = line pull (kN), $r_{pcd}$ = pitch circle radius (m) | Designing the brake & clutch |
| 3 | $W_{chain} = \dfrac{W_{total}}{N_{shots}}$ | $W_{chain}$ = weight per shot, $W_{total}$ = total chain weight, $N_{shots}$ = number of shots (usually 8–12) | Chain weight distribution in chain locker |
| 4 | $L_{5} = 5 \times d$ | $L_5$ = chain pitch (5-link length), $d$ = nominal bar diameter | Wildcat-to-chain compatibility check |
| 5 | $F_{test,pull} = 0.8 \times MBL$ | $F_{test,pull}$ = anchor pull test load, $MBL$ = minimum breaking load | Class survey / commissioning test |
| 6 | $F_{test,drop} = 1.1 \times (W_{anchor} + W_{chain})$ | $F_{test,drop}$ = anchor drop test load | Class drop test |
| 7 | $v_{hauling} \geq 9 \text{ m/min}$ | $v_{hauling}$ = chain hauling speed | Class rule of thumb (ABS / DNV) |
| 8 | $n_{pockets} = \text{odd integer (5, 7, 9, 11)}$ | $n_{pockets}$ = number of wildcat pockets | Wildcat design rule |
| 9 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{gear} \times \eta_{brake}$ | Product of sub-system efficiencies | Power-loss budget |
| 10 | $W_{anchor,holding} = \dfrac{F_{MBL}}{g}$ | Anchor's static holding demand in tonnes | Brake sizing |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in marine engineering practice:**
> 1. **Port turnaround time:** A 1-minute delay in anchoring = thousands of dollars in demurrage. The windlass dictates how fast a ship can drop and lift anchor.
> 2. **Safety at sea:** Failure of brake or riding pawl at deep anchorage = **drifting ship** → collision, grounding, oil spill.
> 3. **SOLAS / Class compliance:** Every windlass is **drop-tested at 1.1× static load** and **pull-tested at 0.8× MBL** before the ship leaves the yard.
> 4. **Wear and tear:** Wildcat pockets wear out (chain link ↔ pocket contact). Periodic gauging is mandatory (Class survey item).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **All derivations below are exhaustively shown — no skipped steps.**

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Required to Lift an Anchor + Chain

### Step 0: Problem Setup
A bulk carrier must weigh (lift) an **anchor weighing 8 tonnes** with **chain cable of total weight 12 tonnes**. The windlass hauls at **9 m/min** with an overall efficiency of **70%**. The chain leaves the water at a vertical lift of **15 m**. Find the **minimum electric motor power rating** required.

### Step 1: Identify Loads
- **Anchor mass:** $m_a = 8 \text{ tonnes} = 8{,}000 \text{ kg}$
- **Chain mass in water:** Note that the chain is partly in water, partly in air. Hydrodynamic weight is **reduced** for in-water portion by the buoyancy factor. For a first-order conservative estimate, KTU textbooks take the **dry weight** of chain.
- **Chain mass (dry, conservative):** $m_c = 12 \text{ tonnes} = 12{,}000 \text{ kg}$
- **Total mass to lift:** $m_{tot} = m_a + m_c = 8{,}000 + 12{,}000 = 20{,}000 \text{ kg}$

### Step 2: Convert Mass to Force
The total gravitational force (apparent) acting on the lifting system:
$$
F = m_{tot} \times g
$$
Substituting values (using $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$):
$$
F = 20{,}000 \times 9.81 \text{ N} = 196{,}200 \text{ N} = 196.2 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 3: Convert Hauling Speed to m/s
The hauling speed is given in **m/min**. KTU examiners love testing this conversion:
$$
v_{m/s} = \dfrac{v_{m/min}}{60}
$$
$$
v_{m/s} = \dfrac{9}{60} = 0.15 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Power Equation
The general mechanical power equation:
$$
P_{out} = F \times v_{m/s}
$$
$$
P_{out} = 196{,}200 \times 0.15 = 29{,}430 \text{ W} = 29.43 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 5: Account for Efficiency Losses
The motor must deliver **more** than the output because the gearbox, brake, and bearings have friction losses. Therefore, the **input power (motor rating)** is:
$$
P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta_{overall}}
$$
Substituting $P_{out} = 29.43$ kW and $\eta = 0.70$:
$$
P_{motor} = \dfrac{29.43}{0.70}
$$
$$
P_{motor} = 42.04 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 6: Apply Safety Factor
Class rules require a **10–20% margin** above the calculated rating to handle:
- Sea-state overloads (chain snatching)
- Brake friction heat
- Cold-start of motor
$$
P_{selected} = 1.20 \times 42.04 = 50.45 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 7: Select Standard Motor Rating
Standard electric motor ratings come in **IEC frames**: 45 kW, 55 kW, 75 kW, … Therefore the engineer selects:
$$
\boxed{P_{selected} = 55 \text{ kW}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical takeaway:** The motor is rated **larger** than the steady-state lifting demand. This is because **dynamic loads** during anchor break-out can spike to **1.5×–2×** the static load.

## 3.2 Derivation: Brake Torque on the Wildcat Shaft

### Step 1: Find the Pitch-Circle Radius
Wildcats are sized so that the pitch circle equals the chain pitch length. For a **diameter 76 mm chain** (e.g., for a Panamax bulker):
$$
L_5 = 5 \times d = 5 \times 76 = 380 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(chain pitch)}
$$
For a 5-pocket wildcat:
$$
D_{pcd} = \dfrac{L_5}{\sin(180°/5)} = \dfrac{380}{0.9511} \approx 399.4 \text{ mm}
$$
But for **engineering simplicity** in KTU problems, take $D_{pcd} \approx L_5$ when the number of pockets $\geq 7$. So:
$$
D_{pcd} \approx 380 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_{pcd} = 0.190 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Torque Equation
For a force $F$ applied tangentially to the pitch circle:
$$
T_{shaft} = F \times r_{pcd}
$$
Using the lifting force $F = 196.2$ kN (from §3.1):
$$
T_{shaft} = 196.2 \times 0.190 = 37.28 \text{ kN·m}
$$

### Step 3: Required Brake Holding Torque
The brake must hold **at least 80% of MBL** load. For this MBL = 600 kN (assumed standard for 76 mm chain):
$$
F_{brake} = 0.8 \times 600 = 480 \text{ kN}
$$
$$
T_{brake,min} = 480 \times 0.190 = 91.2 \text{ kN·m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The brake lining, band tension, and lever arm must together generate a frictional torque of **at least 91.2 kN·m** to pass the Class pull test.

## 3.3 Derivation: Chain Length Calculation for an Anchorage

A ship anchors in water depth $h = 25$ m. The **scope ratio** (length of chain paid out ÷ water depth) is typically **5:1 to 7:1** for good holding. Find the chain length required.

$$
L_{chain} = \text{Scope} \times h
$$
Taking Scope = 6 (middle of range):
$$
L_{chain} = 6 \times 25 = 150 \text{ m}
$$

If the ship has 8 shots of 27.5 m each (total = 220 m), this is sufficient, and **70 m remains inside the chain locker**.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (KTU Examiner's Quick Lookup)

| Scenario | Motor | Brake | Wildcat | Riding Pawl |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Anchor weighing at 0.8 × MBL | **ON, hauling** | **RELEASED** | Engaged to chain | Lifted (inactive) |
| Brake-holding at anchor | **OFF** | **APPLIED** | Stationary | **ENGAGED** (engages pockets from below) |
| Controlled lowering | **OFF** | **CONTROLLED RELEASE** | Reversed slowly | Inactive |
| Mooring (warping) | **ON, slow** | **RELEASED** | Idle | Inactive |
| Chain stoppered for maintenance | **OFF** | **APPLIED** | Free | Inactive |
| Emergency free-fall | **OFF** | **FULLY RELEASED** | Free | **BYPASSED** (for emergency) |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question:** A windlass has a pitch circle diameter of 0.45 m, hauling speed 10 m/min, overall efficiency 72%. The chain total mass lifted is 18 tonnes. Find (a) line pull, (b) motor power, (c) torque on wildcat shaft.

### Part (a): Line Pull
$$
F = m \times g = 18{,}000 \times 9.81 = 176{,}580 \text{ N} = 176.58 \text{ kN}
$$

### Part (b): Motor Power
$$
v_{m/s} = \dfrac{10}{60} = 0.1667 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
P_{out} = 176.58 \times 0.1667 = 29.44 \text{ kW}
$$
$$
P_{motor} = \dfrac{29.44}{0.72} = 40.89 \text{ kW} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Round up to 45 kW}
$$

### Part (c): Torque
$$
r_{pcd} = \dfrac{0.45}{2} = 0.225 \text{ m}
$$
$$
T_{shaft} = 176.58 \times 0.225 = 39.73 \text{ kN·m}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
