# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a windlass and state its main functions on board a ship.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A **windlass** is a deck-mounted machine on the forecastle of a ship used for:
- **(i)** **Weighing (raising) the anchor and chain cable** from the seabed during departure from anchorage.
- **(ii)** **Lowering (paying out) the anchor and chain cable** during anchoring operations.
- **(iii)** **Mooring / warping operations** — handling mooring hawsers, ropes, and wires via the warping drum.

The windlass uses a **wildcat** (sprocket with pockets) for the chain and a **warping drum** for ropes. It is powered by an electric, hydraulic, or steam motor and is fitted with a **band brake** for holding.

---

### **Q2. List any six safety devices fitted on a windlass.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
1. **Riding Pawl** — Prevents the anchor from running back.
2. **Compressor Pawl** — Holds the chain horizontally to disconnect from bitter end.
3. **Chain Stopper** — Secondary chain-holding device.
4. **Band Brake** — Friction brake for holding load.
5. **Spurling Pipe** — Watertight guide from deck to chain locker.
6. **Bitter End Pin** — Final inboard securement of chain.
7. **Chain Cable Compressor** — Holds the bitter end from slipping.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Option A (14 Marks)

### **Question A(a) [7 Marks]: Explain with a neat block diagram the construction and working of a vertical-shaft windlass. State the function of the wildcat, warping drum, and band brake.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
A vertical-shaft windlass consists of:
- An **AC electric motor** housed in the **chain locker space** below the forecastle deck.
- A **reduction gearbox** connecting the motor to a **vertical shaft**.
- A **wildcat** (chain wheel) at the upper end of the shaft with machined pockets matching the stud-link chain.
- A **warping drum** at the top or side of the wildcat for mooring ropes.
- A **brake wheel** and **band brake** assembly.
- A **clutch** to engage/disengage the motor.
- A **cable lifter** (chain stripper) guiding chain into the spurling pipe.
- **Riding pawl** and **chain stopper** as safety devices.

**Working (Weighing Mode):**
1. The motor receives power from the bridge or local console.
2. The clutch is engaged; the motor drives the reduction gearbox.
3. The vertical shaft rotates, turning the wildcat.
4. The wildcat pockets engage the chain links and **lift the chain + anchor** vertically.
5. The chain is guided by the cable lifter into the spurling pipe and chain locker.
6. The band brake is **released** during hauling.

**Working (Lowering Mode):**
- The motor is stopped; the clutch is disengaged.
- The **band brake is slowly released** to let the chain **pay out under gravity**.
- The **riding pawl** prevents uncontrolled free-fall.

**Functions of Key Parts:**
- **Wildcat** — A toothed sprocket with pockets that **mesh with the chain links** to transmit pulling force to the chain.
- **Warping Drum** — A **smooth cylindrical drum** on the same shaft (or coupled) used for **mooring hawsers and warping lines**.
- **Band Brake** — A **friction-based holding device** that grips a brake wheel on the wildcat shaft to **hold the anchor at rest** and during brake-holding mode.

---

### **Question A(b) [7 Marks]: A windlass is required to lift an anchor and chain of total mass 25 tonnes at a hauling speed of 9 m/min. The overall efficiency of the windlass is 70%. Find: (i) the line pull in kN, (ii) the power required at the motor, and (iii) the torque on the wildcat shaft if the pitch circle diameter is 0.40 m.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Total mass $m = 25{,}000$ kg
- Hauling speed $v = 9$ m/min
- Overall efficiency $\eta = 0.70 = 70\%$
- Pitch circle diameter $D = 0.40$ m, so $r = 0.20$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(i) Line Pull (F):**
$$
F = m \times g = 25{,}000 \times 9.81 = 245{,}250 \text{ N}
$$
$$
\boxed{F = 245.25 \text{ kN}}
$$

**(ii) Motor Power:**
Convert speed to m/s:
$$
v_{m/s} = \dfrac{9}{60} = 0.15 \text{ m/s}
$$
Output power:
$$
P_{out} = F \times v = 245.25 \times 0.15 = 36.79 \text{ kW}
$$
Motor power:
$$
P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \dfrac{36.79}{0.70}
$$
$$
\boxed{P_{motor} = 52.56 \text{ kW} \approx 55 \text{ kW (standard motor rating)}}
$$

**(iii) Torque on Wildcat Shaft:**
$$
T = F \times r = 245.25 \times 0.20
$$
$$
\boxed{T = 49.05 \text{ kN·m}}
$$

---

## 5.3 Part B Question — Option B (14 Marks)

### **Question B(a) [7 Marks]: Describe the various safety devices fitted on a marine windlass. Why is the band brake considered the most critical safety device?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Safety Devices on a Marine Windlass:**

1. **Riding Pawl:** A pivoted lever that engages the wildcat pockets **from below** to physically block the chain from running back when the brake is released.

2. **Compressor Pawl:** Holds the chain **horizontally** on deck so that the bitter end can be disconnected safely for inspection.

3. **Chain Stopper:** A secondary **deck-mounted** holding device (often a screw-type or hydraulic) that takes over the load from the brake during long-term anchoring.

4. **Band Brake:** A flexible steel band lined with **friction material** wrapped around a brake wheel on the wildcat shaft. Applied by handwheel or hydraulic actuator.

5. **Spurling Pipe:** A **watertight steel tube** that guides the chain from the forecastle deck down into the chain locker; prevents the chain from damaging the deck.

6. **Bitter End:** The inboard end of the chain, **shackled to the ship's hull** inside the chain locker — the absolute final point of safety.

7. **Chain Cable Compressor:** A small clamping device that **squeezes the last few metres of chain** to prevent the bitter end from slipping.

8. **Hawse Pipe:** The **cast steel pipe in the shell plating** that allows the chain to exit the chain locker to the sea.

9. **Emergency Free-Fall Release:** A system that **bypasses the band brake** in an emergency (e.g., grounding) to let the anchor drop instantly.

**Why the Band Brake is the Most Critical:**
- It is the **only device that can hold a static load of 80% MBL indefinitely**.
- Failure of the brake means the anchor can **free-fall** or the ship can **drift** from its anchorage.
- The brake is **continuously engaged** when the ship is at anchor; it is the primary energy-absorbing device during chain snatching.
- It must be **manually applied** (handwheel) and must be **fail-safe** (spring-applied, hydraulically released on most modern ships).

---

### **Question B(b) [7 Marks]: Compare vertical-shaft and horizontal-shaft windlasses. State which type is preferred on a modern merchant ship and why.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Vertical-Shaft Windlass | Horizontal-Shaft Windlass |
|---|---|---|
| **Shaft orientation** | Vertical | Horizontal |
| **Motor location** | Below the forecastle deck | On the forecastle deck |
| **Deck space** | **Saved** (motor below) | **Occupied** (motor above) |
| **Maintenance** | **Difficult** (motor is in chain locker) | **Easy** (motor is on deck) |
| **Weather protection** | **Good** (motor protected) | **Poor** (motor exposed to spray) |
| **Initial cost** | Higher (longer shaft, more structure) | Lower |
| **Chain wear** | Lower (chain lifts vertically out of pockets) | Higher (chain peels horizontally) |
| **Use case** | Merchant ships, tankers, bulk carriers | Tugs, supply vessels, naval craft |
| **Centre of gravity** | Lower (better stability) | Higher |

**Preferred Type on Modern Merchant Ships:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **vertical-shaft windlass** is preferred on modern merchant ships because:
> 1. **Saves precious deck space** on the forecastle.
> 2. The motor is **protected from weather and sea spray**.
> 3. Provides a **lower centre of gravity** for the ship.
> 4. Better alignment with the **chain locker and spurling pipe** (chain falls directly into the locker).
> 5. **Lower chain wear** because the chain lifts cleanly out of the wildcat pockets.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps — KTU Valuation Key Patterns:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion trap:** Hauling speed is in **m/min** but power formula needs **m/s**. Forgetting to divide by 60 → 0.5–1.0 mark deduction.
> 2. **Efficiency misplacement:** Motor power = $P_{out}/\eta$, **not** $P_{out} \times \eta$. Many students invert this.
> 3. **Wildcat pocket rule:** Students write "5 pockets" generically. KTU expects justification — "**odd** number so that at least two pockets are always engaged with chain links".
> 4. **Brake holding capacity:** Students state "holds the anchor" but KTU requires numerical value — "**80% of MBL**" is the standard.
> 5. **Direction of chain flow:** In vertical-shaft windlass, chain moves **UP** into the spurling pipe; in horizontal, it moves **OUT** to the side. Mixing up = mark loss.
> 6. **Mistaking "warping drum" with "wildcat":** They are on the same shaft but have **different functions** — wildcat = chain, drum = ropes. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 7. **Forgetting to apply 1.1× drop test or 0.8× pull test factors** in numerical questions.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!NOTE]
> **★ KTU Windlass — High-Density Revision Checklist ★**

- [ ] **Windlass** = deck machine for **anchor handling + mooring**. Two main sub-units: **wildcat** (chain) and **warping drum** (ropes).
- [ ] **Vertical-shaft** is standard for merchant ships; **horizontal-shaft** is for tugs/naval.
- [ ] **Wildcat** has **odd number of pockets** (5, 7, 9, 11) for self-locking; **pitch circle = chain pitch**.
- [ ] **Band brake** is the primary safety device; holds **at least 80% MBL**.
- [ ] **Riding pawl** = mechanical backup to brake (engages wildcat pockets from below).
- [ ] **Chain stopper** = secondary load holder (used for long-term anchorage).
- [ ] **Spurling pipe** = tube guiding chain from deck to chain locker.
- [ ] **Bitter end** = final inboard end of chain, secured to ship structure.
- [ ] **Hawse pipe** = shell-plating pipe; chain exits here to sea.
- [ ] **Compressor pawl** = holds chain horizontally for bitter-end disconnection.
- [ ] **Hauling speed ≥ 9 m/min** (ABS / DNV Class rule).
- [ ] **Power formula:** $P_{motor} = (F \times v) / (60 \times \eta)$, where $F$ in kN, $v$ in m/min.
- [ ] **Torque:** $T = F \times r_{pcd}$.
- [ ] **Test loads:** Drop = $1.1 \times$ (anchor + chain) mass; Pull = $0.8 \times MBL$.
- [ ] **Four operating modes:** Hauling, Lowering, Mooring, Brake-Holding.
- [ ] **Driving systems:** Electric (AC motor + gearbox), Electro-hydraulic, Steam (legacy).
- [ ] **Safety factor** of 1.10–1.20 applied to calculated motor rating for sea-state overloads.
- [ ] **Chain pitch rule:** $L_5 = 5d$, where $d$ is the stud-link bar diameter.
- [ ] **Emergency free-fall** bypasses the brake for instant anchor release during grounding.
- [ ] **The motor is disengaged** during brake-holding; only the band brake is active.
- [ ] **Wildcat-to-chain matching** is a Class survey item — wear on pockets is gauged periodically.
- [ ] **Scope ratio** (chain length : water depth) = 5:1 to 7:1 for normal anchorage.
- [ ] **SOLAS / Class compliance** mandates drop test (1.1×) and pull test (0.8× MBL) at commissioning.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mooring Winches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Mooring Winches # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Mooring Winch** is a deck-mounted mechanical apparatus installed on the forecastle and poop deck of a ship, specifically engineered to pay out, heave in, and tension mooring lines, wires, or synthetic ropes for the purpose of securing the vessel to a berth, dolphin, buoy, or another ship. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course PCNST503), the mooring winch is classified as a critical **deck machinery** item, and its design, performance, and braking characteristics are governed by international standards such as **ISO 7825**, **ISO 3730**, and classification society rules from **IRS (Indian Register of Shipping)**, **Lloyd's Register**, and **DNV**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCNST503 - Module 3):** > Mooring winches integrate mechanical, hydraulic, and electrical engineering principles. They are evaluated under the Course Outcomes **CO3** (Design and analyze shipboard machinery systems) and **CO4** (Apply safety and statutory regulations in marine equipment selection). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Giant Fishing Reel" Imagine a colossal **fishing reel** bolted to a ship's deck, but instead of catching fish, it secures a multi-thousand-tonne vessel to the shore. Just as a fisherman uses the reel to: - Let out line (paying out), - Pull in line (heaving in), and - Apply a strong, sustained grip (drag/holding) so a big catch cannot escape, the **mooring winch** does exactly the same with massive steel wires (typically **34 mm to 52 mm** diameter) or synthetic ropes, holding the ship steady against wind, current, and wave action. The **brake** acts like the drag of the reel, the **drum** is the spool, and the **clutch** is the switch that engages the spool to the gears or releases it for free-spooling. ## 1.3 Functional Purpose on a Vessel The fundamental objectives of a mooring winch are: - **Securing the vessel** at berths, anchorages, buoys, or single-point moorings (SPM). - **Heaving in / paying out** mooring lines under controlled tension and speed. - **Maintaining line tension** automatically to compensate for tidal variations, draft changes, and vessel surge. - **Ensuring crew safety** through redundant braking, overload protection, and emergency release mechanisms. - **Supporting cargo operations** indirectly by keeping the ship stable alongside the jetty. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Statutory Metrics (KTU Board Standard Values):** > - Typical drum line pull (1st layer): **150 kN to 350 kN** > - Brake holding load (BHL): **60% to 80%** of **Minimum Breaking Load (MBL)** of the line > - Drum line speed: **15 m/min to 30 m/min** > - Number of mooring lines per winch: **1 to 3** (multi-drum winch) > - Standard mooring line MBL: For a vessel of length 200 m, **MBL ≈ 1,000 kN** ## 1.4 Classification of Mooring Winches Mooring winches are categorized based on multiple criteria: | Classification Basis | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | **By Drive Mechanism** | Electric Mooring Winch | Driven by an electric motor (AC squirrel cage / wound rotor) | | | Hydraulic Mooring Winch | Driven by hydraulic motor (axial piston / radial piston) | | | Steam Mooring Winch | Obsolete; used in older steam-driven vessels | | **By Drum Configuration** | Single Drum | One main drum for one mooring line | | | Double Drum | Two drums stacked, often for split mooring lines | | | Split Drum / Split Type | Drum divided for two separate mooring operations | | **By Mounting Location** | Forecastle Winch | Located at the bow | | | Aft (Poop) Winch | Located at the stern | | **By Control** | Local Manual Control | Operator at winch pedestal | | | Remote Control | Push-button from bridge or deck remote station | > [!TIP] > For a typical **Panamax Bulk Carrier** or **Aframax Tanker**, **electric mooring winches with auto-tensioning features** are the most common installation in modern shipyards. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Principal Components of a Mooring Winch A modern mooring winch is a meticulously engineered assembly. The following components are universally present and form the **evaluative checklist** for KTU exam answers. ### A. Power Transmission Assembly 1. **Prime Mover (Motor)** - **Electric type:** Totally Enclosed Fan Cooled (TEFC) squirrel cage induction motor, typically **3-phase, 440 V, 50/60 Hz**, with power rating between **15 kW to 75 kW**. - **Hydraulic type:** Axial piston hydraulic motor powered by an electric motor-driven hydraulic pump, allowing stepless speed variation. 2. **Reduction Gearbox** - Reduces the high-speed motor output (typically **1450 rpm** for a 4-pole motor) to the required drum speed of **5 to 15 rpm**. - Gear ratio commonly **100:1 to 150:1**. - Type: **Helical/spur** or **planetary** gearboxes. 3. **Clutch Mechanism (Spool Clutch)** - The most critical operational element — engages or disengages the drum from the gear train. - Types: **Friction Disc Clutch** (multi-plate, oil-immersed) or **Jaw Clutch**. - Engaged for **heaving in** (powered operation); disengaged for **paying out under power** or **free-spooling**. ### B. Load Handling Assembly 4. **Main Mooring Drum (Heavy Drum)** - Spool that stores the mooring wire/rope. - Designed with **flanges** to prevent line slippage. - Surface treatment: **Corrugated or grooved** to improve line grip. 5. **Warping Drum (Light Drum)** - Smaller secondary drum used for handling light lines (spring lines, messenger lines) and for moving the ship sideways along the berth. 6. **Wildcat (Chain Cable Wildcat)** - A **sprocket-wheeled drum** with pockets for chain links, used when the mooring winch doubles as a **combined mooring and anchor windlass**. - The wildcat is **engaged via the clutch** and **locked** during anchor operation. 7. **Spur Gear Pinion (Bull Gear)** - A large gear keyed to the drum shaft, driven by the pinion from the gearbox output. ### C. Braking & Safety Assembly 8. **Manual Band Brake (Holding Brake)** - **Friction band** wrapped around a brake wheel on the drum shaft. - Provides static holding when the winch is not in operation. - Operator-controlled via a **hand wheel / lever** with mechanical advantage. 9. **Automatic Disc Brake / Hydraulic Brake** - **Fail-safe spring-applied, hydraulically released** disc brake. - Applies automatically upon power failure — **SOLAS-mandated safety feature**. 10. **Pressure Roller / Wire Rope Press** - A pneumatically or hydraulically actuated roller that presses the wire against the drum during heaving to prevent slippage. 11. **Limit Switches & Overload Protection** - **Slack rope limit switch**, **overload release clutch**, and **emergency stop**. ## 2.2 Operational Modes of a Mooring Winch The winch operates in **three distinct modes**, which examiners frequently test: 1. **Heaving Mode (Hoisting Up)** - Motor drives the drum via engaged clutch. - Brake released. - Line is wound onto the drum. 2. **Paying Out Mode (Under Power)** - Motor acts as a brake (counter-torque). - Used to control line payout speed against load. - Brake remains applied lightly to maintain tension. 3. **Free-Spooling Mode (Gravity / Tension Payout)** - Clutch **disengaged**. - Drum rotates freely on shaft. - Brake controls the descent speed to prevent runaway. - Used when letting out a long length of line quickly. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Conflating the **band brake** and **disc brake**. The band brake is the **static holding brake** (used when the winch is idle), while the disc brake is the **dynamic fail-safe brake** (used during motion and on power failure). Both are essential. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Description | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Drum Line Pull (1st Layer) | $P = \dfrac{2 \cdot T \cdot \eta_{total}}{D_d}$ | kN or N | Where $T$ = motor torque, $D_d$ = effective drum diameter | | 2 | Drum Capacity (Length of Line) | $L = \pi \cdot K \cdot n \cdot (D + n \cdot d)$ | metres | $D$ = barrel dia, $d$ = line dia, $n$ = turns, $K$ = filling factor | | 3 | Line Speed at Drum | $v = \pi \cdot D_d \cdot N_{drum}$ | m/min | $N_{drum}$ = drum rotational speed in rpm | | 4 | Brake Holding Load | $BHL = k \cdot MBL$ | kN | $k$ = factor (typically 0.6 to 0.8) | | 5 | Power Required | $P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{line} \cdot v_{line}}{\eta_{total}}$ | kW | $P_{line}$ = line pull, $v_{line}$ = line speed | | 6 | Filling Factor | $K = 0.85$ to $0.90$ | dimensionless | Accounts for line cross-section not being perfectly hexagonal | | 7 | Total Efficiency | $\eta_{total} = \eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{clutch} \cdot \eta_{bearings}$ | dimensionless | Typically $0.75$ to $0.85$ | | 8 | First Layer Torque | $T_1 = P_1 \cdot \dfrac{D_d}{2}$ | N·m | $P_1$ = first layer line pull | | 9 | Last Layer Torque | $T_n = P_n \cdot \dfrac{D_{d,n}}{2}$ | N·m | Drum diameter increases with added layers | | 10 | Layer-by-Layer Pull Ratio | $\dfrac{P_n}{P_1} = \dfrac{D_d}{D_{d,n}}$ | dimensionless | $P_n$ decreases as $n$ increases | | 11 | Drum Diameter at $n$-th Layer | $D_{d,n} = D_d + 2(n-1) \cdot d$ | m | Drum grows with each layer wound | | 12 | Wildcat Chain Pitch | $p = K_c \cdot d_{chain}$ | mm | $K_c \approx 2.0$ for stud link chain | > [!IMPORTANT] > For the KTU board exam, the **drum capacity formula (Formula #2)** and the **brake holding load relation (Formula #4)** are the most frequently asked derivations and numericals. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The mooring winch is a **non-negotiable safety-critical system** on every commercial vessel. Its engineering importance extends across: - **Vessel Berthing Operations** — without it, a ship cannot be safely secured to a berth. - **Dynamic Positioning (DP) Backup** — on DP vessels, mooring systems provide a fallback when DP fails. - **Offshore Mooring Systems** — at FPSOs and SPM buoys, mooring winches manage multi-leg turret systems under extreme sea states. - **Tug-Assisted Maneuvering** — winches handle the tug's towline and ship's spring lines. - **Classification & Insurance** — mooring winch specifications directly affect a vessel's classification and P&I insurance premiums. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Drum Capacity (Length of Mooring Line Stored) **Problem Statement:** Derive the formula for the total length of mooring wire/rope that can be wound onto a mooring drum, given: - $D$ = barrel (core) diameter of the drum - $d$ = diameter of the mooring line - $n$ = total number of turns in all layers - $K$ = filling factor (winding efficiency) ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Understand the Geometry of a Single Layer** When a mooring line of diameter $d$ is wound onto a drum with barrel diameter $D$, the **mean diameter** of the first layer of winding is: $$ D_{m,1} = D + d $$ This is because the first wrap of the line lies on top of the barrel surface, so its centerline traces a circle of diameter $(D + d)$. **Step 2 — Number of Turns in the First Layer** The width of the drum (axial length available for winding) is $W$. The pitch between consecutive turns equals the line diameter $d$ (assuming touching coils). So the number of turns in the first layer is: $$ n_1 = \dfrac{W}{d} $$ **Step 3 — Length of Line in the First Layer** The length wound in one complete revolution of diameter $D_{m,1}$ is $\pi \cdot D_{m,1}$. For $n_1$ such turns: $$ L_1 = n_1 \cdot \pi \cdot D_{m,1} = \dfrac{W}{d} \cdot \pi \cdot (D + d) $$ **Step 4 — Mean Diameter of the $j$-th Layer** The first layer occupies a radial thickness of $d$. The second layer's mean diameter increases by $2d$ (one $d$ on top, one $d$ on bottom of the layer's centerline): $$ D_{m,j} = D + (2j - 1) \cdot d $$ **Step 5 — Length in the $j$-th Layer** Each subsequent layer has the same number of turns $n_1$ in the available width $W$: $$ L_j = n_1 \cdot \pi \cdot D_{m,j} = \dfrac{W \cdot \pi}{d} \cdot \big[ D + (2j - 1) \cdot d \big] $$ **Step 6 — Sum Over All Layers** For a total of $m$ layers: $$ L_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} L_j = \dfrac{W \cdot \pi}{d} \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{m} \big[ D + (2j - 1) \cdot d \big] $$ **Step 7 — Evaluate the Summation** $$ \sum_{j=1}^{m} \big[ D + (2j - 1) \cdot d \big] = m \cdot D + d \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{m} (2j - 1) $$ Using the identity $\sum_{j=1}^{m} (2j - 1) = m^2$: $$ \sum_{j=1}^{m} \big[ D + (2j - 1) \cdot d \big] = m \cdot D + m^2 \cdot d $$ **Step 8 — Introduce the Filling Factor $K$** Because the wound line does not form a perfect hexagonal close-pack (gaps exist between coils), a **filling factor** $K$ is introduced: $$ L = K \cdot \dfrac{W \cdot \pi}{d} \cdot (m \cdot D + m^2 \cdot d) $$ **Step 9 — Re-express in Terms of Total Turns $n$** The total number of turns is $n = m \cdot n_1 = \dfrac{m \cdot W}{d}$, which gives $m = \dfrac{n \cdot d}{W}$. Substituting and simplifying: $$ L = K \cdot \pi \cdot n \cdot \left( D + n \cdot d \right) $$ This is the **classical KTU drum capacity formula** (Formula #2 in the cheat sheet). ### Verification with Sample Values Let $D = 0.5$ m, $d = 0.04$ m, $n = 200$ turns, $K = 0.85$: $$ L = 0.85 \times \pi \times 200 \times (0.5 + 200 \times 0.04) $$ $$ L = 0.85 \times 628.32 \times 8.5 = 4{,}539 \text{ m} \approx 4.54 \text{ km of line on the drum.} $$ This magnitude is realistic for mooring winches on tankers and bulk carriers. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between Line Pull on Different Layers **Problem Statement:** Show that the line pull decreases as the line is wound onto outer layers, given constant motor torque. ### Derivation The torque transmitted by the gearbox to the drum is fixed (since motor torque and gear ratio are constant): $$ T_{drum} = \text{constant} = T $$ For the first layer, with effective drum diameter $D_{d,1}$: $$ T = P_1 \cdot \dfrac{D_{d,1}}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_1 = \dfrac{2T}{D_{d,1}} $$ For the $n$-th layer, the effective drum diameter is larger: $$ D_{d,n} = D_{d,1} + 2(n - 1) \cdot d $$ So the corresponding line pull is: $$ P_n = \dfrac{2T}{D_{d,n}} = \dfrac{2T}{D_{d,1} + 2(n-1) \cdot d} $$ Taking the ratio: $$ \dfrac{P_n}{P_1} = \dfrac{D_{d,1}}{D_{d,1} + 2(n-1) \cdot d} < 1 $$ **Conclusion:** As the line is wound onto the drum, the effective drum diameter increases, and therefore the **line pull decreases**. This is a key design trade-off: the winch delivers **maximum pull on the bare drum** (first layer) and progressively less as the drum fills up. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Layer-by-Layer Performance Using the derived relations, we can construct a **performance table** for a typical mooring winch: Assume: - $D_{d,1} = 0.6$ m (effective first-layer diameter) - $d = 0.04$ m (line diameter) - $P_1 = 200$ kN (first-layer line pull) - Drum width $W = 0.6$ m - $K = 0.85$ | Layer $n$ | Drum Diameter $D_{d,n}$ (m) | Line Pull $P_n$ (kN) | Turns/Layer | Length Stored (m) | Cumulative Length (m) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0.600 | **200.0** | 15 | 80.1 | 80.1 | | 2 | 0.680 | 176.5 | 15 | 90.8 | 170.9 | | 3 | 0.760 | 157.9 | 15 | 101.5 | 272.4 | | 4 | 0.840 | 142.9 | 15 | 112.1 | 384.5 | | 5 | 0.920 | 130.4 | 15 | 122.8 | 507.3 | | 6 | 1.000 | **120.0** | 15 | 133.5 | 640.8 | > [!NOTE] > This table is exactly the kind of analytical work expected in KTU 14-mark problems on "Mooring Winch Design Calculations." ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Given:** A mooring winch has a barrel diameter $D = 0.4$ m, line diameter $d = 36$ mm, total turns $n = 150$, and filling factor $K = 0.85$. Calculate the length of mooring line that can be stored. **Solution:** Step 1 — Identify parameters. $$ D = 0.4 \text{ m}, \quad d = 0.036 \text{ m}, \quad n = 150, \quad K = 0.85 $$ Step 2 — Apply the drum capacity formula. $$ L = K \cdot \pi \cdot n \cdot (D + n \cdot d) $$ Step 3 — Compute the inner bracket. $$ D + n \cdot d = 0.4 + 150 \times 0.036 = 0.4 + 5.4 = 5.8 \text{ m} $$ Step 4 — Substitute and solve. $$ L = 0.85 \times \pi \times 150 \times 5.8 $$ $$ L = 0.85 \times 3.1416 \times 870 $$ $$ L = 0.85 \times 2{,}733.2 = 2{,}323.2 \text{ m} $$ **Result:** The drum can store approximately **2,323 m** of mooring line. This satisfies the typical requirement of 1,500–2,500 m of mooring line for an Aframax-class vessel. --- ## 3.5 KTU Conceptual Question — Detailed Analytical Answer **Q: Why is the brake holding load (BHL) set at 60% to 80% of MBL and not 100%?** **Analytical Reasoning:** 1. **MBL is the catastrophic failure load** — the load at which the line physically breaks. A winch brake must NEVER be designed to reach this load during normal operation, or the line would part. 2. **Safety margin for dynamic loads** — Mooring lines experience **shock loads** (snap loads) due to vessel motion. The brake must hold the line steady under these dynamic surges, not just static loads. 3. **Crew and equipment safety** — Setting BHL too close to MBL risks line parting, recoiling, and injuring crew or damaging equipment. 4. **Statutory requirement** — Classification societies (IRS, LR, DNV) mandate BHL $= 0.6$ to $0.8 \times$ MBL, typically **60%** for the **design load** and **80%** as the **maximum holding capacity** before release. 5. **Overload release** — A setting above 80% defeats the purpose of the **overload release clutch**, which is designed to slip at a preset fraction of MBL. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Mooring Winch Functional Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a typical electric mooring winch, mapping the **power flow, control signals, and mechanical interactions** from the prime mover to the mooring line. ```mermaid graph TD A[AC POWER SUPPLY

Drum shaft with bearings on **both ends** (pedestal bearings) > 2. Bull gear **keyed** (not loose) to the shaft > 3. Band brake wheel **between** the bull gear and the drum > 4. Clutch hub **between** the bull gear and the gearbox output pinion # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define the term "Brake Holding Load (BHL)" of a mooring winch and state its typical relationship with the Minimum Breaking Load (MBL) of the mooring line.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Brake Holding Load (BHL)** of a mooring winch is the maximum static load that the winch brake (typically the band brake) is designed to hold without slippage when the winch is not in operation (motor stopped, clutch disengaged). It is classified society requirement (as per ISO 7825, IRS Rules) that: $$ BHL = 0.6 \text{ to } 0.8 \times MBL $$ A setting of **0.6 × MBL** is used as the **working/design BHL**, and **0.8 × MBL** is the **maximum** permissible holding capacity to allow for dynamic shock loads and to prevent line parting. **(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — relationship, 1 mark — significance.)** --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **List any four main components of a mooring winch and state the function of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 marks per component):** 1. **Bull Gear & Pinion** — Transmits torque from the gearbox to the drum shaft, providing the necessary speed reduction. 2. **Spool Clutch (Friction Disc Clutch)** — Engages or disengages the drum from the gear train, allowing powered heaving, controlled payout, or free-spooling. 3. **Band Brake (Manual Holding Brake)** — Provides static holding of the load when the winch is not operating, preventing the drum from rotating. 4. **Warping Drum (Light Drum)** — A smaller secondary drum used for handling spring lines and messenger ropes during berthing/un-berthing operations. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]** **A mooring winch is required to store 2,200 m of 38 mm diameter steel wire rope. The barrel diameter is 0.45 m and the filling factor is 0.85. The first layer line pull is 180 kN. The winch is driven by a 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor through a 120:1 reduction gearbox having an efficiency of 0.82. The clutch and bearings combined efficiency is 0.92.** **Calculate:** - **(a)** The number of turns of wire rope on the drum and the corresponding number of layers. (7 marks) - **(b)** The motor power required at the first layer, and the line pull on the 5th layer. (7 marks) #### Solution to Question A: **Part (a) — Number of Turns and Number of Layers (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Apply the drum capacity formula. $$ L = K \cdot \pi \cdot n \cdot (D + n \cdot d) $$ Given: $L = 2200$ m, $D = 0.45$ m, $d = 0.038$ m, $K = 0.85$. **Step 2:** Substitute values. $$ 2200 = 0.85 \times \pi \times n \times (0.45 + 0.038 n) $$ $$ 2200 = 2.6704 \cdot n \cdot (0.45 + 0.038 n) $$ $$ 2200 = 1.2017 \cdot n + 0.1015 \cdot n^2 $$ **Step 3:** Rearrange into standard quadratic form. $$ 0.1015 \cdot n^2 + 1.2017 \cdot n - 2200 = 0 $$ **Step 4:** Solve using the quadratic formula $n = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$. $$ n = \dfrac{-1.2017 \pm \sqrt{(1.2017)^2 + 4 \times 0.1015 \times 2200}}{2 \times 0.1015} $$ $$ n = \dfrac{-1.2017 \pm \sqrt{1.4441 + 893.2}}{0.2030} $$ $$ n = \dfrac{-1.2017 \pm \sqrt{894.64}}{0.2030} = \dfrac{-1.2017 + 29.910}{0.2030} $$ $$ n = \dfrac{28.708}{0.2030} \approx 141.4 $$ **Step 5:** Round to nearest whole number (must be integer number of turns). $$ \boxed{n \approx 142 \text{ turns}} $$ **Step 6:** Calculate the number of layers. Assuming the drum width $W$ is not given, we use the relationship that each layer's mean diameter is $D_{m,j} = D + (2j - 1) \cdot d$, and the total number of turns $n = m \cdot n_1$ where $n_1$ is turns per layer. For typical drum widths, $n_1$ is around 18–22 turns. Let's compute iteratively: Assume $m = 7$ layers, then $n_1 = 142/7 = 20.3$ turns/layer — this is realistic. **Verification:** Compute $L$ for $n = 142$: $$ L = 0.85 \times \pi \times 142 \times (0.45 + 142 \times 0.038) = 0.85 \times 446.1 \times 5.846 = 2216 \text{ m} \approx 2200 \text{ m} \checkmark $$ **Part (b) — Motor Power and 5th Layer Line Pull (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Determine the drum speed at first layer. Motor speed (4-pole, 50 Hz) $= \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500$ rpm. Drum speed at first layer: $N_{drum,1} = \dfrac{1500}{120} = 12.5$ rpm. **Step 2:** Compute line speed at first layer. Effective first-layer drum diameter: $D_{d,1} = D + d = 0.45 + 0.038 = 0.488$ m. $$ v_1 = \pi \times 0.488 \times 12.5 = 19.16 \text{ m/min} = 0.319 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 3:** Compute motor power required at the first layer. Total efficiency: $\eta_{total} = 0.82 \times 0.92 = 0.7544$. $$ P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{line} \times v_1}{\eta_{total}} = \dfrac{180{,}000 \times 0.319}{0.7544} = 76{,}114 \text{ W} \approx 76.1 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 4:** Compute effective drum diameter at 5th layer. $$ D_{d,5} = D_d + 2(5-1) \cdot d = 0.488 + 2 \times 4 \times 0.038 = 0.488 + 0.304 = 0.792 \text{ m} $$ **Step 5:** Apply the layer-line pull relation (constant motor torque). Since motor power is approximately constant and $\eta_{total}$ doesn't change dramatically: $$ P_1 \cdot D_{d,1} = P_5 \cdot D_{d,5} $$ $$ P_5 = P_1 \times \dfrac{D_{d,1}}{D_{d,5}} = 180 \times \dfrac{0.488}{0.792} = 180 \times 0.6162 $$ $$ \boxed{P_5 = 110.9 \text{ kN}} $$ **Final Answers:** - Part (a): **142 turns** on the drum, arranged in **7 layers** (approx. 20.3 turns/layer). - Part (b): **Motor power ≈ 76.1 kW**; **5th layer line pull ≈ 110.9 kN**. --- ### Question B: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]** **Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working of an electric mooring winch. Discuss the role of (a) the spool clutch and (b) the band brake and disc brake in the operation of the winch. (14 marks)** #### Model Answer to Question B: **Introduction (2 marks):** An electric mooring winch is a deck-mounted mechanical device used to handle mooring lines (wires or ropes) for securing a vessel to a berth, buoy, or another ship. It is driven by an electric motor through a reduction gearbox, and it is installed on the forecastle and poop decks. The winch typically consists of a **main mooring drum**, a **warping drum**, a **wildcat** (when combined with anchor windlass), a **spool clutch**, a **band brake**, a **disc brake**, and a **pressure roller**. **Construction Details (4 marks):** 1. **Base Frame (Bedplate):** A heavy steel fabricated structure welded to the deck, supporting all subassemblies. 2. **Drum Shaft:** A high-strength forged steel shaft supported on two pedestal bearings. 3. **Main Mooring Drum:** A heavy cylindrical drum with flanges, keyed to the drum shaft, used to wind the mooring wire/rope. 4. **Warping Drum (Light Drum):** A smaller diameter drum, also on the same shaft, used for handling light lines. 5. **Bull Gear:** A large spur gear keyed to the drum shaft, driven by a pinion from the gearbox. 6. **Spool Clutch:** A multi-disc friction clutch located between the gearbox output and the bull gear, allowing the drum to be engaged or disengaged from the gear train. 7. **Band Brake:** A manually operated friction band brake acting on a brake wheel fixed to the drum shaft — used for static holding. 8. **Disc Brake (Fail-Safe):** A spring-applied, hydraulically released disc brake — applies automatically on power failure. 9. **Pressure Roller:** A pneumatically or hydraulically actuated roller pressing the wire against the drum to prevent slippage during heaving. **Working Principle (2 marks):** The electric motor drives the gearbox, which reduces the high motor speed to a low drum speed. When the operator engages the clutch, the drum rotates and heaves in the line. When the clutch is disengaged, the line can be paid out freely (with the brake controlling payout speed). The brake is applied to hold the load when the winch is idle. **Role of the Spool Clutch (3 marks):** The spool clutch is the **switching element** between the gear train and the drum. Its roles are: - **Engaged** — for powered heaving in of the mooring line. - **Engaged with motor in counter-torque** — for paying out under power (controlled payout). - **Disengaged** — for free-spooling (gravity payout under brake control). It allows the operator to choose between powered and free operation, which is essential for safe and efficient berthing. **Role of the Band Brake and Disc Brake (3 marks):** - **Band Brake (Manual):** A static holding brake, manually applied via a hand lever. It holds the load when the winch is not in motion, preventing the drum from rotating. It is **not** a dynamic brake. - **Disc Brake (Fail-Safe):** A spring-applied, hydraulically released brake that provides **dynamic braking** during motion and applies automatically if power is lost. This is a **SOLAS-mandated** safety feature ensuring the line does not run away uncontrollably during power failure. **Conclusion:** The combination of clutch, band brake, and disc brake gives the mooring winch its three operational modes — heave in, pay out under power, and free-spool — while maintaining the highest level of operational safety. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Mooring Winch Questions:** > 1. **Confusing the band brake with the disc brake.** The band brake is the **static holding** brake; the disc brake is the **dynamic fail-safe** brake. Many students swap these — a sure way to lose 2–3 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the filling factor K.** The drum capacity formula is $L = K \cdot \pi \cdot n \cdot (D + nd)$ — leaving out $K$ loses 1 mark in numericals. > 3. **Unit inconsistencies.** Line speed in m/min must be converted to m/s before computing power in kW: divide by 60. Many students forget this and arrive at values off by a factor of 60. > 4. **Assuming line pull is constant across layers.** The pull DECREASES with outer layers (inverse of drum diameter) — failing to state this loses 1–2 marks in theory questions. > 5. **Omitting the clutch lever position in diagrams.** A mooring winch diagram without the clutch engagement lever is incomplete. Examiners deduct 1 mark. > 6. **Wrong formula for effective drum diameter.** The first-layer effective diameter is $D + d$ (not just $D$). At the $n$-th layer, it is $D + (2n-1)d$ (or equivalently $D_d + 2(n-1)d$ for successive layers). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Quick-Reference Bullet Checklist - **Mooring winch** = deck machinery to handle mooring lines for vessel securing. - **Two main types of drive:** Electric (most common) and Hydraulic (for variable speed). - **Three operational modes:** Heave in, Pay out (under power), Free-spool. - **Main components:** Motor, Gearbox, Spool Clutch, Bull Gear, Main Drum, Warping Drum, Band Brake, Disc Brake, Pressure Roller. - **Band brake** = static, manual, holding brake. - **Disc brake** = dynamic, automatic, fail-safe (spring-applied, hydraulically released). - **Spool clutch** = engages/disengages drum from gear train. - **Drum capacity formula:** $L = K \cdot \pi \cdot n \cdot (D + n \cdot d)$. - **Filling factor K:** typically $0.85$ to $0.90$. - **BHL** $= 0.6$ to $0.8 \times MBL$. - **Line pull decreases** with successive layers; **line speed increases** correspondingly. - **Power relation:** $P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{line} \cdot v_{line}}{\eta_{total}}$. - **Total efficiency** = product of gear, clutch, and bearing efficiencies (typically $0.75$ to $0.85$). - **Classification standards:** ISO 7825, ISO 3730, IRS Rules, LR Rules, DNV Rules. - **SOLAS-mandated feature:** Fail-safe disc brake that auto-applies on power failure. - **Combined winch-windlass** uses a **wildcat** (chain sprocket) on the same drum shaft. - **Pressure roller** prevents wire slippage during heaving. - **Typical power range:** 15 kW to 75 kW for shipboard mooring winches. - **Typical motor speed (4-pole, 50 Hz):** 1500 rpm; **typical drum speed:** 5 to 15 rpm; **gear ratio:** 100:1 to 150:1. ### Mnemonic for Component Memory — **"MGB-DCD-BBP"** - **M**otor - **G**earbox (reduction) - **B**ull gear - **D**rum (main) - **C**lutch (spool) - **D**rum (warping) - **B**rake (band) - **B**rake (disc) - **P**ressure roller

topics.title: Anchors and Anchor Chains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Anchors and Anchor Chains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Anchor — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> An **anchor** is a heavy, usually forged or cast steel device, fitted to the end of a chain or wire rope and attached to a ship's hull via the **hawse pipe**, **chain stopper**, and **windlass**. Its primary function is to provide a **temporary mooring** by gripping the seabed through the resultant combination of the anchor's own weight and the **horizontal pull (catenary pull)** generated by the suspended chain cable.

The anchor must satisfy three functional demands simultaneously:
1. **Sufficient Holding Power** ($P_h$) — Resistance against dragging.
2. **Rapid Embedding & Penetration** — Quick grip on the seabed (sand, mud, clay, rock).
3. **Reliable Recovery** — Ability to be hauled back, breaking out cleanly.

## 1.2 Anchor Chain (Chain Cable) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> The **anchor chain** (also called the **chain cable**) is the connecting medium between the ship and the anchor. It is constructed from **studded forged steel links** of specified grades (U1, U2, U3 per ISO 4565 or equivalent Classification Society standards). The function of the chain is to (a) provide a **weight-induced catenary** that applies a near-horizontal force at the anchor, (b) absorb shock loads elastically, and (c) allow the anchor to be hauled in via the **wildcat** of the windlass.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Helium Balloon on a String" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine tying a heavy iron ball to one end of a **heavy chain** and pulling the other end horizontally. The chain will sag into a **catenary curve** (like a hanging rope). The weight of the suspended chain pulls the iron ball almost **horizontally into the ground**, rather than lifting it upward. This is exactly how a ship is held: the **ship pulls up at the top of the chain**, the **chain's weight creates a near-horizontal pull at the seabed**, and the **anchor (iron ball) holds purely by its fluke friction**, not by being lifted.
>
> The catenary shape is the *invisible hero* — without it, the ship would lift the anchor straight out of the seabed.

## 1.4 Primary Classification of Anchors

| Category | Anchors Included | Holding Mechanism |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Light-weight (Stocked) Anchors** | Admiralty Pattern, Martin (Kedge) | Stock rotates shank; flukes bite |
| **Stockless Anchors** | Hall's, Fisher's, AC-14, Baldt, Flipper Delta | Pivot at crown; flukes flip |
| **High Holding Power (HHP)** | AC-14, Bruce, Danforth, Flipper Delta, Stevpris | Long flukes, large area → high $P_h$ / $W_a$ |
| **Super High Holding Power (SHHP)** | Stevshark, Stevpris Mk 6, Pool TW | Anchor weight reduced up to 75% |
| **Special Purpose** | Mushroom (permanent mooring), Grapnel, Drag | Specific seabed conditions |

## 1.5 Standard Chain Grades and Key Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Chain Grade Notation (per ISO 4565 / IACS):**
> - **Grade U1 (Studless)**, **U2 (Studded)**, **U3 (Studded)** are the three main ISO categories.
> - **Studded chain** is standard for merchant vessels; the **stud** prevents the link from collapsing (kinking) and increases link strength by ~15–20% over studless chain.
> - **Nominal diameter $d$** is the diameter of the **wire rod** before forging the link.
> - **Standard shot length:** 1 shot = **27.5 m (15 fathoms)** (new ISO) or **22.86 m (12.5 fathoms)** (older British standard). For KTU problems, **12.5 fathoms per shot** is conventional unless specified.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Anchor (Detailed)

> [!NOTE]
> The Admiralty Pattern Anchor is the universal *reference geometry*; all stockless anchors are geometric variations of it.

**Parts of an Anchor:**

1. **Shank** — The main vertical/longitudinal member. Carries the load.
2. **Stock** — A heavy cross-bar (in Admiralty pattern) that forces the anchor to lie flat on the seabed.
3. **Crown** — The junction point where the **arms** meet the **shank**.
4. **Arms** — The two members that carry the flukes.
5. **Flukes** — The **triangular / spade-shaped** plates that **penetrate the seabed**.
6. **Bill (Pea)** — The leading tip of the fluke.
7. **Throat** — The point where the arm meets the shank on the underside.
8. **Ring (Shackle Eye)** — The hole at the top of the shank for the **anchor shackle**.
9. **Truss / Buckler** (optional) — A strengthening piece on the shank of large anchors.
10. **Anchor Shackle** — A heavy bow shackle connecting the anchor ring to the first chain shackle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stockless Anchors** replace the *fixed stock* with a **swiveling stock or pivot mechanism** at the **crown**. When the anchor reaches the seabed, a weight on the shank (or fluke geometry) flips the flukes into the digging position, and the shank rotates about the **stockless pivot pin**.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Chain Link and a Studded Link

A **studded common chain link** consists of:

- **Outer link** — Made of a single bar bent into an oval shape, the ends forge-welded.
- **Stud** — A **mild-steel crossbar** inserted transversely through the center of every alternate link (or every link, depending on grade). The stud:
  - Prevents the link from collapsing/kinking.
  - Increases weight by ~10%.
  - Increases strength by ~15–20% over equivalent studless link.
- **Weld zone** — The forge-welded joint of the outer link, normally **opposite to the stud** for balanced strength.

> [!NOTE]
> In a **chain cable**, links alternate in orientation: each link lies at **90° to its neighbor** (perpendicular orientation). This prevents the chain from kinking and distributes load uniformly.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All values in **SI units** unless otherwise stated. **Bold** values are standard.

### 2.3.1 Anchor Holding Power

| # | Formula | Variable Definitions | Application |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $P_h = C_h \cdot W_a$ | $P_h$ = holding power (N); $C_h$ = holding coefficient; $W_a$ = anchor weight (N) | Basic holding power |
| 2 | $C_h = 2 \text{ to } 4$ (ordinary anchor); $C_h = 6 \text{ to } 15$ (HHP) | Holding power per unit weight | Anchor selection |
| 3 | $T = \dfrac{W_c \cdot L_c}{D}$ (for small catenary angles) | $T$ = horizontal pull at anchor; $W_c$ = chain weight per unit length; $L_c$ = length of chain paid out; $D$ = water depth | Catenary effect |
| 4 | Effective holding $= P_h \cdot \mu$ (on seabed) | $\mu$ = seabed friction coefficient | Sand: 0.5–0.7; Mud: 0.3–0.5; Clay: 0.4–0.6 |
| 5 | Number of anchors required: $n = \dfrac{P_{req}}{P_h}$ | $P_{req}$ = required total holding force | Rule of thumb: 3 anchors for first-class ships |

### 2.3.2 Chain Cable Dimensions & Strength

| # | Formula | Variable Definitions | Application |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------|
| 6 | Common link outer length $L_o = 6d$ | $d$ = nominal chain diameter (mm) | Standard link geometry |
| 7 | Common link inner length $L_i = 3.2d$ | — | Inner clearance |
| 8 | Stud diameter $d_s = 1.5d$ (Grade U2) | — | Stud sizing |
| 9 | Pitch (link center-to-center): $P = 3.4d$ to $3.6d$ | — | Per ISO |
| 10 | Weight per meter: $W_{per\,m} = 0.0219 d^2$ (kg/m) approx. for studded | $d$ in mm | Empirical |
| 11 | Better: $W_{per\,m} = k \cdot d^2$ where $k$ depends on grade | — | Use table values |
| 12 | Breaking load: $BL = 0.43 \cdot d^2$ (kN) for U1 studless | $d$ in mm | Proof load = $\frac{2}{5}$ BL |
| 13 | Proof load: $PL = 0.46 \cdot BL$ | — | Acceptance test |
| 14 | D/d ratio (outboard swivel, end link): $D_{shackle} = 6d$ to $7d$ | — | Pin diameter |
| 15 | Number of shots: $n_{shots} = \dfrac{L_{chain}}{15 \text{ fathoms}} = \dfrac{L_{chain}}{27.5\,m}$ | — | Range: 8–12 shots |

### 2.3.3 Catenary & Effective Force Equations

| # | Formula | Variable Definitions | Application |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------|
| 16 | Catenary sag: $s = \dfrac{W_c \cdot L_c^2}{2T}$ (approx.) | $s$ = sag; $T$ = tension | When $s < 0.15 \cdot D$ |
| 17 | Catenary constant: $a = \dfrac{T}{W_c}$ | — | Half-span of catenary |
| 18 | Length of chain: $L_c = 2a \sinh\left(\dfrac{D}{2a}\right) \approx D + \dfrac{8 s^2}{3 D}$ | — | Catenary approximation |
| 19 | Total sag = approx. 0.4 × depth for full catenary | — | Practical design |

### 2.3.4 Range of Cable (Number of Shots Required)

| # | Rule | Application |
|---|------|-------------|
| 20 | $L_c = 4D$ to $6D$ (sheltered water) | Anchor in 50 m → 200–300 m of chain |
| 21 | $L_c = 6D$ to $8D$ (open roadstead) | Standard ocean anchorage |
| 22 | $L_c = 8D$ to $10D$ (exposed/anchorage) | High wind/wave conditions |
| 23 | $L_c = 10D+$ (storm conditions) | Hurricanes, cyclones |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Quick Rule:** The weight of the chain paid out (excluding anchor weight) should equal **3 × D × weight of anchor (W)** in tonnes. This is the **"3·D·W" rule of catenary holding**.

## 2.4 Anchor Chain Accessories (Critical for KTU)

1. **Anchor Shackle (Bow Shackle)** — Connects the anchor ring to the first chain link.
2. **Common Link / Detachable Link** — A symmetrical link with which chain lengths are joined.
3. **Joining Shackle (D-Shackle)** — An asymmetric shackle used to connect detachable links.
4. **Outboard Swivel (Mid-Link Swivel)** — Placed between the 4th and 5th shackle from the anchor; prevents chain twisting.
5. **Inboard Swivel** — Placed in the chain between the bitter end and the windlass.
6. **End Link** — A larger link at each end of a chain section for shackle attachment.
7. **Enlarged Link (Increased Pitch Link)** — Spaced every 22.5 m (12.5 fathoms) to mark shot boundaries.
8. **Bitter End** — The inboard end of the chain secured in the **chain locker** by a **bitters** (a strong pin) and a **slipper**.
9. **Chain Stopper** — A deck-fitted clamping device to take the load off the windlass.
10. **Windlass Wildcat** — A sprocket-grooved drum that engages the chain links.
11. **Hawse Pipe** — A pipe through which the chain passes from the deck to outside the ship.
12. **Spurling Pipe** — A vertical pipe leading chain from deck to chain locker.
13. **Chain Locker** — The compartment below the spurling pipe where chain is stowed.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mooring & Berthing:** Anchors + chains are the **primary dynamic mooring system** for offshore platforms, FPSOs, VLCCs, and naval vessels.
- **Station-Keeping:** Dynamic Positioning (DP) systems use anchors as their **reference points** — the chain is now augmented with synthetic rope or wire rope for ultra-deep water.
- **Emergency Holding:** When a ship loses propulsion (e.g., Suez Canal grounding 2021), anchors + chain are the **last line of safety**.
- **Buoy & Beacon Mooring:** Mushroom anchors + heavy studded chains form permanent seabed moorings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Holding Power of an Anchor

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Setup:** The holding power $P_h$ of an anchor is empirically defined as the **force required to drag the anchor horizontally** through the seabed. It depends on:
> - The weight of the anchor ($W_a$),
> - The holding coefficient ($C_h$) — a function of anchor type, fluke area, and seabed.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** The basic force balance on the seabed:

The seabed exerts a **resistive force** on the anchor flukes. For a given soil failure mechanism, the resistive force per unit area of fluke penetration depth $h$ is:

$$\sigma_r = K_p \cdot \gamma_{soil} \cdot h$$

where:
- $K_p$ = passive earth pressure coefficient (≈ 3 to 5 for sand)
- $\gamma_{soil}$ = unit weight of seabed soil (N/m³)

**Step 2:** Integrate over the fluke area $A_f$ and the penetration depth $h_p$:

$$P_{fluke} = \int_0^{h_p} K_p \cdot \gamma_{soil} \cdot h \cdot b(h) \, dh$$

For a typical triangular fluke where $b(h) = b_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{h}{h_p}\right)$:

$$P_{fluke} = \frac{1}{6} K_p \cdot \gamma_{soil} \cdot b_0 \cdot h_p^2$$

**Step 3:** Empirically, this is condensed into the **practical holding power formula**:

$$\boxed{P_h = C_h \cdot W_a}$$

where $C_h$ is calibrated to each anchor type:
- **Admiralty / Fisher's / Hall's**: $C_h \approx 2$ to $4$
- **AC-14, Bruce**: $C_h \approx 6$ to $10$
- **Stevpris, HHP**: $C_h \approx 10$ to $15$

**Step 4:** Example — for a 10-tonne AC-14 anchor with $C_h = 8$:

$$P_h = 8 \times 10 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 784{,}800\,N \approx 785\,kN$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Catenary Force at the Anchor

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Setup:** A chain hanging in water forms a **catenary curve**. The horizontal pull at the anchor (bottom end) is what gives the anchor its horizontal digging force.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Standard catenary equation (Cartesian form, origin at lowest point):

$$y = a \left[\cosh\left(\frac{x}{a}\right) - 1\right]$$

where $a$ is the **catenary constant** (length) defined as:

$$a = \frac{T_0}{w_c}$$

with:
- $T_0$ = horizontal tension at the lowest point (i.e., the **horizontal pull at the anchor**)
- $w_c$ = chain weight per unit length **in water** (= weight in air × (1 − ρ_water/ρ_steel))

**Step 2:** The length of chain from the lowest point $(x=0, y=0)$ to any point $(x, y)$ is:

$$s = a \cdot \sinh\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)$$

**Step 3:** The total vertical force at the top (where the chain leaves the water) equals the **weight of the chain in the water**:

$$T_y = w_c \cdot s$$

**Step 4:** The **tension at the top** (ship-side end) is:

$$T_{top} = \sqrt{T_0^2 + (w_c \cdot s)^2}$$

**Step 5:** For a **flat catenary** (low tension, deep water, long chain), the small-angle approximation gives:

$$T_0 = \frac{w_c \cdot L_c^2}{2 \cdot D}$$

where $L_c$ is total chain length and $D$ is water depth.

**Step 6:** This horizontal pull $T_0$ is what **rotates the flukes** of a stockless anchor and **drives them into the seabed** — the catenary is the "actuator" of the anchor.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Number of Shots (Length of Cable Out)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Setup:** The standard ship design rule for the **length of chain to be paid out (range of cable)** is based on the ship's length, depth, and sea conditions.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** **Trotman's Rule / Standard KTU approach:**

$$L_c = k \cdot D$$

where $k$ is a factor depending on conditions:
- Sheltered: $k = 4$ to $5$
- Open roadstead: $k = 6$
- Open sea (good holding): $k = 7$
- Open sea (poor holding): $k = 8$ to $10$

**Step 2:** Number of shots:

$$n_{shots} = \frac{L_c}{L_{shot}} = \frac{L_c}{27.5\,m} \quad \text{(or } \frac{L_c}{22.86\,m}\text{ for 12.5 fathoms)}$$

**Step 3:** The **"3·D·W" catenary weight rule:**

The mass of chain in the water should equal **3 × depth × mass of one anchor** to ensure the catenary effect provides sufficient horizontal pull without lifting the anchor.

$$M_{chain} = 3 \cdot D \cdot M_{anchor}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for the **number of shots**:

$$L_c = \frac{3 \cdot D \cdot M_{anchor}}{w_c}$$

**Step 5:** Example Calculation:
- Anchor mass: $M_a = 10$ tonnes
- Water depth: $D = 30$ m
- Chain mass per meter (in water): $w_c = 0.15$ tonnes/m
- Required chain mass: $M_c = 3 \times 30 \times 10 = 900$ tonnes
- Required chain length: $L_c = 900 / 0.15 = 6000$ m

This is an **extreme case**; in practice, $L_c$ is constrained by available shots (~10–12) and seabed conditions.

**Step 6:** Conversion to shots (1 shot = 27.5 m):
$$n_{shots} = \frac{6000}{27.5} \approx 218 \text{ shots}$$

This is unrealistic, so the rule is used as a **design check**, not a primary design rule.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Chain Breaking Load and Proof Load

**Step 1:** For ISO Grade U2 studded chain, the empirical breaking load is:

$$BL = 0.43 \cdot d^2 \text{ (kN, with } d \text{ in mm)}$$

Wait — more accurately, the formula for breaking load in kN is:

$$BL = k_{grade} \cdot d^2$$

where $k_{grade}$ depends on grade:
- U1: $k = 0.29$
- U2: $k = 0.43$
- U3: $k = 0.55$

(All in kN, with $d$ in mm.)

**Step 2:** The **proof load** is the load applied during the test:

$$PL = 0.46 \cdot BL \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot BL$$

**Step 3:** Example: For 50 mm Grade U2 chain:
$$BL = 0.43 \times 50^2 = 0.43 \times 2500 = 1075\,kN$$
$$PL = 0.46 \times 1075 = 494.5\,kN \approx 50.4 \text{ tonnes-force}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Table

| Quantity | Formula | Typical Value (for $d=50\,mm$ U2) |
|----------|---------|----------------------------------|
| Outer link length | $L_o = 6d$ | $300\,mm$ |
| Inner link length | $L_i = 3.2d$ | $160\,mm$ |
| Stud diameter | $d_s = 1.5d$ | $75\,mm$ |
| Link pitch | $P = 3.4d$ | $170\,mm$ |
| Mass per meter (in air) | $m = 0.0219 d^2$ | $54.75\,kg/m$ |
| Mass per meter (in sea water) | $m' = m (1 - 1.025/7.85)$ | $\approx 47.6\,kg/m$ |
| Breaking load | $BL = 0.43 d^2$ | $1075\,kN$ |
| Proof load | $PL = 0.46 \cdot BL$ | $494.5\,kN$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Anchor & Chain Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ship Hull] -->|Shackle| B[Anchor Hawse Pipe]
    B --> C[Chain Stopper]
    C --> D[Windlass Wildcat]
    D --> E[Spurling Pipe]
    E --> F[Chain Locker]
    F --> G[Bitter End and Slipper]
    
    D -->|Paid Out Chain| H[Hawse Pipe Outboard]
    H --> I[Anchor Shackle Bow]
    I --> J[Anchor Ring Eye]
    J --> K[Anchor Shank]
    K --> L[Crown and Pivot]
    L --> M[Flukes]
    M --> N[Seabed Penetration]
    
    H -->|Accessories| O[Joining Shackle]
    O --> P[Detachable Link]
    P --> Q[Outboard Swivel]
    Q --> R[Common Studded Link]
    R --> S[End Link Section]
    
    style A fill:#2c3e50,color:#fff
    style N fill:#e67e22,color:#fff
    style F fill:#27ae60,color:#fff
    style Q fill:#c0392b,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Anchor Type Comparison (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ship Anchors] --> B[Stocked Anchors]
    A --> C[Stockless Anchors]
    A --> D[Special Anchors]
    
    B --> B1[Admiralty Pattern]
    B --> B2[Martin Kedge]
    
    C --> C1[Hall Type]
    C --> C2[Fisher Type]
    C --> C3[AC-14 HHP]
    C --> C4[Bruce HHP]
    C --> C5[Danforth HHP]
    C --> C6[Flipper Delta]
    
    D --> D1[Mushroom Mooring]
    D --> D2[Grapnel]
    D --> D3[Concrete Block]
    
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff
    style B fill:#a93226,color:#fff
    style C fill:#1e8449,color:#fff
    style D fill:#6c3483,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Chain Cable Cross-Sectional Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Common Link Outer] -->|Forge Welded| L2[Common Link Inner]
    L2 -->|Transverse Insert| ST[Stud]
    ST -->|Prevents Kinking| L1
    L2 -->|90 deg Orient| NL[Next Link]
    NL -->|90 deg Orient| NL2[Subsequent Link]
    
    P1[Dimensions] --> P2[Outer Length 6d]
    P1 --> P3[Inner Length 3.2d]
    P1 --> P4[Stud Diameter 1.5d]
    P1 --> P5[Pitch 3.4d]
    
    style L1 fill:#3498db,color:#fff
    style ST fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff
    style P1 fill:#f39c12,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Anchor and Chain Securing Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Step 1: Ship Approaches Anchorage] --> S2[Step 2: Brake Test on Windlass]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Walk Back Anchor to Crown]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Release Brake and Let Go]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Chain Pays Out under Gravity]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Apply Brake to Control Speed]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Pay Out Desired Range of Cable]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Secure Chain via Stopper]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Measure Tension on Chain]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Confirm Anchor Holds]
    
    style S1 fill:#3498db,color:#fff
    style S4 fill:#e67e22,color:#fff
    style S8 fill:#27ae60,color:#fff
    style S10 fill:#c0392b,color:#fff
```

## 4.5 Catenary Force Diagram (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ship Hull at Top] -->|Vertical Load T| B[Top of Chain]
    B -->|Catenary Curve| C[Bottom of Chain at Anchor]
    C -->|Horizontal Pull T0| D[Anchor Flukes]
    D -->|Digging Force| E[Seabed Soil Reaction]
    
    F[Weight of Suspended Chain w_c] -->|Provides Horizontal Pull| C
    G[Anchor Weight W_a] -->|Resists Lifting| D
    
    style A fill:#2c3e50,color:#fff
    style C fill:#e67e22,color:#fff
    style D fill:#c0392b,color:#fff
    style E fill:#27ae60,color:#fff
```
