# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
> **Q: List any three parts of an Admiralty Pattern Anchor and state their function.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The Admiralty Pattern Anchor consists of the following parts:
1. **Shank** — The main longitudinal member that transmits the load from the ring to the arms; it is the backbone of the anchor.
2. **Flukes** — The flat, triangular plates at the ends of the arms that penetrate and grip the seabed.
3. **Stock** — A heavy crossbar fixed to the upper end of the shank that ensures the anchor always lands flat on the seabed (i.e., one fluke downward and one upward), so that a fluke always digs in.

*(Other acceptable parts: Crown, Throat, Bill, Ring, Arms, Truss.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Sessional II]**
> **Q: What is the function of a stud in a chain cable? Mention any two advantages.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The **stud** is a crossbar of mild steel inserted transversely through the center of an alternate (or all) links of a chain cable.

**Functions / Advantages:**
1. **Prevents kinking** — The stud stops the link from collapsing sideways under load, keeping the link geometry intact.
2. **Increases chain strength** — A studded link is approximately **15–20% stronger** than a studless link of the same diameter.
3. **Increases weight** — The stud adds about 10% to the chain weight, which improves the catenary holding effect.
4. **Reduces wear** — The stud minimizes internal abrasion between link surfaces.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, ESE Module 3]**
> **Q (a):** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of an **Admiralty Pattern Anchor**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **Q (b):** A vessel is fitted with **stockless bower anchors of 8 tonnes each**. The windlass has a holding capacity of 25 tonnes at the wildcat. Calculate the **range of cable** required for an anchorage depth of **25 m** in open sea conditions, given the chain weight in water = **0.18 tonnes/m**. State the assumptions made. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction:** The Admiralty Pattern Anchor is a *stocked* anchor consisting of:
- **Shank** — A long vertical forged-steel bar.
- **Stock** — A heavy timber or steel crossbar (typical weight ≈ 1/10 of anchor weight) bolted to the shank near the ring end.
- **Arms** — Two forged members that branch from the **crown** (bottom of shank) at an angle of approximately 45–50°.
- **Flukes** — Triangular spades at the end of each arm.
- **Bill (Pea)** — The sharpened tip of the fluke.
- **Throat** — Junction of the inner curve of the arm and the shank.
- **Ring (Bolt Ring)** — A forged ring at the top of the shank for shackle connection.
- **Shackle** — Connects ring to the first chain link.

**Working Principle:**
1. When the anchor is dropped, the **stock** (being heavy) keeps the anchor oriented such that one fluke points down and the other up.
2. As the ship drifts astern, the **chain pulls horizontally** on the ring, causing the fluke to dig into the seabed.
3. The **deeper the fluke digs**, the greater the **soil resistance**, providing holding force.
4. The fluke reaches a position of **maximum resistance** when the shank lies nearly flat on the seabed.

**Advantages:**
- Reliable in **all seabed conditions** (sand, mud, clay).
- **Stock can be replaced** if damaged.
- **Simple design**, easy to manufacture.
- **Holds reliably** even at extreme pull angles (up to 90°).

**Limitations:**
- **Heavy** (low $C_h / W_a$ ratio).
- **Stock is fragile** — can break on rocky seabeds.
- **Slow handling** — must be hauled crown-up on the cathead.
- **Cannot be stowed in hawse pipe** (requires cathead or bill-board).

*(Award: 2 marks construction, 2 marks working, 1.5 marks advantages, 1.5 marks limitations = 7 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Anchor weight $M_a = 8$ tonnes each (×2 = 16 tonnes total)
- Wildcat holding capacity = 25 tonnes
- Water depth $D = 25$ m
- Chain weight in water $w_c = 0.18$ tonnes/m
- Open sea anchorage → use **$L_c = 7D$ to $8D$**

**Assumptions:**
1. The chain forms a fully developed catenary.
2. The seabed is level.
3. Neglect hydrodynamic forces on the chain.
4. Single anchor is used for analysis (the second is spare).

**Step 1: Compute range of cable using the depth rule:**
$$L_c = 7D = 7 \times 25 = 175\,m \quad \text{(lower bound)}$$
$$L_c = 8D = 8 \times 25 = 200\,m \quad \text{(upper bound)}$$

**Step 2: Use the 3·D·W catenary weight rule to check:**
$$M_{chain} = 3 \cdot D \cdot M_a = 3 \times 25 \times 8 = 600 \text{ tonnes}$$
$$L_{chain} = \frac{M_{chain}}{w_c} = \frac{600}{0.18} = 3333\,m$$

This is **impractical** (too long), so we accept the **practical rule of $L_c = 7D$ to $8D$**.

**Step 3: Compute weight of 200 m of chain in water:**
$$M_{c,200} = 0.18 \times 200 = 36 \text{ tonnes}$$

This is greater than the wildcat capacity (25 t), so the **chain stopper** must be used to take the load before working the windlass.

**Step 4: Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{L_c = 175 \text{ to } 200\,m \;\; \text{of chain (6 to 7 shots at 27.5 m each)}}$$

The chain stopper must be engaged when the windlass is not in use.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, ESE Module 3 Alternate]**
> **Q (a):** Explain with sketches the **constructional features and function** of the following chain cable accessories: (i) **Anchor Shackle**, (ii) **Outboard Swivel**, (iii) **Joining Shackle**, (iv) **Detachable Link**. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **Q (b):** A **50 mm diameter Grade U2 studded chain** is to be used on a vessel. Calculate:
> (i) The **breaking load**,
> (ii) The **proof load**,
> (iii) The **mass per meter** in air and in sea water (density = 1.025 t/m³),
> (iv) The **outer length, inner length, and pitch** of the common link.
> Use the standard ISO 4565 empirical formulas. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

**(i) Anchor Shackle (Bow Shackle):**
- A large, **U-shaped forged shackle** with a removable screw pin.
- Connects the **anchor ring** to the **first end link** of the chain.
- Has a **D/d ratio of 6 to 7** (where D is pin diameter, d is chain diameter).
- Made of **high-tensile forged steel**, designed for full breaking load of the chain.

**(ii) Outboard Swivel (Mid-Link Swivel):**
- Placed between the **4th and 5th shackle** from the anchor (i.e., ~22.5 m from the anchor).
- Allows the chain to **rotate freely** about its axis, preventing the formation of **kinks, twists, and false bights**.
- Consists of an inner and outer member with a bearing surface, locked with a nut.
- Must be **completely sealed** to prevent water ingress into the bearing.

**(iii) Joining Shackle (D-Shackle):**
- A **D-shaped shackle** used to **join two detachable links** together.
- Asymmetric design means it can only be fitted in **one orientation**.
- Used where a **detachable connection** is needed but a swivel is not.

**(iv) Detachable Link:**
- A symmetrical, **C-shaped (or pear-shaped) link** with a slot at one end.
- The two ends are **pushed together** and locked with a **wedge pin** (or round pin).
- Used to **join two chain sections** at the manufacturing/servicing level; can be detached for repair.
- Standard design ensures **no reduction in chain strength** at the joint.

*(Allocation: 1.75 marks per accessory. 0.5 marks for sketch, 0.75 marks for function, 0.5 marks for design feature.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** Chain diameter $d = 50$ mm, Grade U2 studded.

**(i) Breaking Load:**
$$BL = 0.43 \times d^2 = 0.43 \times 50^2 = 0.43 \times 2500 = 1075\,kN$$
$$\boxed{BL = 1075\,kN \approx 109.7 \text{ tonnes-force}}$$

**(ii) Proof Load:**
$$PL = 0.46 \times BL = 0.46 \times 1075 = 494.5\,kN$$
$$\boxed{PL = 494.5\,kN \approx 50.4 \text{ tonnes-force}}$$

**(iii) Mass per meter:**

In air (empirical):
$$m_{air} = 0.0219 \times d^2 = 0.0219 \times 2500 = 54.75\,kg/m$$

In sea water (with steel density $\rho_{steel} = 7.85$ t/m³):
$$m_{sw} = m_{air} \times \left(1 - \frac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{steel}}\right) = 54.75 \times \left(1 - \frac{1.025}{7.85}\right)$$
$$m_{sw} = 54.75 \times (1 - 0.1306) = 54.75 \times 0.8694 = 47.60\,kg/m$$

$$\boxed{m_{air} = 54.75\,kg/m \quad ; \quad m_{sw} = 47.60\,kg/m}$$

**(iv) Link Geometry:**
$$L_o = 6d = 6 \times 50 = 300\,mm$$
$$L_i = 3.2d = 3.2 \times 50 = 160\,mm$$
$$P = 3.4d = 3.4 \times 50 = 170\,mm$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (and Marks Lost):**
>
> 1. **Confusing the catenary constant $a$ with the sag $s$.** — $a$ is a length, not a force. Use $a = T_0 / w_c$ correctly.
> 2. **Forgetting the 15–20% strength boost from the stud** when calculating breaking load.
> 3. **Mixing studded and studless chain formulas.** Grade U1 is **studless**, while U2 and U3 are **studded**. Apply correct empirical constants.
> 4. **Using $L_c = 4D$ in open sea conditions** — this is for sheltered water only. Open sea requires 7D–8D.
> 5. **Forgetting the 3·D·W catenary weight rule** — Examiners specifically test this in problems involving catenary and anchor selection.
> 6. **Not mentioning chain stopper** in part (b) of the numerical problem when chain weight exceeds wildcat capacity — this loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 7. **Skipping the "assumption" statement** in numerical problems — KTU valuation always deducts 0.5 marks for missing assumptions.
> 8. **Confusing anchor types** — Many students write about the Bruce anchor when asked about AC-14. Memorize: AC-14 is the **standard HHP anchor used by most modern ships**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Anchors and Anchor Chains**

### Anchors
- ✅ **Admiralty Pattern** is the reference design; has a **stock**.
- ✅ **Stockless anchors** (Hall, Fisher, AC-14) pivot at the crown.
- ✅ **HHP** (High Holding Power) = $C_h \cdot W_a$ with $C_h = 6$ to $15$.
- ✅ **Ordinary anchor** = $C_h = 2$ to $4$.
- ✅ Anchor parts: **Shank, Stock, Crown, Arms, Flukes, Bill, Throat, Ring**.
- ✅ The **fluke** is the digging member; the **stock** ensures correct orientation.
- ✅ **AC-14, Bruce, Danforth, Flipper Delta** are HHP types; reduce anchor weight by 25–75%.

### Anchor Chains
- ✅ **Studded chain** is the marine standard; stud adds 15–20% strength.
- ✅ Common link: **$L_o = 6d$**, **$L_i = 3.2d$**, **Pitch = 3.4d**, **Stud $\phi = 1.5d$**.
- ✅ Mass per meter (in air): **$m = 0.0219 d^2$** (kg/m, d in mm).
- ✅ Grade U1 (studless), U2 (studded), U3 (studded).
- ✅ **Breaking load:** $BL = 0.43 d^2$ (kN, Grade U2).
- ✅ **Proof load:** $PL = 0.46 \cdot BL$.
- ✅ **1 shot = 27.5 m (15 fathoms)**; older British = 22.86 m (12.5 fathoms).

### Catenary and Holding
- ✅ Catenary constant: $a = T_0 / w_c$.
- ✅ Catenary equation: $y = a [\cosh(x/a) - 1]$.
- ✅ Horizontal pull at anchor: $T_0 = w_c L_c^2 / (2D)$ (small-sag approximation).
- ✅ **Range of cable rule:** 4D (sheltered) to 10D+ (storm).
- ✅ **3·D·W rule:** Chain weight = 3 × depth × anchor weight.

### Accessories
- ✅ **Anchor Shackle** (Bow) — connects anchor to first chain link.
- ✅ **Outboard Swivel** — between 4th and 5th shackle, prevents twisting.
- ✅ **Joining Shackle (D)** — connects detachable links.
- ✅ **Detachable Link** — C-shaped with wedge pin.
- ✅ **Chain Stopper** — takes load off windlass.
- ✅ **Bitter End** — inboard end secured in chain locker.
- ✅ **Enlarged Link** — marks every 22.5 m (12.5 fathoms), i.e., every shot.
- ✅ **Hawse Pipe** — passage from deck to shell.
- ✅ **Spurling Pipe** — vertical pipe to chain locker.

### Standards
- ✅ **ISO 4565** — chain grades and dimensions.
- ✅ **IACS** — International Association of Classification Societies.
- ✅ **Classification Society Rules** — Lloyd's, DNV, ABS, BV define chain testing.
- ✅ Proof load test: every chain link must withstand 46% of breaking load.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU questions on this topic commonly appear as **(i) 7-mark descriptive** (parts of anchor, working, accessories) and **(ii) 7-mark numerical** (holding power, catenary, range of cable, chain dimensions). Practice drawing **sketches** — full marks on descriptive questions require neat diagrams with **labels**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Davits and Lifeboats Lowering Mechanism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Davits and Lifeboats Lowering Mechanism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **davit** is a shore- or deck-mounted structural projection (typically a curved arm, radial arm, or pillar) fitted on board a vessel, designed to support, swing outboard, and lower a survival craft (lifeboat, liferaft, or rescue boat) safely to the water surface during an emergency or drill.

A **lifeboat lowering mechanism** is the integrated mechanical, electro-mechanical, or hydraulic system — comprising falls (wire ropes), winches, brakes, release hooks, blocks, and limit switches — that controls the controlled descent of the lifeboat from its stowed position to the embarkation deck and finally to the sea under gravity, with the ability to be arrested and held at any intermediate position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLAS Chapter III & LSA Code Compliance:** Every davit-launched lifeboat must comply with the International Life-Saving Appliance (LSA) Code, Chapter VI, and IMO Resolution MSC.48(66). The davit-launched survival craft, when loaded with its full complement of persons and equipment, must be capable of being launched in **not more than 10 minutes** under the worst prevailing conditions of trim, list, and windage.

> [!NOTE]
> **SWL (Safe Working Load):** The rated load of a davit equals the **total mass of the survival craft + full complement of persons + equipment + fuel/water** as per the Maker's Certificate (LSA Code §4.4.1). Typical SWL ranges from **1,500 kg to 25,000 kg** depending on boat capacity (e.g., a 150-person lifeboat ≈ 20,000 kg).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

### Analogy: The "Crane Arm at Home" 🍴
Imagine a **fishing rod with a reel** strapped to the side of a balcony. The rod (davit arm) holds the weight of a heavy basket (lifeboat). The reel (winch) lets out fishing line (falls) in a controlled manner so the basket reaches the ground (water) gently — not a free-fall crash, and not a stuck halt. A **thumb on the reel's drag** (brake) lets you stop the basket mid-air and hold it steady. That, in essence, is a shipboard lifeboat davit + lowering mechanism.

### Plain-English Explanation
On a large ship (tanker, bulk carrier, passenger vessel), lifeboats are **stowed high up on davits** along the upper deck, well above the waterline (often 15–30 m). In an abandon-ship situation, the crew must:
1. **Release the gripes** (straps holding the boat to its cradle).
2. **Swing the davit outboard** so the boat is clear of the ship's hull.
3. **Lower the boat** steadily to the water using a controlled gravity-based system, so people on board are not injured and the boat does not slam into the sea.

The mechanism must be **reliable under stress, darkness, list (up to 20°), and wind** — which is why regulatory bodies (SOLAS, Classification Societies like DNV, LR, BV) demand **dual-release systems, secondary braking, and fail-safe gravity lowering**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Davits (LSA Code §6.1)

| S.No. | Davit Type | Drive Method | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Gravity (Radial) Davit** | Pure gravity + centrifugal brake | Cargo ships, tankers |
| 2 | **Luffing / Arc Davit** | Gravity with arc-track swing-out | Passenger vessels |
| 3 | **Pivot / Sheath-screw Davit** | Mechanical screw jack for swing-out | Mid-size ships |
| 4 | **Single-Point Suspension Davit** | Single fall wire + on-load release | SOLAS fast-rescue boats |
| 5 | **Free-Fall Davit** | Gravity with rapid-release | Ro-Ro, FPSO, naval |
| 6 | **Telescopic / Slewing Davit** | Hydraulic slewing + luffing | Offshore platforms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Slewing** = horizontal rotation (azimuth). **Luffing** = vertical pivot (elevation). A combined slewing-and-luffing davit gives the widest operational envelope.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standards (Bolded for Recall)

- **Maximum launching time (SOLAS): 10 minutes** from "all hands at stations" to boat afloat.
- **Maximum list for launching: 20°** to either side (LSA Code §6.1.2.3).
- **Maximum wind speed for testing: 30 knots** during lowering.
- **Maximum lowering speed: ≤ 1.0 m/s** (LSA Code §6.1.2.4) to avoid impact injury.
- **Minimum lowering speed: ≥ 0.3 m/s** to prevent hanging mid-air.
- **Screening factor (falls): typically 10–12** falls per side for gravity davits.
- **Brake holding capacity: 1.5 × SWL** (with safety margin).
- **Person mass for calculation: 75 kg per person** (LSA Code, for cold climate, often 100 kg with gear).

---

## 1.5 Functional Requirements (LSA Code Summary)

1. **Lightweight construction** yet capable of repeated lowering under full load.
2. **Self-sustaining gravity lowering** — must lower even with loss of ship's main power.
3. **Limit switches** to prevent over-hoist (top) and over-travel (bottom).
4. **Manual (hand) operation** as a backup if power fails.
5. **Easy embarkation** at the embarkation deck level with minimal vertical drop.
6. **On-load release gear** — the hook must release under full load at the waterline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Free-Fall Exception:** Lifeboats launched by free-fall do NOT use traditional davits — they slide down a ramp. The term "davit-launched" specifically excludes free-fall boats.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Working of a Gravity Davit

A **gravity davit** is the most common type on board merchant vessels. Its lowering relies on the boat's own weight overcoming a controlled brake friction.

### 2.1.1 The Five Operational Phases

**Phase 1 — Stowed Position**
- The lifeboat sits in its cradle on the **deck-mounted skids**.
- **Gripe wires** lash the boat to the cradle (forward, aft, and sometimes amidships).
- The **falls (wire ropes)** are wound on the **winch drum** with the hook end attached to the lifting point (suspension link) of the boat.
- The **centrifugal brake band** is **engaged** (spring-loaded closed).
- The **hand brake** is **off**, but the centrifugal brake holds the load.

**Phase 2 — Preparation (Gripe Release)**
- Crew releases the forward and aft gripes.
- Centrifugal brake remains engaged — boat does NOT fall yet.
- Davit is still in the inboard position (boat rests on skids).

**Phase 3 — Outboard Swing**
- Davit arms (radial type) pivot outward about the **gooseneck pin** (top of mast).
- For radial gravity davits, the swing-out is **gravity-assisted**: as the boat is hoisted slightly, the centre of gravity moves outboard of the pivot, causing the arm to rotate outward.
- A **gooseneck fitting** connects the davit arm to the mast — allows articulation.
- The arm locks in the **outboard position** by a spring-loaded latch.

**Phase 4 — Lowering (Controlled Descent)**
- The **centrifugal brake lever** is moved to the **lower position**.
- The brake band **lifts off** the drum progressively — controlled by the operator.
- The boat descends under gravity.
- **Mechanical advantage** comes from the **falls** (multiple reeving): the load on each fall is `W / n` where `n` = number of falls per side.
- The winch operator controls descent speed via the **hand brake band** (secondary control).

**Phase 5 — Release at Waterline**
- The boat touches the water.
- The **on-load release hook** is operated (by pulling a cable from inside the boat or by hydrostatic release).
- The boat separates from the falls and is now waterborne.
- The falls are hauled back up by the **hand crank / electric motor**.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Component

| Component | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|
| **Centrifugal brake** | Self-regulating speed — faster rotation → more friction → self-limiting. Cannot fail open. |
| **Falls (wire ropes, 6×36 IWRC)** | Multiple reeving divides load; redundancy in case one fall parts. |
| **Sheave blocks** | Redirect force 180° without slipping; reduce winch drum torque. |
| **Limit switch (top)** | Cuts power when boat reaches stowed position — prevents over-hoist damage. |
| **Limit switch (slack)** | Detects slack rope and stops winch to avoid un-reeling on the drum. |
| **Hand brake band** | Operator override — applies static friction; holds the boat at any position. |
| **On-load release hook** | Allows release while load is on the hook (unlike a normal hook which would bend). |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these formulas** — they appear in 2-mark and 7-mark questions regularly.

### Table 2.3.1 — Core Davit & Lowering Equations

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Load on Davit** | $W_{total} = W_{boat} + (n_p \times m_p) + W_{eq}$ | SWL: boat + persons + equipment |
| 2 | **Load per Fall** | $F_{fall} = \dfrac{W_{total}}{n_{falls}}$ | $n_{falls}$ = number of falls per side |
| 3 | **Mechanical Advantage (Falls)** | $MA = \dfrac{n_{falls}}{2}$ | For block-and-tackle with 2 sheaves |
| 4 | **Drum Torque (Lowering)** | $T_{drum} = F_{fall} \times r_{drum}$ | $r_{drum}$ = effective drum radius |
| 5 | **Brake Torque (Holding)** | $T_{brake} = \mu \times F_{band} \times r_{drum} \times N$ | $N$ = number of friction surfaces |
| 6 | **Lowering Speed (Boat)** | $v_{boat} = \dfrac{v_{rope}}{n_{falls}/2}$ | Rope pays out faster than boat descends |
| 7 | **Hoist Power Required** | $P = \dfrac{W_{total} \times v_{hoist}}{\eta_{mech}}$ | $\eta_{mech}$ = mechanical efficiency (0.7–0.85) |
| 8 | **Design Factor (Wire Rope)** | $SF = \dfrac{UTS_{rope}}{F_{fall}}$ | Typically **SF = 4.0–5.0** for life-saving |
| 9 | **Heel Compensation** | $F_{heel} = W_{total} \times \sin(\theta)$ | Side force when ship lists by angle $\theta$ |
| 10 | **Free-fall Energy Absorbed** | $E = m \times g \times h$ | For free-fall davit energy dissipation check |

### Table 2.3.2 — Friction, Materials & Braking

| # | Parameter | Formula / Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 11 | **Centrifugal Force on Weights** | $F_c = m_c \times \omega^2 \times r_c$ | $m_c$ = brake weight mass; $\omega$ = drum angular velocity |
| 12 | **Friction Coefficient (Brake Lining)** | $\mu \approx 0.35$ (asbestos-free) | LSA Code tested lining material |
| 13 | **Drum Rotational Speed** | $\omega = \dfrac{v_{rope}}{r_{drum}}$ | rad/s |
| 14 | **Holding Torque Margin** | $T_{hold} \geq 1.5 \times T_{load}$ | Safety margin per LSA Code |

### Table 2.3.3 — Wire Rope Selection

| # | Parameter | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 15 | **Required Rope Diameter** | $d = \sqrt{\dfrac{F_{fall} \times SF}{K \times \sigma_{uts}}}$ | $K$ = factor (0.36 for 6×36) |
| 16 | **Minimum Breaking Load** | $MBL = d^2 \times K \times \sigma_{uts}$ | kN |
| 17 | **Number of Falls (Empirical)** | $n = \dfrac{W_{total}}{W_{per\_fall}}$ | $W_{per\_fall}$ ≈ 2,000–2,500 kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU exam:** A 150-person lifeboat weighs ~ **20,000 kg** loaded. With **12 falls** (6 per side), each fall carries ~ **1,700 kg**. The wire rope chosen is typically **24 mm 6×36 IWRC** galvanized steel with MBL ≈ 380 kN.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Passenger Ships (Cruise Liners):** Use **slewing & luffing davits** because the embarkation deck is far above the waterline (20+ m) and the ship may be at a list.
2. **Tankers (Oil/Chemical):** Use **gravity davits** because they are simple, fail-safe, and don't depend on power (important for hazardous-area ships where explosion risk is high).
3. **FPSOs & Offshore Platforms:** Use **slewing telescopic davits** for rescue boats — the boat must be lowered in seas up to **Sea State 6**.
4. **Naval Vessels:** Combine **davit** with **free-fall** capability for fast-craft (RHIBs, fast-rescue craft).

### Industry Failure Case Studies (Pedagogical Examples)
- **Costa Concordia (2012):** Improper davit operation under severe list (≥ 20°) led to a partial lifeboat slide-off; highlighted the **20° list limit** in LSA Code.
- **MV Estonia (1994):** The bow visor failure prevented full use of davits — shows the **davit must be at multiple stations**, not just one side.
- **Tricolor (2002):** Davit operation tested in heavy weather — confirmed **30-knot wind operational envelope** as per LSA Code §1.2.2.

---

## 2.5 Comparison: Mechanical vs Hydraulic Davits

| Criterion | Mechanical (Gravity) | Hydraulic |
|---|---|---|
| Power dependency | Zero (fully gravity) | High (needs pumps) |
| Maintenance cost | Low | High |
| Speed of lowering | Fixed (gravity-determined) | Variable (controlled) |
| Heel resistance | Limited (depends on weights) | Excellent |
| Initial cost | Lower | Higher |
| Common on | Tankers, bulkers | Cruise ships, naval |
| Failure mode | Brake wear | Pump / seal failure |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mechanical Advantage of the Falls System

### Problem Setup
A lifeboat of total loaded mass $M$ is suspended by $n$ falls (wire ropes) per side, all reeved through sheave blocks at the davit head. Derive the expression for **load per fall** and **drum torque required**.

### Given
- Total suspended weight: $W = M \cdot g$ (Newtons)
- Number of falls per side: $n$
- Sheave block configuration: 2-sheave top + 2-sheave bottom (typical)

### Derivation (Each Step Explicit)

**Step 1 — Total number of supporting falls**

The boat has two suspension points (one on each side of the boat). Each side has $n$ falls. The TOTAL number of rope segments supporting the boat (between the two blocks) is:

$$N_{segments} = 2n$$

**Reasoning:** Each fall is one continuous rope; on one side, $n$ parallel rope segments share the load of the boat's half-weight.

**Step 2 — Load on each individual fall**

The total weight $W$ is supported equally by all $N_{segments}$ rope segments. Therefore:

$$F_{fall} = \dfrac{W}{2n} = \dfrac{W_{total}}{2n}$$

**Step 3 — Winch drum rope tension**

The winch drum on each side pays out ONE continuous fall that reeves through the blocks. By the **pulley principle** (frictionless sheaves assumed), the tension throughout a single fall is constant and equals $F_{fall}$:

$$T_{drum} = F_{fall} = \dfrac{W_{total}}{2n}$$

**Step 4 — Torque on the winch drum**

The winch drum has effective radius $r$. The torque required to hold (or hoist) the boat is:

$$T_{required} = T_{drum} \times r = \dfrac{W_{total} \times r}{2n}$$

**Step 5 — Mechanical Advantage**

Mechanical advantage is defined as the ratio of load lifted to the input force on the rope. Since the operator/motor applies tension $T_{drum}$ to lift the load $W_{total}$:

$$MA = \dfrac{W_{total}}{T_{drum}} = \dfrac{W_{total}}{W_{total}/(2n)} = 2n$$

$$\boxed{MA = 2n \quad \text{(for } n \text{ falls per side)}}$$

### Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Problem)

**Given:** $W_{total} = 200,000$ N (20,000 kg × 9.81), $n = 6$ falls per side, $r_{drum} = 0.15$ m.

**Step A — Calculate load per fall:**

$$F_{fall} = \dfrac{200{,}000}{2 \times 6} = 16{,}667 \text{ N} \approx 16.67 \text{ kN}$$

**Step B — Drum tension:**

$$T_{drum} = 16.67 \text{ kN}$$

**Step C — Required drum torque:**

$$T_{required} = 16{,}667 \times 0.15 = 2{,}500 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step D — Mechanical advantage:**

$$MA = 2 \times 6 = 12$$

**Step E — Verification via hoisting force:**
A crew member applying just 1,667 N can hoist 20,000 N load (approx. 170 kg pull for a 20-tonne boat — a single person can winch up an empty boat).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Centrifugal Brake Holding Torque

### Problem Setup
The centrifugal brake consists of two weights attached to the rotating drum. As the drum spins, the weights swing outward under centrifugal force, pressing against a stationary friction band. Derive the **self-regulating braking torque**.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Centrifugal force on a brake weight**

A brake shoe of mass $m_c$ is pivoted at the drum centre at radius $r_c$ from the pivot. When the drum rotates at angular velocity $\omega$:

$$F_c = m_c \times \omega^2 \times r_c$$

**Step 2 — Normal force on the brake band**

The weight pivots outward until the centrifugal force component is balanced by the band's reaction. Assuming the weight is at the same radius as the band contact, the normal force $N$ on the band is approximately equal to the centrifugal component:

$$N \approx F_c = m_c \omega^2 r_c$$

**Step 3 — Friction force**

The friction force on the band from one shoe:

$$F_{friction} = \mu \times N = \mu \times m_c \times \omega^2 \times r_c$$

**Step 4 — Braking torque from both shoes**

With 2 shoes diametrically opposite, total braking torque is:

$$T_{brake} = 2 \times F_{friction} \times r_{drum}$$

$$T_{brake} = 2 \times \mu \times m_c \times \omega^2 \times r_c \times r_{drum}$$

**Step 5 — Self-regulating nature (key insight)**

Notice that $T_{brake} \propto \omega^2$. This means:
- If $\omega$ **increases** (boat falls faster) → $T_{brake}$ **increases rapidly** → drum slows.
- If $\omega$ **decreases** (boat slows) → $T_{brake}$ **decreases** → drum speeds up.

This creates a **negative-feedback equilibrium** at a constant terminal angular velocity:

$$\boxed{\omega_{terminal} = \sqrt{\dfrac{W_{total} \times r_{drum}}{2n \times 2 \times \mu \times m_c \times r_c \times r_{drum}}}}$$

$$\omega_{terminal} = \sqrt{\dfrac{W_{total}}{4n \times \mu \times m_c \times r_c}}$$

### Numerical Example

**Given:** $W_{total} = 200,000$ N, $n = 6$, $m_c = 2.5$ kg (each weight), $r_c = 0.10$ m, $\mu = 0.35$.

**Step A — Compute inside the square root:**

$$\dfrac{200{,}000}{4 \times 6 \times 0.35 \times 2.5 \times 0.10} = \dfrac{200{,}000}{2.1} = 95{,}238$$

**Step B — Take square root:**

$$\omega_{terminal} = \sqrt{95{,}238} \approx 308.6 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step C — Convert to RPM:**

$$N = \dfrac{308.6 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 2{,}947 \text{ rpm}$$

**Note:** In practice, the brake is designed so the **terminal speed** corresponds to the **rope payout speed of ~0.4–1.0 m/s**, giving a boat descent of **0.4–1.0 m/s** (which equals the rope speed divided by $n$). The brake design is tuned via the weight mass $m_c$ and pivot radius $r_c$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lowering Speed of the Lifeboat

### Setup
Find the boat's downward velocity in terms of the rope payout velocity and the number of falls.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry of reeving**

If the boat moves down by distance $h$, each rope segment must lengthen by $h$ (since blocks move with the boat). The total rope length consumed (paid out from the drum) is:

$$L_{rope} = 2n \times h$$

**Step 2 — Velocity relation**

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$v_{rope} = 2n \times v_{boat}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for boat velocity:**

$$v_{boat} = \dfrac{v_{rope}}{2n}$$

### Numerical Example

If the rope pays out at **6 m/s** and there are 6 falls per side ($n = 6$):

$$v_{boat} = \dfrac{6}{2 \times 6} = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$$

This is **within the LSA Code limit** of $\leq 1.0$ m/s. ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Wire Rope Diameter Selection

### Setup
Select a 6×36 IWRC galvanized wire rope (Construction Factor $K = 0.36$, UTS = 1,800 MPa) for a fall carrying $F_{fall} = 16.67$ kN with a Design Safety Factor $SF = 5$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Required minimum breaking load:**

$$MBL_{required} = F_{fall} \times SF = 16.67 \times 5 = 83.35 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Solve diameter formula:**

The MBL of a 6×36 wire rope is given by:

$$MBL = K \times d^2 \times \sigma_{uts}$$

where $d$ is in cm and $\sigma_{uts}$ in kg/mm² (or consistent units).

In SI units (d in mm, $\sigma$ in MPa = N/mm²):

$$MBL_{kN} = \dfrac{K \times d^2 \times \sigma_{uts}}{1000}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d$:**

$$d = \sqrt{\dfrac{MBL_{required} \times 1000}{K \times \sigma_{uts}}}$$

$$d = \sqrt{\dfrac{83{,}350}{0.36 \times 1800}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{83{,}350}{648}} = \sqrt{128.6} \approx 11.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Standard size selection:**

Round up to next standard size: **$d = 12$ mm** (next standard 6×36 IWRC size).

**Step 5 — Verification of MBL:**

$$MBL_{actual} = \dfrac{0.36 \times 12^2 \times 1800}{1000} = \dfrac{93{,}312}{1000} = 93.31 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Actual safety factor achieved:**

$$SF_{actual} = \dfrac{93.31}{16.67} = 5.6$$

This is **above the minimum of 5.0** ✓ and falls within the typical LSA Code requirement of **SF ≥ 4.5 to 5.5**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Brake Band Sizing

### Setup
Determine the band-clamping force $F_{band}$ to hold the boat with **1.5× safety margin**.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Required braking torque (with margin):**

$$T_{brake,req} = 1.5 \times T_{load} = 1.5 \times F_{fall} \times r_{drum}$$

**Step 2 — Friction torque from a single band:**

A band wrapped around the drum with clamping force $F_{band}$ on each side provides friction torque:

$$T_{band} = \mu \times F_{band} \times r_{drum} \times 2$$

(the factor 2 accounts for both sides of the band — both create friction in the same direction)

**Step 3 — Equate and solve for $F_{band}$:**

$$2 \mu F_{band} r_{drum} = 1.5 \times F_{fall} \times r_{drum}$$

$$F_{band} = \dfrac{1.5 \times F_{fall}}{2 \mu} = \dfrac{0.75 \times F_{fall}}{\mu}$$

### Numerical Example

$F_{fall} = 16.67$ kN, $\mu = 0.35$:

$$F_{band} = \dfrac{0.75 \times 16.67}{0.35} = 35.7 \text{ kN}$$

This is the force the brake lever must apply — easily achieved by a hand-operated lever with mechanical advantage.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix (Quick Revision)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value (20 t boat, 6 falls/side) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Load per fall | $W/(2n)$ | 16.67 kN |
| 2 | Mechanical advantage | $2n$ | 12 |
| 3 | Drum torque | $F_{fall} \cdot r_{drum}$ | 2,500 N·m (r = 0.15 m) |
| 4 | Lowering speed | $v_{rope}/(2n)$ | 0.5 m/s |
| 5 | Wire rope diameter | $\sqrt{MBL \cdot 1000 / (K \sigma)}$ | 12 mm (6×36 IWRC) |
| 6 | Brake band force | $0.75 F_{fall} / \mu$ | 35.7 kN |
| 7 | Centrifugal brake $\omega$ | $\sqrt{W/(4n\mu m_c r_c)}$ | ~308 rad/s |
| 8 | Heel side force | $W \sin\theta$ | 68.4 kN at 20° list |
