# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Q: Define a "Davit" as applied to lifeboat launching on ships. List any four SOLAS requirements for a davit-launched lifeboat.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for points):**

A **davit** is a deck-mounted mechanical device consisting of an arm, sheave block, winch, brake, and falls, used to lower a lifeboat from its stowed position to the water under controlled conditions.

**Four SOLAS requirements:**
1. **Launching time ≤ 10 minutes** from "all hands at stations" to boat afloat (LSA Code Ch. VI).
2. Must be capable of launching the fully-loaded craft with the ship at a **list of up to 20°** to either side and a **trim of up to 10°**.
3. Must be capable of operation under **wind speeds up to 30 knots**.
4. Must have a **secondary braking system** in addition to the primary brake.
5. Must be operable by **hand (manual) power** if primary power fails.

*(Any 4 points, 0.5 mark each = 2 marks)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Q: What is the function of a "centrifugal brake" in a lifeboat winch? Why is it preferred over a friction-only brake?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **centrifugal brake** uses weighted shoes that swing outward under centrifugal force when the drum rotates, applying a friction force on a stationary band. This creates a **self-regulating braking torque proportional to $\omega^2$** (drum angular velocity squared).

**Why preferred over friction-only brake:**
1. **Self-regulating:** Faster drum rotation → greater braking force → automatically limits speed. Cannot run away.
2. **Fail-safe:** Spring-loaded closed; cannot be left in the "off" position accidentally.
3. **No external power required:** Works purely by gravity, centrifugal force, and spring action.
4. **Smooth deceleration:** Provides gradual, not abrupt, braking.

*(Definition = 1 mark; 3 advantages = 2 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Q: (a)** With the help of a labeled sketch, describe the construction and working of a **gravity (radial) davit** for a 150-person lifeboat on a bulk carrier. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A lifeboat of total loaded mass 20,000 kg is suspended by 6 falls per side. The drum radius is 0.15 m, and the rope is 6×36 IWRC with construction factor K = 0.36 and UTS 1,800 MPa. Determine: **(i)** load per fall, **(ii)** drum torque, **(iii)** mechanical advantage, and **(iv)** minimum wire rope diameter for a safety factor of 5. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Construction & Working (7 Marks)

**1. Constructional Details (3.5 Marks):**
- **Mast / King Post:** Vertical structural column, welded to deck, supports the entire davit.
- **Gooseneck Fitting:** At top of mast — allows the davit arm to pivot.
- **Davit Arm (Cradle Arm):** Curved or straight arm carrying the boat cradle; pivots outboard.
- **Suspension Links & Sheave Blocks:** Carry the falls from drum to boat.
- **Winch Drum:** On mast, with electric motor drive AND hand crank backup.
- **Centrifugal Brake:** Spring-loaded, with weights and friction band.
- **Hand Brake Band:** Operator-controlled, applies additional friction.
- **Limit Switches:** Top limit, slack rope limit.
- **Falls (Wire Ropes):** 6 strands per side (12 total), 6×36 IWRC galvanized.
- **Gripe Wires:** Forward + aft, hold the boat in cradle.

**2. Working (Launching Sequence) — (3.5 Marks):**
- **Step 1:** Release forward and aft gripes.
- **Step 2:** Hoist slightly — the boat's centre of gravity moves outboard of the gooseneck pin → the arm **swings outboard automatically** under the off-centre weight.
- **Step 3:** The outboard swing is locked by a **spring-loaded latch**.
- **Step 4:** Crew embarks. The lowering lever is moved to the LOWER position.
- **Step 5:** The centrifugal brake band **lifts off** progressively — the boat descends under gravity.
- **Step 6:** The **winch operator controls descent speed** via the hand brake band.
- **Step 7:** When the boat reaches the waterline, the **on-load release hook** is operated.
- **Step 8:** The falls detach; the boat is now waterborne.
- **Step 9:** The falls are hauled back up; the davit is returned to the stowed position.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $W_{total} = 20{,}000 \times 9.81 = 196{,}200$ N
- $n = 6$ falls per side
- $r_{drum} = 0.15$ m
- Wire rope: 6×36 IWRC, $K = 0.36$, $\sigma_{uts} = 1800$ MPa
- Safety Factor $SF = 5$

**Step (i) — Load per Fall (2 Marks):**

$$F_{fall} = \dfrac{W_{total}}{2n} = \dfrac{196{,}200}{2 \times 6} = 16{,}350 \text{ N} \approx 16.35 \text{ kN}$$

**Step (ii) — Drum Torque (2 Marks):**

$$T_{drum} = F_{fall} \times r_{drum} = 16{,}350 \times 0.15 = 2{,}452.5 \text{ N·m} \approx 2.45 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step (iii) — Mechanical Advantage (1 Mark):**

$$MA = 2n = 2 \times 6 = 12$$

**Step (iv) — Minimum Wire Rope Diameter (2 Marks):**

$$MBL_{required} = F_{fall} \times SF = 16.35 \times 5 = 81.75 \text{ kN} = 81{,}750 \text{ N}$$

Using $MBL = K \cdot d^2 \cdot \sigma_{uts}$ (with d in mm, $\sigma$ in MPa = N/mm², MBL in N):

$$81{,}750 = 0.36 \times d^2 \times 1800$$

$$d^2 = \dfrac{81{,}750}{0.36 \times 1800} = \dfrac{81{,}750}{648} = 126.16$$

$$d = \sqrt{126.16} \approx 11.23 \text{ mm}$$

**Round up to standard size → $d = 12$ mm.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: (a)** Explain the **on-load release hook** mechanism used in lifeboat davits. Why is a normal hook not used? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal brake has two brake weights of 2 kg each at a pivot radius of 10 cm. The drum radius is 15 cm, and the friction coefficient of the lining is 0.35. If the boat weight transmitted per fall is 15 kN and there are 6 falls per side, calculate: **(i)** the required drum speed to hold the boat, and **(ii)** the rope payout speed. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — On-Load Release Hook (7 Marks)

**1. Definition (1 Mark):**
An **on-load release hook** is a special hook mechanism that allows the falls to be released from the boat **while the hook is under full load** (i.e., when the boat is suspended).

**2. Construction (3 Marks):**
- The hook body is split into two halves, normally closed by a **cam or toggle action**.
- A **release cable** runs from the hook up to the boat (or down from the boat), so that the crew inside the boat can operate the release.
- The hook has a **secondary safety latch** that must be deliberately disarmed before release.
- An **indicator flag** (usually a small red/green indicator) shows whether the hook is in "loaded" or "released" mode.
- Some hooks have **hydrostatic release units (HRUs)** that automatically release at a preset water pressure (i.e., when the boat sinks below a depth).

**3. Why a normal hook is not used (3 Marks):**
- A **normal hook** is curved and latched. When a load is applied, the latch is jammed against the load-bearing surface of the hook — it **cannot be opened under load** because the friction holds it shut.
- Attempting to open a normal hook under load would **bend the hook or break the latch**, causing the boat to fall uncontrollably.
- An on-load release hook uses a **toggle/cam mechanism that moves the load-bearing surface out of the way** when the release cable is pulled, even with full load on it.
- SOLAS/LSA Code mandates the use of on-load release gear on all davit-launched lifeboats.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $m_c = 2$ kg (each weight), 2 weights
- $r_c = 0.10$ m (pivot radius)
- $r_{drum} = 0.15$ m
- $\mu = 0.35$
- $F_{fall} = 15$ kN
- $n = 6$ falls per side

**Step (i) — Drum Speed to Hold the Boat (4 Marks):**

**Braking force required per side:**

Total load on drum (per side) = $F_{fall} = 15$ kN = 15,000 N

**Setting braking torque = load torque:**

$$2 \times \mu \times m_c \times \omega^2 \times r_c \times r_{drum} = F_{fall} \times r_{drum}$$

**Simplify (cancel $r_{drum}$):**

$$2 \mu m_c \omega^2 r_c = F_{fall}$$

**Solve for $\omega^2$:**

$$\omega^2 = \dfrac{F_{fall}}{2 \mu m_c r_c} = \dfrac{15{,}000}{2 \times 0.35 \times 2 \times 0.10}$$

$$\omega^2 = \dfrac{15{,}000}{0.14} = 107{,}142.86 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\omega = \sqrt{107{,}142.86} \approx 327.3 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Convert to RPM:**

$$N = \dfrac{\omega \times 60}{2\pi} = \dfrac{327.3 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 3{,}126 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step (ii) — Rope Payout Speed (3 Marks):**

Rope payout speed is the linear speed of the rope on the drum:

$$v_{rope} = \omega \times r_{drum} = 327.3 \times 0.15 = 49.1 \text{ m/s}$$

**Boat descent speed:**

$$v_{boat} = \dfrac{v_{rope}}{2n} = \dfrac{49.1}{2 \times 6} = 4.09 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Critical Issue in This Problem!**
>
> **The calculated boat speed of 4.09 m/s EXCEEDS the LSA Code limit of 1.0 m/s!**
>
> This is because the brake design parameters in the question are too weak for a 15 kN per-fall load. In a real design, either:
> - The brake weight $m_c$ would be **increased** (e.g., to 5–6 kg), or
> - The pivot radius $r_c$ would be **reduced**, or
> - The number of falls $n$ would be **increased** to reduce $F_{fall}$.
>
> **Marks lost if you don't flag this:** 1–2 marks. The examiner expects you to **comment on whether the result is within SOLAS limits** and suggest a correction.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Top 7 Ways KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mass to weight** — using kg instead of N in torque equations. Always multiply by $g = 9.81$ m/s² first.
> 2. **Confusing "falls per side" with "total falls"** — the formula is $W / (2n)$, NOT $W/n$. This is the most common error.
> 3. **Forgetting the "1.5× safety margin" on brake holding torque** — LSA Code requires this; omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Using $v_{rope} = v_{boat}$** instead of $v_{rope} = 2n \cdot v_{boat}$. The boat moves slower than the rope because the rope is reeved.
> 5. **Drawing a free-body diagram (FBD) of the boat only** instead of the boat + falls + drum system. The examiner wants to see the **rope tension** clearly marked.
> 6. **Omitting the SOLAS/LSA Code clause** numbers in the answer. Every davit specification should reference **LSA Code Chapter VI** or **SOLAS Chapter III**.
> 7. **Not mentioning the manual/hand backup** for the winch. A davit is expected to operate without ship's power — always state this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section for last-minute exam revision — read 5 times before walking into the hall.**

### Core Concepts (Must Know)
- ☐ **Davit** = mechanical arm + winch + brake + falls for lifeboat launching.
- ☐ **6 SOLAS Limits** = 10-min launch / 20° list / 10° trim / 30-knot wind / ≤1.0 m/s descent / ≥0.3 m/s descent.
- ☐ **LSA Code Chapter VI** governs all davit-launched survival craft.
- ☐ **Gravity davit** = primary type on tankers/bulkers; uses centrifugal brake.
- ☐ **Slewing-luffing davit** = primary on cruise ships; hydraulic.

### Key Components
- ☐ **Centrifugal brake** — self-regulating; $T_{brake} \propto \omega^2$.
- ☐ **Hand brake band** — operator control; holds boat at any position.
- ☐ **On-load release hook** — allows release under full load; mandatory per LSA Code.
- ☐ **Gooseneck pin** — pivot point for davit arm swing-out.
- ☐ **Limit switches** — top limit + slack rope limit.

### Must-Memorize Formulas
- ☐ $F_{fall} = W_{total} / (2n)$
- ☐ $MA = 2n$
- ☐ $T_{drum} = F_{fall} \cdot r_{drum}$
- ☐ $v_{boat} = v_{rope} / (2n)$
- ☐ $T_{brake} = 2 \mu m_c \omega^2 r_c r_{drum}$
- ☐ $F_{band} = 0.75 F_{fall} / \mu$ (with 1.5× safety margin)
- ☐ $d = \sqrt{MBL / (K \cdot \sigma_{uts})}$
- ☐ $SF = UTS_{rope} / F_{fall} \geq 4.5$ (typically 5)

### Typical Numerical Values (Memorize for Quick Substitution)
- ☐ 150-person lifeboat ≈ 20,000 kg loaded
- ☐ Person mass = 75 kg (100 kg with arctic gear)
- ☐ Standard falls per side: 6 (12 total)
- ☐ Drum radius: 0.10–0.20 m
- ☐ Rope: 12–16 mm 6×36 IWRC galvanized
- ☐ Brake weight mass: 2–5 kg
- ☐ Centrifugal brake lining $\mu$ ≈ 0.35
- ☐ Winch power: 5–15 kW (electric) + hand crank backup

### SOLAS / LSA Code Quick-Reference
- ☐ **SOLAS Chapter III** = Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements.
- ☐ **LSA Code** = International Life-Saving Appliance Code (IMO Res. MSC.48(66)).
- ☐ **Chapter VI of LSA Code** = Launching and stowage appliances (includes davits).
- ☐ **§4.4.1** = SWL definition.
- ☐ **§6.1.2.3** = 20° list launching requirement.
- ☐ **§6.1.2.4** = 1.0 m/s lowering speed limit.
- ☐ **§1.2.2** = 30-knot wind operational limit.

### One-Sentence Exam Definition (For 2-Mark Questions)
> "A **davit** is a deck-mounted mechanical device, comprising a pivoting arm, sheave block, winch drum, centrifugal brake, and wire-rope falls, used to launch a lifeboat safely under SOLAS/LSA Code requirements of 10-minute launch, 20° list, and 1.0 m/s max descent speed."

### Final Exam Mantra
> *"2n for the falls, $g \cdot m$ for the weight, $\omega^2$ for the brake, and LSA Code for the limits."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cargo Handling Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cargo Handling Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cargo Handling Equipment (CHE)** on a ship refers to the complete integrated system of mechanical, hydraulic, electrical, and structural apparatus permanently installed on board a vessel (or temporarily rigged using ship's gear) for the purpose of **loading, discharging, transferring, stowing, and securing** of cargo between the ship and the shore (or between holds and the deck). These systems form a critical subset of the ship's **outfit** and are governed by classification society rules (e.g., Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS) and the ILO/IMO **Code of Safe Practice for Cargo Stowage and Securing (CSS Code)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, CHE is broadly classified into two categories:

1. **Ship's Own Gear (Permanent Installed Equipment):** Cargo cranes, derricks, gantries, winches, and capstans permanently fitted to the vessel.
2. **Ship's Loose Gear (Portable/Temporarily Rigged):** Shackles, blocks, turnbuckles, slings, cargo runners, and chain falls that are rigged to the permanent gear to perform a lift.

The **Safe Working Load (SWL)** is the maximum load that a piece of equipment is certified to lift under specified environmental conditions. For KTU examinations, SWL is always stated in **metric tonnes (t)** or **kilonewtons (kN)**, with a standard design factor of safety ranging from **1.5 to 2.0** against the Minimum Breaking Load (MBL) of the wire rope.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Floating Toolbox":**
> Imagine a giant floating multi-tool kit. Just as a Swiss Army knife has different attachments for different jobs (blade, screwdriver, scissors), a cargo ship carries a versatile set of mechanical arms and pulleys. When the ship reaches port, instead of relying entirely on shore cranes, it can use its **own arms (cranes/derricks)** to grab containers or bags of grain and lift them over the side. The "muscles" are **winches and hydraulic rams**, the "bones" are **booms and masts**, and the "nervous system" is the **electrical/hydraulic control circuitry** — all coordinated to move heavy loads safely between ship and shore.

This mental picture is helpful: every piece of CHE on a ship is essentially a **force-multiplier** that converts a small input (motor torque, hydraulic pressure) into a large output (lifting tonnes of cargo), while the ship's hull provides the reaction base.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Standards)

- **Safe Working Load (SWL):** Defined per equipment; typically **5 t to 50 t** for single-lift shipboard cranes, with heavy-lift vessels up to **1,000 t** (SWL).
- **Design Factor of Safety:** **1.5 minimum** (for wire ropes, often 2.0).
- **Hoisting Speed:** **15 to 30 m/min** for cargo cranes; **60 m/min** for high-speed winches.
- **Slewing Speed:** **0.5 to 1.5 rpm** for typical deck cranes.
- **Luffing Time:** **30 to 60 seconds** for full boom raise/lower.
- **Standard Outreach Radius:** **10 m to 30 m** depending on boom length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically expects students to know the distinction between **derricks** (traditional, rope-rigged, king-post supported) and **deck cranes** (modern, electro-hydraulic, slewing). Always cite the SWL on the equipment nameplate and the corresponding **Proof Load** (1.25 × SWL for cranes, 1.5 × SWL for loose gear per ISO 12482/12480).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cargo Handling Equipment (CHE)

### 2.1.1 Based on Permanence and Rigging Type

| Category | Examples | Typical Application |
|----------|----------|---------------------|
| **Permanent (Fixed) Gear** | Deck cranes, gantry cranes, hatch covers, masts | Modern bulk, container, multi-purpose vessels |
| **Semi-Permanent (Rigid) Gear** | King posts, samson posts, Derrick posts | Traditional general cargo ships |
| **Portable (Loose) Gear** | Snatch blocks, cargo runners, slings, shackles | Used to multiply rigging configurations |
| **Power Units** | Electric winches, hydraulic power packs, pneumatic hoists | Provide input energy for lifting |

### 2.1.2 Based on Motion Axes

A modern deck crane provides **up to 4 degrees of freedom (DOF)** for the lifted load:

1. **Hoisting (Up/Down):** Vertical translation via winch and wire.
2. **Luffing (Up/Down of Boom):** Changes the boom angle, altering radius.
3. **Slewing (Rotation):** Horizontal rotation about the crane column.
4. **Traveling (On rails):** Forward/backward motion along deck rails (gantry only).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "4 DOF" matters in KTU problems:** When you calculate the **working radius** of a load, you must consider the boom angle and the slewing position. A load placed at the maximum radius has the highest moment on the crane pedestal and the highest heel-induced side load on the vessel.

## 2.2 Operational Principles — The "Why" and "How"

### 2.2.1 Derrick Systems (Traditional)

A derrick is a simple **inclined boom** supported at the bottom by a **gooseneck fitting** and held aloft by a **topping lift** from a king-post or samson post. The cargo is hoisted by a **cargo fall** running over a **top block** at the boom head and a **bottom block** at the boom base, both connected via a **cargo winch**.

- **Why use it?** Derricks are simple, robust, and require minimal maintenance. Two derricks can be **union-rigged** (married together) to lift heavier loads than either could alone.
- **How it works:** The topping lift controls boom angle; the cargo winch controls the hook height. Manual or power slewing is done by warping a **swinging derrick** using guy ropes or hydraulic slewing gear.

### 2.2.2 Electro-Hydraulic Deck Cranes (Modern)

A deck crane is essentially a **slewing column** with a knuckle-boom (or telescopic boom) that uses **hydraulic cylinders** for luffing and hoisting, and a **slewing ring (slew bearing)** for rotation. The hydraulic power is supplied by an **electric motor-driven hydraulic pump**.

- **Why use it?** Higher SWL, faster operation, precise load control, and operator cabin visibility. They can be **manually operated** or **automated with load moment indicators (LMI)**.
- **How it works:** Hydraulic pressure applied to the luffing cylinder pushes/pulls the boom up/down; the hoisting cylinder (or winch) raises/lowers the hook. The slew motor rotates the upper structure via a planetary gearbox.

### 2.2.3 Gantry Cranes (Container/Bulk)

A gantry crane spans the deck on rails, supported at both ends by **gantry legs** (or one end on a rail and the other on a slewing column). It typically has a **trolley** that travels along the boom to vary the load radius.

- **Why use it?** Ideal for **self-discharging bulk carriers** and **geared container ships** that need to service multiple hatches without using shore cranes.
- **How it works:** The trolley moves along the boom (changing radius), the hoist lifts, and the entire gantry travels along the deck rails to align with different holds.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield, Board-Exam Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the **must-know formulas** for KTU ESE. Master the units, boundary conditions, and typical values.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Typical KTU Context |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Safe Working Load from MBL** | $SWL = \dfrac{MBL}{n}$ | $MBL$ in kN; $n$ = Factor of Safety (1.5 to 2.0) | Derive SWL of wire rope |
| 2 | **Single-Part Line Tension** | $F_1 = W \cdot \dfrac{1}{\eta_{block}}$ | $W$ = load weight (kN); $\eta$ = block efficiency ($\approx 0.95$ per sheave) | Multi-sheave block problems |
| 3 | **Multi-Part Reeving Tension** | $F_1 = \dfrac{W}{m \cdot \eta^m}$ | $m$ = number of parts supporting load | 2-part, 4-part, 6-part reeving |
| 4 | **Crane Overturning Moment** | $M_{OT} = W \cdot R$ | $W$ = load weight; $R$ = working radius (m) | Pedestal stress check |
| 5 | **Righting Moment (Ship)** | $RM = W_{ship} \cdot GZ$ | $GZ$ = righting lever (m) | Heel-angle stability |
| 6 | **Heel-Induced Side Load** | $F_{heel} = W \cdot \tan(\phi)$ | $\phi$ = static heel angle (degrees) | Cargo operation at angle |
| 7 | **Heel Angle from Off-Center Load** | $\tan(\phi) = \dfrac{W \cdot y}{W_{disp} \cdot GM}$ | $y$ = transverse offset (m); $GM$ = metacentric height (m) | Stability during lift |
| 8 | **Derrick Union Rig SWL** | $SWL_{union} = 0.8 \cdot (SWL_1 + SWL_2)$ | Empirical rule for two-derrick lifts | Rigging selection |
| 9 | **Hoist Power Required** | $P = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{\eta_{total}}$ | $v$ = hoist speed (m/s); $P$ in kW | Winch motor sizing |
| 10 | **Proof Load Test (Crane)** | $F_{proof} = 1.25 \cdot SWL$ | Static overload test value | Classification society test |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipes & Tables Rule:** In all answer sheets, avoid using the vertical pipe `\vert` symbol inside table cells — always replace with $\vert$ or $\mid$ to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cargo handling equipment bridges the gap between **mechanical engineering** (gear design, hydraulics), **structural engineering** (boom stress, pedestal welds), **electrical engineering** (motor drives, LMI systems), and **naval architecture** (stability, hull deflection). A marine engineer's task is to ensure that during a lift, the **maximum heeling moment** of the cargo does not exceed the **righting moment** of the ship, otherwise a capsize risk arises. This is why KTU problems often combine SWL with a small heel-angle stability sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: SWL of a Wire Rope from MBL

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A wire rope has a Minimum Breaking Load (MBL) of **480 kN** and is used on a cargo winch with a design factor of safety **$n = 2.0$**. Find the Safe Working Load (SWL). If the rope is reeved over a **4-sheave block** with efficiency **$\eta = 0.95$** per sheave, and the load is hoisted at **$v = 10 \text{ m/min}$**, find the **hoist motor power** assuming a gearbox and winch efficiency of **0.85**.

### Step 1 — State the Governing Equation

The SWL of any lifting gear is the maximum allowable working load and is defined as the MBL divided by the Factor of Safety:

$$
SWL = \dfrac{MBL}{n}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Known Values

$$
SWL = \dfrac{480 \text{ kN}}{2.0} = 240 \text{ kN}
$$

Converting to tonnes (1 t ≈ 9.81 kN):

$$
SWL = \dfrac{240}{9.81} \approx 24.46 \text{ t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** In marine practice, the **tonne-force (tf)** is sometimes used. $1 \text{ tf} = 1,000 \text{ kgf} = 9.81 \text{ kN}$. Always show both kN and tonnes in the final answer for full KTU marks.

### Step 3 — Account for Block Efficiency (Reeving)

For a 4-sheave block supporting the load on **$m = 2$** parts (simplest case: one sheave top, one sheave bottom, both single), the effective SWL after friction loss in the block is:

$$
F_{line} = \dfrac{SWL}{m \cdot \eta^m}
$$

For a 4-sheave block, $m = 4$ parts and total sheaves = 4. So:

$$
F_{line} = \dfrac{240 \text{ kN}}{4 \cdot (0.95)^4}
$$

Compute $(0.95)^4$:

$$
(0.95)^2 = 0.9025
$$

$$
(0.95)^4 = 0.9025^2 = 0.81450625
$$

Therefore:

$$
F_{line} = \dfrac{240}{4 \cdot 0.8145} = \dfrac{240}{3.258} \approx 73.66 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The winch must be able to apply **73.66 kN** of line pull at the rope drum. If the line pull capacity is less, either the block efficiency is overstated, or the SWL must be reduced.

### Step 4 — Compute Hoist Motor Power

The required mechanical power at the hoist drum is:

$$
P_{drum} = F_{line} \cdot v
$$

Convert velocity: $v = 10 \text{ m/min} = \dfrac{10}{60} \text{ m/s} = 0.1667 \text{ m/s}$.

$$
P_{drum} = 73.66 \times 0.1667 \approx 12.28 \text{ kW}
$$

Apply gearbox and winch efficiency (0.85) to find the motor input power:

$$
P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{drum}}{\eta_{total}} = \dfrac{12.28}{0.85} \approx 14.45 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Answer Summary

- **SWL = 240 kN (≈ 24.46 t)**
- **Line Pull at Drum = 73.66 kN**
- **Hoist Motor Power = 14.45 kW** (selected next standard size: **15 kW**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Practice:** Always round UP to the next commercially available motor size (e.g., 15 kW or 18.5 kW) and verify the motor's starting torque can exceed the static load torque by at least 1.6×.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heel Angle from an Off-Center Cargo Lift

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A bulk carrier of **displacement $W_{disp} = 20,000$ t** and **metacentric height $GM = 0.8$ m** is discharging cargo using its deck crane. A hatch cover of weight **$W = 8$ t** is lifted with the crane positioned at a **transverse offset $y = 12$ m** from the centerline. Calculate the **static heel angle** $\phi$ produced.

### Step 1 — State the Heel-Angle Equation

For small angles, the heel angle produced by an off-center weight transfer is governed by:

$$
\tan(\phi) = \dfrac{W \cdot y}{W_{disp} \cdot GM}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Known Values

Convert all weights to tonnes (consistent units):

$$
\tan(\phi) = \dfrac{8 \cdot 12}{20,000 \cdot 0.8} = \dfrac{96}{16,000} = 0.006
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Heel Angle

For small angles, $\tan(\phi) \approx \phi$ (in radians):

$$
\phi \approx 0.006 \text{ rad}
$$

Converting to degrees:

$$
\phi = 0.006 \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi} \approx 0.344^\circ
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A heel of less than **0.5°** is well within the safe operating envelope (most cargo operations allow up to **1° to 2°** of heel during lifts). However, repeated or larger lifts at the same offset can cause progressive listing, especially if the ship has partially discharged ballast or fuel.

### Step 4 — Check Heel-Induced Side Load

Using the previously derived side-load formula:

$$
F_{heel} = W \cdot \tan(\phi) = 8 \text{ t} \cdot 0.006 = 0.048 \text{ t} = 0.471 \text{ kN}
$$

This is a **negligible side load** compared to the cargo weight (8 t), confirming the operation is safe from a stability standpoint.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU 14-Mark Question Blueprint

The following matrix shows the **exact cognitive levels** KTU examiners test on this topic, aligned with the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** under the **2024 NEP-2020 OBE framework**.

| Sub-Part | Bloom Level | Marks | Action Verb | Sample Cue |
|----------|-------------|-------|-------------|------------|
| (a)(i) | **Remember** | 2 | State / List | "State the types of cargo handling gear" |
| (a)(ii) | **Understand** | 3 | Explain / Describe | "Explain the working of a derrick" |
| (b)(i) | **Apply** | 4 | Calculate / Determine | "Calculate SWL of a 6-part reeved block" |
| (b)(ii) | **Analyze** | 5 | Compare / Justify | "Compare derricks vs deck cranes" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** KTU examiners award full marks only when you (1) draw a neat **labelled sketch**, (2) show **every algebraic step** with units, and (3) conclude with a **boxed final answer** and a **short engineering interpretation**. Never end a derivation at a numerical value — always comment on whether the result is safe/feasible.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cargo Handling Equipment Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram classifies CHE into its functional architecture. Per the safeguarding rules, all node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters, and all labels are raw uppercase text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[CARGO HANDLING EQUIPMENT] --> B1[PERMANENT GEAR]
    A1 --> B2[PORTABLE LOOSE GEAR]
    A1 --> B3[POWER UNITS]
    A1 --> B4[CONTROL SYSTEMS]

    B1 --> C1[DECK CRANES]
    B1 --> C2[DERRICKS]
    B1 --> C3[GANTRY CRANES]
    B1 --> C4[CARGO WINCHES]
    B1 --> C5[CAPSTANS]

    C1 --> D1[ELECTRO HYDRAULIC]
    C1 --> D2[ELECTRIC]
    C1 --> D3[KNUCKLE BOOM]
    C1 --> D4[TELESCOPIC BOOM]

    C2 --> E1[SINGLE DERRICK]
    C2 --> E2[UNION PURCHASE]
    C2 --> E3[SWINGING DERRICK]
    C2 --> E4[STIFFENED DERRICK]

    C3 --> F1[CONTAINER GANTRY]
    C3 --> F2[BULK CARRIER GANTRY]
    C3 --> F3[SELF UNLOADER]

    B2 --> G1[BLOCKS AND TACKLES]
    B2 --> G2[SHACKLES AND HOOKS]
    B2 --> G3[SLINGS AND CHAINS]
    B2 --> G4[TURNBUCKLES]

    B3 --> H1[HYDRAULIC POWER PACK]
    B3 --> H2[ELECTRIC MOTORS]
    B3 --> H3[DIESEL DRIVE]

    B4 --> I1[LOAD MOMENT INDICATOR]
    B4 --> I2[ANTI TWO BLOCK]
    B4 --> I3[OPERATOR CABIN]
    B4 --> I4[REMOTE CONTROL]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Derrick Hoisting Operation

The next diagram shows the **energy flow** and **mechanical sequence** of a typical cargo derrick lifting a load. It is rendered as a block-level functional flow to comply with the Mermaid safeguards.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[PRIME MOVER] --> P2[GEARBOX]
    P2 --> P3[CARGO WINCH DRUM]
    P3 --> P4[CARGO FALL WIRE ROPE]
    P4 --> P5[TOP BLOCK AT BOOM HEAD]
    P5 --> P6[BOTTOM BLOCK AT HOOK]
    P6 --> P7[CARGO LOAD]

    T1[TOPPING LIFT WINCH] --> T2[TOPPING LIFT WIRE]
    T2 --> T3[KING POST HEAD]
    T3 --> T5[BOOM GOOSENECK]
    P7 --> H1[HEEL MOMENT ON SHIP]
    P7 --> SIDE1[SIDE LOAD ON PEDESTAL]

    LMI[LOAD MOMENT INDICATOR] -.MONITORS.-> P7
    LMI -.FEEDBACK.-> P1
```

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture — Derrick vs Deck Crane

| Architecture Feature | Derrick (Traditional) | Deck Crane (Modern) |
|---------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Prime Mover** | Steam or electric winch | Electro-hydraulic power pack |
| **Boom Type** | Fixed-length tubular or lattice | Knuckle or telescopic |
| **Slewing** | Manual guy ropes or hydraulic | Hydraulic slew motor + bearing |
| **Luffing** | Topping lift wire + winch | Hydraulic cylinder |
| **Hoisting** | Wire rope + sheave block | Wire rope + hydraulic winch |
| **Operator Position** | Deck-level, manual signaling | Enclosed cabin with joystick |
| **Safety Aids** | Visual estimation | LMI, ATB, anemometer |
| **SWL Range** | 3 to 30 t (single), up to 60 t (union) | 5 to 50 t (single), up to 1,000 t (heavy-lift) |
| **Maintenance** | Low, simple mechanics | High, hydraulic specialist needed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schematic Note:** For a 14-mark KTU question that asks you to "describe a cargo crane with a neat sketch," the **functional flow above** is the level of detail expected. Always label the **gooseneck fitting, topping lift, cargo fall, king post, pedestal bearing, and slew gear**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each question carries **3 marks**, 60 seconds per mark. KTU expects a **definition + one supporting line + a sketch or example** for full marks.

### Question 1 (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Pattern]**
> *Define Safe Working Load (SWL). How is it related to the Minimum Breaking Load (MBL) of a wire rope? State the standard Factor of Safety used for wire ropes in cargo handling gear.*

#### Model Answer:

**Safe Working Load (SWL):** SWL is the maximum load (in tonnes or kN) that a piece of cargo handling equipment is certified to lift under normal service conditions, as marked on its nameplate by the manufacturer and approved by the classification society.

**Relation to MBL:** The SWL is derived from the MBL of the weakest link in the lifting system (typically the wire rope) divided by a Factor of Safety:

$$
SWL = \dfrac{MBL}{n}
$$

**Standard Factor of Safety:** For wire ropes used in ship's cargo gear, the **Factor of Safety $n$** is typically **1.5 to 2.0** as per ISO 4308 and classification society rules (e.g., Lloyd's Register, DNV 2.7-1 for offshore containers). A higher factor (2.0) is used for personnel lifts, while 1.5 is acceptable for cargo-only lifts.

> **Marks Distribution (3/3):** Definition 1M + Equation 1M + Factor of Safety value with standard 1M.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**
> *Explain the difference between a derrick and a deck crane. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.*

#### Model Answer:

**Derrick:** A traditional inclined boom supported at the gooseneck fitting and held aloft by a topping lift from a king post. Hoisting is done by a cargo fall running over a top block at the boom head. Slewing is achieved by swinging the boom using guy ropes or warping winches.

**Deck Crane:** A modern, fully slewing, luffing machine mounted on a pedestal. Boom movements (luffing, hoisting, slewing) are powered by electro-hydraulic actuators. Often fitted with an operator cabin and Load Moment Indicator (LMI).

**Comparison:**

| Feature | Derrick | Deck Crane |
|---------|---------|------------|
| **Advantage** | Simple, cheap, low maintenance | High SWL, fast, precise, safer |
| **Disadvantage** | Slow, manual slinging, low SWL | High initial cost, complex hydraulics |

> **Marks Distribution (3/3):** Each definition 1M + one advantage/disadvantage comparison 1M.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Choice Question

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern requires **Module 3 internal choice**: two alternative 14-mark questions, with the student answering **ONE**. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each** (often (a) = descriptive, (b) = numerical/comparative). Provide full step-by-step model solutions for **both** options.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 3 Choice Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** *With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of an **electro-hydraulic deck crane** used on a general cargo ship. List at least **four safety features** fitted to it.  **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** *A cargo winch has a wire rope with MBL of **320 kN**. The wire is reeved through a **4-sheave block** with an efficiency of **0.94 per sheave**, supporting the load on **6 parts**. Calculate:*
> - *(i) The Safe Working Load of the system. (3 Marks)*
> - *(ii) The line pull required at the winch drum. (2 Marks)*
> - *(iii) The hoist motor power required if the load is lifted at **$v = 12$ m/min** and total mechanical efficiency is **0.82**. (2 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — Descriptive (7 Marks):

**Construction:**

The electro-hydraulic deck crane consists of the following major components:

1. **Pedestal / Slew Bearing:** The vertical column mounted on a reinforced foundation on the ship's deck. It contains a slew bearing (large-diameter ball or roller race) that allows 360° rotation of the upper structure.

2. **Upper Structure / Slewing Platform:** Houses the operator cabin, hydraulic power pack, slewing motor, and the slewing pinion that engages the slew bearing.

3. **Boom (Jib):** A welded steel lattice or box-section arm that can be of the **knuckle-boom** (with a hinged elbow joint) or **telescopic** (multi-stage sliding sections) type. The boom carries the top block at its tip.

4. **Hydraulic Cylinders:** Two double-acting cylinders — one for **luffing** (raising/lowering the boom) and one for **hoisting** (lifting the hook). Smaller cylinders actuate the knuckle joint if fitted.

5. **Hydraulic Power Pack:** An electric motor drives a variable-displacement hydraulic pump that supplies high-pressure oil (typically 200–300 bar) to the cylinders and motors.

6. **Hook Block:** Bottom block carrying the cargo hook, attached to the load via slings.

**Working:**

When the operator commands a lift from the cabin, the **hoist cylinder** retracts, pulling the wire rope and raising the hook. Once the load is clear, the **slew motor** rotates the upper structure to position the load over the hatch or shore. The **luffing cylinder** extends or retracts to adjust the boom angle (and thus the working radius). The Load Moment Indicator (LMI) continuously monitors $W \times R$ and cuts off motion if the safe envelope is exceeded.

**Safety Features (Any Four):**

1. **Load Moment Indicator (LMI):** Prevents overloading by monitoring radius and weight.
2. **Anti-Two-Block (ATB) Device:** Stops hoisting before the hook block contacts the boom head.
3. **Anemometer:** Cuts off operation if wind speed exceeds the safe limit (typically 20 m/s for cargo cranes).
4. **Emergency Stop Button:** Immediately shuts down all motion.
5. **Overload Relief Valve:** Vents hydraulic pressure if a preset threshold is exceeded.
6. **Limit Switches:** Mechanical stops for maximum hoist, luff, and slew travel.
7. **Hydraulic Accumulator:** Provides emergency power to lower the load in case of engine failure.

> **Marks Distribution (7/7):** Sketch 2M + Six components labeled 2M + Working 2M + Four safety features 1M.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):

**Given:**
- $MBL = 320$ kN
- 4-sheave block, $\eta = 0.94$ per sheave
- 6 parts supporting the load
- $v = 12$ m/min $= 0.2$ m/s
- $\eta_{total} = 0.82$
- Standard Factor of Safety for wire rope: $n = 2.0$

**Step (i) — SWL of the System:**

$$
SWL = \dfrac{MBL}{n} = \dfrac{320}{2.0} = 160 \text{ kN}
$$

Convert to tonnes:

$$
SWL = \dfrac{160}{9.81} \approx 16.31 \text{ t}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The bare wire can lift 16.31 t directly. With 6-part reeving, this is further divided among the parts (each takes 16.31/6 ≈ 2.72 t of line tension).

**Step (ii) — Line Pull at the Winch Drum:**

The total sheaves = 4 (top block) + 4 (bottom block) — but for a 4-sheave block supporting 6 parts, the actual configuration is typically **3 sheaves top + 3 sheaves bottom = 6 sheaves total**. Apply the multi-part reeving formula with 6 sheaves:

$$
F_{line} = \dfrac{SWL}{\eta_{totalSheaves}}
$$

For $\eta_{sheave} = 0.94$ over 6 sheaves:

$$
\eta_{totalSheaves} = (0.94)^6
$$

Compute stepwise:

$$
(0.94)^2 = 0.8836
$$

$$
(0.94)^4 = (0.8836)^2 = 0.78074896
$$

$$
(0.94)^6 = (0.94)^4 \cdot (0.94)^2 = 0.78074896 \cdot 0.8836 \approx 0.6899
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** In KTU problems, the **block efficiency** is applied per sheave encountered by the rope. If the problem says "4-sheave block" but "6 parts," assume the configuration has 4 sheaves total (top block has 2, bottom has 2, with reeving giving 6 parts) — this is a common exam simplification. The examiner's expected formula is $F_{line} = SWL / (\eta_{sheave})^{n_{sheaves}}$.

**Using 4 sheaves (per the problem statement):**

$$
\eta_{block} = (0.94)^4 = 0.7807
$$

$$
F_{line} = \dfrac{160}{0.7807} \approx 204.94 \text{ kN}
$$

> **Marks Distribution (3M):** Formula 1M + Substitution 1M + Final answer with units 1M.

**Step (iii) — Hoist Motor Power:**

The drum power is:

$$
P_{drum} = F_{line} \cdot v = 204.94 \text{ kN} \times 0.2 \text{ m/s} = 40.99 \text{ kW}
$$

The motor input power accounting for mechanical efficiency:

$$
P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{drum}}{\eta_{total}} = \dfrac{40.99}{0.82} \approx 49.99 \text{ kW} \approx 50 \text{ kW}
$$

> **Marks Distribution (2M):** Drum power 1M + Motor power with efficiency 1M.

**Final Boxed Answer:**

$$
\boxed{SWL = 160 \text{ kN (16.31 t)} \quad ; \quad F_{line} = 204.94 \text{ kN} \quad ; \quad P_{motor} = 50 \text{ kW}}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A standard next-size motor of **55 kW** would be selected for a 1.1× service factor margin.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module 3 Choice Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** *With a neat sketch, describe the **union purchase** method of rigging two derricks. Under what condition is union purchase preferred over a single derrick?  **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** *A ship of **displacement 12,000 t** and **metacentric height GM = 0.6 m** is using a single derrick to lift a **5 t** cargo unit at a **transverse offset y = 8 m** from the centerline. Calculate:*
> - *(i) The heeling moment produced by the lift. (3 Marks)*
> - *(ii) The static heel angle. (2 Marks)*
> - *(iii) If the derrick SWL is **10 t**, check whether the lift is safe. State the maximum allowable SWL if the heel is to be limited to **1.5°**. (2 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — Descriptive (7 Marks):

**Union Purchase (Married Derricks):**

In union purchase, **two cargo derricks** — one port and one starboard — are rigged together to lift a single heavy load. The boom heads are connected by a **spaner (or union pennant)** — a short length of wire rope with a hook or shackle at its midpoint. The two derricks lean toward each other, and the angle between them typically ranges from **60° to 90°** at the boom heads.

**Construction & Rigging:**

1. **Port Derrick (Derrick A):** Topping lift fixed to port king post; cargo fall from port winch.
2. **Starboard Derrick (Derrick B):** Topping lift fixed to starboard king post; cargo fall from starboard winch.
3. **Spaner:** A short wire (length ≈ 2 to 3 m) rigged between the two boom heads, with the main cargo hook at its center.
4. **Cargo Load:** Lifted by both cargo falls acting in tandem through the spaner.

**Working:**

- The two derricks are boomed out (using topping lifts) and angled inward so that the spaner hangs at a low point.
- Both cargo winches are operated simultaneously to take up the load. The combined force from the two booms supports the load.
- The angle of the spaner (and thus the load's position) can be adjusted by selectively paying out one winch and hauling on the other — this is how the load is slewed or positioned fore-aft.

**When Union Purchase is Preferred:**

- When the **load exceeds the SWL of a single derrick** but is within the combined union SWL.
- When the ship is **not fitted with a heavy-lift crane** but must occasionally handle machinery or expedition cargo.
- When **two holds must be served simultaneously** with one load transfer.
- Empirical rule: $SWL_{union} \approx 0.8 \times (SWL_1 + SWL_2)$.

> **Marks Distribution (7/7):** Sketch with spaner labeled 2M + Working 3M + Preferred conditions 2M.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):

**Given:**
- $W_{disp} = 12,000$ t
- $GM = 0.6$ m
- $W = 5$ t
- $y = 8$ m
- $SWL_{derrick} = 10$ t
- Allowable heel = $1.5°$

**Step (i) — Heeling Moment:**

$$
HM = W \cdot y = 5 \text{ t} \cdot 8 \text{ m} = 40 \text{ t·m}
$$

> **Marks Distribution (3M):** Formula 1M + Substitution 1M + Final answer 1M.

**Step (ii) — Static Heel Angle:**

$$
\tan(\phi) = \dfrac{W \cdot y}{W_{disp} \cdot GM} = \dfrac{5 \cdot 8}{12,000 \cdot 0.6} = \dfrac{40}{7200} = 0.005556
$$

$$
\phi = \arctan(0.005556) \approx 0.3183^\circ
$$

> **Marks Distribution (2M):** Formula 1M + Final answer 1M.

**Step (iii) — Safety Check & Maximum Allowable SWL:**

**Safety Check:** Since the actual load (5 t) is **less than the derrick SWL (10 t)**, the lift is **safe from a derrick-strength perspective**. The heel angle of **0.32°** is also well within the typical 1° to 2° operational limit. **The lift is SAFE.** ✓

**Maximum Allowable SWL for Heel ≤ 1.5°:**

Convert allowable heel to radians:

$$
\phi_{max} = 1.5 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 0.02618 \text{ rad}
$$

For small angles, $\tan(\phi_{max}) \approx \phi_{max} = 0.02618$.

Solve for $W_{max}$:

$$
W_{max} = \dfrac{W_{disp} \cdot GM \cdot \tan(\phi_{max})}{y}
$$

$$
W_{max} = \dfrac{12,000 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.02618}{8} = \dfrac{188.5}{8} \approx 23.56 \text{ t}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The maximum cargo weight that can be lifted at an 8 m offset without exceeding 1.5° of heel is **23.56 t**, which is well above the derrick's SWL of 10 t. Therefore, the **stability criterion is more permissive than the strength criterion** — derrick SWL is the limiting factor.

> **Marks Distribution (2M):** Safe check reasoning 1M + Maximum SWL calculation 1M.

**Final Boxed Answer:**

$$
\boxed{HM = 40 \text{ t·m} \quad ; \quad \phi = 0.32^\circ \text{ (SAFE)} \quad ; \quad W_{max,heel} = 23.56 \text{ t}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Cargo Handling Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Slip-ups:** Forgetting to convert $m/min$ to $m/s$ when computing power. **Always:** $v_{m/s} = v_{m/min} / 60$. Lost marks: **1–2 per slip**.
>
> 2. **Block Efficiency Mis-application:** Applying the block efficiency to the load instead of dividing by it. The correct formula is $F_{line} = W / (m \cdot \eta^m)$, NOT $W \cdot m \cdot \eta^m$. Lost marks: **2–3**.
>
> 3. **Confusing SWL with MBL:** Writing $SWL = MBL$ is the most common error. Always state $SWL = MBL / n$ with $n \geq 1.5$.
>
> 4. **Skipping the Sketch:** KTU awards **2 marks minimum** for a labelled diagram in descriptive questions. A missing or unlabelled sketch is an automatic **−2 to −3 marks** penalty.
>
> 5. **No "Engineering Interpretation":** KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP-2020 aligned) explicitly tests **higher-order thinking (Bloom's Analyze/Evaluate)**. Ending a calculation with a bare number without a concluding sentence ("The lift is safe because...") costs **0.5–1 mark**.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the Gooseneck / King Post Labels:** In a derrick sketch, the gooseneck fitting, king post, topping lift, and cargo fall are the **four mandatory labels**. Missing any one = **−0.5 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Cargo Handling Equipment)**

- [x] **Definition:** CHE = mechanical/hydraulic/electrical systems on board for loading, discharging, and securing cargo. Two main types: **Ship's Own Gear (permanent)** and **Ship's Loose Gear (portable)**.
- [x] **SWL Definition:** $SWL = MBL / n$ with $n = 1.5$ (cargo) or $2.0$ (personnel).
- [x] **Wire Rope Factor of Safety:** **1.5 to 2.0** as per ISO 4308 and classification rules.
- [x] **Derrick Components:** Gooseneck, king post, topping lift, cargo fall, top/bottom blocks, cargo winch, spaner (for union purchase).
- [x] **Deck Crane Components:** Pedestal, slew bearing, knuckle/telescopic boom, hydraulic cylinders, hydraulic power pack, LMI, operator cabin.
- [x] **Union Purchase SWL Rule:** $SWL_{union} = 0.8 \times (SWL_1 + SWL_2)$ — empirical, two-derrick lift.
- [x] **Crane Overturning Moment:** $M_{OT} = W \cdot R$ where $R$ is the working radius.
- [x] **Heel Angle Formula:** $\tan(\phi) = W \cdot y / (W_{disp} \cdot GM)$ for small angles.
- [x] **Heel-Induced Side Load:** $F_{heel} = W \cdot \tan(\phi)$ — adds to boom stress.
- [x] **Multi-Part Reeving Tension:** $F_{line} = W / (m \cdot \eta^m)$ with $\eta$ per sheave.
- [x] **Hoist Motor Power:** $P_{motor} = (F_{line} \cdot v) / \eta_{total}$ with $v$ in m/s.
- [x] **Proof Load Test:** $1.25 \times SWL$ for cranes; $1.5 \times SWL$ for loose gear.
- [x] **Safety Devices (Know All):** LMI, ATB (anti-two-block), anemometer, overload relief valve, limit switches, emergency stop, hydraulic accumulator.
- [x] **Operating Limits:** Wind speed < 20 m/s; heel < 1°–2°; maximum boom deflection < 1°; never lift over personnel or occupied spaces.
- [x] **Numerical Tip:** Convert kN to tonnes using $1 \text{ t} = 9.81 \text{ kN}$; convert m/min to m/s using $\div 60$.
- [x] **Diagram Tip:** Always include a **neat, labelled sketch** with at least 5–6 component labels in any 7-mark descriptive sub-question.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Cargo Handling Equipment.** *Prepared under the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 / OBE) framework for PCNST503 — Marine Engineering.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shafting and Propellers: Stern Tubes and Glands, Oil Lubricated Stern Tubes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shafting and Propellers: Stern Tubes and Glands, Oil Lubricated Stern Tubes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Stern Tube (Aft Bearing Housing)**: A watertight cylindrical casing forming part of the ship's hull structure, located at the aft end of the vessel, through which the propeller shaft passes to transmit rotational power from the main engine to the propeller. It houses the aftermost bearings supporting the tailshaft and incorporates sealing arrangements (glands) to prevent seawater ingress and lubricant egress.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stern Gland (Stuffing Box Assembly)**: The sealing device fitted at the aft end of the stern tube, designed to provide a controlled seal between the rotating propeller shaft and the stationary hull, accommodating shaft misalignment, thermal expansion, and minor wear while maintaining the integrity of the internal lubrication environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oil-Lubricated Stern Tube (OLST)**: A modern sealing and bearing arrangement in which the propeller shaft bearings within the stern tube are continuously supplied with a pressurized flow of lubricating oil, providing hydrodynamic film lubrication, heat removal, corrosion protection, and positive sealing — replacing traditional water-lubricated or grease-lubricated systems on most commercial and naval vessels.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine the **propeller shaft** as a giant metal rod that spins many times per minute underwater. If you just stuck a spinning rod through a wall, two bad things would happen:
1. **Friction** would grind the shaft and the wall into dust very quickly.
2. **Water from the sea** would gush into the ship's engine room, sinking the vessel.

A **stern tube** is essentially a hollow steel tunnel built into the ship's hull, lined inside with a soft metal sleeve (called **white metal** or **Babbitt metal**) so the spinning shaft can rest on a gentle, low-friction surface. The **gland** is the door at each end of that tunnel — keeping the water out on the outside, and keeping the lubricating oil in on the inside.

Now, instead of using **water** to lubricate (which is cheap but causes rapid wear and corrosion), we use **clean, cooled, filtered oil** — much like the engine oil in your car engine. The oil is continuously circulated by pumps, cooled by a heat exchanger, filtered, monitored for water contamination, and returned. This is the **Oil-Lubricated Stern Tube** system — the gold standard in modern shipping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industry Metrics**:
> - **Lubricating oil viscosity** at the bearing: typically **ISO VG 32 to VG 68** (depending on system)
> - **Bearing surface speed**: 3 to 15 m/s for typical merchant vessels
> - **White metal (Babbitt) hardness**: 17–25 HB (Brinell) — intentionally soft
> - **Stern tube length**: 5 to 15 metres depending on vessel size

### 1.3 Historical Evolution & Engineering Context

| Generation | Period | Lubricant | Typical Application |
|------------|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| Grease packed | Pre-1950s | Tallow/Grease | Small vessels |
| Water-lubricated | 1950s–1980s | Seawater + rubber bearings | Tugs, small cargo |
| Oil-lubricated (flooded) | 1970s onwards | Mineral oil + white metal | Tankers, bulk carriers |
| Environmentally Acceptable Lubricant (EAL) | 2010s onwards | Synthetic ester / PAO | ECA zones, USCG VGP compliance |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Functional Requirements of a Stern Tube

A stern tube assembly must simultaneously satisfy four conflicting engineering demands:

1. **Mechanical support** of the propeller shaft against hydrodynamic and gravity loads.
2. **Rotational freedom** with minimum friction (low wear rate).
3. **Sealing** against bidirectional leakage (water IN, oil OUT).
4. **Thermal management** of frictional and hydrodynamic heat generation.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Oil Lubrication

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Engineering Rationale**: A hydrodynamic oil film between the shaft journal and the white metal bearing is **thousands of times more wear-resistant** than water lubrication. Under full film lubrication, the two metal surfaces NEVER touch — they are separated by a film of oil typically **50–150 micrometres** thick. This eliminates abrasive wear, prevents corrosion, and reduces frictional losses by an order of magnitude.

### 2.3 Detailed Component Architecture

#### 2.3.1 Stern Tube Casing
- **Material**: Fabricated mild steel, cast steel, or corrosion-resistant bronze.
- **Bolted or welded** to the aft peak bulkhead forward and the boss bearing / transom plate aft.
- Internal bore is machined and lined with **white metal**.

#### 2.3.2 White Metal (Babbitt) Bearings
- **Composition**: Tin-based (80–90% Sn, 3–8% Cu, 7–12% Sb) or lead-based alloys.
- **Bonding**: Centrifugal casting or static pour onto the steel shell, with a **tinned bond** ensuring metallurgical adhesion.
- **Why soft?**: The soft matrix wears preferentially and embeds dirt particles, protecting the expensive shaft from scoring.

#### 2.3.3 Sealing Arrangement
Three principal seal types are employed:

| Seal Type | Construction | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|-----------|--------------|------------|---------------|
| **Simplex Lip Seal** | Single rubber/elastomer lip with garter spring | Simple, low cost | Limited life, single barrier |
| **Mechanical Face Seal** | Rotating face + stationary face (SiC/SiC or carbon) | High integrity, long life | Expensive, requires clean oil |
| **Combination Seal** | Multiple lip + face seals in series | Redundancy, dry running capability | Complex assembly |

> [!NOTE]
> **Forward Seal (inboard)**: Prevents leakage of oil from the engine room side into the stern tube oil chamber. **Aft Seal (outboard)**: Prevents seawater ingress from sea to the oil chamber. Most modern systems use **two independent aft seals** in a "tandem" arrangement for redundancy.

#### 2.3.4 Lubrication Oil System Components

- **Gravity/head tank** (located above the engine top plate, typically 8–12 metres above the shaft centreline) — provides static head to maintain positive pressure.
- **Drain tank** with level switches and temperature probes.
- **Sump tank** with low-level and high-level alarms.
- **Oil pumps** (one duty, one standby).
- **Coolers** (plate-type or shell-and-tube, sea-water cooled).
- **Filters** (duplex, 25–60 micron).
- **Monitoring instruments**: Flow meters, pressure transmitters, temperature probes, water-in-oil sensors.

### 2.4 Oil Lubricated Stern Tube — Configurations

#### 2.4.1 Flooded Type (Most Common)
- Stern tube completely filled with oil.
- Oil is in **direct contact** with seawater at the aft seal — risk of contamination.
- Oil flows over the bearings and around the shaft.

#### 2.4.2 Independent Type (Closed Loop)
- Oil is **physically isolated** from seawater by mechanical seals at both ends.
- Requires extremely reliable seal integrity.
- Used on naval vessels and environmentally sensitive applications.

#### 2.4.3 Simplex Compact Type
- Smaller, integrated unit with bearings and seals in one cartridge.
- Common in smaller vessels and smaller diameter shafts.

#### 2.4.4 Bulkhead / Watertight Stern Tube
- Bolted through a watertight bulkhead to maintain subdivision.
- Required for SOLAS-compliant tankers and naval ships.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **examination-tested** and must be memorised with their units and boundary conditions.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Projected Bearing Area** | $A_p = L \times D$ | mm² | L = bearing length, D = shaft diameter |
| 2 | **Bearing Pressure (Projected)** | $P = \dfrac{W}{L \times D}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | W = total load on bearing |
| 3 | **Allowable Bearing Pressure (White Metal, Oil Lub)** | $P_{allow} = 2.5 \text{ to } 3.5$ | N/mm² | Empirical, classification society rule |
| 4 | **Bearing Surface Velocity** | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$ | m/s | D in metres, N in rpm |
| 5 | **PV Factor (Wear Criterion)** | $PV = P \times V_s$ | N·m/(mm²·s) or MPa·m/s | Should not exceed 20 MPa·m/s for white metal |
| 6 | **Heat Generated by Friction** | $Q = \mu \cdot W \cdot V_s$ | Watts | μ = coefficient of friction (0.001–0.005 for oil-lub) |
| 7 | **Oil Flow Required for Cooling** | $Q_{oil} = \dfrac{Q}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}$ | m³/s | ρ = oil density, cp = specific heat, ΔT = allowable temp rise |
| 8 | **Diametral Clearance** | $C_d = 0.001 \text{ to } 0.002 \times D$ | mm | Empirical for hydrodynamic film |
| 9 | **Flow through Annular Gap (Poiseuille approx.)** | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot c^3 \cdot \Delta P}{12 \cdot \mu \cdot L}$ | m³/s | c = radial clearance, μ = dynamic viscosity |
| 10 | **Reynolds Number (Annular Flow)** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot V \cdot c}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | For laminar vs. turbulent flow check |
| 11 | **Oil Specific Heat** | $c_p \approx 1.9 \text{ to } 2.1$ | kJ/(kg·K) | Mineral oil |
| 12 | **Oil Density** | $\rho \approx 870 \text{ to } 900$ | kg/m³ | At 60°C |
| 13 | **Static Head Pressure** | $P_{head} = \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa | h = head tank height above shaft (typically 8–12 m) |
| 14 | **Sommerfeld Number** | $So = \dfrac{\mu \cdot N}{P} \cdot \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | r = shaft radius, c = radial clearance |
| 15 | **Specific Loading (for SKF classification)** | $K = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D^2}$ | N/mm² | — |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Oil-lubricated stern tubes are not merely an academic concept. They are the **regulatory and operational backbone** of:
- **MARPOL Annex VI** compliance for environmentally sensitive waters.
- **USCG Vessel General Permit (VGP)** discharge regulations.
- **Classification society rules** (DNV, LR, ABS, BV) for shaft alignment and bearing life.
- **Predictive maintenance** programs, since oil quality and temperature can be continuously monitored, providing early warning of seal failure or bearing wear.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Oil Flow Required for Heat Removal

**Problem Context (Standard KTU Module 4 Question):** A marine propulsion shaft is supported by a white-metal stern tube bearing. Determine the minimum oil flow required through the bearing to limit the oil temperature rise to 10°C, given the operating parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Engineering Logic**: Heat is generated in the bearing by viscous friction. To prevent overheating, this heat MUST be removed by the circulating oil. The mass flow rate of oil needed is therefore: **Heat In = Heat Out** (steady-state energy balance).

#### Step 1: Identify Given Parameters

Let us define a standard KTU-style problem with the following given data:

- Shaft diameter, $D = 400$ mm $= 0.4$ m
- Bearing length, $L = 1.2$ m
- Rotational speed, $N = 120$ rpm
- Load on bearing, $W = 120{,}000$ N (i.e., 120 kN)
- Coefficient of friction, $\mu = 0.002$ (typical for oil-lubricated hydrodynamic journal)
- Oil density, $\rho = 880$ kg/m³
- Oil specific heat, $c_p = 2.0$ kJ/(kg·K) $= 2000$ J/(kg·K)
- Permissible temperature rise, $\Delta T = 10$ °C $= 10$ K

#### Step 2: Compute the Surface Velocity $V_s$

The surface velocity of the shaft at the bearing interface is given by:

$$V_s = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \frac{\pi \times 0.4 \times 120}{60} \\
V_s &= \frac{150.796}{60} \\
V_s &= 2.513 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic**: This is the linear velocity at which the journal surface drags oil through the bearing — the higher this velocity, the more heat is generated per unit friction force.

#### Step 3: Compute the Heat Generated by Friction $Q$

The frictional power dissipated as heat:

$$Q = \mu \cdot W \cdot V_s$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 0.002 \times 120{,}000 \times 2.513 \\
Q &= 603.19 \text{ W} \\
Q &\approx 0.603 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic**: Of the total mechanical power transmitted through the shaft, a small fraction (0.1%–0.5%) is lost to viscous friction in the bearing. This energy is converted to heat.

#### Step 4: Apply Steady-State Energy Balance

The heat absorbed by the oil per second equals the heat generated:

$$\dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = Q$$

Where $\dot{m}$ is the **mass flow rate** of oil in kg/s.

#### Step 5: Solve for Mass Flow Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= \frac{Q}{c_p \cdot \Delta T} \\
\dot{m} &= \frac{603.19}{2000 \times 10} \\
\dot{m} &= \frac{603.19}{20{,}000} \\
\dot{m} &= 0.03016 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6: Convert to Volumetric Flow Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} \\
\dot{V} &= \frac{0.03016}{880} \\
\dot{V} &= 3.43 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
\dot{V} &= 34.3 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} \\
\dot{V} &= 2.06 \text{ L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight**: The required oil flow is small because the bearing is highly efficient. However, in practice, oil flow is set **3 to 5 times higher** than this minimum to ensure (a) adequate flushing of wear debris, (b) sufficient velocity to maintain turbulent heat transfer, and (c) margin for transient load spikes.

#### Step 7: Final Answer (KTU Examiner-Friendly Format)

$$
\boxed{\dot{V}_{oil} \approx 2.06 \text{ L/min (minimum theoretical)}}
$$

**Practical design value**: $\dot{V}_{design} \approx 6 \text{ to } 10 \text{ L/min}$ (with safety factor of 3–5).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Typical KTU Examination Problem Types

| Question Type | Concept Tested | Standard Approach | Common Pitfall |
|---------------|----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| Define stern tube | Terminology | Mention hull structure, propeller shaft passage, after bearing | Forgetting to mention sealing function |
| Sketch OLST | Visual + Components | Draw long section showing casing, bearing, seals, glands | Oiling system components missing |
| Calculate bearing pressure | Application of $P = W/(L \cdot D)$ | Identify W, L, D, divide | Forgetting the **projected** area definition |
| Calculate PV factor | Material selection | Compute P and $V_s$ separately, then multiply | Confusing peripheral speed with rpm directly |
| Compare water vs. oil lubrication | Comparative analysis | Tabulate 6–8 points on wear, cooling, sealing, environment | Missing environmental compliance angle |
| Describe sealing arrangements | Component knowledge | Discuss lip seal, mechanical seal, tandem arrangement | Not explaining why two aft seals are used |
| Why head tank? | System design logic | Mention static head to maintain positive pressure | Forgetting to mention loss of oil → air ingress → seal damage |
| Why white metal? | Material science | Soft matrix, embedability, conformability, corrosion resistance | Forgetting the tinned bonding layer |
| Maintenance of OLST | Operational knowledge | Daily: oil level, temperature, pressure. Weekly: filters. Survey: bearing inspection | Missing oil sampling / water-in-oil testing |
| EAL (Environmentally Acceptable Lubricant) | Environmental regulation | Mention EPA VGP, biodegradability, ester/PAO chemistry | Forgetting to mention viscosity range differences |
