## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

---

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**"Define the term 'Stern Tube' and state its function in a marine propulsion system. List the materials used for the bearing lining."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **stern tube** is a watertight cylindrical casing forming part of the ship's hull at the aft end, through which the propeller shaft passes from the engine room to the externally-mounted propeller.

**Functions:**
1. Provides a supported bearing surface for the rotating propeller shaft.
2. Transmits the propeller's thrust reaction to the hull structure.
3. Houses the sealing arrangement (stern gland) to prevent seawater ingress and lubricant leakage.

**Bearing lining materials**:
- **White metal (Babbitt metal)** — tin-based or lead-based alloys (e.g., 88% Sn, 8% Sb, 4% Cu).
- **Bearing brass** (used in older vessels).
- **Phosphor bronze** (for non-metallic composite bearings in some designs).

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for functions, 1 mark for materials. Diagram is optional but earns extra credit.)*

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**"What is an oil-lubricated stern tube? State two advantages and one disadvantage of this system."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **oil-lubricated stern tube** is a stern tube arrangement in which the propeller shaft bearings are continuously supplied with clean, cooled, filtered lubricating oil under pressure, in contrast to water-lubricated systems.

**Advantages (any 2):**
1. **Low wear rate**: Hydrodynamic oil film separates shaft and bearing, eliminating metal-to-metal contact.
2. **Effective heat removal**: Circulating oil carries away frictional heat, maintaining bearing temperature.
3. **Corrosion protection**: Oil film prevents seawater corrosion of the shaft and bearing surfaces.
4. **Environmental compliance**: Modern sealed designs prevent oil discharge to the sea.
5. **Longer bearing life**: Typically 25,000+ operating hours between overhauls.

**Disadvantages (any 1):**
1. Higher initial cost and complex piping/cooling/filtering system.
2. Risk of oil leakage to sea in case of seal failure (pollution risk).
3. Requires continuous monitoring of oil pressure, temperature, and water content.

---

### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 SCHEME — INTERNAL CHOICE FORMAT**: As per KTU 2024 regulations, Part B questions of 14 marks carry an **internal choice** — students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions are presented below. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 MARKS) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] With the aid of a sketch, describe the construction and working of an oil-lubricated stern tube. Explain the function of each major component.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction**: An oil-lubricated stern tube assembly consists of the following main components, arranged from forward (engine room) to aft (seawater):

1. **Stern Tube Casing**: A fabricated or cast steel cylindrical tube, internally bored and machined. It is fitted to the ship's hull by an inboard flange bolted to the aft peak bulkhead and an outboard flange connected to the boss of the propeller bracket or transom plate.

2. **Forward Seal (Inboard Gland)**: A lip-type seal located at the forward end of the stern tube. It prevents leakage of oil from the stern tube into the engine room and excludes foreign matter.

3. **White Metal Bearings**: The inner bore of the stern tube is lined with **white metal (Babbitt)** — a soft alloy of tin, copper, and antimony — cast onto a tinned steel shell. The propeller shaft rotates on this bearing surface, separated by a hydrodynamic oil film.

4. **Main Oil Space**: The annular space between the shaft and the bearing is continuously filled with circulating lubricating oil.

5. **Aft Seals (Outboard Glands)**: Modern designs use a **tandem sealing arrangement** — typically two mechanical face seals in series, with an intermediate drainage chamber. This provides redundancy: any leakage from the primary seal is captured in the drainage chamber and alarmed, without reaching the seawater.

**Working**: Clean lubricating oil is supplied from a **gravity head tank** (located 8–12 m above the shaft) to the forward end of the stern tube. Oil flows along the bearing, exits at the aft end, returns via drain lines to a sump tank, and is pumped through a **cooler** (sea-water cooled) and a **filter** before returning to the head tank. The oil film — typically 50–150 µm thick — supports the shaft load hydrodynamically. Instrumentation continuously monitors **oil pressure, temperature, flow, and water content**.

*(Examiner's note: Sketch carries 2 marks, component names 2 marks, working description 3 marks.)*

#### (b) [7 Marks] A marine propeller shaft of 500 mm diameter is supported by a stern tube bearing of length 1.5 m. The total load on the bearing is 200 kN and the shaft speed is 100 rpm. Assuming hydrodynamic lubrication with μ = 0.0025, calculate: (i) the bearing pressure, (ii) the surface velocity, (iii) the heat generated, and (iv) comment on the suitability of this design.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $D = 500$ mm $= 0.5$ m
- $L = 1.5$ m
- $W = 200{,}000$ N
- $N = 100$ rpm
- $\mu = 0.0025$

**(i) Bearing Pressure:**

$$
P = \frac{W}{L \times D} = \frac{200{,}000}{1500 \times 500} = 0.267 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 0.267 \text{ MPa}
$$

*(Note: For L and D in mm, $A_p$ in mm². Convert to MPa after division.)*

**Suitability Check**: $P = 0.267$ MPa is **well within the allowable limit** of 2.5–3.5 MPa for white metal bearings. ✓

**(ii) Surface Velocity:**

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi \times D \times N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.5 \times 100}{60} = 2.618 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iii) Heat Generated:**

$$
Q = \mu \cdot W \cdot V_s = 0.0025 \times 200{,}000 \times 2.618 = 1309 \text{ W} \approx 1.31 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iv) Comment on Suitability:**

- **Bearing pressure (0.267 MPa)** is significantly lower than the maximum allowable 2.5 MPa. This indicates the bearing is **under-loaded** — possibly oversized, but operationally safe.
- **PV factor**: $PV = 0.267 \times 2.618 = 0.699$ MPa·m/s, well below the 20 MPa·m/s limit. ✓
- **Heat dissipation** of 1.31 kW is easily handled by a standard oil circulation system of 6–10 L/min with a 5–10°C temperature rise.
- **Conclusion**: The design is **safe and conservative** but not optimised. A shorter bearing (e.g., 1.0 m) could be used to reduce friction surface area, or the bearing material could be evaluated for cost reduction.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 MARKS) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compare water-lubricated and oil-lubricated stern tube bearings under the following heads: (i) Lubricant used, (ii) Bearing material, (iii) Wear rate, (iv) Cooling, (v) Environmental impact, (vi) Maintenance.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | Water-Lubricated | Oil-Lubricated |
|---|-----------|------------------|----------------|
| (i) | **Lubricant** | Sea water (direct) or fresh water (closed loop) | Mineral or synthetic lubricating oil (ISO VG 32–68) |
| (ii) | **Bearing material** | Rubber, lignum vitae, synthetic composite, phenolic resin | White metal (Babbitt) on steel shell |
| (iii) | **Wear rate** | High (shaft wear 0.5–2 mm/year), bearing wear significant | Very low (<0.05 mm/year shaft wear), 25,000+ hours bearing life |
| (iv) | **Cooling** | Limited — water carries away some heat but cavitation risk | Excellent — oil flow, cooler, and thermostatic control |
| (v) | **Environmental impact** | Safe (water discharge acceptable) | Risk of oil pollution if seal fails; mitigated by sealed designs and EAL oils |
| (vi) | **Maintenance** | Frequent bearing inspection and renewal | Periodic oil sampling, filter change, seal inspection; less frequent overhauls |

**Additional Points:**
- Water-lubricated systems have **higher friction** (μ ≈ 0.01–0.05) and hence greater fuel penalty.
- Oil systems allow **monitoring-based condition-based maintenance** (oil analysis, vibration analysis).
- Modern regulations (USCG VGP, EU EMSA) **strongly favour EAL oil-lubricated systems** for vessels operating in special areas.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark per comparison point. Tabulated answers are preferred.)*

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the construction and function of (i) the head tank, (ii) the duplex filter, and (iii) the heat exchanger in an oil-lubricated stern tube system.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Head Tank:**
- **Construction**: A small cylindrical or rectangular oil storage tank mounted in the engine room **8–12 metres above the propeller shaft centreline**. It has a capacity of typically 50–200 litres, fitted with a sight glass, vent, fill cap, and a low-level alarm.
- **Function**: Provides a **constant gravity feed** of clean oil to the stern tube at a steady, **positive hydrostatic pressure** of approximately 0.8–1.2 bar. This positive pressure ensures that any minor seal leakage is **outward** (oil leaks out) rather than **inward** (seawater leaks in). It also acts as a **reservoir** and an **expansion volume** for thermal expansion of oil, and serves as a **de-aeration point**.

**(ii) Duplex Filter:**
- **Construction**: A filter housing containing **two identical filter elements** mounted in parallel, with **inlet and outlet change-over valves**. Each filter is typically a 25–60 micron stainless steel mesh or pleated paper element.
- **Function**: Continuously removes **particulate contaminants** (wear debris, sand, pipe scale) from the circulating oil. The duplex arrangement allows **online filter change-over** without stopping the system — one filter is in service while the other is on standby or being cleaned. Differential pressure gauges indicate filter condition; high ΔP triggers filter cleaning.

**(iii) Heat Exchanger (Plate Type or Shell-and-Tube):**
- **Construction**: A compact heat exchanger through which the hot oil (from the bearing drain) flows on one side, and cold seawater (from the sea chest) flows on the other side, separated by metallic plates or tubes. Usually made of **titanium plates** or **copper-nickel tubes** for seawater resistance.
- **Function**: Removes the **frictional heat** picked up by the oil in the bearing, reducing the oil temperature typically from 50–65°C (drain) to 35–45°C (return to head tank). A **temperature control valve** regulates the seawater flow to maintain oil inlet temperature. This ensures the oil viscosity remains in the optimal range for hydrodynamic film formation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP REASONS STUDENTS LOSE MARKS ON THIS TOPIC (KTU 2024 SCHEME):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Bearing Pressure" and "PV Factor"** — They are DIFFERENT quantities. Bearing pressure is in MPa, PV factor is in MPa·m/s. Many students write them as if interchangeable.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversions** — Shaft diameter in mm, length in mm, force in N. Final answer must be in **N/mm² (MPa)**. Not bar, not kg/cm².
> 3. **Missing the role of the head tank** — The most common exam trick. Students describe it as "an oil reservoir" but miss the **positive pressure** function. Always mention both.
> 4. **Drawing lip seals but not labelling them as "tandem"** — Modern stern tubes use TWO seals. Single seal answers are outdated and lose marks.
> 5. **Ignoring environmental compliance** — In KTU 2024, examiners reward answers that mention **MARPOL, USCG VGP, and EAL oils** for environmental awareness.
> 6. **Confusing white metal with white metal "alloys"** — White metal is a SOFT ALLOY, not a "white-coloured metal." Mark this carefully.
> 7. **Skipping the "why" in descriptive answers** — "What is a white metal bearing?" alone earns 2 marks. Adding **"why tin-based alloy? — embedability, conformability, corrosion resistance"** earns full 7 marks.
> 8. **Not sketching a LONGITUDINAL SECTION** — A block diagram is NOT a longitudinal section. Examiners specifically ask for "sketch" — students must draw the actual cut-away view with shaft, bearing, and seals.

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY SUMMARY — REVISIT BEFORE EXAM**

- **Stern Tube**: Aft hull casing supporting the propeller shaft; houses bearings and seals.
- **Stern Gland**: Sealing device at aft end; prevents water IN, oil OUT.
- **Two main types of lubrication**: **Water** (cheap, high wear) and **Oil** (expensive, low wear, environmental concerns).
- **White metal (Babbitt)**: Soft Sn-Cu-Sb alloy, 17–25 HB, cast on steel shell with tinned bond.
- **Why white metal?** → Soft matrix embeds debris → protects expensive shaft.
- **Bearing Pressure Formula**: $P = W / (L \times D)$, allowable 2.5–3.5 MPa.
- **Surface Velocity Formula**: $V_s = \pi D N / 60$, m/s.
- **PV Factor Limit**: ≤ 20 MPa·m/s for white metal.
- **Heat Generated**: $Q = \mu W V_s$, Watts.
- **Oil Flow for Cooling**: $\dot{V} = Q / (\rho c_p \Delta T)$, m³/s.
- **Diametral Clearance**: $C_d = 0.001 \text{ to } 0.002 \times D$.
- **Three seal types**: Lip seal, Mechanical face seal, Tandem combination.
- **Tandem seals**: TWO independent seals aft + drainage chamber for redundancy.
- **Head tank**: 8–12 m above shaft, provides static head = positive pressure = leak direction OUT.
- **Duplex filter**: Two filters, one on standby, online change-over.
- **Heat exchanger**: Sea-water cooled, reduces oil temperature 50°C → 40°C.
- **Sump tank**: Collects drain oil, has level and temperature sensors.
- **Monitoring parameters**: Pressure, temperature, flow, **water-in-oil ppm**.
- **EAL**: Environmentally Acceptable Lubricant — required in USCG VGP zones, based on esters/PAO.
- **SOLAS relevance**: Stern tube bulkhead penetrations must maintain subdivision integrity.
- **Classification society rules**: IACS members (DNV, LR, ABS, BV) govern bearing pressure, PV limits, and material specifications.
- **Common shaft diameters**: 200 mm (small) to 800+ mm (VLCC, naval).
- **Oil viscosity at bearing**: ISO VG 32 to 68 depending on size and speed.
- **Shaft wear check**: < 0.1 mm/year typical for oil-lub; < 2 mm/year for water-lub.
- **Flooded vs. Independent type**: Flooded = oil contacts sea at seal; Independent = oil isolated by mechanical seals.
- **White metal pouring**: Centrifugal casting for high-speed, static pour for low-speed.
- **Common failure modes**: Seal wear → oil leak; Loss of oil pressure → bearing seizure; Water ingress → bearing corrosion; Oil overheating → film breakdown.
- **Predictive maintenance**: Oil sampling every 1000 hours, particle count, viscosity, water content.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Stern Tube & Rotating Machinery Shaft Seal):**
> A **shaft seal** is a mechanical sealing device installed at the interface between a rotating shaft and a stationary housing, engineered to **prevent the axial or radial leakage** of process fluids (seawater, lubricating oil, hydraulic fluid, bunker fuel) along the shaft axis, while simultaneously accommodating shaft rotation, misalignment, and axial movement.

In marine engineering, shaft seals are most critically employed in the **stern tube assembly** — the tubular casing that houses the propeller shaft as it exits the ship's hull — and in **pumps, gearboxes, turbochargers, and propeller shaft bearings**. They are the **primary barrier** between the internal machinery space (dry) and the external marine environment (wet, corrosive, pressurized by sea head).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Faucet Around a Spinning Rod"

Imagine holding a garden hose (the **rotating shaft**) and trying to wrap a towel around it tightly enough to stop water leaking out — but the rod is **spinning at 100+ rpm**, vibrating, and the water pressure outside is equal to a column of seawater several meters tall. A shaft seal is exactly that "smart towel" — engineered with precision-machined faces, elastomeric lips, or hydrodynamic grooves that **maintain a controlled liquid or gas barrier** under continuous rotation. 

The sealing principle relies on one of three physical mechanisms:
1. **Mechanical contact** (face-to-face or lip-to-shaft) — squeezing the gap to zero.
2. **Hydrodynamic / fluid film** — maintaining a thin lubricating layer via the sealed fluid itself.
3. **Non-contact labyrinth** — forcing the fluid through a tortuous, restrictive path.

## 1.3 Engineering Classification Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Marine Shaft Seals (KTU Module 4 Syllabus Mapping):**

| Class | Examples | Typical Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Contact Lip Seals** | Radial lip seals (rotary shaft seals) | Stern tube aft seal, gearbox input/output |
| **Mechanical Face Seals** | Cartridge mechanical seals, split seals | Pumps, propeller shaft sealing |
| **Packing / Stuffing Box** | Braided compression packing | Older vessels, auxiliaries |
| **Labyrinth Seals** | Multiple-stage non-contact seals | Turbochargers, high-speed shafts |
| **Hydrostatic / Air Barrier** | Air-conditioned seal (Aqua-Lub) | Specialized seawater-lubricated stern tubes |
| **O-Ring / Gasket Seals** | Static/dynamic elastomer rings | Ancillary equipment |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Operating Limits (Bolded)

- **Shaft circumferential surface speed (typical marine propeller shaft):** $v = 4 \text{ to } 12 \text{ m/s}$ (≤ **15 m/s** for lip seals).
- **Seawater head pressure at stern tube (typical large vessel):** $p \approx 0.1 \text{ to } 0.3 \text{ MPa}$ (**1 to 3 bar**).
- **Operating temperature range:** $-10\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (polar service) to **+90 °C** (tropical water, oil-lubricated).
- **PV factor (Pressure × Velocity) limit for rubber lip seals:** $PV \leq 1 \text{ MPa·m/s}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle of the Stern Tube Shaft Seal Assembly

The stern tube on a modern motor vessel is a **horizontal cylindrical casing** supporting the propeller shaft between the aftermost engine bearing (or thrust bearing) and the propeller. It contains a series of bearings and seals whose order (from forward/inboard → aft/outboard) is typically:

1. **Forward bearing** (white metal or resin) — supports shaft radially.
2. **Forward (inboard) seal** — prevents lube oil from leaking into the engine room.
3. **Intermediate bearing(s)** (water-lubricated in modern eco-designs) — provide radial support.
4. **Aft seal** (outboard) — prevents seawater ingress into the stern tube.
5. **Propeller** (outboard).

The aft seal bears the entire **seawater hydrostatic head** ($\rho g h$), typically $h = 8\text{–}15\,\text{m}$, so it must seal against $\approx 0.1$–$0.15\,\text{MPa}$ of external pressure — while the shaft rotates.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Working Logic (Lip Seal — Most Common Marine Type)

A radial lip seal consists of:
- A **metal case** (casing) for structural rigidity.
- An **elastomeric body** (NBR, FKM, HNBR, or PTFE) bonded to the case.
- A **primary sealing lip** with a precisely machined **helical garter spring** providing radial load.
- A **secondary dust lip** (optional) excluding external debris.

**Operating sequence:**
1. The **garter spring** exerts a controlled radial interference (typically **0.2 to 0.5 mm**) of the lip onto the shaft, creating a **contact band width** $b \approx 0.25$–$0.5\,\text{mm}$.
2. As the shaft rotates, microasperities on the elastomer lip **drag a thin oil film** from the sealed side across the contact band by **hydrodynamic wedge action**.
3. This film ($\approx 0.5$–$2\,\mu\text{m}$ thick) **lubricates the lip** and prevents frictional overheating — yet is thin enough to **prevent bulk fluid leakage**.
4. The opposite side (seawater) is held back because the oil film breaks down via **surface tension / capillary action** at the air-side edge.
5. Excess heat generated by viscous shearing of the oil film is **conducted through the lip** into the shaft (which acts as a heat sink via forced cooling water or oil circulation).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Lip Seal Paradox (KTU favourite viva question):**
> A perfect "zero leakage" lip seal would generate **infinite friction** and burn out in seconds. The seal is therefore designed to leak a **microscopically controlled amount of oil** — just enough to maintain the hydrodynamic film. This is called the **"controlled micro-leakage"** regime.

## 2.3 Mechanical Face Seal Operating Principle

A mechanical face seal uses **two perfectly flat faces** perpendicular to the shaft axis:
- **Rotating face** (often SiC, tungsten carbide, or carbon) — rotates with the shaft.
- **Stationary face** (often carbon graphite vs. SiC pair) — held axially by a secondary seal (O-ring/pusher).

A thin **liquid film** ($h \approx 0.1$–$1\,\mu\text{m}$) is maintained between the faces by:
- **Hydrodynamic grooves** laser-etched on the rotating face (spiral or radial).
- **Surface tension** of the sealed fluid.

Face seals are used where **higher pressures (>0.5 MPa)** or **abrasive fluids** would destroy elastomeric lip seals.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Formula Bank — Shaft Seals**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shaft surface (circumferential) speed | $v = \pi D N / 60$ | m/s | $D$ in m, $N$ in rpm |
| 2 | Angular velocity | $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ | rad/s | — |
| 3 | PV factor | $PV = p \cdot v$ | MPa·m/s | **Limit ≤ 1** for rubber lip seals |
| 4 | Friction torque (lip seal) | $T_f = f \cdot F_r \cdot r_s$ | N·m | $f$ = friction coeff. (≈ 0.05–0.15 wet), $r_s$ = shaft radius |
| 5 | Radial lip interference force | $F_r = k \cdot \delta$ | N | $k$ = lip stiffness (N/mm), $\delta$ = interference (mm) |
| 6 | Friction power loss | $P_f = T_f \cdot \omega$ | W | Direct heat dissipation load |
| 7 | Heat generated (face seal) | $Q_{gen} = \mu \cdot A \cdot v \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r / h$ | W | $h$ = film thickness |
| 8 | Reynolds (couette) flow film thickness | $h = \sqrt{2\mu v L / \Delta p}$ | m | Parallel-face squeeze film |
| 9 | Leakage rate (face seal, laminar) | $Q_L = \dfrac{\pi D h^3 \Delta p}{12 \mu L}$ | m³/s | Parallel-plate flow analog |
| 10 | Seawater head pressure | $p_{sw} = \rho_{sw} g h_{draft}$ | Pa | $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 11 | Garter spring radial load | $F_{spring} = \dfrac{E \cdot b \cdot t^3}{4 R^2} \cdot \delta$ | N | $E$ = spring modulus, $R$ = coiled radius |
| 12 | Shaft misalignment tolerance | $\theta_{max} \approx 0.5^{\circ}$ | degrees | Lip seal; face seals tolerate less |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these formulas appear in real marine practice:**
> - **Friction torque $T_f$** is directly added to the ship's **propulsion resistance** at the design stage — it is part of the brake-specific fuel consumption (BSFC) calculation.
> - **PV factor** is the universal life-prediction parameter for any rotary elastomer seal (used by SKF, Trelleborg, Eriks).
> - **Leakage rate** determines whether the seal is "fit for purpose" against IMO Marpol Annex VI bilge-discharge rules.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier)** with a 750 mm diameter propeller shaft rotating at 90 rpm:
- $v = \pi \times 0.75 \times 90/60 \approx 3.53\,\text{m/s}$.
- Sealing lip load ≈ **80–120 N** per cm of shaft circumference.
- Stern tube oil volume ≈ **2000–4000 L** of EP (extreme pressure) gear oil.
- A failure of the aft seal can sink the vessel — hence **classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS)** mandate redundant sealing arrangements.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shaft Circumferential Speed and PV Factor

We begin with a propeller shaft of diameter $D$ rotating at $N$ revolutions per minute. The **linear surface speed** at the seal location is:

$$
v = \pi D \cdot \frac{N}{60} \quad \text{(m/s)}
$$

$$
\text{(since one revolution = circumference = } \pi D \text{ metres, and } 1\,\text{min} = 60\,\text{s)}
$$

For a typical merchant vessel stern tube shaft:
- $D = 0.65\,\text{m}$, $N = 110\,\text{rpm}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \pi \times 0.65 \times \frac{110}{60} \\
  &= 3.1416 \times 0.65 \times 1.8333 \\
  &= 3.1416 \times 1.1917 \\
  &= 3.744\,\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

The PV factor is then:

$$
PV = p \cdot v
$$

With seawater head pressure $p = \rho_{sw} \, g \, h_{draft}$.

For a draft of $h = 12\,\text{m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 12 \\
  &= 120{,}621\,\text{Pa} \\
  &\approx 0.1206\,\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= 0.1206 \times 3.744 \\
   &\approx 0.4516\,\text{MPa·m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **below the 1 MPa·m/s** lip-seal limit — safe operation confirmed. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Friction Torque and Power Loss at the Lip Seal

The radial interference force of the lip on the shaft is provided by the garter spring. For a typical interference $\delta = 0.3\,\text{mm}$ and lip stiffness $k \approx 50\,\text{N/mm}$:

$$
F_r = k \cdot \delta = 50 \times 0.3 = 15\,\text{N per cm of lip width}
$$

For a full 360° lip of axial contact width $b = 0.4\,\text{mm}$, the equivalent contact force scales with circumference. Adopting the equivalent distributed radial load:

$$
F_{r,\text{total}} \approx 15\,\text{N/cm} \times \pi D = 15 \times \pi \times 65 \approx 3063\,\text{N}
$$

The friction torque (with wet friction coefficient $\mu \approx 0.1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= \mu \cdot F_{r,\text{total}} \cdot r_s \\
    &= 0.1 \times 3063 \times \frac{0.65}{2} \\
    &= 0.1 \times 3063 \times 0.325 \\
    &\approx 99.5\,\text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Power dissipation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_f &= T_f \cdot \omega \\
    &= 99.5 \times \frac{2\pi \times 110}{60} \\
    &= 99.5 \times 11.519 \\
    &\approx 1146\,\text{W} \approx 1.15\,\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

This heat must be removed; otherwise the lip temperature can rise by $\Delta T \approx \dfrac{P_f}{m_{lip} \, c_{lip}}$, necessitating either oil circulation cooling or shaft-fin cooling in the stern tube.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Leakage Rate Through a Mechanical Face Seal

For a parallel-faced mechanical seal with mean diameter $D_m$, face width $L$, and lubricant film thickness $h$, laminar Couette–Poiseuille flow gives:

$$
Q_L = \frac{\pi D_m \, h^3 \, \Delta p}{12 \, \mu \, L}
$$

Given:
- $D_m = 0.20\,\text{m}$
- $h = 1 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$ ($1\,\mu\text{m}$)
- $\Delta p = 0.2 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa}$ (seawater head)
- $\mu = 0.001\,\text{Pa·s}$ (water at 25 °C)
- $L = 0.005\,\text{m}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_L &= \frac{\pi \times 0.20 \times (10^{-6})^3 \times 0.2 \times 10^6}{12 \times 0.001 \times 0.005} \\
    &= \frac{3.1416 \times 0.20 \times 10^{-18} \times 2 \times 10^{5}}{6 \times 10^{-5}} \\
    &= \frac{1.257 \times 10^{-13}}{6 \times 10^{-5}} \\
    &= 2.09 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} \\
    &\approx 7.5\,\text{ml/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **typical design leakage** for a face seal — considered acceptable bilge water ingress (within class limits).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Seal Selection Decision Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Matrix for KTU Problems:**

| Application | Pressure (MPa) | Speed (m/s) | Temp (°C) | Recommended Seal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stern tube aft (sea water) | 0.1–0.3 | 3–10 | 0–40 | Lip seal (FKM/NBR) |
| Stern tube forward (oil) | 0.05–0.1 | 3–10 | 50–80 | Lip seal + drain |
| Centrifugal pump | 0.3–1.5 | 5–20 | 60–120 | Mechanical face seal |
| Turbocharger | 0.05–0.4 | 30–80 | 200–500 | Labyrinth + carbon ring |
| Hydraulic cylinder | 5–35 | 0.5–2 | 50–80 | O-ring + backup ring |
| Propeller shaft (water-lub.) | 0.1–0.2 | 3–8 | 0–30 | Air-conditioned seal (Simplex/Lamelle) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Stern Tube Seal System (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Room / Thrust Bearing] --> B[Forward Oil Seal]
    B --> C[Stern Tube Housing]
    C --> D[Intermediate Bearing - White Metal]
    D --> E[Forward Water Seal]
    E --> F[Aft Lip Seal - Seawater Side]
    F --> G[Stern Tube Oil Reservoir]
    G --> H[Oil Fill and Drain]
    F --> I[Propeller Shaft]
    I --> J[Propeller Hub]
    J --> K[Sea Water - External]
    K -. pressure head .-> F
    H -. recirculation .-> C
```

## 4.2 Component Architecture — Radial Lip Seal Cross-Function

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Metal Case / Outer Casing] --> nodeB[Elastomer Body - Bonded]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Primary Sealing Lip]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Secondary Dust Lip]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Helical Garter Spring]
    nodeE -. radial load .-> nodeC
    nodeF[Rotating Shaft] -. contact band .-> nodeC
    nodeF -. micro oil film .-> nodeC
    nodeC -. blocks .-> nodeG[Seawater Side]
    nodeH[Oil Side] -. micro leak .-> nodeC
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Mechanical Face Seal Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[Process Fluid - Pressurized] --> nodeQ[Seal Chamber]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Rotating Face - SiC]
    nodeR -- thin liquid film h micron --> nodeS[Stationary Face - Carbon]
    nodeS --> nodeT[Secondary O-Ring - Static]
    nodeT --> nodeU[Atmosphere Side]
    nodeR -. rotates with shaft .-> nodeV[Drive Collar]
    nodeS -. spring loaded .-> nodeW[Single Spring or Bellows]
```

## 4.4 Seal-Type Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Selection Starting Point]) --> q1{Sealing Fluid Type}
    q1 -- Seawater --> q2{Pressure Level}
    q1 -- Lubricating Oil --> q6{Shaft Speed m per s}
    q2 -- Low pressure under 0.3 MPa --> q3{Continuous Contact Acceptable}
    q2 -- High pressure above 0.3 MPa --> q5[Mechanical Face Seal]
    q3 -- Yes --> q4[Radial Lip Seal with Garter Spring]
    q3 -- No --> q5
    q6 -- Under 15 --> q7[Lip Seal]
    q6 -- Over 15 --> q8[Labyrinth Seal]
    q4 --> done([Final Selection])
    q5 --> done
    q7 --> done
    q8 --> done
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a physical component or a decision node in the seal selection logic. Arrows show flow direction (fluid flow or signal flow). Dotted arrows represent non-mechanical couplings (pressure, force, or feedback).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a shaft seal. List any two types of shaft seals used in marine engineering with one application each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A shaft seal is a device used to prevent the leakage of fluid (oil, water, fuel) along a rotating shaft where it passes through a stationary housing.

**(1 mark — definition)**

Two types:
1. **Radial Lip Seal** — used in stern tube assemblies to prevent seawater ingress into the stern tube.
2. **Mechanical Face Seal** — used in centrifugal pumps and main propeller shaft sealing cartridges.

**(2 marks — 1 mark each type + application)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the significance of the PV factor in shaft seal design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **PV factor** is the product of the **sealing pressure** ($p$) and the **shaft surface speed** ($v$) at the seal interface. 

It quantifies the **combined thermal-mechanical loading** on the seal lip:

**(1 mark — definition)**

- It is the **primary life-prediction parameter** for elastomeric rotary seals.
- It must remain **below the material-specific limit** (≈ **1 MPa·m/s** for NBR/FKM lip seals) to prevent **premature wear, overheating, and seal failure**.
- Exceeding the limit causes lip hardening, blistering, and catastrophic leakage.

**(2 marks — significance and limit)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a radial lip seal used in a marine stern tube. Discuss the role of the garter spring and the concept of controlled micro-leakage. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks)

**Construction:**
A radial lip seal consists of:
1. **Metal case** — provides rigidity and press-fit retention in the housing bore.
2. **Elastomeric body** (NBR/FKM/HNBR/PTFE) — bonded to the case; provides the sealing geometry.
3. **Primary sealing lip** — the radial contact edge that touches the rotating shaft.
4. **Garter spring** — a helical coil spring seated in a groove on the back of the lip; provides continuous radial interference load.
5. **Secondary dust lip** (optional) — on the air-side to exclude dirt and moisture from the contact band.

**Working Principle (step-wise):**
1. The garter spring keeps the lip pressed against the shaft with controlled interference $\delta$ (typically 0.2–0.5 mm).
2. As the shaft rotates, **microscopic asperities** on the lip surface drag a thin film of oil from the sealed side across the **contact band** (≈ 0.25–0.5 mm wide) by hydrodynamic action.
3. This thin film (0.5–2 µm thick) **lubricates the lip–shaft interface**, preventing dry friction and overheating.
4. The film is **thin enough** to prevent bulk fluid leakage but **thick enough** to carry away frictional heat.
5. **Capillary action and surface tension** at the air-side edge of the contact band prevent oil from escaping in measurable quantities.

**(Full marks: 7 — labelled sketch 2 marks + construction 2 marks + working 3 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Garter Spring Role and Controlled Micro-Leakage (7 marks)

**Role of the Garter Spring:**
- It provides the **constant radial interference force** that maintains lip contact throughout the seal's life, even as the elastomer ages and relaxes.
- It **compensates for lip wear** and thermal expansion.
- It ensures a uniform 360° contact pressure, preventing "high-spot" leakage.
- Spring force is typically **5–20 N per cm of circumference**, tuned for the application.
- The spring is usually made of **stainless steel** (corrosion resistance) or **carbon steel with coating**.

**Controlled Micro-Leakage:**
- A theoretical "perfect seal" with zero leakage would require **infinite lip interference**, leading to **infinite friction**, **infinite heat generation**, and immediate lip destruction.
- Therefore, the lip seal is designed to allow a **minute, controlled oil leak** (typically **< 1 ml per 24 hours** per seal) to maintain the hydrodynamic lubricating film.
- This regime balances **friction, heat, and leakage** optimally — known as the **mixed-lubrication regime** of seal tribology.
- The micro-leakage is **below operational concern** but is critical for seal longevity.

**(Full marks: 7 — garter spring 3 marks + micro-leakage concept 4 marks)**

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Explain the construction and working of a mechanical face seal used in marine pumps. Derive an expression for the leakage rate through the seal faces and compute the leakage for the following data: Mean diameter of seal face $D_m = 0.15\,\text{m}$, film thickness $h = 1.5\,\mu\text{m}$, pressure difference $\Delta p = 0.4\,\text{MPa}$, face width $L = 0.004\,\text{m}$, fluid viscosity $\mu = 0.001\,\text{Pa·s}$. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working of Mechanical Face Seal (7 marks)

**Construction:**
A mechanical face seal comprises:
1. **Rotating face** (primary ring) — typically made of silicon carbide (SiC), tungsten carbide, or alumina ceramic; rotates with the shaft.
2. **Stationary face** (mating ring) — usually carbon-graphite (against SiC) or bronze; held axially stationary.
3. **Secondary seal** — an elastomer O-ring or PTFE wedge that seals the stationary face to the housing (static sealing).
4. **Spring** — single-coil, multi-coil, or metal bellows that maintains axial contact load between the faces.
5. **Drive mechanism** — pins or notches on the rotating face engage with the shaft sleeve to ensure rotation.
6. **Set screws / gland plate** — used to set and adjust face alignment.

**Working Principle:**
1. The two faces are lapped to a **surface roughness of < 0.05 µm Ra** and a **flatness of < 0.0006 mm** — almost optically flat.
2. The spring pushes the stationary face against the rotating face with a controlled axial load (typically **1–3 N per cm² of face area**).
3. A **thin liquid film** (0.1–1 µm) is maintained between the faces — by hydrodynamic grooves on the rotating face or by the fluid being sealed.
4. This film **lubricates** the faces, carries away frictional heat, and prevents wear.
5. The film thickness is self-regulating: too thin → wear; too thick → leakage.

**(Full marks: 7 — labelled sketch 2 marks + construction 3 marks + working 2 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Derivation and Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Derivation of Leakage Rate:**

Consider the seal faces as two parallel plates separated by film thickness $h$, with mean circumference $\pi D_m$ and axial width $L$. The fluid is in **laminar combined Couette–Poiseuille flow**.

Neglecting the rotational Couette component (small $h$), the pressure-driven volumetric flow rate is:

$$
Q_L = \frac{\pi D_m \, h^3 \, \Delta p}{12 \, \mu \, L}
$$

For the given data:
- $D_m = 0.15\,\text{m}$
- $h = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$
- $\Delta p = 0.4 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa}$
- $L = 0.004\,\text{m}$
- $\mu = 0.001\,\text{Pa·s}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_L &= \frac{\pi \times 0.15 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-6})^3 \times 0.4 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times 0.001 \times 0.004} \\
    &= \frac{3.1416 \times 0.15 \times 3.375 \times 10^{-18} \times 4 \times 10^{5}}{4.8 \times 10^{-5}} \\
    &= \frac{6.36 \times 10^{-13}}{4.8 \times 10^{-5}} \\
    &= 1.325 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} \\
    &\approx 47.7\,\text{ml/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The leakage rate of approximately **48 ml/hour** is within the **acceptable design limit** for a marine centrifugal pump mechanical seal.

**(Full marks: 7 — derivation 3 marks + substitution 2 marks + final answer with units 2 marks)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on Shaft Seal questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — KTU examiners deduct ½ to 1 mark if $Q_L$ is given without **m³/s or ml/hour**.
> 2. **Confusing "lip seal" with "face seal"** — A lip seal contacts the shaft *radially*; a face seal contacts *axially* along the perpendicular face. Mixing the diagrams costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the garter spring explanation** — Examiners specifically look for the spring's role in maintaining contact pressure. Vague answers like "it holds the lip" score zero.
> 4. **Not stating the PV limit numerically** — Writing only "low PV factor" instead of "$PV \leq 1\,\text{MPa·m/s}$" loses ½ mark.
> 5. **Wrong assumption of laminar vs. turbulent flow** — At micro-film thicknesses in face seals, flow is **always laminar**; Reynolds number should ideally be calculated and stated to justify.
> 6. **Missing the "controlled micro-leakage" concept** — This is a **mandatory viva point**. Omitting it costs at least 1 mark.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **shaft seal** prevents fluid leakage along rotating shafts — most critical application is the **marine stern tube**.
- ✅ **Radial lip seal** = elastomer lip with garter spring; cheap, robust, used for low-to-medium pressure.
- ✅ **Mechanical face seal** = two flat faces perpendicular to shaft; used for high pressure and abrasive fluids.
- ✅ **PV factor** (Pressure × Velocity) is the universal life indicator for rotary seals; **limit ≈ 1 MPa·m/s** for rubber lips.
- ✅ **Garter spring** provides constant radial interference force, compensating for lip wear and thermal ageing.
- ✅ **Controlled micro-leakage** is essential — zero leakage would cause infinite friction and seal destruction.
- ✅ **Circumferential speed:** $v = \pi D N / 60$; in marine stern tubes typically **3–10 m/s**.
- ✅ **Friction torque:** $T_f = \mu F_r r_s$; **friction power:** $P_f = T_f \omega$.
- ✅ **Face seal leakage rate (Poiseuille flow):** $Q_L = \pi D_m h^3 \Delta p / (12 \mu L)$.
- ✅ **Seawater pressure head:** $p = \rho_{sw} g h$ with $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$.
- ✅ **Classification societies** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, IRS) mandate **redundant sealing** in stern tubes.
- ✅ **Material selection:** NBR (default), FKM (high temp), PTFE (chemical), SiC + Carbon (face seals).
- ✅ **Common failure modes:** lip hardening, abrasion, chemical attack, overheating (PV > limit), shaft wear (sleeve replacement needed).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shaft Alignment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shaft Alignment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Shaft Alignment** is the precise geometric process of establishing a predetermined, continuous centre-line path through the line of shafting (comprising the main engine crankshaft, intermediate shaft(s), tail shaft, and propeller shaft) and its supporting bearings, such that the rotational axis of every component lies in a single, smooth, designed plane — either straight or with a calculated intentional offset — to ensure efficient power transmission, minimum vibration, and prolonged bearing life.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Definition** — Shaft alignment is the science of correcting both the **parallel (offset)** and **angular** deviations of the shaft line so that the line of centres coincides with the **designed cold offset**, which itself compensates for the **running (hot) condition** of the engine and ship.

In marine engineering practice, the alignment is *never* done for the hot, running condition directly. Instead, the alignment is performed on the **cold (ambient) ship**, and a **designed cold offset** is intentionally introduced so that, when the ship is underway, the engine settles to its true straight-line position.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine threading a long, heavy needle through several floating corks on a pond. The needle is the shaft, the corks are the bearings. If you simply align the corks in a straight line on the water's surface, the needle (being heavy) will *sag* in the middle and rub against the corks. The clever trick used by shipbuilders is to **deliberately bend the corks' line into a gentle upward curve** before threading the needle. Once the needle is in place and the system is "running" (the needle is spinning fast and self-supporting by its gyroscopic stiffness), it straightens out and floats perfectly in the centre of every cork.

This upward pre-bend is mathematically called the **"sag allowance"**, and the curved line of corks is the **"cold alignment offset"**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Numerical Benchmarks (Board Favourite Values)**
> - **Young's Modulus of Marine Shafting Steel (E):** $\mathbf{2.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^2}$ (or **207 GPa** for higher-grade steels).
> - **Coefficient of Linear Thermal Expansion (α) for Steel:** $\mathbf{1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per } ^\circ C}$.
> - **Standard Permissible Parallel Misalignment (Offset):** **0.05 mm to 0.10 mm per metre** of coupling diameter pitch.
> - **Standard Permissible Angular Misalignment:** **0.05 mm to 0.10 mm per 100 mm** of coupling diameter.
> - **Typical Ship Stern-Tube Length (L):** **10 m to 25 m**.
> - **Propeller Weight Range:** **5 tonnes to 80 tonnes** depending on vessel class.

### 1.4 Why Shaft Alignment is Critical in Marine Engineering

| Consequence of Poor Alignment | Engineering Impact |
|-------------------------------|--------------------|
| **Excessive Stern-tube bearing wear** | Bearing temperature rises; oil film collapses; white-metal wears in days. |
| **Vibration & Whirling** | Critical speed is lowered; LOM (Lack of Oil film) cavitation appears. |
| **Hull vibration transmission** | Accommodation noise exceeds SOLAS limits; structural fatigue. |
| **Propeller-induced excitation** | Blade-rate and shaft-rate forces amplify; bearing failures occur. |
| **Loss of fuel efficiency** | Increased frictional torque; specific fuel oil consumption (SFOC) rises. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Misalignment (KTU Classification)

Shaft misalignment is classified into three principal geometric conditions:

1. **Parallel (Offset) Misalignment** — The two shaft axes are parallel but laterally displaced by a distance *e* (mm).
2. **Angular Misalignment** — The two shaft axes intersect at an angle *θ* (millimetres per 100 mm diameter).
3. **Combined Misalignment** — A superposition of (1) and (2); the most common in real ships.

> [!NOTE]
> The **KTU Board expects** students to be able to *differentiate* between offset and angular misalignment, and to *calculate both* during alignment surveys.

### 2.2 The Three Phases of Shaft Alignment

**Phase I — Quasi-Static Sag:** Deflection of the shafting under its own weight in still water. This is calculated from beam-deflection theory.

**Phase II — Thermal Sag:** Differential expansion of the hull girder (warmer topside, cooler shell plating) causes the engine to settle *downward* relative to the propeller.

**Phase III — Dynamic Sag:** The rotating shafting becomes stiffer due to gyroscopic action. The dynamic deflection is **less** than the static deflection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Equation of Marine Shaft Alignment:**
> $$\boxed{\text{Design Cold Offset} = \text{Hot (Running) Offset} + \text{Quasi-Static Sag} - \text{Thermal Sag} - \text{Dynamic Correction}}$$
> 
> In most KTU numerical problems, the "hot offset" is taken as **zero** (perfect straight line at design condition), simplifying the equation.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving Part-B numericals on shaft alignment. Every value of constants must be remembered.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Deflection of a **simply supported beam** (uniformly distributed load) | $\delta = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ | mm | Intermediate shaft, two-bearing span |
| 2 | Deflection of a **continuous beam on three supports** (centre span) | $\delta = \dfrac{wL^4}{185EI}$ (approx.) | mm | Engine, intermediate, tail shaft line |
| 3 | Thermal expansion of shaft | $\Delta L = \alpha L \Delta T$ | mm | Hot-condition offset correction |
| 4 | Angular misalignment limit | $\theta \le \dfrac{0.10}{100} \text{ mm/mm}$ | mm/100mm | Coupling acceptance criterion |
| 5 | Parallel misalignment limit | $e \le \dfrac{0.10}{1000} \times D \text{ mm}$ (D = coupling dia. in mm) | mm | Coupling acceptance criterion |
| 6 | Critical speed of shaft (Rayleigh) | $N_c = \dfrac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g \cdot \sum W_i \cdot y_i}{\sum W_i \cdot y_i^2}}$ | rpm | Whipping check |
| 7 | Section modulus (solid circular shaft) | $I = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$ | mm⁴ | Deflection substitution |
| 8 | Slope of deflected shaft (simply supported) | $\theta = \dfrac{wL^3}{24EI}$ | rad | Angular offset at supports |
| 9 | Propeller immersion pressure correction | $P_{corr} = P_{atm} + \rho g h$ | Pa | Alignment draft correction |
| 10 | Final cold offset (sag method) | $y_{cold} = y_{hot} + \delta_{sag} - \delta_{thermal}$ | mm | Survey board answer |

> [!NOTE]
> In the above table, **w = weight per unit length (N/mm)**, **L = span (mm)**, **E = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²**, **I = second moment of area (mm⁴)**, and **g = 9.81 m/s²**.

### 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter in Real Engineering

- The **simply supported beam formula** (Row 1) is used by shipyards to compute how much the intermediate shaft sags *between* two plummer blocks — this sag is added back as an *upward* cold offset.
- The **thermal expansion** (Row 3) explains why the engine appears to "rise" when hot — the steel hull girder expands more on the top (sun-exposed) side, **tilting the engine upward** at the aft end.
- The **critical speed** (Row 6) defines the rotational speed at which the shaft will resonate transversely. Misalignment lowers this speed dangerously close to the running rpm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** Marks are awarded *only* if the correct **unit conversion** is shown. *Always convert metres to millimetres* before substituting in deflection equations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deflection of a Simply Supported Intermediate Shaft (Quasi-Static Sag)

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Module 4. We model a single span of intermediate shafting as a uniform, simply supported beam carrying its own weight.

**Step 1 — Identify the load.**
The shaft has a uniformly distributed self-weight. Let:
- $w$ = weight per unit length (N/mm) = $\rho g A$
- $L$ = length of the span between the two plummer block centres (mm)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the shaft (mm²) = $\dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$

**Step 2 — State the governing differential equation.**
For a uniform beam under distributed load, the Euler-Bernoulli equation gives:

$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

**Step 3 — Bending moment expression.**
For a simply supported beam with UDL, the bending moment at a distance $x$ from the left support is:

$$M(x) = \frac{wL}{2} x - \frac{w x^2}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate once to obtain slope.**
Integrating $\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = \dfrac{M(x)}{EI}$ once with respect to $x$:

$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{wL}{4} x^2 - \frac{w x^3}{6} + C_1$$

**Step 5 — Apply boundary condition for slope at the centre (symmetry).**
For a symmetric simply supported beam, the slope is zero at the mid-span $(x = L/2)$:

$$0 = \frac{wL}{4}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2 - \frac{w}{6}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^3 + C_1$$

$$0 = \frac{wL^3}{16} - \frac{wL^3}{48} + C_1$$

$$C_1 = -\frac{wL^3}{16} + \frac{wL^3}{48} = -\frac{3wL^3 - wL^3}{48} = -\frac{2wL^3}{48} = -\frac{wL^3}{24}$$

**Step 6 — Integrate again to obtain deflection.**
Substituting $C_1$ back:

$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{wL}{4} x^2 - \frac{w x^3}{6} - \frac{wL^3}{24}$$

$$EI y = \frac{wL}{12} x^3 - \frac{w x^4}{24} - \frac{wL^3}{24} x + C_2$$

**Step 7 — Apply deflection boundary condition.**
At $x = 0$ (left support), the deflection is zero: $y(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$.

**Step 8 — Find the maximum deflection at mid-span $(x = L/2)$.**

$$EI y_{max} = \frac{wL}{12}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^3 - \frac{w}{24}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^4 - \frac{wL^3}{24}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)$$

$$EI y_{max} = \frac{wL^4}{96} - \frac{wL^4}{384} - \frac{wL^4}{48}$$

Finding a common denominator (384):

$$EI y_{max} = \frac{4wL^4}{384} - \frac{wL^4}{384} - \frac{8wL^4}{384} = \frac{(4 - 1 - 8)wL^4}{384} = \frac{-5wL^4}{384}$$

Taking the magnitude (deflection is downward, hence negative):

$$\boxed{y_{max} = \delta_{sag} = \frac{5wL^4}{384 EI}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the canonical KTU result.** The negative sign simply indicates the beam sags *downward*; the magnitude is what we add as an *upward* cold offset to compensate.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermal Sag of the Hull Girder

The engine sits on the upper deck, while the propeller is submerged in cold seawater. The topside of the hull expands more than the bottomside, causing the engine to *rise* relative to the stern tube.

**Step 1 — Define the temperature gradient.**
Let the average top-deck temperature be $T_t$ and the bottom-shell temperature be $T_b$. The differential temperature is:

$$\Delta T_{girder} = T_t - T_b$$

**Step 2 — Free thermal expansion of a fibre of the hull at distance h from the neutral axis.**
For a ship with depth $D_{depth}$ (moulded), the maximum fibre distance is $D/2$:

$$\Delta L_{thermal} = \alpha \cdot \frac{D}{2} \cdot \Delta T_{girder}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to vertical sag at the engine bedplate.**
Using the principle of similar triangles over the engine-to-propeller span $L_{eng-prop}$:

$$\delta_{thermal} = \alpha \cdot \frac{D \cdot L_{eng-prop}}{2} \cdot \Delta T_{girder}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{thermal} = \frac{\alpha \, D \, L_{eng-prop} \, \Delta T}{2}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Combined Cold Offset Equation (Sag Method)

**Step 1 — Identify the four contributions to cold offset.**

| Symbol | Term | Direction |
|--------|------|-----------|
| $y_{hot}$ | Design running offset (usually 0) | Reference |
| $\delta_{qs}$ | Quasi-static sag (downward) | - |
| $\delta_{th}$ | Thermal sag (engine rises, so subtract) | - |
| $\delta_{dyn}$ | Dynamic correction (shaft stiffens when rotating) | + |

**Step 2 — Algebraic combination.**
The cold offset is the algebraic sum that brings the cold shaft line to the design curve:

$$y_{cold} = y_{hot} - \delta_{qs} + \delta_{th} - \delta_{dyn}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the standard case (y_hot = 0, δ_dyn ignored for slow-speed 2-stroke):**

$$\boxed{y_{cold} = \delta_{th} - \delta_{qs} = \frac{\alpha D L_{eng-prop} \Delta T}{2} - \frac{5 w L^4}{384 EI}}$$

**Step 4 — Worked numeric example (KTU standard).**

**Given:** Intermediate shaft, length $L = 8000 \text{ mm}$, diameter $d = 400 \text{ mm}$, $E = 2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$, material density $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Find:** Quasi-static sag at the centre of the span.

**Step 4a — Compute cross-sectional area.**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (400)^2}{4} = 125\,664 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4b — Compute the second moment of area.**

$$I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (400)^4}{64} = 1.2566 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 4c — Compute weight per unit length.**

$$w = \rho g A = 7850 \times 9.81 \times 125\,664 \times 10^{-9} \text{ N/mm}$$

$$w = 7850 \times 9.81 \times 0.125664 = 9677 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 4d — Substitute into the sag formula.**

$$\delta_{sag} = \frac{5 \times 9677 \times (8000)^4}{384 \times (2.0 \times 10^5) \times (1.2566 \times 10^9)}$$

$$\delta_{sag} = \frac{5 \times 9677 \times 4.096 \times 10^{15}}{384 \times 2.513 \times 10^{14}}$$

$$\delta_{sag} = \frac{1.982 \times 10^{20}}{9.650 \times 10^{16}} = 2.054 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{sag} \approx 2.05 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Valuation Tip:** A common student error is to forget the **10⁻⁹ conversion** when going from $kg/m^3$ and $m^2$ into $N/mm$. Always express *all quantities in N and mm* before substitution.

### 3.4 Permissible Misallowance Table (Standard Classification Society)

| Misalignment Type | Permissible Limit (Class Notation) |
|-------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Parallel offset at flexible coupling | $\le 0.05 \text{ to } 0.10 \text{ mm}$ |
| Angular offset at flexible coupling | $\le 0.05 \text{ to } 0.10 \text{ mm/100 mm dia.}$ |
| Stern-tube bearing clearance | $0.5 \text{ to } 1.5 \text{ mm}$ per side |
| Intermediate bearing clearance | $0.3 \text{ to } 0.8 \text{ mm}$ per side |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Marine Shaft Alignment System

The diagram below maps the **functional interaction between physical components, measurement tools, and correction logic** of a typical main-propulsion shaft alignment procedure on a merchant vessel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Engine] --> B[Gearbox / Reduction Gear]
    B --> C[Intermediate Shaft Section 1]
    C --> D[Plummer Block 1]
    D --> E[Intermediate Shaft Section 2]
    E --> F[Plummer Block 2]
    F --> G[Tail Shaft]
    G --> H[Stern Tube Bearing]
    H --> I[Propeller Shaft]
    I --> J[Propeller Hub]

    K[Dial Gauge Set 1] -.-> D
    L[Dial Gauge Set 2] -.-> F
    M[Laser Alignment Tool] -.-> C
    N[Laser Alignment Tool] -.-> G

    P[Sag Calculation Sheet] --> Q[Quasi Static Sag Delta]
    R[Hull Temperature Log] --> S[Thermal Sag Delta]
    T[Engine Builder Curve] --> U[Hot Offset Reference]
    Q --> V[Combined Cold Offset Equation]
    S --> V
    U --> V
    V --> W[Alignment Workshop Card]
    W --> X[Engine Bedplate Shimming]
    X --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Step-by-Step Alignment Survey Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Step1[Surveyor Reads Ship Drawing] --> Step2[Identify Bearing Centres and Engine Datum]
    Step2 --> Step3[Measure Existing Cold Offset with Dial Gauges]
    Step3 --> Step4{Cold Offset Matches Sag Value?}
    Step4 -- No --> Step5[Calculate Shimming Correction]
    Step5 --> Step6[Lift Engine and Adjust Chocks]
    Step6 --> Step7[Re-Measure with Dial Gauges]
    Step7 --> Step4
    Step4 -- Yes --> Step8[Lock Engine Bolts at Working Load]
    Step8 --> Step9[Record Final Cold Offset in Workshop Card]
    Step9 --> Step10[Sign-Off by Chief Engineer and Surveyor]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Coupling Misalignment Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ShaftA[Shaft A Axis] -->|Parallel Offset e| ShaftB[Shaft B Axis]
    ShaftA -->|Angular Theta| ShaftC[Shaft C Tilted Axis]
    ShaftA -->|Combined e and Theta| ShaftD[Shaft D Both Offset and Tilted]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid for the Mermaid Diagrams:**
> - **Solid arrows** indicate *physical power/shaft connection* between components.
> - **Dotted arrows** indicate *measurement / sensor coupling* to the bearing.
