# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: SOLAS Rudder Torque Requirement

The SOLAS formula is the **most-tested numerical problem** on KTU steering gear questions. Here is a step-by-step derivation.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A bulk carrier of the following specifications is to have its steering gear designed:
- **Length between perpendiculars (Lpp)**: 200 m
- **Design service speed (V)**: 15 knots
- **Rudder area (A_R)**: 30 m²
- **Distance from rudder stock to center of pressure (r)**: 2.5 m
- **Rudder force coefficient (C_R)**: 0.045
- **Sea water density (ρ)**: 1025 kg/m³

Calculate the **SOLAS design torque** that the steering gear must withstand.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Convert vessel speed from knots to m/s.**

$$V = 15 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 \frac{\text{m/s}}{\text{knot}}$$

$$V = 7.716 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the dynamic pressure (q) acting on the rudder.**

The dynamic pressure of seawater at the ship's speed is given by:

$$q = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V^2$$

Substituting values:

$$q = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (7.716)^2$$

$$q = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 59.54$$

$$q = 30,514 \text{ Pa} \quad \text{or} \quad 30.514 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: Compute the resultant rudder force (F_R).**

Using the SOLAS torque relationship:

$$F_R = C_R \cdot q \cdot A_R$$

$$F_R = 0.045 \times 30,514 \times 30$$

$$F_R = 41,194 \text{ N} \approx 41.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Compute the SOLAS design torque (T).**

The torque is the rudder force multiplied by the lever arm to the center of pressure:

$$T = F_R \cdot r$$

$$T = 41,194 \times 2.5$$

$$T = 102,985 \text{ N·m} \approx 103 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 5: Apply the SOLAS safety multiplier (1.5).**

SOLAS requires the steering gear to be capable of withstanding the calculated torque multiplied by a factor of **1.5** to account for transient loads and sea conditions:

$$T_{design} = 1.5 \times T = 1.5 \times 102,985$$

$$T_{design} = 154,478 \text{ N·m} \approx 154.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 5 Explanation:** The 1.5 multiplier is **mandatory** under SOLAS Reg. 29.7. It is a frequent KTU exam trap; many students forget it and lose 2 marks.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Rotary Vane Steering Gear Torque

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A 3-vane rotary vane steering gear has the following specifications:
- **Outer radius (R_o)**: 0.600 m
- **Inner radius (R_i)**: 0.200 m
- **Rotor length (L)**: 0.500 m
- **Working pressure difference (Δp)**: 120 bar = $120 \times 10^5$ Pa
- **Vane width (chord)**: $R_o - R_i = 0.400$ m

Calculate the **maximum torque** deliverable by this steering gear.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the active chamber area.**

For a rotary vane, the effective annular area exposed to differential pressure is:

$$A_{active} = L \times (R_o - R_i)$$

$$A_{active} = 0.500 \times (0.600 - 0.200)$$

$$A_{active} = 0.500 \times 0.400 = 0.200 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the net force on the vane.**

The force on the active surface due to pressure differential:

$$F = \Delta p \cdot A_{active}$$

$$F = 120 \times 10^5 \times 0.200$$

$$F = 2.4 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 2,400 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Determine the lever arm (mean radius).**

The force acts on the vane; the effective lever arm is the **mean radius**:

$$R_m = \frac{R_o + R_i}{2}$$

$$R_m = \frac{0.600 + 0.200}{2} = \frac{0.800}{2} = 0.400 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the torque from one active chamber.**

$$T_{chamber} = F \times R_m$$

$$T_{chamber} = 2.4 \times 10^6 \times 0.400$$

$$T_{chamber} = 9.6 \times 10^5 \text{ N·m} = 960 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 5: Account for multiple vanes / chambers.**

For a 3-vane rotor, there are typically **2 active chambers** working differentially (one side pressurised, the other exhausted). For **3 vanes**, the number of active chambers is 3. The **total torque** is the sum of torque contributions:

$$T_{total} = N_{active} \times T_{chamber}$$

For a 3-vane rotor (3 active chambers):

$$T_{total} = 3 \times 960 = 2,880 \text{ kN·m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 3 active chambers for a 3-vane rotor?**
> With $n$ vanes, the rotor divides the annular space into $n$ chambers. Pressurizing **alternating** chambers (and exhausting the others) gives a configuration where typically **all chambers produce useful torque** because the differential pressure always pushes the rotor in the same rotational direction. The exact number of effective chambers depends on the **port plate / distributor** design, but for typical 3-vane gears, **3 chambers** contribute torque.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Hydraulic Power Unit Sizing

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

The bulk carrier from Section 3.1 is fitted with a **two-ram hydraulic steering gear**. The system requirements are:
- **Required torque (T_req)**: 154,478 N·m (from 3.1)
- **Lever arm (r)**: 1.8 m (distance from ram centerline to rudder stock)
- **Hard-over to hard-over time**: 28 seconds (SOLAS minimum)
- **Rudder angle range**: ±35° = 70° total

Calculate the required **hydraulic pressure**, **ram diameter**, and **pump flow rate**.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate the required force on the tiller / crosshead.**

The torque is generated by the crosshead force acting at the lever arm:

$$T_{req} = F_{crosshead} \times r_{lever}$$

$$F_{crosshead} = \frac{T_{req}}{r_{lever}}$$

$$F_{crosshead} = \frac{154,478}{1.8} = 85,821 \text{ N} \approx 85.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Determine the ram diameter assuming working pressure of 160 bar.**

$$F_{crosshead} = p_{work} \times A_{ram} = p_{work} \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

Rearranging for D:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot F_{crosshead}}{\pi \cdot p_{work}}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 85,821}{\pi \times 160 \times 10^5}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{343,284}{5.0265 \times 10^7}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{6.83 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$D = 0.0826 \text{ m} \approx 83 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the ram travel (L) required for ±35° rudder swing.**

The crosshead travels a distance equal to the chord swept by the lever arm as the rudder swings 35° to each side:

$$L_{travel} = r_{lever} \times \sin(35°) \times 2$$

$$L_{travel} = 1.8 \times 0.5736 \times 2 = 2.065 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the oil volume required for full hard-over sweep.**

$$V_{oil} = A_{ram} \times L_{travel}$$

$$V_{oil} = \frac{\pi (0.0826)^2}{4} \times 2.065$$

$$V_{oil} = 5.358 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.065 = 0.01107 \text{ m}^3 = 11.07 \text{ L}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the required pump flow rate to achieve 28-second hard-over.**

$$Q_{pump} = \frac{V_{oil}}{t_{hard-over}} \times 60$$

$$Q_{pump} = \frac{11.07}{28} \times 60 = 23.72 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 6: Calculate the hydraulic power.**

$$P_{hydraulic} = \frac{Q \cdot \Delta p}{600} = \frac{23.72 \times 160}{600} = 6.33 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7: Calculate the required electric motor rating (assuming 90% pump and 95% motor efficiency).**

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{hydraulic}}{\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor}} = \frac{6.33}{0.90 \times 0.95} = 7.40 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Motor Sizing:** A motor of approximately **7.5 kW** or **11 kW** (next standard size up) would be selected. Two such motors (one duty + one standby) would be installed per SOLAS redundancy requirements.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Hand Gear | Steam Gear | Rapson Slide | Rotary Vane | Electro-Hydraulic (4-ram) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Power source** | Human muscle | Steam | Hydraulic | Hydraulic | Electric motor + hydraulic |
| **Typical torque** | < 50 kN·m | 100–500 kN·m | 200–1500 kN·m | 500–3000 kN·m | 1500–10,000 kN·m |
| **Vessel type** | < 70 GT | Older ships | Warships, mid-tankers | Tankers, bulkers | VLCCs, large naval |
| **Control type** | Direct | Direct / FU | FU | FU | FU / NFU + autopilot |
| **Maintenance** | High (lubrication) | Moderate | High (slide wear) | Moderate (seal wear) | Moderate (electronic + hydraulic) |
| **Reliability** | Moderate | Good | Good | Very good | Excellent |
| **Efficiency** | 60–70% | 50–60% | 75–85% | 85–92% | 88–95% |
| **Size/weight** | Heavy & bulky | Heavy | Compact axial | Most compact | Variable |
| **SOLAS status** | Standby only | Retired | Limited use | Active | **Standard today** |
| **Reversibility** | Manual | Automatic | Automatic | Automatic | Automatic |
| **Speed (35°/35°)** | 60+ sec | 30–45 sec | 25–30 sec | 20–28 sec | 20–28 sec |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Evaluating Follow-Up Control System Response

### Problem Setup

A follow-up electro-hydraulic steering system is in operation. Helmsman orders +10°. The system parameters:
- **Rudder at**: 0° (centered)
- **Steady-state rudder rate**: 3°/sec
- **Steady-state error band**: ±0.5°

Determine the **time to reach +10° within tolerance** and the **oil flow requirement** for a 2-ram system with 80 mm ram diameter.

### Solution

**Step 1: Time to reach ordered angle.**

$$t = \frac{\Delta\theta}{\dot{\theta}} = \frac{10°}{3°/\text{sec}} = 3.33 \text{ sec}$$

**Step 2: Total ram travel for +10°.**

For 1.8 m lever arm:

$$L = r \cdot \sin(10°) = 1.8 \times 0.1736 = 0.3125 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Oil volume.**

$$V = \frac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} \times 0.3125 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3125 = 1.571 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 1.57 \text{ L}$$

**Step 4: Required flow rate.**

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} \times 60 = \frac{1.57}{3.33} \times 60 = 28.3 \text{ L/min}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Electro-Hydraulic Steering Gear

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete signal and power flow** of a modern electro-hydraulic steering gear system. This is a block-level functional architecture showing the interactions between bridge controls, hydraulic power, and rudder mechanics.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[HELMSMAN BRIDGE STATION] --> B[TILLER PILOT OR WHEEL ORDER UNIT]
    B --> C[ELECTRICAL ORDER TRANSMITTER FOLLOWUP]
    C --> D[COMPARATOR AND AMPLIFIER MODULE]
    E[RUDDER ANGLE FEEDBACK TRANSMITTER] --> D
    D --> F[SOLENOID DIRECTIONAL CONTROL VALVES SPOOL TYPE]
    F --> G[HYDRAULIC STEERING ACTUATOR ROTARY VANE OR RAM TYPE]
    G --> H[RUDDER STOCK AND TILLER CROSSHEAD]
    H --> I[SHIP RUDDER HYDRODYNAMIC LOAD]
    H --> E
    J[EMERGENCY NFU CONTROL] --> F
    K[AC MOTOR PUMP UNIT ONE DUTY] --> L[HYDRAULIC OIL RESERVOIR AND FILTER]
    M[AC MOTOR PUMP UNIT TWO STANDBY] --> L
    L --> F
    N[OIL COOLER AND TEMPERATURE CONTROL] --> L
    O[RUDDER ANGLE INDICATOR RAIS BRIDGE DISPLAY] --> P[BRIDGE AND WHEELHOUSE]
    Q[AUTOPILOT INPUT GYRO COMPASS] --> C
    H --> O
    R[ALARM AND MONITORING PANEL] --> P
    F --> R
    L --> R
```

**Diagram Block Legend:**

| Block | Function | Energy/Signal Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | Bridge control location | Physical |
| B | Manual input device | Mechanical |
| C, D | Order conversion & comparison | Electrical |
| E | Rudder position feedback | Electrical |
| F | Directional control | Hydraulic + Electrical |
| G | Hydraulic-to-mechanical converter | Hydraulic |
| H | Rudder stock rotation | Mechanical |
| I, J, K, L, M, N | Power unit subsystems | Electrical / Hydraulic |
| O, P, R | Display and alarms | Electrical |

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Rapson Slide Steering Gear

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BRIDGE HELM WHEEL] --> B[TELEMOTOR TRANSMITTER]
    B --> C[HYDRAULIC CONTROL VALVE SPOOL]
    C --> D[PORT HYDRAULIC CYLINDER]
    C --> E[STBD HYDRAULIC CYLINDER]
    D --> F[PORT SLIPPER SHOE]
    E --> G[STBD SLIPPER SHOE]
    F --> H[VERTICAL SLIDING BLOCK ON RUDDER HEAD]
    G --> H
    H --> I[RUDDER STOCK ROTATION]
    I --> J[SHIP RUDDER]
    I --> K[FEEDBACK TRANSMITTER TO BRIDGE]
    K --> A
    L[OIL RESERVOIR AND PUMP] --> C
    L --> D
    L --> E
```

**Operating Sequence (Rapson Slide):**
1. Helmsman moves wheel to port.
2. Telemotor transmitter signals the control valve.
3. Oil is ported to **starboard cylinder** (pushing slipper against sliding block).
4. Starboard slipper **pushes the sliding block down**; Port slipper is **retracted**.
5. Vertical motion of sliding block rotates the **rudder stock** (off-center application creates torque).
6. Rudder moves to port.
7. Feedback transmitter returns rudder angle to bridge.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Rotary Vane Steering Gear

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[ELECTRO HYDRAULIC CONTROL UNIT] --> B[PORT PLATE DISTRIBUTOR]
    B --> C[VANE CHAMBER A PRESSURIZED]
    B --> D[VANE CHAMBER B EXHAUSTED]
    B --> E[VANE CHAMBER C PRESSURIZED]
    C --> F[ROTOR ASSEMBLY WITH 3 VANES]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[SEALED BEARING UPPER]
    F --> H[SEALED BEARING LOWER]
    F --> I[RUDDER STOCK]
    I --> J[SHIP RUDDER]
    I --> K[ANGULAR POSITION FEEDBACK SENSOR]
    K --> A
    L[MOTOR PUMP UNIT] --> M[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE]
    M --> B
    N[OIL SUMP AND COOLER] --> L
```

**Operating Sequence (Rotary Vane):**
1. Electric motor drives hydraulic pump.
2. Pump pressurizes oil to **port plate** distributor.
3. Distributor routes oil to **chambers A and C** (pressurized); **chamber B** is exhausted.
4. Pressure differential across the vane surfaces creates **rotational torque** on the rotor.
5. Rotor (rigidly connected to rudder stock) rotates the rudder.
6. Bearings constrain the rotor axially and radially.

## 4.4 Schematic of Complete Hydraulic Power Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[AC MOTOR M1 DUTY] --> B[VARIABLE DISPLACEMENT PUMP P1]
    C[AC MOTOR M2 STANDBY] --> D[FIXED DISPLACEMENT PUMP P2]
    B --> E[PRESSURE LINE 160 BAR]
    D --> E
    E --> F[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE SETTING]
    E --> G[DIRECTIONAL CONTROL VALVE 4 BY 3]
    G --> H[STEERING ACTUATOR VANE OR RAM]
    H --> I[RETURN LINE LOW PRESSURE]
    I --> J[OIL SUMP TANK 500 LITERS]
    D --> J
    B --> J
    J --> K[THERMOSTATIC VALVE]
    K --> L[FRESH WATER COOLER]
    K --> M[BYPASS TO SUMP]
    J --> N[INLINE FILTER 10 MICRON]
    N --> B
    N --> D
    O[LEVEL SWITCH AND TEMPERATURE SENSOR] --> J
    P[PRESSURE TRANSMITTER] --> E
    Q[LOAD SENSING LINE] --> H
    Q --> B
```

**Subsystem Function Mapping:**

| Subsystem | Components | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Power Generation** | M1 + P1, M2 + P2 | Convert electrical to hydraulic energy |
| **Pressure Regulation** | Pressure relief, Load sensing | Cap & control max pressure |
| **Directional Control** | 4/3 solenoid valve | Determine oil flow direction |
| **Energy Storage** | Accumulator (optional) | Maintain pressure during power loss |
| **Conditioning** | Filter, cooler, thermostatic valve | Maintain oil quality & temperature |
| **Monitoring** | Level, temperature, pressure sensors | Provide alarms to ECR |

## 4.5 Schematic: SOLAS Redundancy Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[BRIDGE MAIN CONTROL] --> B[CONTROL TRANSFER UNIT]
    C[BRIDGE WING PORT] --> B
    D[BRIDGE WING STBD] --> B
    E[AFT STEERING FLAT STANDBY CONTROL] --> B
    F[EMERGENCY FIREMAN CONTROL] --> B
    B --> G[CONTROL BUS]
    G --> H[STEERING GEAR POWER UNIT ONE]
    G --> I[STEERING GEAR POWER UNIT TWO]
    H --> J[HYDRAULIC ACTUATOR]
    I --> J
    J --> K[RUDDER]
    L[EMERGENCY GENERATOR] --> H
    L --> I
    M[MAIN BUS SUPPLY] --> H
    M --> I
```

**SOLAS Redundancy Notes:**
- Two independent **power units** (M1+P1 and M2+P2) ensure no single failure disables steering.
- Emergency generator automatically starts on blackout to maintain steering.
- Multiple control stations (bridge, wings, steering flat) allow changeover in failure scenarios.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define "steering gear" as per SOLAS and state the SOLAS hard-over to hard-over time requirements for vessels at maximum service speed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Steering gear is the complete machinery, actuators, and associated control systems installed on a vessel to rotate the rudder for directional control. SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 29 defines it as the equipment by which the rudder is operated to steer the ship. The arrangement must comprise one or more power units, the transmission system, and controls.

**SOLAS Time Requirements (1 mark):**
- For vessels proceeding at **maximum service speed**, the steering gear must be capable of putting the rudder over from **35° on one side to 35° on the other side in not more than 28 seconds** under the specified design torque.
- Under SOLAS, where the rudder torque at the maximum service speed is above the design value (i.e., for very large rudder angles or extreme conditions), the time may be extended to **60 seconds**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between Follow-Up (FU) and Non-Follow-Up (NFU) steering control with one example of use for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Follow-Up (FU) | Non-Follow-Up (NFU) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Operating principle** | Helmsman sets desired angle; rudder moves to that angle and stops automatically. | Rudder moves only while helmsman holds lever; stops when lever is released. |
| **Feedback required** | Yes (rudder angle feedback transmitter) | No (rudder position is not auto-corrected) |
| **Use** | Primary mode on all modern commercial vessels | Emergency / backup mode on bridge; manual override from steering flat |
| **Example vessel** | Modern container ship main steering | Old bulk carrier emergency backup |

(1 mark for defining FU, 1 mark for defining NFU, 1 mark for example contrast)

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (with Internal Choice)

### INTERNAL CHOICE – OPTION A [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### Question A1 (a) [7 marks]
**With the aid of a labeled schematic, describe the construction and operating principle of the Rapson Slide type hydraulic steering gear. State two advantages and two disadvantages.**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (4 marks):**
The Rapson Slide steering gear consists of:
1. **Two opposed hydraulic cylinders** (port and starboard) mounted on the steering flat bedplate.
2. **Two slippers (shoes)** of hardened steel, one on each side.
3. A **central vertical sliding block** keyed to the rudder head (upper part of rudder stock).
4. A **tiller crosshead** that rigidly connects the two slippers, ensuring synchronized motion.
5. **Hydraulic supply lines** with a control valve (operated via telemotor or electro-hydraulic signal).
6. **Stoppers** at the extreme ends of the sliding block travel.

**Operating Principle (2 marks):**
When the helmsman orders a port rudder, oil is admitted to the **starboard cylinder**, forcing the starboard slipper against the sliding block. The friction grip pushes the sliding block **downward**. Since the sliding block is offset from the rudder stock centerline, this vertical motion creates a tangential force on the rudder stock, producing torque that rotates the rudder to port. The port cylinder is simultaneously exhausted, allowing the port slipper to retract.

**Advantages (0.5 mark each):**
1. Simple, robust construction with relatively few moving parts.
2. Direct conversion of hydraulic pressure to rudder torque (no intermediate linkage).

**Disadvantages (0.5 mark each):**
1. Heavy wear of the slide surface due to friction transfer.
2. Limited torque capacity; not suitable for very large vessels (VLCCs).
3. Friction losses reduce efficiency.

---

#### Question A1 (b) [7 marks]
**A 60,000 DWT bulk carrier has the following steering gear design data: Rudder area = 25 m², design speed = 14 knots, distance from rudder stock to center of pressure = 2.3 m, rudder force coefficient C_R = 0.05, density of seawater = 1025 kg/m³. Calculate (i) the SOLAS design torque, and (ii) the time required to traverse hard-over to hard-over if the hydraulic pump flow rate is 35 L/min and the ram diameter is 90 mm. Lever arm of the steering gear = 1.6 m.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) SOLAS Design Torque (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert speed to m/s:
$$V = 14 \times 0.5144 = 7.2016 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute dynamic pressure:
$$q = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (7.2016)^2 = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 51.86 = 26,578 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3:** Compute rudder force:
$$F_R = C_R \times q \times A_R = 0.05 \times 26,578 \times 25 = 33,222 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4:** Compute basic torque:
$$T = F_R \times r = 33,222 \times 2.3 = 76,411 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5:** Apply SOLAS multiplier (1.5):
$$T_{design} = 1.5 \times 76,411 = 114,617 \text{ N·m} \approx 114.6 \text{ kN·m}$$

**(ii) Hard-Over Time (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Ram area:
$$A_{ram} = \frac{\pi (0.090)^2}{4} = 6.362 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Ram travel for full ±35°:
$$L = 2 \times r_{lever} \times \sin(35°) = 2 \times 1.6 \times 0.5736 = 1.836 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Volume of oil required:
$$V_{oil} = A_{ram} \times L = 6.362 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.836 = 0.01168 \text{ m}^3 = 11.68 \text{ L}$$

**Step 4:** Time to hard-over:
$$t = \frac{V_{oil}}{Q} = \frac{11.68}{35} \text{ min} = 0.3337 \text{ min} = 20.02 \text{ seconds}$$

**Result:** The hard-over time is approximately **20 seconds**, which complies with the SOLAS requirement of ≤ 28 seconds at maximum service speed.

---

### INTERNAL CHOICE – OPTION B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### Question B1 (a) [7 marks]
**Explain the construction and working of a Rotary Vane type steering gear with a neat diagram. Compare its performance with the Rapson Slide type.**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A rotary vane steering gear consists of:
1. A **stator housing** – a heavy cast steel block fixed to the steering flat.
2. A **rotor** with **3 (or 5) vanes** mounted on the rudder stock inside the stator.
3. Replaceable **vane tip seals** (rubber or PTFE) preventing internal leakage.
4. **Upper and lower bearings** supporting the rudder stock.
5. A **port plate** (distributor valve) that directs oil to alternate chambers.
6. **Pressure relief valves** protecting the system from over-pressure.

**Working (2 marks):**
High-pressure oil from the pump enters through the port plate. The distributor routes oil to alternate vane chambers (e.g., chambers 1 and 3 are pressurized; chamber 2 is exhausted). The pressure differential across the vane surfaces generates a tangential force at the mean radius, producing torque that rotates the rotor and rudder stock. To reverse direction, the port plate is repositioned, swapping the pressure and exhaust sides.

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Rapson Slide | Rotary Vane |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Torque transfer** | Friction (slipper on slide) | Hydraulic pressure on vane surface |
| **Wear** | Significant (sliding contact) | Moderate (seal wear) |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent | Less frequent |
| **Efficiency** | Lower (~75–85%) | Higher (~85–92%) |
| **Torque capacity** | Limited | Higher; suitable for larger vessels |
| **Compactness** | Long axial length | Compact axial design |

---

#### Question B1 (b) [7 marks]
**With reference to SOLAS regulations, state the design requirements for the steering gear of a vessel of 100,000 GT proceeding at 25 knots. List the various arrangements of steering gear control positions as required by SOLAS.**

**Model Answer:**

**SOLAS Design Requirements (4 marks):**

For a 100,000 GT vessel at 25 knots:
1. **Two independent power units** are mandatory – one operational, one standby. Each unit must be capable of putting the rudder over from **35° to 35° in not more than 28 seconds** at the maximum service speed.
2. The steering gear must be capable of withstanding the **1.5× calculated torque** under worst-case conditions.
3. **Auxiliary steering gear** is required if the main gear cannot be repaired at sea; it should give **adequate maneuverability** at a navigable speed.
4. **Power supply redundancy**: Two independent sources (main bus + emergency generator).
5. **Automatic changeover** in case of failure of the duty pump unit.
6. **Rudder angle indicator** at the bridge and steering flat.

**Control Position Arrangements (3 marks):**

SOLAS requires the following **steering control positions**:
1. **Bridge wheelhouse** (primary control) – with wheel or tiller pilot.
2. **Bridge wing control stations** (port and starboard) – for maneuvering in port.
3. **Steering flat / steering gear room** – emergency local control.
4. **Emergency fireman's control** – adjacent to the steering gear for use if the steering flat is inaccessible due to fire.
5. **Changeover arrangements** between control positions, with clear indication of which position is in command.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU 2024 Steering Gear Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.5× SOLAS safety multiplier** on torque calculations → 2 marks lost.
> 2. **Using outer radius (R_o) instead of mean radius (R_m)** for vane torque lever arm → entire question becomes incorrect.
> 3. **Mixing up vane chamber count**: For a 3-vane rotor, students often count 2 chambers instead of 3 (or vice versa) → depends on port plate design; KTU expects **N active chambers = number of vanes** in the standard 3-vane configuration.
> 4. **Confusing double-acting stroke frequency**: Steam gear calculation must use **2× rpm** for double-acting.
> 5. **Direction of rudder motion**: A common error is mapping hydraulic pressure to the wrong cylinder (e.g., "pressurize port cylinder → rudder goes port" when in fact pressurizing the port cylinder pushes the crosshead one way and rotates the rudder to **starboard**).
> 6. **Forgetting to convert knots to m/s** in hydrodynamic calculations → entire numerical answer wrong.
> 7. **Mistaking Rapson Slide for friction drive vs. hydraulic grip**: KTU expects the explanation that "slipper friction grips sliding block" — not a hydraulic ram pushing the rudder directly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### ⭐ High-Yield Concepts
- [x] **SOLAS mandates 2 independent power units** for ships > 10,000 GT; each must achieve 35°-35° in **≤ 28 sec** at max service speed.
- [x] **Hard-over torque = 1.5 × $C_R \cdot 0.5 \rho V^2 A_R r$** (always multiply by 1.5).
- [x] **Hand steering gear** is permitted only on vessels < 70 GT; elsewhere it is **emergency backup only**.
- [x] **Steam steering gear** is a **reciprocating double-acting cylinder** with piston rod → crosshead → connecting rod → tiller arrangement.
- [x] **Rapson Slide** uses **friction grip** (slipper on slide) to convert hydraulic force to rudder torque; limited to small/medium vessels.
- [x] **Rotary Vane** uses **hydraulic pressure differential on vane surfaces**; compact, efficient, suitable for large vessels.
- [x] **Four-Ram / Two-Ram** uses **hydraulic rams on crosshead**; high torque capacity for VLCCs and warships.
- [x] **Electro-hydraulic** = modern standard: electric motor + pump + hydraulic actuator + follow-up electronic control.
- [x] **Follow-Up (FU)** control: rudder auto-positions to ordered angle; **Non-Follow-Up (NFU)**: rudder moves only while control lever is held.
- [x] **Telemotor**: a hydraulic transmitter that conveys the helm order from bridge to steering gear with high fidelity and minimal lag.
- [x] **Rudder force coefficient $C_R$** is typically 0.035 to 0.055 depending on profile.
- [x] **Sea water density** = **1025 kg/m³**; standard value in all KTU numerical problems.
- [x] **Conversion: 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s** — remember this for every speed-based question.
- [x] **Hydraulic working pressure** typically **80–200 bar** in modern steering gears.
- [x] **Vane torque formula**: $T = \Delta p \cdot L \cdot R_m \cdot (R_o - R_i)$ — **mean radius**, not outer radius.
- [x] **SOLAS Reg. 29.7** mandates the 1.5× torque multiplier — this is **not** a designer's choice, it is regulatory.

### 🔑 KTU 2024 Likely Exam Traps
- Numerical questions often test the **5-step SOLAS torque calculation** with the 1.5 multiplier. Practice writing all 5 steps explicitly.
- Compare-contrast questions on **Rapson Slide vs Rotary Vane** are very common (asked in 2022, 2023, and 2024).
- Short notes on **steam steering gear** and **telemotor systems** are frequent 3-mark questions.
- Drawing a **labeled sketch** of a Rapson Slide or Rotary Vane is worth up to 4 marks in long-answer questions.

### 📚 Key Definitions for Quick Reference
| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Steering gear** | Complete machinery to rotate the rudder |
| **Telemotor** | Hydraulic transmitter of helm order to steering gear |
| **Tiller** | Lever arm on top of rudder stock |
| **Rapson Slide** | Friction-grip hydraulic steering gear |
| **Rotary Vane** | Hydraulic steering gear with vane rotor on rudder stock |
| **Follow-Up** | Auto-positioning control mode |
| **Non-Follow-Up (NFU)** | Manual hold-to-move control mode |
| **Hard-over** | Rudder at maximum angle (35°) |
| **SOLAS torque** | 1.5× calculated hydrodynamic torque |
| **Balanced rudder** | Stock forward of mid-chord; reduces torque demand |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ship Stabilizers: Types, Operations of Different Types of Passive and Active Ship Stabilizing Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST503 - MARINE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ship Stabilizers: Types, Operations of Different Types of Passive and Active Ship Stabilizing Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ship Stabilizer:** A ship stabilizer is a hydrodynamic / mechanical device or system fitted on a vessel, either externally on the hull or internally within the hull structure, that generates a counteracting moment, force, or restoring couple about the longitudinal axis of the ship to **reduce the amplitude and frequency of roll motion** caused by waves, wind gusts, maneuvering, or asymmetric loading.

In the language of **NEP-2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** adopted by KTU, the study of ship stabilizers enables the student to map three engineering competencies simultaneously:
- **CO 4.1 (Remembering/Understanding):** Classify stabilizers and recall the physical principles behind each.
- **CO 4.2 (Applying/Analyzing):** Evaluate roll reduction performance from first principles (formulas, lever arms, hydrodynamic coefficients).
- **CO 4.3 (Evaluating):** Critically compare passive vs. active systems for a given vessel service profile (warship, cruise, cargo, ferry).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the topic in the syllabus?** Rolling is the single most uncomfortable and dangerous motion of a ship. The International Maritime Organization (IMO), through the **Code on Intact Stability (MSC.1/Circ.1627)** and the **STCW Convention**, treats excessive roll as a casualty-causing hazard (cargo shift, capsize, crew injury). Stabilizers are therefore a **mandatory or strongly recommended engineering subsystem** on passenger ships, naval combatants, research vessels, and cable-layers.

### 1.2 The Rolling Problem – Why Ships Roll

A floating ship is a free body with **six degrees of freedom**: Surge, Sway, Heave, Roll, Pitch, Yaw. **Roll** is the rotation about the longitudinal (fore–aft) axis, and is excited by:
- Wave moments (dominant excitation in beam/quartering seas)
- Wind heeling moment (sail area × wind pressure × lever)
- Asymmetric cargo or flooding
- Turning (centrifugal moment combined with free-surface effect)

The mathematical model of ship roll is a forced, damped, single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) oscillator:

$$I_{xx}\,\ddot{\varphi} + B_{rr}\,\dot{\varphi} + \Delta\,g\,\overline{GM}\,\varphi = M_{\text{wave}}(t)$$

where $\varphi$ is the roll angle in radians, $I_{xx}$ is the moment of inertia about the longitudinal axis through G, $B_{rr}$ is the damping coefficient (N·m·s/rad), $\Delta$ is the displacement, $\overline{GM}$ is the **metacentric height** (m), and $M_{\text{wave}}$ is the wave-induced external moment.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Quick Reference:** If the exam asks *"Why is a stabilizer required even if GM is sufficient for stability?"* the answer is that **GM controls the *stiffness* (righting moment per radian) but not the *damping***. A ship with high GM and zero damping will still roll violently in resonance. Stabilizers primarily add **damping and negative excitation** — they target roll amplitude, not metacentric height.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (First-Reading Friendly)

Imagine you are walking on a **2-inch wide balance beam** in a gymnasium. You instinctively:

| Human Reflex | Equivalent Ship System |
|---|---|
| Outstretch arms sideways | **Bilge keels** – small fins welded to the hull, dragging through water, dissipating roll energy |
| Shift body weight against tilt | **Anti-Roll Tank (ART)** – water sloshing from the low side to the high side, exactly out of phase with the wave |
| Use a long pole with weights at the ends | **Fin Stabilizer** – hydrodynamic wing generating a counter-force far from the roll axis |
| Spin a bicycle wheel on a hand-grip | **Gyroscopic Stabilizer** – a heavy spinning disc whose precession resists the roll torque |

> [!NOTE]
> The **passive systems** are like the outstretched arms – they work *automatically, without any external energy input*, but their roll-reduction capability is moderate (typically 20–40 %).
> The **active systems** are like the hand-spoked bicycle wheel – they require **hydraulic, electric, or kinetic energy** to operate, but they provide significantly higher roll reduction (up to 90 % in modern fin systems).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used by KTU / Classification Societies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics (KTU expects familiarity):**
> - **Roll Reduction Ratio (RRR):** $\eta = \dfrac{\varphi_{\text{without}}}{\varphi_{\text{with}}} \times 100\%$
> - **Stabilizer Effectiveness Index (SEI):** $\text{SEI} = \dfrac{\text{Roll moment generated by device}}{\text{Wave moment at design sea state}}$
> - **Critical Roll Damping Coefficient ($B_c$):** minimum damping required to limit roll to IMO Code values (typically ≤ **20°** in any condition, ≤ **10°** in regular service).
> - **Natural Roll Period ($T_\varphi$):** 2π·k/√(g·GM) — measured in **seconds**, ideally **not equal to wave encounter period** (to avoid resonance).
> - **Lever Arm ($l_r$):** vertical distance between the device's center of action and the roll axis (G), measured in **metres (m)**.
> - **Design Speed:** Stabilizers are designed for a specific ship speed envelope (e.g., fin stabilizers: 8–35 knots).

### 1.5 IMO / Class Society Stability Limits (Recall Block)

> [!NOTE]
> For a KTU 2014/2024 question on "regulatory need":
> - IMO MSC.1/Circ.1627: area under GZ curve ≥ 0.055 m·rad up to 30°, and ≥ 0.09 m·rad up to 40°, or down-flooding angle.
> - Weather Criterion: Righting energy in roll between 0° and 30° must be ≥ 1.4 times the heeling energy from a gusting beam wind.
> - SOLAS Ch. II-1: every passenger ship must demonstrate compliance; for **Ro-Ro passenger ships**, a **specific gravity equal to 1** is required for the damage-stability check — stabilizers are part of the *intact* stability demonstration.

This regulatory backdrop is what makes stabilizers a *systems-engineering* subject in Module 4 of PCNST503, not just a machinery topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Ship Stabilizers (Master Taxonomy)

The two **top-level families** accepted universally (and demanded by KTU 2024 Module-4 syllabus) are:

1. **Passive Stabilizers** – operate *without* external energy input; work by *extracting kinetic energy* from the roll motion.
2. **Active Stabilizers** – require *external energy* (hydraulic / electric / kinetic) to generate a *counter-roll moment*.

A third (sometimes-listed) category is **Hybrid / Controllable Passive** – e.g., *flap-type bilge keels* with controllable incidence, considered by IACS as a *semi-active* hybrid. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly groups these under "passive" since their principal function remains energy dissipation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory aid (KTU exam technique):** If a question says *"without external power"* → Passive. *"Requires hydraulic pump / motor / spinning flywheel"* → Active. *"Controllable flap" → semi-active hybrid*, classify under passive in KTU scheme.

### 2.2 PASSIVE STABILIZERS — Detailed Operating Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Bilge Keels (most common passive device)

A bilge keel is a long, narrow, flat fin (typically **300–1200 mm** long along ship length, **150–300 mm** projected depth, **6–12 mm** plate thickness) welded to the turn-of-the-bilge on each side of the hull, running roughly 30–60 % of the ship's length Lpp.

**Physical principle:**
- During roll, the bilge keel on the rising side moves *upward + outward* through the water → creates a **velocity-dependent pressure field** on the keel.
- Pressure on the leading face (forward face of fin) is higher than on the trailing face, producing a **drag-type force** *opposing* the upward motion.
- The two bilge keels (port + starboard) create a **couple** about the roll axis.
- This couple is *dissipative* (out of phase with roll velocity), so it acts as a **damping moment**, not a stiffness moment.

**Step-by-step operation logic:**
1. Ship begins to roll to starboard with angular velocity $\dot{\varphi}$.
2. Port bilge keel is rising; its leading face sees higher pressure → drag force $F_D$ acts **downward** on port keel.
3. Starboard bilge keel is sinking; its drag force acts **upward** on starboard.
4. The two forces form a couple opposing the roll motion → **damping moment** $M_{bk} = -B_{rr}^{bk}\cdot\dot{\varphi}$.
5. Bilge keel is purely a **damping** device; it does **not** provide a righting (stiffness) moment.

**Numerical insight:**
- Effective damping coefficient added: $B_{rr}^{bk} \approx (2/3)\,\rho\,A_{bk}\,l_{r}^{2}\,V$ at moderate speeds, where $A_{bk}$ is projected area of one keel, $l_r$ is its lever arm from G.
- Typical roll reduction: **20–35 %** of unstabilized roll amplitude.
- **Zero effective power required** — operates on the roll energy itself.

**Advantages / Disadvantages (KTU 2-mark expected):**

| Aspect | Bilge Keel |
|---|---|
| Cost | Very low (fabrication only) |
| Maintenance | Almost zero (no moving parts) |
| Weight | Negligible |
| Effectiveness in harbor / zero speed | **Nil** — relies on translational water velocity to produce drag |
| Risk | Slight increase in hull resistance (typically 0.5–2 % in calm water) |
| Typical vessels | Tankers, bulk carriers, naval auxiliaries |

#### 2.2.2 Hull Form-Based Passive Stabilization

A **flared / bulged hull** at the deck edge increases the righting moment at large heel angles (because the centre of buoyancy shifts *outward* in a flared section), giving extra stability. The **free-surface effect** in partially filled tanks can also act *adversely* — anti-roll tanks exploit it *positively* (covered later).

> [!NOTE]
> Hull-form features (spray strakes, chine, sheer) are sometimes called **"passive"** stabilizers, but strictly they are **stiffness modifiers**, not stabilizers. KTU expects you to know the distinction.

### 2.3 ACTIVE STABILIZERS — Detailed Operating Analysis

#### 2.3.1 Fin Stabilizers (the workhorse of modern shipping)

A fin stabilizer is a **hydrodynamic wing** (NACA 0015 / NACA 0024 profile, typical span 1.5–4 m, chord 0.8–1.5 m) mounted on the **turn of the bilge** amidships, retractable into a fin box inside the hull, capable of being rotated about its longitudinal axis (the *trailing-edge flap* or *full-foil rotation*).

**Operating principle:**
1. Ship moves forward at speed $V$ (forward speed, m/s).
2. Fin presents an angle of attack $\alpha$ to the oncoming water.
3. Hydrodynamic lift $L$ is generated normal to the flow.
4. Lift is approximately: $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^2\,S_f\,C_L(\alpha)$, where $S_f$ is the fin planform area and $C_L$ is the lift coefficient.
5. The lift acts at the fin centre, which is at vertical distance $l_r$ (typically **0.7 T** to **1.0 T**, where T is draft) below G.
6. The **fin moment** about G is $M_f = L \cdot l_r$.
7. By reversing $\alpha$ (flap rotation) on the **opposite** fin during the **opposite** half-roll, the fin moment *opposes* the wave moment at all phases.
8. The fin's $\alpha$ is servo-controlled by a **roll-rate gyroscope** that senses the instantaneous roll velocity and commands the fin actuator to lead the wave by 90° for maximum damping.

**Sub-classifications of fin stabilizers (KTU must know):**
- **Non-retractable (fixed) fins** — warships, older ferries; minimal maintenance, slight drag penalty.
- **Retractable fins** — cruise liners, cargo vessels; retracted in harbor to avoid damage and resistance.
- **Full-retraction vs. partial-retraction** — some modern systems (e.g., Naiad, Rolls-Royce) retract fully into a streamlined pod.
- **Stabilizer fin with flap** — small main foil + large trailing-edge flap (a *2-element airfoil*), giving high lift at low speed.

**Step-by-step operation (control loop):**
1. **Sensor (gyroscope)** measures $\dot{\varphi}$ (roll rate) and $\varphi$ (roll angle).
2. **Controller (PID or lead-lag network)** computes required fin angle $\alpha_{\text{cmd}}$.
3. **Hydraulic actuator** rotates the fin to $\alpha_{\text{cmd}}$.
4. **Fin produces lift L**, generating stabilizing moment $M_f$.
5. **Roll amplitude decreases** → feedback loop closes.

**Typical performance:**
- Roll reduction **80–90 %** in design speed envelope.
- Effective at forward speed > 8–10 knots; below this, lift collapses ($L \propto V^2$).
- **Power consumption:** 30–75 kW hydraulic power per fin pair (warship) up to 300 kW (large cruise).

**Forces diagram (textual):**
- Port fin: water flow from forward → angle of attack produces lift *upward* → moment counteracts roll to port.
- Starboard fin: water flow from forward → angle of attack produces lift *downward* → moment counteracts roll to starboard.
- The two lifts *always oppose* the roll direction; their signs are reversed by the controller twice per roll cycle.

#### 2.3.2 Anti-Roll Tanks (ART)

An anti-roll tank is a **partially or fully filled U-shaped water reservoir** fitted athwartships (cross-ship) inside the hull, often in the double-bottom or wing tanks. The water in the tank is free to slosh from one side to the other.

**Operating principle:**
- The natural period of water sloshing in the U-tube is **tuned to match the ship's natural roll period** $T_\varphi$.
- When the ship rolls to starboard, the water (due to inertia) momentarily moves *toward the port* side of the tank.
- This mass shift creates a moment that **opposes** the wave-induced roll.
- The water effectively acts as a *moving weight* generating a counter-couple.

**Three variants (KTU must distinguish):**
1. **Free-surface tank (Flume / Free-surface Anti-Roll Tank):** Partially filled rectangular tank with a central baffle. Sloshing is the energy-dissipating mechanism. Self-contained, no external power.
2. **U-tube tank (Intersonde / Brown Brothers / Vosper):** Two side chambers connected by a duct at the bottom. The water column oscillates like a pendulum.
3. **Passive Controlled tank (PACT):** A U-tube fitted with a *throttle valve* in the bottom duct to vary damping, controlled by the ship's roll sensor.
4. **Active / Pumped anti-roll tank (Toshiba / Mitsubishi):** Pumps drive water from one side to the other, with active control.

**Tuning equation (KTU 2024 high-yield):**

The U-tube has a natural period:
$$T_t = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{l_t + l_s}{2g}}$$

where $l_t$ is the length of the water column measured along the centre-line of the duct, and $l_s$ accounts for water added mass effects. The tank is **tuned** such that $T_t = T_\varphi$ (ship's natural roll period). When the two periods are equal, the tank moves in **anti-phase** to the ship, producing maximum damping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial distinction for KTU:** Anti-roll tanks are sometimes misclassified as *active* because modern ones have pumps / valves. In the KTU 2024 scheme, classify by the **dominant physics**:
> - *Free-surface tank, plain U-tube, PACT* (no pump) → **Passive** (or *controllable passive*).
> - *Pumped active tank* (with active control) → **Active**.

#### 2.3.3 Gyroscopic Stabilizers

A heavy flywheel spinning at very high speed (typically **3000–10000 rpm**) is mounted in gimbals. When the ship rolls, the flywheel's spin axis precesses; by **Newton's third law**, the precession creates a **torque** on the ship that resists the rolling motion.

**Key formulas:**

Spin angular momentum: $H = I_f\,\omega_f$

Applied roll torque from ship: $T_s$

Precession rate produced: $\Omega_p = T_s / H$

The required counter-torque is supplied by the gimbal motor, but the *kinetic energy* comes from the flywheel (which must be **spun up** by an electric motor initially).

**Advantages:**
- Effective at **zero forward speed** (unique advantage over fins).
- Useful for submarines, yachts, and small warships.
- Almost no appendage drag.

**Disadvantages:**
- Very high gyroscopic forces during turn → restricted turn rate, design penalty.
- Heavy (1–5 % of displacement).
- Limited effectiveness in violent seas; complex.

#### 2.3.4 Rudder / Steering-Gear Roll Stabilization

Modern integrated bridge systems can use the **rudder itself** as a roll-stabilizer: by applying small, fast rudder deflections in *anti-phase* with the roll, a yaw-roll coupling produces a roll-reducing moment.

**Operating principle:**
- The ship's velocity $V$ and a rudder angle $\delta$ produce a **lateral force** $F = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^2\,A_R\,C_Y(\delta)$.
- A small $\delta$ modulated at roll frequency produces a small lateral force → a small **roll moment** through the offset of the rudder force from the roll axis.
- Used in conjunction with an autopilot; fuel penalty is small.

This is a **free-by-product** of auto-steering, not a dedicated device, but KTU 2024 expects awareness.

#### 2.3.5 Comparison Snapshot (for KTU table-type questions)

| Stabilizer Type | Active / Passive | Power Required | Speed Range | Roll Reduction | Typical Vessel |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bilge keel | Passive | None | Any (small) | 20–35 % | Tankers, bulk carriers |
| Hull form flare | Passive | None | Any | 5–10 % | All |
| Free-surface ART | Passive | None | Any | 30–50 % | Ferries, Ro-Ro |
| U-tube ART (uncontrolled) | Passive | None | Any | 30–60 % | Ferries, naval auxiliaries |
| PACT (U-tube + valve) | Controllable passive | < 5 kW | Any | 40–70 % | Cruise ships, ferries |
| Pumped ART | Active | 50–200 kW | Any | 50–80 % | Large cruise |
| Retractable fin | Active | 75–300 kW | > 8 kn | 80–90 % | Cruise, naval |
| Non-retractable fin | Active | 75–300 kW | > 8 kn | 80–90 % | Warships |
| Gyroscopic | Active | 50–200 kW | Any | 50–70 % | Submarines, yachts |
| Rudder roll | Active (parasitic) | Negligible | > 6 kn | 20–40 % | All modern vessels |

### 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use the formula below as the definitive reference for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments.** All quantities are in **SI units (m, kg, s, N, rad)** unless explicitly stated. Pipe symbols $\vert$ and $\mid$ are used in place of `|` to avoid breaking the markdown table.

| # | Formula | Symbol / Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_\varphi = 2\pi k / \sqrt{g\,\overline{GM}}$ | Natural roll period; $k$ = transverse radius of gyration, $\overline{GM}$ = metacentric height | s |
| 2 | $M_{\text{right}} = \Delta \cdot g \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin\varphi$ | Righting moment at heel $\varphi$ | N·m |
| 3 | $M_{\text{wave}} \approx 0.55 \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot \zeta^2 \cdot B^2 \cdot L$ | Wave-exciting roll moment; $\zeta$ = wave amplitude, $B$ = beam, $L$ = Lpp | N·m |
| 4 | $I_{xx} \approx (\Delta / g)\,(k^2 + \overline{KG}^2)$ | Transverse moment of inertia (added-mass neglected) | kg·m² |
| 5 | $B_{rr}^{bk} \approx (2/3)\,\rho\,A_{bk}\,l_r^{2}\,V$ | Bilge-keel damping coefficient | N·m·s/rad |
| 6 | $L_f = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^2\,S_f\,C_L(\alpha)$ | Lift on a fin at angle of attack $\alpha$ | N |
| 7 | $C_L(\alpha) \approx a_0 + 2\pi\,\alpha$ (small $\alpha$) | Linear lift-coefficient approximation, $a_0 \approx 0$, slope 2π/rad | – |
| 8 | $M_f = L_f \cdot l_r$ | Stabilizing moment from a single fin (lever arm $l_r$) | N·m |
| 9 | $\eta = (\varphi_{\text{no}} - \varphi_{\text{yes}}) / \varphi_{\text{no}} \times 100\%$ | Roll Reduction Ratio (RRR) | % |
| 10 | $T_t = 2\pi \sqrt{(l_t + l_s)/(2g)}$ | Natural period of a U-tube ART | s |
| 11 | $M_{ART} = m_w \cdot a_{w,\text{tangential}} \cdot d$ | Moment from sloshing water; $m_w$ mass, $d$ offset, $a_w$ tangential accel. | N·m |
| 12 | $H = I_f\,\omega_f$ | Angular momentum of gyroscopic flywheel | kg·m²/s |
| 13 | $\Omega_p = T_{\text{applied}} / H$ | Precession rate of a gyroscope | rad/s |
| 14 | $F_{rudder} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^2\,A_R\,C_Y(\delta)$ | Rudder lateral force | N |
| 15 | $W_{\text{ext}} = E_{k,\text{ship}} - E_{k,\text{ship,stable}}$ | External work done by stabilizer per roll cycle | J |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU 2024 NEP-2020 context:** Marine Engineering is a *systemic* discipline. The same principles (damping, restoring moment, hydrodynamic lift, gyroscopic precession) are reused in:
> - **Offshore platform design** (spar, semi-submersible roll motion in hurricanes).
> - **Aircraft design** (Dutch roll damping — a gyroscopic problem).
> - **Floating wind turbines** (active nacelle yaw control uses fin-stabilizer logic).
> - **Submarine trim tanks** (anti-roll concept inverted for depth control).
> Recognising these cross-domain mappings earns CO-4 attainment points in KTU Bloom-level-3 / 4 questions.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Goal of this Section:** KTU 2024 Scheme is shifting from rote formula recall to **derivation + numerical problem solving**. The three derivations below are the *core engineer's toolkit* for the topic. Practice them with a calculator and the formula sheet from Section 2.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Natural Roll Period $T_\varphi$ of a Ship

**Setup:** A ship of mass $m$ and mass moment of inertia about roll axis $I_{xx}$ is displaced from equilibrium by a small roll angle $\varphi$. Restoring moment is provided by gravity acting on the displaced buoyancy centre B' relative to G.

**Step 1 — Identify the restoring moment.**

For small angles, $\sin\varphi \approx \varphi$. The righting arm is GZ = $\overline{GM}\sin\varphi \approx \overline{GM}\,\varphi$. The righting moment is therefore:
$$M_{\text{right}} = W \cdot \text{GZ} = \Delta\,g \cdot \overline{GM}\,\varphi$$

where $\Delta$ is the displacement (mass) in kg, and $g$ is gravitational acceleration in m/s².

**Step 2 — Identify the moment of inertia about the roll axis.**

For a homogeneous ship, the transverse radius of gyration is $k$ (m), so:
$$I_{xx} = m \cdot k^{2} = \frac{\Delta}{g}\,k^{2}$$

Added water (virtual mass) is neglected in this simplified derivation; KTU 2024 accepts the simplification unless the problem statement says "include added mass".

**Step 3 — Write Newton's rotational equation of motion.**

$$I_{xx}\,\ddot{\varphi} = -M_{\text{right}} = -\Delta\,g\,\overline{GM}\,\varphi$$

(The negative sign denotes that the moment opposes the displacement.)

**Step 4 — Rearrange into the standard SHM form.**

$$\ddot{\varphi} + \underbrace{\left(\dfrac{\Delta\,g\,\overline{GM}}{I_{xx}}\right)}_{\omega_n^{2}}\,\varphi = 0$$

Substituting $I_{xx} = \Delta\,k^{2}/g$:

$$\omega_n^{2} = \dfrac{\Delta\,g\,\overline{GM}}{(\Delta\,k^{2}/g)} = \dfrac{g^{2}\,\overline{GM}}{k^{2}}$$

Therefore:
$$\omega_n = \dfrac{g\,\sqrt{\overline{GM}}}{k}$$

**Step 5 — Convert angular frequency to natural period.**

$$T_\varphi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{k}{g\,\sqrt{\overline{GM}}}$$

$$\boxed{T_\varphi = \dfrac{2\pi\,k}{\sqrt{g\,\overline{GM}}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Quick Sanity Check:** $T_\varphi$ for a typical cargo ship is **8–14 s**, for a warship **5–8 s**, for a fishing vessel **3–5 s**. Period of a wave is **3–10 s**. The closer $T_\varphi$ is to the wave period, the worse the roll → a stabilizer's job is partly to *shift the effective natural period* so resonance is avoided.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Roll Reduction Ratio of a Fin Stabilizer

**Setup:** A ship of displacement $\Delta$ at speed $V$ has a pair of retractable fin stabilizers, each of planform area $S_f$, installed at vertical lever arm $l_r$ below G. The lift coefficient varies linearly with fin angle as $C_L = 2\pi \alpha$ for small $\alpha$ (thin-airfoil theory).

**Step 1 — Write the fin lift.**

$$L_f = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S_f\,C_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S_f\,(2\pi\alpha) = \pi\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S_f\,\alpha$$

**Step 2 — Compute the stabilizing moment from one fin.**

$$M_f = L_f \cdot l_r = \pi\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S_f\,l_r\,\alpha$$

**Step 3 — Maximum stabilising moment.**

The fin is usually restricted to $\alpha_{\max} = \pm 22.5° = \pm 0.393$ rad (mechanical stops). Hence the maximum moment is:
$$M_{f,\max} = \pi\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S_f\,l_r\,\alpha_{\max} \approx 1.235\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S_f\,l_r$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of ship displacement.**

Substituting $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ (seawater) and the lever arm $l_r \approx 0.85\,T$ (T = mean draft), the non-dimensional moment is:
$$\dfrac{M_{f,\max}}{\Delta\,g\,\overline{GM}} = \dfrac{\pi\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S_f\,l_r\,\alpha_{\max}}{\Delta\,g\,\overline{GM}}$$

**Step 5 — Derive the roll reduction ratio.**

If the uncontrolled roll amplitude in waves is $\varphi_0$ and the controlled amplitude is $\varphi_s$, for a linearised damping system the ratio is approximately:
$$\dfrac{\varphi_s}{\varphi_0} \approx \dfrac{1}{1 + (M_{f,\max}/M_{c,\text{crit}})}$$

where $M_{c,\text{crit}}$ is the critical wave moment. KTU accepts the qualitative result:
$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{fin}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\pi\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S_f\,l_r\,\alpha_{\max}}{\Delta\,g\,\overline{GM}}}} \quad (\text{linearised, small roll})$$

**Step 6 — Numerical illustration (KTU 2024 expected style).**

Given: $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $V = 15$ m/s, $S_f = 4$ m² (each), $l_r = 4.5$ m, $\alpha_{\max} = 0.4$ rad, $\Delta = 25{,}000$ t = $2.5\times 10^7$ kg, $\overline{GM} = 1.2$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

Compute $M_{f,\max}$ (single fin):
$$M_{f,\max} = \pi \cdot 1025 \cdot 15^{2} \cdot 4 \cdot 4.5 \cdot 0.4 = \pi \cdot 1025 \cdot 225 \cdot 4 \cdot 4.5 \cdot 0.4$$

$$= \pi \cdot 1025 \cdot 225 \cdot 7.2 = \pi \cdot 1{,}660{,}500 = 5{,}217{,}275 \text{ N·m} \approx 5.22 \text{ MN·m}$$

Compute the righting moment per radian:
$$M_{\text{right}}' = \Delta\,g\,\overline{GM} = 2.5 \times 10^{7} \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1.2 = 2.943 \times 10^{8} \text{ N·m/rad}$$

Compute roll reduction ratio:
$$\eta_{\text{fin}} \approx 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + 5.22 \times 10^{6} / 2.943 \times 10^{8}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.01774} \approx 0.01744 = 1.74\%$$

The above is conservative because we used a *single* fin and a static lift-coefficient; in real service both fins work *anti-phase* and the controller adds *damping*, boosting efficiency to 80–90 %.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Natural Period of a U-tube Anti-Roll Tank

**Setup:** A U-tube anti-roll tank is a mass $m_w$ of water oscillating between two side chambers through a bottom duct. The vertical distance between the water surface centres is $h$ (related to the duct length $l_t$ and side-tank geometry).

**Step 1 — Define coordinates.**

Let $x$ be the horizontal displacement of the water column in the duct (from equilibrium). The water level in the port chamber falls by $x$ and in the starboard chamber rises by $x$ (assuming equal cross-sections).

**Step 2 — Restoring force on the water column.**

The weight of the displaced water column $m_w\,g$ acts at the centre of mass, and a small displacement $x$ creates a head difference of $2x$ (from one side to the other), so the restoring acceleration on the water mass is:
$$a_{\text{rest}} = 2g\,\dfrac{x}{l_t}$$

(For a duct of length $l_t$ with side-chambers of large cross-section.)

**Step 3 — Equation of motion of the water column.**

$$m_w \ddot{x} = -m_w \cdot 2g \cdot \dfrac{x}{l_t}$$

$$\ddot{x} + \dfrac{2g}{l_t}\,x = 0$$

**Step 4 — Natural angular frequency and period.**

$$\omega_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{l_t}}$$

$$\boxed{T_t = 2\pi\,\sqrt{\dfrac{l_t}{2g}}}$$

**Step 5 — Including the water added mass in side tanks.**

Empirically, a correction $l_s$ is added: $T_t = 2\pi\sqrt{(l_t + l_s)/(2g)}$.

**Step 6 — Tuning criterion.**

For maximum anti-roll effectiveness, the tank period is tuned to match the ship's natural roll period:
$$T_t = T_\varphi \implies l_t = 2g\,\left(\dfrac{T_\varphi}{2\pi}\right)^{2}$$

**Numerical illustration:**

For a ferry with $T_\varphi = 8$ s, required duct length:
$$l_t = 2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \left(\dfrac{8}{2\pi}\right)^{2} = 19.62 \cdot (1.273)^{2} = 19.62 \cdot 1.621 = 31.8 \text{ m}$$

This is why anti-roll tanks are large and fitted in **double-bottom spaces**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU 2024 Decision Framework

| Performance Criterion | Bilge Keel | Free-Surface ART | U-tube ART | PACT | Pumped ART | Fin Stabilizer | Gyroscope | Rudder Roll |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Zero-speed effectiveness | Low | High | High | High | High | None | High | None |
| Forward-speed effectiveness | High | High | High | High | High | Very high | High | Moderate |
| Power consumption | 0 | 0 | 0 | < 5 kW | 50–200 kW | 75–300 kW | 50–200 kW | Negligible |
| Initial cost (relative) | 1× | 3× | 5× | 6× | 15× | 20× | 30× | 1.1× |
| Maintenance burden | Minimal | Low | Low | Moderate | High | High | Very high | Low |
| Risk to crew (catastrophic failure) | None | None | None | None | None | Hydraulic burst | Flywheel explosion | None |
| Habitability improvement (passenger comfort) | Low | Moderate | High | High | Very high | Very high | High | Moderate |
| Warship suitability | Yes | No | Limited | Limited | No | Yes (fixed fin) | Yes (with limit) | No |
| Cruise liner suitability | Marginal | Limited | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Limited | Yes |

### 3.5 Comparative Effectiveness Analysis (Why Ships Choose What They Choose)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reasoning framework expected by KTU 2024:**
> - **Tankers / bulk carriers** → bilge keels (cost-driven, no speed sensitivity).
> - **Ro-Ro ferries** → PACT or U-tube (low speed, high comfort requirement, IMO compliance).
> - **Cruise liners** → fin stabilizers (high forward speed, premium comfort).
> - **Naval combatants** → non-retractable fins + fixed bilge keels (redundancy).
> - **Submarines** → gyroscopic (zero forward speed).
> - **Research vessels** → retractable fins + ART (dual system for varied missions).

```mermaid
    waveNodeA[SEA WAVE EXCITATION] -->|MOMENT M WAVE| shipPlantNodeA
    shipPlantNodeA -->|ROLL MOTION PHI| sensorNodeA
    shipPlantNodeA -->|ROLL MOTION PHI| sensorNodeB
    finPlantNodeA -->|STABILIZING MOMENT M FIN| shipPlantNodeA
```

> [!TIP]
> The feedback arrow from `shipPlantNodeA` back to `sensorNodeA` and `sensorNodeB` closes the loop. The summing junction is implicit at the ship roll plant, where the **stabilising moment $M_f$ opposes the wave moment $M_{wave}$**. KTU 2024 examiners expect students to **label the polarity** of opposing signals in their notebook sketch.

### 4.3 Diagram C — U-tube Anti-Roll Tank Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    rollInputNodeA[SHIP ROLL ANGLE PHI T] --> tankPlantNodeA[U TUBE TANK WATER COLUMN]
    rollInputNodeA --> tuningNodeA[TUNING CHECK TANK VS SHIP PERIOD]
    tuningNodeA -->|IF MISMATCH| valveAdjNodeA[ADJUST THROTTLE VALVE IN DUCT]
    valveAdjNodeA --> tankPlantNodeA
    tankPlantNodeA -->|MOMENT M ART| shipRollNodeA[REDUCED SHIP ROLL PHI STABILISED]
    rollInputNodeA -->|RAW EXCITATION| shipRollNodeA
```

### 4.4 Diagram D — Bilge Keel Drag and Damping Schematic (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    rollNodeA[SHIP ROLLING WITH ANGULAR VELOCITY PHI_DOT] --> fluidNodeA[WATER FLOW OVER BILGE KEEL]
    fluidNodeA -->|RELATIVE VELOCITY V REL| dragNodeA[PRESSURE DRAG ON KEEL FACE]
    dragNodeA -->|FORCE F D OPPOSING MOTION| coupleNodeA[COUPLE BETWEEN PORT AND STARBOARD KEEL]
    coupleNodeA -->|DAMPING MOMENT M BK| rollNodeA
    rollNodeA -->|ENERGY DISSIPATED AS HEAT| ambientNodeA[AMBIENT SEA WATER]
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    motorNodeA[ELECTRIC SPIN UP MOTOR] -->|DRIVES| flywheelNodeA[HEAVY FLYWHEEL HIGH RPM]
    flywheelNodeA -->|ANGULAR MOMENTUM H| gimbalNodeA[GIMBAL ASSEMBLY TWO AXIS]
    shipRollNodeA[SHIP ROLL TORQUE T S APPLIED] -->|INDUCES| gimbalNodeA
    gimbalNodeA -->|PRECESSION OMEGA P GENERATES| counterTorqueNodeA[COUNTER TORQUE ON SHIP]
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    startNodeA[START VESSEL SPECIFICATION] --> q1{VESSEL SPEED PROFILE}

    q1 -->|HIGH SPEED OVER 10 KNOTS CRUISE| ansC[FIN STABILIZER]
    q1 -->|VARIABLE SPEED CARGO| ansD[BILGE KEEL PLUS FIN]
    q1 -->|HIGH MANEUVERABILITY NAVY| ansE[NON RETRACTABLE FIN PLUS BILGE KEEL]

    ansB --> costCheckNodeA

    ansD --> costCheckNodeA

    costCheckNodeA -->|ACCEPTED| finalNodeA[FINAL STABILIZER CHOICE]

```

The same five diagrams above, drawn by hand in under 5 minutes, will earn full marks.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution as per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:**
> - Module 4 carries a weightage of 15–20 % in PCNST503 end-semester exam.
> - The two-module combined ESE (Part B) allocates 14 marks per question.
> - 3-mark direct questions come from Part A (typically answerable in 50–60 words).

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
> *"Differentiate between passive and active ship stabilizers. Give two examples of each."*

**Model Answer (≈ 60 words):**

> Passive stabilizers require **no external energy**; they dissipate the roll's kinetic energy as the ship moves through water. Examples: **bilge keels** and **uncontrolled U-tube anti-roll tanks**. Active stabilizers need **external power** (hydraulic, electric) to generate a counter-roll moment. Examples: **retractable fin stabilizers** and **gyroscopic stabilizers**.

**Marking Scheme:**
- 1 mark — Clear definition of passive.
- 1 mark — Two examples of passive.
- 1 mark — Clear definition of active + examples.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Past Pattern]**
> *"Explain why a fin stabilizer is ineffective at zero forward speed. State the speed range for which a fin stabilizer is designed."*

**Model Answer (≈ 55 words):**

> Fin lift is given by $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S_f C_L$. At $V = 0$, lift is **zero**, so no stabilizing moment is produced. Fin stabilizers are typically designed for a forward speed range of **8 to 35 knots** (≈ 4 to 18 m/s), with peak effectiveness around 15–25 knots.

**Marking Scheme:**
- 1.5 marks — Formula and $L \propto V^2$ reasoning.
- 1 mark — Numerical speed range.
- 0.5 mark — Reference to forward speed for naval/cruise design.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module-4 Long-Answer Question (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE 2024 Scheme — Module 4]**

#### ✅ Question Choice A (14 Marks)

> **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **retractable fin stabilizer system** used in modern cruise liners. Include details of the fin profile, actuator, control sensor, and operational envelope. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A passenger ship of displacement $\Delta = 18{,}000$ tonnes, metacentric height $\overline{GM} = 1.0$ m, and transverse radius of gyration $k = 5.0$ m is fitted with a pair of fin stabilizers, each of planform area $S_f = 3.0$ m², lever arm $l_r = 4.0$ m, and maximum fin angle $\alpha_{\max} = 22.5°$. The ship cruises at $V = 14$ m/s. Seawater density $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³. Compute **(i)** the natural roll period $T_\varphi$, **(ii)** the maximum lift on one fin, and **(iii)** the maximum stabilising moment from the pair of fins. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

1. **Construction:** A retractable fin stabilizer consists of (i) a fin box recessed into the hull at the turn of the bilge, amidships; (ii) a **NACA 00-series** (typically NACA 0015 or 0024) hydrodynamic fin, span 1.5–4 m, chord 0.8–1.5 m, retractable through a streamlined slot; (iii) a **hydraulic ram** (or electro-mechanical actuator) rotating the fin about its longitudinal axis; (iv) a **roll-rate gyroscope** mounted on the bridge deck, sometimes supplemented with a roll-angle sensor and accelerometer; (v) a **PID controller** (or modern adaptive controller) that commands fin angle; and (vi) **hydraulic power unit (HPU)** with electric motor, accumulator, and oil reservoir.

2. **Working Principle:** As the ship rolls with angular velocity $\dot\varphi$, the roll-rate gyroscope outputs a voltage proportional to $\dot\varphi$. The controller computes the required fin angle $\alpha_{cmd}$ and drives the hydraulic actuator. The fin, presenting this $\alpha$ to the oncoming water at speed $V$, produces a hydrodynamic lift $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S_f C_L(\alpha)$. The lift acts at vertical distance $l_r$ below G, producing a stabilizing moment $M_f = L \cdot l_r$ that opposes the roll. By reversing $\alpha$ on the opposite fin during the opposite half-cycle, the moment is always *anti-phase* with the wave moment.

3. **Operational Envelope:** Speed: 8–35 knots. Roll period: typically 6–14 s. Maximum fin angle: ±22.5°. Hydraulic power: 75–300 kW. Effectiveness: 80–90 % roll reduction at design speed.

4. **Sketch (text-based):** Draw a side elevation of the ship with fin box, fin in extended position, and arrow indicating $V$ and $L$ direction.

**Model Solution to (b):**

> **(i) Natural roll period:**
>
> $$T_\varphi = \dfrac{2\pi k}{\sqrt{g\,\overline{GM}}} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 5.0}{\sqrt{9.81 \cdot 1.0}} = \dfrac{31.416}{\sqrt{9.81}} = \dfrac{31.416}{3.132} \approx 10.03 \text{ s}$$

> **(ii) Maximum lift on one fin:**
>
> Using $C_L = 2\pi \alpha$ (thin-airfoil), with $\alpha_{\max} = 22.5° = 0.3927$ rad:
> $$C_{L,\max} = 2\pi \cdot 0.3927 = 2.467$$
> $$L_{\max} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S_f\,C_{L,\max} = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 14^{2} \cdot 3.0 \cdot 2.467$$
> $$L_{\max} = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 196 \cdot 3.0 \cdot 2.467 = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 196 \cdot 7.402$$
> $$L_{\max} = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 1450.6 = 743{,}440 \text{ N} \approx 743.4 \text{ kN}$$

> **(iii) Maximum stabilising moment from a pair of fins (acting together in anti-phase):**
>
> Single fin moment: $M_{f,\max} = L_{\max} \cdot l_r = 743{,}440 \cdot 4.0 = 2{,}973{,}760$ N·m $\approx 2.97$ MN·m.
>
> Pair (one up, one down) = $2 \cdot 2.97 = 5.95$ MN·m.

> **Sanity check:** Righting moment per radian $= \Delta g \overline{GM} = 18{,}000{,}000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1.0 = 176.6$ MN·m/rad. The pair moment is $5.95/176.6 = 3.37\%$ of the stiffness — sufficient to give 70–90 % roll reduction with proper phase control (controller is essential).

---

#### ✅ Question Choice B (14 Marks)

> **(a)** Describe with sketches the construction and operating principle of a **U-tube anti-roll tank**. Derive the natural period of water oscillation in the U-tube. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare bilge keels, anti-roll tanks, and fin stabilizers with respect to: **(i)** power consumption, **(ii)** speed envelope, **(iii)** roll reduction efficiency, **(iv)** maintenance, and **(v)** typical vessel application. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

1. **Construction:** A U-tube anti-roll tank consists of two side chambers (port and starboard) located athwartships in the double-bottom or wing tanks, connected at the bottom by a duct (sometimes with a controllable throttle valve in PACT variants). The chambers are partially filled with seawater; the duct and side chambers form a closed U-shape. The water surface in each chamber is free.

2. **Working Principle:** When the ship rolls, the water in the tank (due to inertia and gravity) tends to remain momentarily still. As the ship rolls to starboard, the water in the starboard chamber initially appears to *fall* (in ship-relative frame) and flows through the bottom duct to the port chamber, where it *rises*. This mass shift creates a heeling moment that **opposes** the wave-induced roll. The system is analogous to a pendulum: water mass is the bob, duct length is the pendulum length.

3. **Tuning:** For maximum effectiveness, the natural period of water sloshing $T_t$ is tuned to equal the ship's natural roll period $T_\varphi$ (typically achieved by adjusting duct length $l_t$ and water depth).

4. **Sketch (text-based):** Two vertical chambers on port and starboard, connected by a horizontal bottom duct; arrows showing water flowing from one chamber to the other; ship rolled to one side.

5. **Derivation of natural period:** (See Section 3.3 above for the full derivation; key result is $T_t = 2\pi\sqrt{l_t/(2g)}$.)

**Model Solution to (b) — Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Bilge Keel | Anti-Roll Tank | Fin Stabilizer |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Power consumption | Zero | Zero (uncontrolled); <5 kW (PACT) | 75–300 kW hydraulic |
| (ii) Speed envelope | All speeds, marginal at low speed | All speeds | 8–35 knots |
| (iii) Roll reduction | 20–35 % | 30–70 % | 80–90 % |
| (iv) Maintenance | Almost zero | Low (valve, baffles) | High (HPU, hydraulics) |
| (v) Typical vessel | Tankers, bulk carriers | Ferries, Ro-Ro, naval auxiliaries | Cruise liners, warships |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 PCNST503 Module 4:**
> 1. **Confusing damping with stiffness.** Students often say *"fin stabilizers increase the metacentric height GM."* This is **wrong**. Fin stabilizers add **damping and a counter-moment**, not stiffness. The examiner deducts 1 mark for this confusion.
> 2. **Misclassifying U-tubes as active.** U-tubes with *no* pump are *passive*. Adding a throttle valve (PACT) makes them *controllable passive*, not active. Examiner expects a clear statement of "no external pump ⇒ passive."
> 3. **Forgetting the $V^{2}$ dependence of fin lift.** If the question specifies low speed, the student must explicitly say "lift falls as $V^{2}$" and "fin is ineffective at zero speed." Generic answers lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong sign convention in U-tube derivation.** The head difference is **2x** (fall on one side, rise on the other), not x. Examiner's check: 50 % of students write $T = 2\pi\sqrt{l_t/g}$ instead of $T = 2\pi\sqrt{l_t/(2g)}$.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers.** Always write **seconds**, **metres**, **newtons** in the final answer. A number without a unit is half-credit at best.
> 6. **Treating IMO Code as "optional."** IMO MSC.1/Circ.1627 is *mandatory* for passenger ships and Ro-Ro vessels. Stating it as "recommended" loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Hand-drawn diagrams without arrow labels.** A diagram without a clear indication of force, lever arm, or moment direction is treated as "incomplete" and attracts partial credit only.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (use in last 10 minutes of revision):**

- ☐ **Rolling equation of motion:** $I_{xx}\ddot\varphi + B_{rr}\dot\varphi + \Delta g \overline{GM}\,\varphi = M_{\text{wave}}(t)$.
- ☐ **Natural roll period:** $T_\varphi = 2\pi k / \sqrt{g \overline{GM}}$.
- ☐ **Passive stabilizers** — Bilge keel, hull form, free-surface tank, U-tube tank. **No external power.**
- ☐ **Active stabilizers** — Fin, pumped ART, gyroscope, rudder roll. **Require external power.**
- ☐ **Fin lift:** $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S_f C_L$; **stabilising moment** $= L \cdot l_r$.
- ☐ **Fin lift ∝ V²**, so ineffective at zero / very low speed.
- ☐ **Design speed envelope for fins:** 8–35 knots.
- ☐ **U-tube natural period:** $T_t = 2\pi\sqrt{l_t/(2g)}$; **tuned to $T_\varphi$**.
- ☐ **Gyroscopic moment:** $H = I_f \omega_f$; counter-torque via precession $\Omega_p = T/H$.
- ☐ **Bilge keel damping:** $B_{rr}^{bk} \approx (2/3)\rho A_{bk} l_r^2 V$.
- ☐ **Roll Reduction Ratio (RRR):** $\eta = (\varphi_{\text{no}} - \varphi_{\text{yes}})/\varphi_{\text{no}} \times 100\%$.
- ☐ **IMO Code MSC.1/Circ.1627** — area under GZ curve ≥ 0.055 m·rad up to 30°, ≥ 0.09 m·rad up to 40°.
- ☐ **Vessel-selection rule of thumb:** Submarines → gyroscope. Ferries → ART. Cruise/warships → fin. Tankers → bilge keel.
- ☐ **Modern rudder-roll control** is parasitic on autopilot, not a dedicated device.
- ☐ **Fin profile:** NACA 00-series; typical max angle ±22.5°; typical $C_L = 2\pi \alpha$.
- ☐ **Always include units** in numerical answers; **always label** sign convention in derivations.

> **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Ship Stabilizers.**
